\tau_{inj} = \underbrace{\tau_{base}(N, MAP)}_{\text{base map}} \times \underbrace{F_{warmup}(T_{coolant})}_{\text{cold enrichment}} \times \underbrace{F_{accel}(\dot{m}_{air}/\dot{m}_{air_{ref}})}_{\text{accel enrichment}} \times \underbrace{F_{closed-loop}(O_2)}_{\text{lambda correction}}
$$

Each factor is an *independently calibrated* function, computed every **5 to 10 ms**. This multi-dimensional compensation is **physically impossible** with a mechanical device.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Mechanical vs Electronic Engine Control

This is the *single most important table* a KTU 2024 student should be able to reproduce from memory in the examination:

| Feature | Mechanical (Carburetor) | Electronic (ECU + EFI) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Calibration Flexibility** | Fixed (single jet) | Multi-dimensional 3-D maps | ECU |
| **AFR Accuracy** | $\pm 20$ % error typical | $\pm 1$ % ($\lambda$ closed-loop) | ECU |
| **Response Time** | 100 ms to 1 s | < 10 ms (loop) / < 1 ms (knock) | ECU |
| **Emission Compliance** | BS-II (2001) at best | BS-VI / Euro 6 (2020) | ECU |
| **Fuel Economy** | ~10–12 km/l (typical Indian car) | ~15–22 km/l | ECU |
| **Cold Start Enrichment** | Manual choke (operator-dependent) | Automatic, software-controlled | ECU |
| **Altitude Compensation** | None | Automatic via MAP sensor | ECU |
| **Self-Diagnosis (OBD)** | None | DTCs + MIL + freeze-frame data | ECU |
| **Knock Control** | None (engine damage risk) | Real-time spark retard | ECU |
| **Driveability** | Surges, hesitation, stalls in traffic | Smooth, consistent | ECU |
| **Cost (Initial)** | Low | Higher (offset by fuel savings) | Carburetor |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent tune-ups | Largely self-adaptive | ECU |
| **Adaptive Learning** | None | KAM (Keep Alive Memory) learns wear | ECU |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Time Constant Argument

A KTU 2024 exam favourite. Compute how long a mechanical distributor's centrifugal advance takes to respond versus the ECU.

**Given:**
- Centrifugal advance mechanism: rotational inertia $J = 5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg·m}^2$, viscous damping $b = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{N·m·s/rad}$, spring stiffness $k = 0.5\,\text{N·m/rad}$.
- The mechanism is a 2nd-order rotational system: $J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} + k\theta = T_{driver}$.

**Step 1 — Natural Frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{J}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.5}{5 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{10\,000} = 100\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Damping Ratio:**

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{kJ}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \sqrt{0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2
$$

**Step 3 — Settling Time (within 2 %):**

$$
t_s = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\,\omega_n} = \dfrac{4}{0.2 \times 100} = 0.2\,\text{s} = 200\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with ECU:**

$$
t_{s,ECU} \approx 10\,\text{ms (calculation)} + 5\,\text{ms (actuator delay)} = 15\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Speed of Response Ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{t_{s,mech}}{t_{s,ECU}} = \dfrac{200}{15} \approx 13.3 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion for the Exam:** The mechanical spark advance is roughly **13 times slower** than the ECU. At 6000 rpm, a 200 ms delay corresponds to **20 crank revolutions** of timing error — the engine would be either knocking or running extremely inefficiently. An ECU's 15 ms delay is **less than 2 crank revolutions**, easily fast enough to maintain optimal combustion.

### 3.4 Derivation: The Engine's Optimal Spark Advance as a 2-D Map

The optimum spark advance is a function of two primary variables: **engine speed $N$** and **load (often expressed as manifold absolute pressure, MAP)**. The map is stored in the ECU's flash memory as a 16×16 (or 32×32) lookup table:

$$
\theta_{opt}(N, MAP) = f(N, MAP)
$$

Typical values (in degrees BTDC) for a 1.5 L naturally-aspirated petrol engine:

| MAP (kPa) → / RPM ↓ | 20 (idle) | 40 (cruise) | 60 (part load) | 80 (WOT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1000 rpm** | 12° | 18° | 24° | 30° |
| **2500 rpm** | 18° | 26° | 32° | 38° |
| **4000 rpm** | 24° | 32° | 38° | 42° |
| **6000 rpm** | 28° | 36° | 40° | 44° |

Notice the monotonic increase: **higher RPM and higher load both demand more spark advance**, because there is less time for the flame kernel to grow and propagate. The ECU interpolates *bilinearly* between adjacent cells. A mechanical distributor approximates this surface using two 1-D curves (one for centrifugal advance vs RPM, one for vacuum advance vs MAP) — a fundamentally coarser representation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Electronic Engine Control Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the *complete* closed-loop signal flow for AFR control in a modern Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) system. Every block represents a real physical component on the vehicle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Driver["Driver Accelerator Pedal Input"] --> TPS["Throttle Position Sensor TPS"]
    APPS["Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor"] --> ECU
    TPS --> ECU
    MAFS["Mass Air Flow Sensor MAFS"] --> ECU
    MAPS["Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor MAPS"] --> ECU
    CTS["Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS"] --> ECU
    IATS["Intake Air Temperature Sensor IATS"] --> ECU
    CPS["Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP"] --> ECU
    HEGO["Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen Sensor HEGO"] --> ECU
    KS["Knock Sensor Accelerometer"] --> ECU
    ECU["Engine Control Unit ECU Microprocessor"]
    VCM["Vehicle Control Module VCM"]
    ECU --> FIP["Fuel Injector Pulse Width Command"]
    ECU --> ICOil["Ignition Coil IGBT Driver Command"]
    ECU --> ISCV["Idle Speed Control Valve ISCV"]
    ECU --> VVTcm["Variable Valve Timing Solenoid VVT"]
    ECU --> CanBus["CAN Bus to ABS ESP BCM"]
    FIP --> Engine["Engine Combustion Cylinder"]
    ICOil --> Engine
    Engine --> ExhaustManifold["Exhaust Manifold"]
    ExhaustManifold --> TWC["Three Way Catalytic Converter TWC"]
    TWC --> HEGO
    CanBus --> VCM
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** is the *sensing* domain (operator + environment).
- The **ECU** in the centre is the *decision* domain.
- The **right side** is the *actuation* domain.
- The **bottom loop** is the *feedback* path: the HEGO sensor's voltage signal closes the loop through the catalyst to the engine to the exhaust gas composition back to the sensor.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Five-Layer Justification Stack

This Mermaid diagram visualises the "five layers" argument from Section 2, showing how each engineering layer adds a reason why electronic control is required.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Layer 1 Thermodynamics Three different optimum AFRs for Power Economy Emissions"]
    L2["Layer 2 Engine Dynamics 2.22 ms spark lead at 3000 rpm Mechanical lag 200 ms vs ECU 15 ms"]
    L3["Layer 3 Emissions TWC needs lambda equals 1.00 plus or minus 0.01 95 percent conversion only in this window"]
    L4["Layer 4 Fuel and Environment RON 87 to 98 Altitude 0 to 3000 m Ethanol 0 to 20 percent Knock sensor adaptive retard"]
    L5["Layer 5 Driver Demand and Safety TCS ESC ACC OBD II CAN bus communication"]
    L1 --> Conclusion["Need for Electronic Engine Control ECU with closed loop feedback multi dimensional maps sub 10 ms response"]
    L2 --> Conclusion
    L3 --> Conclusion
    L4 --> Conclusion
    L5 --> Conclusion
```

### 4.3 System-Level Block Diagram — ECU Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INP["Input Signal Conditioning Front End"]
        AnIn["Analog Inputs MAF MAP TPS CTS IATS Battery Voltage"]
        DigIn["Digital Inputs CKP Camshaft Position Sensor Frequency"]
        ComIn["Communication Inputs CAN LIN FlexRay from ABS BCM TCM"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["ECU Core Processing"]
        ADC["Analog to Digital Converter 12 bit 1 MSPS"]
        MUX["Input Multiplexer"]
        CPU["32 bit Microcontroller Infineon Aurix TC3xx"]
        Flash["Flash Memory 4 MB Maps and Calibration"]
        RAM["RAM 512 kB Volatile Working Memory"]
        EEPROM["EEPROM or KAM 64 kB Adaptive Learn Values"]
        WDT["Watchdog Timer Safety"]
    end
    subgraph OUTP["Output Driver Stage"]
        HSD["High Side Driver for Fuel Injector 1 to 4 A"]
        IGBT_DRV["IGBT Driver for Ignition Coil"]
        LS_DRV["Low Side Driver for ISCV Relays"]
        CAN_DRV["CAN Transceiver TJA1051"]
    end
    AnIn --> MUX
    DigIn --> MUX
    ComIn --> CAN_DRV
    MUX --> ADC
    ADC --> CPU
    CPU --> Flash
    CPU --> RAM
    CPU --> EEPROM
    WDT --> CPU
    CPU --> HSD
    CPU --> IGBT_DRV
    CPU --> LS_DRV
    CPU --> CAN_DRV
```

### 4.4 Evolution Timeline — From Carburetor to ECU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["1900s to 1960s Simple Carburetor Fixed Venturi Jet"] --> A2["1970s Feedback Carburetor with Choke and Idle Circuits"]
    A2 --> A3["1980s Mechanical Fuel Injection K Jetronic Bosch 1967 Continuous Injection"]
    A3 --> A4["1990s Electronic Fuel Injection L Jetronic 1973 D Jetronic MAP based M Motronic 1979 combined fuel and spark"]
    A4 --> A5["2000s OBD II Mandated 1996 in US 2018 BS VI in India Multi port injection with closed loop lambda"]
    A5 --> A6["2010s to 2020s Drive by Wire Turbo DI GDI Direct Injection VVT VTG Cylinder Deactivation 48 V Mild Hybrid"]
    A6 --> A7["2025 and Beyond Electrification BEV PHEV FCEV plus residual ICE for hybrids ECU becomes VCU Vehicle Control Unit"]
```

### 4.5 Failure Mode Comparison (Carburetor vs ECU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MECH["Mechanical Carburetor Failure Modes"]
        M1["Vapor lock in summer"] --> M2["Engine stalls in hot idle"]
        M3["Carb ice in cold humid weather"] --> M4["Rough idle hesitation"]
        M5["Worn throttle shaft"] --> M6["Vacuum leak unmetered air"]
        M7["Fouled spark plugs from rich idle"] --> M8["Hard cold start"]
    end
    subgraph ELEC["ECU Mitigation Strategies"]
        E1["Fuel pump pressure regulator maintains 3 bar"] --> E2["Closed loop lambda correction STFT and LTFT"]
        E3["Software controlled purge valve"] --> E4["Adaptive idle learns IACV steps"]
        E5["Knock sensor retards spark 1 degree at a time"] --> E6["OBD II sets P0171 P0174 codes MIL on"]
    end
    MECH --> ELEC
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define Electronic Engine Control. List the three primary engine parameters that are electronically controlled in a modern passenger car.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Electronic Engine Control is a microprocessor-based, closed-loop control system that continuously monitors engine operating conditions via sensors and regulates fuel delivery, ignition timing, and idle speed through actuators to optimise performance, fuel economy, and emissions.

**Three primary parameters (2 marks — 1 mark for each, ½ mark for brief explanation):**

1. **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):** Maintained at $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ for catalyst efficiency, using the HEGO (Lambda) sensor as the feedback element.
2. **Ignition Timing (Spark Advance):** Controlled in real time based on RPM and load, using the crankshaft position sensor for crank-angle synchronous firing.
3. **Idle Speed:** Maintained at the target RPM (typically 700 ± 25 rpm) under varying accessory loads (alternator, AC compressor, power steering) using the Idle Speed Control Valve (ISCV) or electronic throttle body.

*(Other valid parameters: Variable Valve Timing, Exhaust Gas Recirculation, Cam Phasing — but the three above are the KTU-mandated "core three".)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Why is a purely mechanical carburetor unable to meet Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) emission norms?*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A mechanical carburetor fails to meet BS-VI norms for **three interrelated reasons** (1 mark each):

1. **No closed-loop feedback:** A carburetor cannot measure the exhaust gas oxygen content. Without the HEGO (Lambda) sensor, the AFR cannot be held within the narrow $\pm 1$ % window required by the three-way catalytic converter to achieve > 95 % conversion of CO, HC, and NOx. BS-VI limits NOx at 0.06 g/km — only achievable with closed-loop catalyst control.

2. **Fixed calibration across a wide operating envelope:** The carburetor's main jet is calibrated at the factory for one specific RPM/load condition. As derived in Section 3, the carburetor's AFR scales as $\sqrt{\dot{m}_{air}}$, so it drifts out of the lambda window at off-design speeds. Modern BS-VI calibration must hold $\lambda = 1.00$ from 700 to 7000 rpm.

3. **No cold-start or transient enrichment control:** During cold start and warm-up, the engine requires AFRs as rich as 9:1 to 11:1 to run smoothly. A manual choke is operator-dependent and inconsistent. The ECU automatically enriches the mixture based on coolant temperature, ambient air temperature, and battery voltage, reducing cold-start HC emissions by 60–70 %.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**Q.A (a)** *Explain the limitations of a purely mechanical engine control system. Discuss why a closed-loop electronic fuel injection (EFI) system is necessary to comply with modern emission regulations. (7 marks)*

**Q.A (b)** *Describe the architecture of a modern Engine Control Unit (ECU). With a block diagram, explain the function of each major subsystem. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
A mechanical engine control system, as used in carburetted engines manufactured before the early 1990s, relies on fixed orifices, mechanical linkages, centrifugal weights, vacuum diaphragms, and thermal bimetallic strips to manage fuel delivery and ignition timing. While robust and inexpensive, such a system has fundamental limitations that render it inadequate for the simultaneous demands of fuel economy, performance, and emission compliance imposed by BS-VI norms.

**2. The "Five Defects" of Mechanical Control (3 marks — ½ mark each for clear explanation):**

- **Fixed Calibration:** A single jet and a single advance curve cannot be optimal across the 0–7000 rpm and 0–100 % load operating envelope. The AFR drifts out of the stoichiometric window as shown by the $\sqrt{\dot{m}_{air}}$ relationship derived in Section 3.1.
- **No Feedback:** The carburetor is an *open-loop* device — it has no sensor to measure the actual exhaust AFR. Consequently, the three-way catalyst can never reach its > 95 % conversion efficiency window ($\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$).
- **Wear and Drift:** Centrifugal advance weights wear at their pivot pins, vacuum diaphragms develop leaks, and the choke mechanism corrodes. Calibration degrades over a typical service life of 50 000 to 80 000 km, leading to gradually worsening emissions and economy.
- **Slow Response:** The mechanical spark advance has a settling time of ~200 ms (derived in Section 3.3), corresponding to ~20 crank revolutions at 6000 rpm. An ECU's 15 ms response is over 13 times faster.
- **No Self-Diagnosis:** A vacuum leak in a 1990s carburetted engine may take hours of mechanic time to locate. An ECU detects unmetered air within seconds via the lambda sensor and the long-term fuel trim (LTFT) deviation, and stores DTC P0171 (System Too Lean, Bank 1) or P0174 (Bank 2).

**3. Why Closed-Loop EFI is Necessary for BS-VI (2 marks):**
BS-VI norms limit NOx to 0.06 g/km and CO to 1.0 g/km for petrol cars. Achieving these limits requires:
- Lambda held within $\pm 1$ % of stoichiometry (closed-loop feedback from HEGO sensor at ≥ 1 Hz).
- Cold-start HC reduction via software-controlled enrichment, cold-start injection timing, and rapid catalyst light-off (achieved by retarded spark during warm-up to raise exhaust temperature to > 400 °C within 30 s).
- Adaptive knock control to permit the use of maximum brake torque (MBT) spark advance on every cycle, improving thermal efficiency by 2–3 % over a conservative fixed advance.
- OBD-II self-diagnostics mandated for all BS-VI vehicles (AIS-137 / ISO 15031).

**4. Conclusion (1 mark):**
A purely mechanical system is mathematically and thermodynamically incapable of meeting BS-VI / Euro 6 emission limits. The closed-loop EFI + ECU architecture is not merely an improvement — it is the *only* known technology that simultaneously satisfies the conflicting demands of modern automotive regulations.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks:**

**1. ECU Architecture Introduction (1 mark):**
A modern Engine Control Unit (ECU) is a ruggedised, automotive-qualified embedded computer housed in a sealed aluminium enclosure. It typically contains a 32-bit microcontroller (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC3xx, NXP MPC57xx, or Renesas RH850), multi-channel ADC, digital I/O, communication transceivers, and high-current driver stages — all operating over the automotive temperature range of **−40 °C to +125 °C** and withstanding vibration up to 50 g.

**2. Major Subsystems (5 marks — describe each in 1 mark's worth):**

| Subsystem | Function | Real Component Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Microcontroller** | Executes the real-time control algorithm at a loop rate of 5–10 ms | Infineon Aurix TC3xx, 300 MHz, dual-core lockstep |
| **Memory** | Flash for calibration maps, RAM for working data, EEPROM/KAM for adaptive learn values | 4 MB Flash, 512 kB SRAM, 64 kB EEPROM |
| **Input Signal Conditioning** | Amplifies, filters, and digitises analog and digital sensor signals | 12-bit SAR ADC, Schmitt triggers, RC low-pass filters |
| **Output Driver Stage** | Switches the high-current actuators (injectors, coils, valves) | Smart High-Side Driver (HSD), IGBT coil driver, low-side relay drivers |
| **Communication** | Interfaces with the vehicle CAN/LIN/FlexRay bus | TJA1051 CAN transceiver, ISO 11898 compliant |
| **Power Supply** | Derives stable 5 V and 3.3 V from the noisy 12 V battery rail, with reverse-battery and load-dump protection | Buck converter + LDO, EMI filters |
| **Safety & Watchdog** | Hardware watchdog timer resets the μC in case of software lockup, complying with ISO 26262 ASIL-D | External watchdog + window watchdog inside μC |

**3. Working Principle (1 mark):**
Sensors → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Microcontroller (computes control output using lookup maps + feedback) → Driver Stage → Actuators → Engine → Exhaust → Lambda Sensor (closes the loop). The complete cycle runs in 5–10 ms.

**4. Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 block diagram) — (marks allocated within the above 5).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q.B (a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop control of air-fuel ratio in a port fuel injection (PFI) engine. Derive the relationship that shows why a mechanical carburetor's AFR cannot be held constant across the RPM range. (7 marks)*

**Q.B (b)** *Explain the role of the following sensors in electronic engine control: (i) HEGO / Lambda sensor, (ii) Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAFS), (iii) Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP), (iv) Knock Sensor. (7 marks — 1.75 marks each)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The closed-loop AFR control loop in a PFI engine is shown in the diagram from Section 4.1. The signal flow is:
- **Reference input:** Desired AFR = 14.7 (or $\lambda = 1.00$).
- **Controller:** ECU computes the required injector pulse width $\tau_{inj}$ using the base map (RPM, MAP), multiplied by closed-loop correction factor.
- **Plant:** The engine + fuel injector.
- **Disturbance:** Variation in fuel pressure, air temperature, humidity, fuel quality.
- **Feedback sensor:** HEGO (Lambda) sensor in the exhaust stream.
- **Comparator:** Subtraction of measured AFR from desired AFR.

**2. Derivation of Carburetor AFR Drift (3 marks):**

Starting with the air mass flow equation:

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{\eta_v \, \rho_{air} \, V_d \, N}{120}
$$

And the fuel mass flow through a venturi jet:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = C_d A_j \sqrt{2\rho_{fuel}\,\Delta P} \quad \text{with} \quad \Delta P \propto \dot{m}_{air}^{\,2}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \dot{m}_{fuel} = K\,\sqrt{\dot{m}_{air}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
AFR_{carb} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{K\sqrt{\dot{m}_{air}}} = \dfrac{1}{K}\sqrt{\dot{m}_{air}}
$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_{air} \propto N$ (assuming constant $\eta_v$):

$$
AFR_{carb} \propto \sqrt{N}
$$

**Numerical substitution (1 mark):** If $AFR = 14.7$ at $N = 2000\,\text{rpm}$, then at $N = 4000\,\text{rpm}$, $AFR = 14.7 \times \sqrt{2} = 20.8$ (lean by 41 %); at $N = 1000\,\text{rpm}$, $AFR = 14.7 / \sqrt{2} = 10.4$ (rich by 29 %). The catalyst cannot operate at either of these points.

**3. Conclusion (2 marks):**
The square-root dependence of AFR on RPM means a single-jet carburetor *must* deliver a different AFR at every speed. Since the three-way catalyst requires $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$, a mechanical system is fundamentally incapable of meeting BS-VI norms. The ECU solves this by using a 3-D fuel map $\tau_{base}(N, MAP)$ that is itself calibrated to deliver $\lambda = 1.00$ at every operating point, with the HEGO sensor providing continuous closed-loop correction.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) HEGO / Lambda Sensor (1.75 marks):**
A Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen Sensor is a **galvanic / zirconia-based electrochemical cell** installed in the exhaust manifold (or downpipe ahead of the catalyst). It produces a voltage that switches sharply from **~0.1 V (lean)** to **~0.9 V (rich)** as the AFR crosses stoichiometry. The "switching" voltage is fed to the ECU, which adjusts the injector pulse width up or down to keep the average voltage around **0.45 V** (the stoichiometric setpoint). The sensor is heated internally (HO2S heater) so it reaches operating temperature (~350 °C) within 15–30 s of cold start, enabling closed-loop control quickly. Bandwidth: 1–2 Hz typical.

**(ii) Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAFS) (1.75 marks):**
The MAFS measures the *mass* (not just volume) of air entering the engine. Modern hot-film MAF sensors (e.g., Bosch HFM) work by maintaining a heated platinum film at a constant temperature differential above the incoming air; the electrical current required to maintain this differential is directly proportional to the air mass flow. The signal is used as the primary input to the **speed-density** or **mass-airflow** fuel equation:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR_{target}}
$$

Accuracy: ±2 % over the full flow range. Failure of the MAFS triggers DTC P0100–P0103 and forces the ECU into a **fail-safe** strategy using MAP-based estimation.

**(iii) Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) (1.75 marks):**
A **magnetic reluctance** or **Hall-effect** sensor mounted near a 60-minus-2 tooth (or 36-minus-1 tooth) reluctor wheel on the crankshaft. It produces a pulsed waveform that the ECU uses to:
- Compute engine speed $N$ (RPM).
- Determine crank angle for precise ignition timing and sequential injection.
- Detect misfire (OBD-II misfire monitoring, mandated by BS-VI).
The "missing tooth" provides the absolute reference for cylinder #1 TDC. Resolution: better than 0.5° crank angle at all speeds.

**(iv) Knock Sensor (1.75 marks):**
A **wide-band piezoelectric accelerometer** bolted to the engine block (typically between cylinders 2 and 3). It detects the high-frequency (5–20 kHz) vibration signature of **abnormal combustion (knock)**, which occurs when the air-fuel mixture auto-ignites ahead of the flame front. The ECU continuously monitors this signal and, when knock is detected, **retards the spark advance by 1–2° per detection cycle** until the knock disappears, then slowly re-advances. This allows the engine to operate at the **Minimum Spark advance for Best Torque (MBT)** under all conditions, regardless of fuel octane. Failure triggers DTC P0325–P0329.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic**
> 1. **Conflating "electronic fuel injection" with "electronic engine control":** A student who answers "EFI = electronic engine control" loses 1 mark immediately. EFI is one *function* of the EEC; the EEC also includes ignition control, idle control, VVT, OBD, etc.
> 2. **Forgetting the word "closed-loop":** Many emission answers get half-marks because the student describes the sensor but not the *feedback action*. Always say "the HEGO sensor closes the loop on the fuel injector pulse width."
> 3. **Quoting the lambda window incorrectly:** The catalyst window is $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$, NOT $\pm 0.1$. A factor-of-10 error here is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Missing the regulatory context:** The 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to link the "need" to BS-VI norms (introduced 2020) and to OBD-II. A pure technical answer without the regulatory framing is treated as incomplete.
> 5. **Confusing the MAFS with the MAPS:** MAF measures air *mass* flow directly; MAP measures manifold *absolute pressure* (an indirect method using the speed-density equation). Examiners regularly test this distinction.
> 6. **Not labelling the Mermaid-style block diagram in the exam:** In the exam, students should draw and *label* the closed-loop block diagram (Setpoint → ECU → Injector → Engine → Exhaust → Lambda Sensor → Comparator → ECU) — even a hand-drawn version with clear arrow labels fetches full marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — 30-Second Summary for the KTU Exam Hall**
> - **Need for EEC = (Emission Compliance) + (Fuel Economy) + (Performance) + (Driveability) + (Self-Diagnosis) + (Safety Features).**
> - **AFR stoichiometric = 14.7 : 1 (gasoline)**, equivalently $\lambda = 1.00$.
> - **TWC efficiency > 95 %** only when $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ — this is the single most important number.
> - **Carburetor AFR $\propto \sqrt{N}$** — the mathematical proof of why mechanical control fails.
> - **Mechanical spark advance settling time ≈ 200 ms; ECU ≈ 15 ms — a 13× speed advantage.**
> - **ECU cycle time = 5 to 10 ms**; **HEGO sensor switching ≥ 1 Hz**; **knock sensor bandwidth 5–20 kHz.**
> - **BS-VI limits:** CO ≤ 1.0 g/km, NOx ≤ 0.06 g/km (petrol); mandate **OBD-II** with **MIL** and **DTC** storage.
> - **Five Defects of Mechanical Control:** Fixed calibration, no feedback, wear/drift, slow response, no self-diagnosis.
> - **ECU subsystems:** μC + Memory + Input Conditioning + Output Driver + Communication + Power Supply + Watchdog.
> - **Four Core Sensors (must memorise):** HEGO (lambda), MAF, CKP, Knock.
> - **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) example:** P0171 = System Too Lean, Bank 1.
> - **Communication bus:** CAN (ISO 11898), LIN, FlexRay — all ECUs must "speak" CAN.
> - **Closed-loop transfer function:** $G_{cl}(s) = G(s) / (1 + G(s)H(s))$ — the formula for the *existence* of closed-loop control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Sentence Takeaway for a KTU Viva:**
> *"The need for electronic engine control arises because the modern spark-ignition engine must operate within three mutually exclusive AFR optima (power, economy, emissions) across a 10:1 speed range and a wide load/temperature envelope, and only a closed-loop, microprocessor-based ECU with sub-10 ms response time and continuous lambda feedback can satisfy the BS-VI / Euro 6 emission norms (CO ≤ 1.0 g/km, NOx ≤ 0.06 g/km) while simultaneously delivering competitive fuel economy and driveability."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electronic Engine Control System (EECS)** — also referred to as the **Engine Control Unit (ECU) system** or **Engine Management System (EMS)** — is an integrated, real-time, closed-loop electronic control network that continuously monitors the operating state of an internal combustion engine via an array of sensors, processes the acquired data through a microcontroller/processor-based digital controller, and precisely commands electromechanical actuators to regulate critical engine parameters such as **air-fuel ratio (AFR)**, **ignition timing**, **idle speed**, **exhaust gas recirculation**, and **knock suppression**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Snapshot:**  
> *"An Electronic Engine Control System is a microprocessor-based, closed-loop feedback network comprising sensors, an Electronic Control Unit, and actuators, designed to optimize engine performance, fuel economy, drivability, and exhaust emissions simultaneously and in real time."*

In the KTU 2024 framework, the EECS is treated as the **principal subsystem of automotive electronics**, sitting at the heart of every modern On-Board Diagnostics (OBD), Engine Control Module (ECM), and Powertrain Control Module (PCM) architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engine Brain" Metaphor

Imagine the human body:

| Human Body System | Equivalent in EECS | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Brain | Engine Control Unit (ECU) | Decision-making |
| Eyes, Ears, Skin | Sensors (MAP, MAF, CKP, TPS, O2) | Data acquisition |
| Muscles | Actuators (Injector, Ignition Coil, EGR Valve) | Action execution |
| Nervous System | Wiring Harness & Communication Buses (CAN, LIN) | Signal transmission |
| Bloodstream | Fuel, Air, Exhaust | Working medium |

> [!NOTE]  
> Just as the human brain does not consciously think about every heartbeat but constantly monitors and adjusts it, the ECU continuously samples engine conditions thousands of times per second (typically every **1–10 ms**, i.e., sampling rates of **100 Hz to 1 kHz**) and makes micro-corrections to keep the engine running at peak efficiency.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline):** $14.7 : 1$ (mass basis)
- **Combustion Flame Front Speed:** $20$–$30$ m/s
- **Engine Crankshaft Speed Range:** $600$–$7000$ RPM
- **ECU Operating Temperature:** $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+125^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Sensor Sampling Rate:** $100$ Hz – $10$ kHz
- **Communication Bus Speed (CAN):** $250$ kbps – $1$ Mbps
- **Standardized Diagnostic Protocol:** OBD-II (SAE J1962 / ISO 15765)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Motivation for Electronic Engine Control

Before the 1980s, engines relied on **mechanical and pneumatic controls** (carburetors, mechanical distributors, vacuum-advance distributors, port vacuum-driven EGR). These suffered from:

- Poor tolerance to altitude, temperature, and load transients
- Inability to simultaneously optimize **performance**, **fuel economy**, and **emissions** (the so-called *trade-off triangle*).
- Mechanical wear causing long-term calibration drift
- Inability to meet tightening emission norms (Euro-I onward, EPA Tier 1/2/3)

The introduction of **microprocessor-based ECUs** (pioneered by Bosch with the **Motronic** system, **1982–1983**, and the **D-Jetronic** analog system preceding it in **1967**) solved these limitations by enabling **programmable, adaptive, closed-loop control**.

## 2.2 The "How" — Operational Architecture

The EECS operates in a continuous **Sense → Compute → Actuate** loop:

### 2.2.1 Stage 1 — Sensing (Input Phase)
Sensors convert physical/chemical quantities into analog/digital electrical signals:

- **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure):** Measures intake manifold pressure → estimates engine load
- **MAF (Mass Air Flow):** Measures the mass of air entering the engine
- **CKP (Crankshaft Position):** Detects piston TDC and engine RPM
- **CMP (Camshaft Position):** Identifies valve timing and firing order
- **TPS (Throttle Position Sensor):** Reports driver demand
- **ECT (Engine Coolant Temperature):** Measures coolant temperature
- **IAT (Intake Air Temperature):** Measures incoming air temperature
- **O2 / Lambda Sensor:** Measures residual oxygen in exhaust
- **Knock Sensor (Piezoelectric):** Detects engine knock/detonation

### 2.2.2 Stage 2 — Computation (Processing Phase)
The **ECU** consists of:

1. **Microcontroller/DSP core** (typically 32-bit, e.g., Infineon Aurix TC2xx/TC3xx, NXP MPC5xxx, Renesas RH850)
2. **ROM/Flash memory** for calibration maps (3-D lookup tables)
3. **RAM** for transient variables
4. **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter) — usually 10–12 bit resolution
5. **Input Capture & PWM modules** for sensor timing and actuator control
6. **Communication controllers** (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet)

### 2.2.3 Stage 3 — Actuation (Output Phase)
Actuators execute commands from the ECU:

- **Fuel Injector** (solenoid or piezo) — pulses with $T_{\text{inj}}$ duration
- **Ignition Coil** (DIS or COP — Coil on Plug) — fires at precisely $\theta_{\text{ign}}$
- **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)**
- **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)**
- **EGR Valve, Variable Valve Timing (VVT) Solenoids**
- **Variable Geometry Turbo (VGT) Actuator**
- **Evaporative Emission (EVAP) Purge Valve**

## 2.3 Closed-Loop vs. Open-Loop Control

| Feature | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop Control |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | No feedback sensor | Uses $\lambda$ (O2) sensor feedback |
| Operating Phase | Cold start, wide-open throttle (WOT), warm-up | Steady-state cruise, light load |
| Accuracy | Lower (calibration-dependent) | Higher (self-correcting) |
| Transient Response | Fast | Slightly slower (PID lag) |
| Stability | Cannot drift (no feedback) | Must ensure stability margins |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> KTU 2024 emphasizes that the **$\lambda$-O2 closed-loop control** is the single most important feedback mechanism in the EECS, allowing the AFR to be maintained within $\pm 1\%$ of the stoichiometric value for proper three-way catalytic converter operation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> The following table is the master reference for all quantitative problems on this topic. Memorize these before every ESE/IA.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_{\text{displaced}} \cdot \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \dfrac{N}{2}$ | Mass air flow rate per cylinder per cycle | kg/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{AFR_{\text{target}}}$ | Required fuel mass flow | kg/s |
| 3 | $T_{\text{inj}} = K_{\text{inj}} \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{comp}}$ | Injector pulse width (compensated) | ms |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Lambda value (equivalence ratio inverse) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta_{\text{ign}} = \theta_{\text{MBT}} - \theta_{\text{knock}} - \theta_{\text{trans}}$ | Spark advance calculation | degrees BTDC |
| 6 | $P_{\text{man}} = f(\text{RPM}, \text{Load})$ | MAP sensor output equation | kPa |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{actual}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless ($\leq 1$) |
| 8 | $E_{\text{ign}} = \dfrac{1}{2} L \left(\dfrac{V_{\text{pri}}}{R}\right)^{2}$ | Stored ignition coil energy | J |
| 9 | $T_{\text{dwell}} = f(V_{\text{batt}}, L, R_{\text{coil}})$ | Coil charging time | ms |
| 10 | $\omega_{\text{engine}} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s |

> [!WARNING]  
> KTU Board Examiner's Tip: In Part B numericals, you will be required to use **Eq. 1, Eq. 3, and Eq. 4** most frequently. Memorize the stoichiometric AFR (14.7) and the equivalence relation $\lambda = 1 \rightarrow$ stoichiometric, $\lambda < 1 \rightarrow$ rich, $\lambda > 1 \rightarrow$ lean.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EECS is the **enabling technology** for every modern automotive regulatory compliance:

- **BS-VI / Euro 6** emission standards are *impossible* without closed-loop AFR control
- **CAFE Corporate Average Fuel Economy** norms are met only through electronic precision
- **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** mandates real-time monitoring of catalyst efficiency, misfire, and EVAP — all performed by the ECU
- **Hybrid and Electric vehicles** use the same ECU architecture for energy management
- **Autonomous driving stacks** rely on ECU data for predictive control

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Air Flow Rate from Engine Speed and Volumetric Efficiency

We derive the rate at which air enters a **4-stroke, 4-cylinder spark-ignition engine** for a given speed and volumetric efficiency.

### Given Assumptions
- 4-stroke engine: 1 working cycle per **2 crank revolutions** per cylinder
- Number of cylinders: $n_c$
- Engine speed: $N$ in RPM
- Total displaced volume: $V_d$ in m³
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_{\text{vol}}$ (dimensionless)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Express the theoretical air volume drawn in **one minute**.
A 4-stroke engine completes $\dfrac{N}{2}$ working cycles per minute per cylinder. Across $n_c$ cylinders:

$$V_{\text{per min}} = \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot V_d \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]$$

**Step 2:** Convert minutes to seconds to obtain a flow rate.

$$\dot{V}_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{per min}}}{60} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_d}{120} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Step 3:** Apply volumetric efficiency to get actual volume flow rate.

$$\dot{V}_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{theoretical}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot V_d}{120}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by air density to obtain mass flow rate.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{actual}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot V_d}{120}$$

**Step 5:** Re-express in the standard textbook form (combined with $N/2$ per cylinder grouping).

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot \dfrac{n_c}{60}}$$

> **Logic Check:** At $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1$, $N = 3000$ RPM, $V_d = 2\text{ L} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³: $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \cdot 1 \cdot 2\times 10^{-3} \cdot 3000/2 / 60 = 0.06$ kg/s — matches standard textbook values.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Injector Pulse Width from Lambda Feedback

The ECU does **not** inject a fixed quantity; it adjusts the pulse width $T_{\text{inj}}$ based on the $\lambda$ sensor voltage.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Target fuel flow from target $\lambda$:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel,target}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{AFR_{\text{stoich}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{target}}}$$

**Step 2:** The injector delivers fuel at a fixed rate $\dot{m}_{\text{inj}}$ (kg/s) per unit pulse. The mass per pulse is:

$$m_{\text{fuel,pulse}} = \dot{m}_{\text{inj}} \cdot T_{\text{inj}}$$

**Step 3:** Equate mass per pulse to the fuel requirement per cylinder event. With one injection per cycle ($N/2 \cdot n_c / 60$ events per second):

$$T_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel,target}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{inj}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N \cdot n_c}{120}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$ from Derivation 1 and include a fuel compensation constant $C_{\text{comp}}$ (for battery voltage correction):

$$\boxed{T_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{air}} \cdot \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot V_d}{\dot{m}_{\text{inj}} \cdot AFR_{\text{stoich}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{target}} \cdot n_c \cdot \dfrac{N}{120}} + C_{\text{comp}}}$$

**Step 5:** Closed-loop correction — the ECU adds a multiplicative trim $K_{\text{ftrim}}$ from the $\lambda$ sensor integrator:

$$T_{\text{inj,closed-loop}} = T_{\text{inj,open-loop}} \cdot K_{\text{ftrim}}$$

> The integrator uses a **PI (Proportional-Integral) law** of the form:
>
> $$K_{\text{ftrim}}(t) = K_P \cdot e(t) + K_I \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$$
>
> where the error is $e(t) = \lambda_{\text{target}} - \lambda_{\text{measured}}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spark Advance Calculation (Ignition Timing)

The optimal spark advance $\theta_{\text{ign}}$ (in degrees Before Top Dead Centre, BTDC) is computed from three terms:

**Step 1:** Base timing from a 3-D lookup table indexed by RPM and engine load:

$$\theta_{\text{base}} = f_{\text{MAP}}(N, \text{Load})$$

**Step 2:** Coolant temperature compensation (advance when cold, retard when hot):

$$\theta_{\text{coolant}} = g(\text{ECT})$$

**Step 3:** Knock-sensor-based retard (if knock is detected, retard by an adaptive amount $\Delta \theta_{\text{knock}}$):

$$\theta_{\text{knock}} = \Delta \theta_{\text{knock}} \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{knock detected}}$$

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{ign}} = \theta_{\text{base}} + \theta_{\text{coolant}} - \theta_{\text{knock}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mechanical vs. Electronic Engine Control

| Performance Metric | Mechanical / Carburetor | Electronic EECS |
|---|---|---|
| AFR accuracy | $\pm 10\%$ | $\pm 1\%$ |
| Cold-start fuel enrichment | Manual choke, crude | Software-mapped, adaptive |
| Ignition timing precision | $\pm 3^{\circ}$ crank | $\pm 0.5^{\circ}$ crank |
| Altitude compensation | None | Automatic via MAP sensor |
| Diagnostics (OBD) | None | Full OBD-II compliance |
| Adaptability to fuel quality | None | Adaptive learning via O2 sensor |
| Component count (analogous) | $\sim 200$ | $\sim 50$ (with sensors) |
| Cost (1970s equivalent) | Lower | Higher (now comparable) |
| Emission compliance | Pre-Euro | BS-VI / Euro-6 capable |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall EECS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air Intake and Fuel Supply] --> B[Engine Cylinder Combustion]
    B --> C[Crankshaft and Camshaft]
    B --> D[Exhaust Manifold]
    
    E[MAP Sensor] --> ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    F[MAF Sensor] --> ECU
    G[CKP Sensor] --> ECU
    H[CMP Sensor] --> ECU
    I[Throttle Position Sensor TPS] --> ECU
    J[Coolant Temp Sensor ECT] --> ECU
    K[Intake Air Temp IAT] --> ECU
    L[Lambda O2 Sensor] --> ECU
    M[Knock Sensor] --> ECU
    
    C --> G
    A --> I
    B --> J
    A --> K
    D --> L
    B --> M
    
    ECU --> N1[Fuel Injector Driver]
    ECU --> N2[Ignition Coil Driver]
    ECU --> N3[Idle Air Control Valve]
    ECU --> N4[Electronic Throttle Body]
    ECU --> N5[EGR Valve]
    ECU --> N6[VVT Solenoid]
    
    N1 --> A
    N2 --> B
    N3 --> A
    N4 --> A
    N5 --> D
    N6 --> A
    
    ECU --> O[CAN Bus to Other ECUs]
    ECU --> P[OBD-II Diagnostic Port]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Diagram:**  
> - The left side shows **sensors** (E through M) reporting into the ECU.  
> - The right side shows **actuators** (N1 through N6) commanded by the ECU.  
> - The center cylinder (B) is the controlled plant.  
> - Feedback arrows from the engine back to sensors close the loop.

---

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Lambda Control Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Lambda Target 1.0] --> sum[Summing Junction]
    sum --> ctrl[ECU PI Controller]
    ctrl --> inj[Fuel Injector]
    inj --> engine[Engine Combustion]
    engine --> exh[Exhaust Manifold]
    exh --> o2[Lambda O2 Sensor]
    o2 --> fb[Feedback Signal]
    fb --> sum
```

> **Interpretation:** This is a classical **unity-feedback closed-loop system**. The controller continuously compares the target $\lambda = 1.0$ with the measured $\lambda$ from the O2 sensor, and adjusts injector pulse width via a PI law to drive the error $e(t) \to 0$.

---

## 4.3 ECU Internal Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR_INTERFACE [Sensor Signal Conditioning and ADC]
        S1[Analog Sensors MAP, TPS, ECT, IAT] --> ADC[12-bit ADC Multiplexer]
        S2[Frequency Sensors CKP, CMP] --> CAP[Input Capture Timer]
        S3[Lambda O2 Sensor] --> COMP[Comparator with Reference]
    end
    
    subgraph CORE [Microcontroller Core 32-bit]
        ADC --> CPU[CPU DSP Core]
        CAP --> CPU
        COMP --> CPU
        CPU --> MEM[Flash ROM with Calibration Maps]
        CPU --> RAM[Working RAM]
    end
    
    subgraph ACTUATOR_DRIVERS [Actuator Driver Stage]
        CPU --> PWM1[PWM for Injector Driver]
        CPU --> PWM2[PWM for Ignition Coil]
        CPU --> DAC1[Low-side Driver for IACV]
        CPU --> DAC2[H-bridge for Throttle Body]
    end
    
    subgraph COMMS [Communication Layer]
        CPU --> CAN[CAN Controller]
        CPU --> LIN[LIN Slave]
        CPU --> KLINE[ISO 9141 K-Line for OBD]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always identify the **three functional layers** — *Sensing*, *Computing*, *Actuation* — when asked to draw or describe the EECS. The ECU is the **only** intelligent block; everything else is either a transducer or an effector.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define an Electronic Engine Control System (EECS). List any four sensors used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

> An Electronic Engine Control System is a microprocessor-based, closed-loop feedback system that uses sensors to monitor engine operating conditions and actuators to control fuel injection, ignition timing, and emission-related parameters in real time.
>
> **Four sensors used in EECS:**
> 1. **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor** — measures intake manifold pressure to estimate engine load.
> 2. **Crankshaft Position (CKP) sensor** — detects TDC and engine RPM.
> 3. **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** — reports the driver's throttle demand.
> 4. **Lambda (O2) sensor** — measures residual oxygen to enable closed-loop AFR control.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control in an EECS. In which operating conditions is each preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | No $\lambda$ feedback used | Uses O2 sensor feedback |
| AFR accuracy | Lower, calibration-based | $\pm 1\%$ of stoichiometric |
| Operating Phase | Cold start, WOT, warm-up, idle | Steady cruise, part load |
| Adaptability | Fixed map | Adaptive (PI trim) |

> Open-loop is preferred during **cold start** (O2 sensor not yet hot) and **wide-open throttle** (transient enrichment). Closed-loop is preferred during **steady-state cruise** for emission and fuel economy optimization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Electronic Engine Control System. Describe the role of the ECU, sensors, and actuators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has a total displacement of $2.0$ L. The volumetric efficiency is $85\%$, the engine speed is $3000$ RPM, and the air density is $1.2$ kg/m³. The target lambda is $1.0$ (stoichiometric). The stoichiometric AFR is $14.7$ and the injector flow rate is $0.005$ kg/s at a battery-compensated pulse width. Calculate:
1. The mass air flow rate.
2. The required fuel flow rate.
3. The injector pulse width per cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The EECS consists of three main blocks:

- **Sensors** (input): MAP, MAF, CKP, CMP, TPS, ECT, IAT, O2, Knock — convert physical quantities into electrical signals.
- **ECU** (processing): a 32-bit microcontroller with ADC, Flash memory holding calibration maps, CPU, RAM, and communication interfaces (CAN, LIN).
- **Actuators** (output): Fuel injector, Ignition coil, Idle air control valve, EGR valve, Electronic throttle body, VVT solenoid.

The ECU runs a real-time OS (typically OSEK/VDX or AUTOSAR) and executes control loops with a cycle time of **1–10 ms**. The O2 sensor closes the loop by providing exhaust AFR feedback to maintain stoichiometric combustion.

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1).**

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V_d = 2.0$ L $= 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 0.85$
- $N = 3000$ RPM
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $\lambda = 1.0$
- $AFR_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$
- $\dot{m}_{\text{inj}} = 0.005$ kg/s
- $n_c = 4$

**(1) Mass air flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot \dfrac{n_c}{60}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \dfrac{3000}{2} \cdot \dfrac{4}{60}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.0\overline{6}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.02 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 100 = 0.204 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(2) Required fuel flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{AFR_{\text{stoich}} \cdot \lambda} = \dfrac{0.204}{14.7 \cdot 1.0}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = 0.01388 \text{ kg/s} = 13.88 \text{ g/s}}$$

**(3) Injector pulse width per cycle:**

Number of injection events per second (4-stroke, 4-cyl):

$$f_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{N \cdot n_c}{120} = \dfrac{3000 \cdot 4}{120} = 100 \text{ events/s}$$

Fuel per pulse:

$$m_{\text{pulse}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}}{f_{\text{inj}}} = \dfrac{0.01388}{100} = 1.388 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/pulse}$$

Pulse width:

$$T_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{pulse}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{inj}}} = \dfrac{1.388 \times 10^{-4}}{0.005}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{inj}} = 0.02776 \text{ s} = 27.76 \text{ ms}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Explain the closed-loop lambda control strategy used in modern EECS. Why is lambda $= 1.0$ critical for the three-way catalytic converter? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine operates at $1800$ RPM with $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 0.92$. The intake air density is $1.15$ kg/m³ and the displacement is $6.5$ L. The target equivalence ratio is $\phi = 0.7$ (lean operation). Given that the stoichiometric AFR for diesel is $14.5$ and the injector flow rate is $0.008$ kg/s, compute: **(7 marks)**
1. The mass of air inducted per second.
2. The required fuel mass per second.
3. The injector pulse width per cycle.

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Closed-Loop Lambda Control Strategy:**

The O2 (lambda) sensor produces a voltage that switches sharply near $\lambda = 1.0$ (for a narrow-band sensor, the transition is at $\sim 450$ mV). The ECU uses a **PI controller** to drive the fuel trim such that the measured $\lambda$ oscillates tightly around 1.0.

**Control law:**

$$K_{\text{ftrim}}(t) = K_P \cdot (\lambda_{\text{target}} - \lambda_{\text{meas}}) + K_I \int (\lambda_{\text{target}} - \lambda_{\text{meas}})\, dt$$

**Why $\lambda = 1.0$ is critical:**

The three-way catalytic converter (TWC) performs three simultaneous reactions:
- **CO** $\to$ **CO₂** (oxidation)
- **HC** $\to$ **CO₂ + H₂O** (oxidation)
- **NOₓ** $\to$ **N₂ + O₂** (reduction)

The TWC achieves high efficiency (>$95\%$) **only in a narrow window** of $\lambda = 1.0 \pm 0.005$. At $\lambda > 1.0$ (lean), excess O₂ reduces NOₓ efficiency; at $\lambda < 1.0$ (rich), CO and HC oxidation efficiency drops. Therefore, **maintaining stoichiometry is non-negotiable** for emission compliance.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:**
- $n_c = 6$, $N = 1800$ RPM
- $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 0.92$
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.15$ kg/m³
- $V_d = 6.5$ L $= 6.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $\phi = 0.7$, $AFR_{\text{stoich}} = 14.5$, $\dot{m}_{\text{inj}} = 0.008$ kg/s

**Conversion:** $\phi = 0.7$ means $\lambda = 1/\phi = 1.4286$ (lean operation)

**(1) Mass of air per second:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.15 \cdot 0.92 \cdot 6.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \dfrac{1800}{2} \cdot \dfrac{6}{60}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.058 \cdot 6.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 900 \cdot 0.1$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.058 \cdot 0.0065 \cdot 90 = 0.6189 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(2) Required fuel per second:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{AFR_{\text{stoich}} \cdot \lambda} = \dfrac{0.6189}{14.5 \cdot 1.4286}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{0.6189}{20.7147} = 0.02988 \text{ kg/s} = 29.88 \text{ g/s}$$

**(3) Injector pulse width per cycle:**

$$f_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{N \cdot n_c}{120} = \dfrac{1800 \cdot 6}{120} = 90 \text{ events/s}$$

$$m_{\text{pulse}} = \dfrac{0.02988}{90} = 3.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/pulse}$$

$$T_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{3.32 \times 10^{-4}}{0.008} = 0.0415 \text{ s} = 41.5 \text{ ms}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $n_c/60$ in air flow** — many students omit the per-cylinder grouping, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\lambda$ with equivalence ratio $\phi$** — remember $\phi = 1/\lambda$. The question says "$\phi = 0.7$", so you must first convert to $\lambda$ before applying the AFR formula.
> 3. **Not converting mL to m³** — the most common unit error in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Forgetting battery-voltage compensation term $C_{\text{comp}}$** — examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this in ESE.
> 5. **Using $14.7$ for diesel** — stoichiometric AFR is fuel-specific: $14.7$ for gasoline, $14.5$ for diesel, $6.4$ for methanol, $9.0$ for ethanol.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **EECS = Sense → Compute → Actuate** closed-loop system, centered on a 32-bit ECU.
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) = 14.7 : 1**; $\lambda = 1.0$ is mandatory for TWC efficiency.
- [x] **O2 sensor** is the most critical feedback element; it closes the fuel-control loop.
- [x] **MAP / MAF** sensors give engine load; **CKP / CMP** give position and RPM.
- [x] **Ignition timing** is computed as $\theta_{\text{ign}} = \theta_{\text{base}} + \theta_{\text{coolant}} - \theta_{\text{knock}}$.
- [x] **PI control law** is used for $\lambda$ feedback: $u(t) = K_P e(t) + K_I \int e\, dt$.
- [x] **Open-loop** is used during cold-start and WOT; **closed-loop** during steady cruise.
- [x] **4-stroke engine** has one working cycle per **2 crank revolutions**; factor $N/2$ always appears.
- [x] **Standard diagnostic protocol** is OBD-II (SAE J1962); communication buses are CAN, LIN, FlexRay.
- [x] **ECU cycle time** = 1–10 ms; **O2 sensor** reaches operating temp at $\sim 350^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- [x] Modern ECUs are built on **AUTOSAR** architecture with **OSEK/VDX** RTOS underneath.
- [x] Memorize: $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \eta_{\text{vol}} V_d \cdot (N/2) \cdot (n_c/60)$ — appears in nearly every Part B numerical.
- [x] **TWC efficiency window**: $\lambda = 1.0 \pm 0.005$ — this is the single most-tested concept in KTU ESE for EECS.
- [x] **Battery voltage compensation** of injector pulse width is required because injector flow rate is pressure-dependent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inputs and Outputs to Engine Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inputs and Outputs to Engine Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Engine Control System?

The **Engine Control System (ECS)**, in modern automotive engineering, is centred around an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** — a ruggedised, real-time, embedded microcontroller-based computer that continuously monitors engine operating conditions, performs high-speed computational decisions using pre-calibrated **lookup tables (LUTs)** and control algorithms (like **PID control**), and commands actuators to maintain optimal engine performance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Engine Control System is formally defined as:

> A closed-loop feedback system that processes electrical signals from various **input devices (sensors)** representing real-time engine parameters, executes a series of decision algorithms within the **Engine Control Module (ECM)**, and generates command signals to drive **output devices (actuators)** that physically alter the engine's operating state.

The KTU syllabus specifically categorises these into two streams:
- **Inputs:** Sensors that convert physical/chemical quantities into electrical signals.
- **Outputs:** Actuators that convert electrical commands into mechanical/physical actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For the ESE (End Semester Examination), students *must* be able to draw the **functional block diagram** of an engine management system and clearly distinguish between **active sensors** (require external excitation, e.g., piezoresistive MAP sensor) and **passive sensors** (generate their own signal, e.g., thermocouple). This is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Human Nervous System

Imagine the human body. Your **brain** is the ECU. Your **eyes, ears, skin, and nose** are the *input sensors* — they detect light, sound, pressure, and smell. Your **hands, legs, and vocal cords** are the *output actuators* — they act on the world.

When you touch a hot stove (input → pain signal → brain), your brain instantly commands your hand to withdraw (output). The ECU does exactly the same: it receives a signal from the **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** indicating overheating, processes it, and commands the **Electric Cooling Fan Relay** to turn ON. The control loop is analogous to a **sensory-motor reflex arc**.

```
Analogy Mapping:
Human Body              →    Automotive Engine
─────────────                ─────────────────
Eyes / Ears / Skin     →    Sensors (CKP, MAP, MAF, TPS)
Spinal Cord / Nerves   →    Wiring Harness
Brain (Cerebrum)       →    ECU / ECM
Motor Neurons          →    Driver Circuits
Muscles / Limbs        →    Actuators (Injectors, Coil, Motor)
Reflex Action          →    Closed-Loop Control
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Board Exam:** Always mention the three pillars of an engine control system — **Sensing (Input), Decision (Processing), and Actuation (Output)**. Examiners award marks for this structured vocabulary.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **industry-standard metrics** must be memorised for the KTU examination:

- **Operating Voltage of ECU:** **12 V DC** (nominal automotive battery), regulated internally to **5 V DC** and **3.3 V DC** for microcontroller logic.
- **Operating Temperature Range of ECU:** **−40 °C to +125 °C** (under-bonnet environment).
- **Standard CAN Bus Speed:** **500 kbps** (high-speed CAN for powertrain) and **125 kbps** (low-speed CAN for body electronics).
- **Standard Engine Operating Speed:** **800 RPM** (idle) to **7000 RPM** (redline).
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) for Petrol:** **14.7 : 1** (by mass).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **14.7:1 AFR** is a "magic number" in automotive electronics. Lambda (λ) = 1 represents this exact ratio. λ < 1 is *rich*, λ > 1 is *lean*. KTU questions frequently test the conversion between AFR and λ.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inputs to the Engine Control System (The "Senses" of the ECU)

Inputs are devices that **transduce** (convert) physical phenomena into voltage or frequency signals readable by the ECU's **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** or **digital input pins**.

### 2.1.1 Major Engine Sensors (The Sensor Suite)

| # | Sensor Name | Parameter Measured | Output Signal Type | Working Principle |
|---|-------------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **CKP** (Crankshaft Position) | Crank angle / RPM | Variable Reluctance (AC) or Hall-effect (Digital) | Magnetic pickup on toothed reluctor |
| 2 | **CMP** (Camshaft Position) | Cam angle / cylinder ID | Hall-effect (Digital square wave) | Hall effect on shutter wheel |
| 3 | **MAP** (Manifold Absolute Pressure) | Intake manifold pressure (kPa) | Analog Voltage (0–5 V) | Piezoresistive diaphragm |
| 4 | **MAF** (Mass Air Flow) | Air mass (kg/h) | Analog Voltage (0–5 V) or Frequency | Hot-film anemometer |
| 5 | **TPS** (Throttle Position Sensor) | Throttle plate angle (%) | Dual Analog Voltage (potentiometric) | Potentiometer (redundant) |
| 6 | **CTS / ECT** (Coolant Temp) | Coolant temperature (°C) | Resistance (NTC thermistor) | Negative Temperature Coefficient |
| 7 | **IAT** (Intake Air Temp) | Intake air temperature (°C) | Resistance (NTC thermistor) | NTC thermistor |
| 8 | **O2 / Lambda Sensor** | Exhaust O₂ concentration | Voltage (0.1–0.9 V, narrow-band) | Zirconia electrochemical cell |
| 9 | **Wideband O2 (UEGO)** | Exact AFR / Lambda | Current (pumped cell) | Zirconia + pump cell |
| 10 | **Knock Sensor** | Engine knock/vibration | Analog AC voltage | Piezoelectric crystal |
| 11 | **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** | Wheel speed (km/h) | Hall-effect or VR (frequency) | Hall / magnetic pickup |
| 12 | **Accelerator Pedal Position (APP)** | Driver torque demand | Dual analog voltage (redundant) | Hall-effect or potentiometric |
| 13 | **Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor** | Rail pressure (kPa) | Analog voltage (ratiometric) | MEMS piezoresistive |
| 14 | **Oil Pressure/Temperature** | Oil state | Resistance / Analog | NTC / piezoresistive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Rule (KTU Examiner Favourite):**
> - **Active sensors** produce a signal *without* external power (e.g., thermocouple, piezoelectric knock sensor, variable reluctance CKP).
> - **Passive sensors** need **excitation voltage** (typically 5 V reference) from the ECU to produce a signal (e.g., MAP, TPS, NTC thermistor).
> - **Digital sensors** output frequency or PWM (e.g., Hall-effect CKP, MAF frequency-type).

### 2.1.2 Signal Conditioning Inside the ECU

Raw sensor signals almost always need processing before ADC conversion:

1. **Amplification** (using op-amps) — e.g., a knock sensor's 50 mV signal must be amplified ~100×.
2. **Filtering** (RC low-pass) — to remove EMI/EMC noise.
3. **Level Shifting** — converting 12 V signal to 0–5 V for ADC.
4. **Pull-up / Pull-down resistors** — required for open-collector sensor outputs.
5. **Schmitt Trigger** — to square up a noisy sine wave from a VR sensor into a clean digital pulse.

### 2.1.3 Sensor Characterisation: The Transfer Function

Every sensor is described by a **transfer function** relating output to the physical input.

For an NTC thermistor used as a Coolant Temperature Sensor:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (Kelvin)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 298 K = 25 °C)
- $B$ = material-specific **β-constant** (typically 3435 K for common NTC)

The ECU reads $R_T$ via a **voltage divider** with a known pull-up resistor $R_{ref}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}}$$

---

## 2.2 Outputs from the Engine Control System (The "Muscles" of the ECU)

Outputs are actuators that the ECU commands via **driver circuits** (low-side switches, high-side switches, or H-bridge drivers like the VNH5019 or BTS7960).

### 2.2.1 Major Engine Actuators (The Actuator Suite)

| # | Actuator | Function | Driver Type | Power Level |
|---|----------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Fuel Injector** | Meter fuel into port/cylinder | Solenoid (low-side NPN) | 2–4 A peak, hold 1 A |
| 2 | **Ignition Coil (COP/DIS)** | Generate high-voltage spark | IGBT low-side driver | 7–10 A |
| 3 | **Idle Air Control (IAC) Valve** | Bypass air around throttle | Stepper motor / duty-cycle solenoid | 0.5–1 A |
| 4 | **Throttle Body Motor (DBW)** | Electronic throttle control | DC motor + H-bridge (PWM) | 5–8 A |
| 5 | **EGR Valve** | Recirculate exhaust gas | Solenoid or stepper motor | 1–2 A |
| 6 | **VVT Solenoid (VVT-i / VANOS)** | Cam phaser oil control | PWM duty-cycle solenoid | 1–2 A |
| 7 | **Evaporative Purge Valve** | Control canister purge | PWM solenoid | 0.5 A |
| 8 | **Cooling Fan Relay** | Drive radiator fan | Relay coil (low-side) | 150–300 mA coil |
| 9 | **A/C Compressor Clutch Relay** | Engage compressor | Relay low-side | 150 mA |
| 10 | **Fuel Pump Relay** | Power fuel pump | Relay high-side | 200 mA coil, 8 A pump |
| 11 | **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** | Dashboard warning | Low-side bulb/LED | 100 mA |
| 12 | **Variable Intake Manifold Runner** | Tune intake length | Vacuum solenoid | 0.5 A |

### 2.2.2 Output Driver Topology

The ECU uses three fundamental driver configurations:

1. **Low-Side Switch (LSS):** ECU sinks current to ground. The actuator's other terminal is tied to +12 V. The ECU pin is on the "low" side. Most common.
2. **High-Side Switch (HSS):** ECU sources current from +12 V to the load. Used when load is chassis-grounded. Implemented with a **P-channel MOSFET** or a high-side driver IC.
3. **H-Bridge:** Four switches arranged in an H, allowing a DC motor to be driven in both directions (forward/reverse). Used for **DBW throttles**, **stepper motors**, and **fuel pump speed control** (via PWM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flyback Diodes / Freewheeling Diodes:** Inductive actuators (injector solenoids, relay coils, motors) generate a high-voltage back-EMF when switched off. The driver circuit *must* include a flyback diode or use a **smart MOSFET** with built-in avalanche protection. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically includes "driver circuits" — be ready to draw a flyback-protected injector driver.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. Memorise every row. KTU 2024 ESE typically has a 7-mark question directly testing these.

| # | Concept | Equation / Symbol | Description |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (petrol) | $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ | Mass ratio of air to fuel |
| 2 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | $\lambda = 1$ → stoich; < 1 → rich; > 1 → lean |
| 3 | Engine volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{displaced}} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_d}$ | Ratio of inducted to theoretical air mass |
| 4 | Indicated mean effective pressure | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{V_d}$ | Pa |
| 5 | Engine indicated power | $P_i = \dfrac{p_{imb} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot K}{60}$ | 4-stroke: K=½; 2-stroke: K=1 |
| 6 | NTC thermistor resistance | $R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Exponential, B in Kelvin |
| 7 | Voltage divider (sensor interface) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \dfrac{R_{sensor}}{R_{sensor} + R_{ref}}$ | Ratiometric |
| 8 | ADC resolution | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | n = ADC bit depth |
| 9 | Fuel injection pulse width (speed-density) | $t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{C \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}}$ | Empirical linear model: $t_{inj} = K \cdot \dfrac{P_{map}}{T_{map}} \cdot \tau$ |
| 10 | Ignition dwell time | $t_{dwell} = \dfrac{L \cdot I_{peak}}{V_{bat} - V_{sat}}$ | To ramp current to I_peak |
| 11 | Engine RPM from CKP | $RPM = \dfrac{60}{n_{teeth} \cdot T_{pulse}}$ | n_teeth = crank teeth; T_pulse in seconds |
| 12 | Injector duty cycle | $D = \dfrac{t_{open}}{T_{period}}$ | For PWM-controlled injectors |
| 13 | Engine torque (indicated) | $T_i = \dfrac{P_i \cdot 60}{2\pi n}$ | Nm |
| 14 | Throttle angle vs. flow | $m_{air} \approx C_d \cdot A(\theta) \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | $A(\theta)$ depends on geometry |
| 15 | Engine displacement | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} b^2 \cdot s \cdot N_{cyl}$ | b=bore, s=stroke, N_cyl = cylinders |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Input-Output System

- **Emissions Compliance:** The O₂ sensor → ECU → fuel injector loop is what enables cars to meet **BS-VI / Euro 6** emission norms.
- **Drive-by-Wire (DBW):** The APP sensor → ECU → throttle motor loop replaces the mechanical throttle cable, enabling **Traction Control (TCS)**, **Cruise Control**, and **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**.
- **Variable Valve Timing (VVT):** The CMP sensor → ECU → VVT solenoid loop optimises cam phasing for both low-end torque and high-end power.
- **Engine Start-Stop Systems (Auto Stop-Start):** Crank position + battery voltage → ECU → starter relay + fuel injector cut-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1: Engine RPM from a Crankshaft Position Sensor

### Problem Statement
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a **60-tooth reluctor wheel** on the crankshaft. The CKP sensor outputs a clean digital pulse train. The **time between two consecutive falling edges** is measured by the ECU's **Input Capture Unit (ICU)** as **T_pulse = 2.0 ms**. Compute the engine speed in **RPM** and in **rad/s**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship between teeth and crank rotation.**

A full crankshaft revolution = **360°**. The 60-tooth wheel produces one pulse (one tooth) for every:

$$\theta_{tooth} = \frac{360°}{60} = 6° \text{ of crank rotation}$$

**Step 2 — Convert tooth pulse time to crank rotational frequency.**

One pulse → 6° rotation. Time per pulse is 2.0 ms. The angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \frac{6°}{T_{pulse}} = \frac{6 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} \text{ rad}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}$$

$$\omega = \frac{0.10472 \text{ rad}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} = 52.36 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert rad/s to RPM.**

The conversion factor between rad/s and RPM is $\dfrac{60}{2\pi}$:

$$RPM = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 52.36 \times 9.5493$$

$$RPM = 500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using the alternative formula:**

Using the KTU cheat sheet formula directly:

$$RPM = \frac{60}{n_{teeth} \cdot T_{pulse}} = \frac{60}{60 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{60}{0.12} = 500 \text{ RPM} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 60-tooth wheel is called a **36-minus-2** or **60-minus-2** crank wheel in real engines — the two missing teeth create a unique reference pattern so the ECU can identify **Top Dead Centre (TDC)** of cylinder #1 within one revolution. This is essential for **sequential fuel injection** and **coil-on-plug (COP) ignition**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2: Coolant Temperature from NTC Thermistor

### Problem Statement
A Coolant Temperature Sensor uses an NTC thermistor with **R₀ = 10 kΩ at T₀ = 25 °C (298 K)** and **B = 3435 K**. The ECU uses a **pull-up resistor R_ref = 2.2 kΩ** and a 5.0 V reference. The ECU's ADC reads **V_out = 2.50 V** at an unknown coolant temperature. Compute the coolant temperature in **°C**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Invert the voltage divider to find the sensor resistance.**

The voltage divider equation is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}}$$

Solving for $R_T$:

$$R_T = \frac{V_{out} \cdot R_{ref}}{V_{ref} - V_{out}} = \frac{2.50 \cdot 2200}{5.00 - 2.50} = \frac{5500}{2.50} = 2200 \ \Omega = 2.2 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the NTC β-equation to solve for T (Kelvin).**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

Take the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) = B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{B} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{298} + \frac{1}{3435} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2200}{10000}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 0.003356 + 0.000291 \cdot (-1.5141)$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 0.003356 - 0.000441 = 0.002915 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Final temperature calculation.**

$$T = \frac{1}{0.002915} = 343.05 \text{ K}$$

Convert to °C:

$$T_{°C} = 343.05 - 273.15 = 69.9 \text{ °C} \approx 70 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Normal operating coolant temperature is **85 °C – 95 °C**. At 70 °C, the engine is still warming up. The ECU uses this temperature to advance the **cold-start fuel enrichment** (richer mixture) and to delay the **closed-loop O₂ feedback** until the O₂ sensor reaches its operating temperature of ~315 °C (light-off temperature).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3: Lambda to AFR Conversion

### Problem Statement
A wideband O₂ sensor reports **λ = 0.85**. Compute the actual Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) and the **mass of air** required to fully combust **1 kg of gasoline**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the λ definition.**

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} \implies AFR_{actual} = \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$AFR_{actual} = 0.85 \times 14.7 = 12.495$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

$$\text{AFR}_{actual} \approx 12.5 : 1$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass of air for 1 kg of fuel.**

By definition, 12.5 kg of air is required for 1 kg of fuel at λ = 0.85:

$$m_{air} = 12.5 \times m_{fuel} = 12.5 \times 1.0 = 12.5 \text{ kg of air}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why λ = 0.85 matters:** This is a **"power enrichment"** mixture. Under wide-open throttle (WOT) or high-load conditions, the ECU intentionally enriches the mixture to:
> - **Cool the combustion chamber** (latent heat of vaporisation of excess fuel absorbs heat).
> - **Prevent detonation/knock** at high cylinder pressures.
> - **Maximise power output** (peak power occurs at λ ≈ 0.85 for petrol).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Inputs to Outputs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE — Model Question Mapping Table (Memorise This!):**
> Examiners frequently ask: *"For the following driving condition, identify the sensors used and the actuators commanded."* The following matrix is the master answer key.

| Driving Condition | Primary Input Sensors | ECU Action (Logic) | Actuators Commanded |
|-------------------|----------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Cold Start** | CTS (cold), CKP, IAT | Inject extra fuel (choke equivalent) | Fuel injectors (longer pulse), IAC valve opens |
| **Idle Speed Control** | CKP (RPM), CTS, APP | Maintain target RPM (e.g., 800 RPM) | IAC stepper / Electronic throttle |
| **Cruise (Steady State)** | TPS, MAP, MAF, VSS, O₂ | Closed-loop λ = 1 ± 0.01 | Injectors (small trim), VVT |
| **Wide-Open Throttle (WOT)** | APP (max), MAP (high), MAF (max) | Power enrichment, spark retard | Injectors (rich), ignition coil, knock control |
| **Engine Braking (Overrun)** | TPS (0%), MAP (low), CKP | Cut fuel entirely (λ → ∞, "DFCO") | Injectors OFF |
| **Knock Detected** | Knock sensor (vibration) | Retard spark timing 3°–10° | Ignition coil (retard) |
| **Overheat** | CTS (>105 °C), fan state | Turn on radiator fan, enrich | Cooling fan relay, injectors |
| **A/C Compressor ON** | A/C switch, MAP (load change) | Increase idle target RPM | IAC / throttle, A/C clutch relay |
| **Gear Shift (Manual)** | VSS, TPS, gear sensor | Cut fuel briefly to reduce torque | Injectors OFF (flat-shift) |
| **Battery Low (< 11 V)** | Battery voltage | Disable non-essentials, reduce loads | Disable rear defog, idle up |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Engine Control System Input/Output Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORS["INPUT SIDE - SENSORS"]
        CKP["CKP Sensor\nCrankshaft Position"]
        CMP["CMP Sensor\nCamshaft Position"]
        MAP["MAP Sensor\nManifold Pressure"]
        MAF["MAF Sensor\nAir Mass Flow"]
        TPS["TPS Sensor\nThrottle Position"]
        CTS["CTS Sensor\nCoolant Temp"]
        IAT["IAT Sensor\nIntake Air Temp"]
        O2["O2 Sensor\nLambda / AFR"]
        KNOCK["Knock Sensor\nVibration"]
        APP["APP Sensor\nPedal Position"]
        VSS["VSS\nVehicle Speed"]
    end

    subgraph ECU["ENGINE CONTROL UNIT - ECU"]
        INTF["Signal Conditioning\nAmplify / Filter / Level Shift"]
        ADC["Analog to Digital\nConverter - 12 bit"]
        CPU["Microcontroller Core\n32 bit / 100 MHz"]
        MEM["Memory\nFlash + RAM + LUTs"]
        DIAG["Diagnostics\nOBD-II / UDS"]
        DRV["Output Driver Stage\nLSS / HSS / H-Bridge"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATORS["OUTPUT SIDE - ACTUATORS"]
        INJ["Fuel Injectors\nSolenoid Valves"]
        COIL["Ignition Coil\nCOP / DIS"]
        TB["Throttle Body Motor\nDBW"]
        IAC["Idle Air Control\nStepper Motor"]
        VVT["VVT Solenoid\nCam Phaser"]
        FAN["Cooling Fan Relay"]
        FP["Fuel Pump Relay"]
        MIL["MIL - Check Engine\nWarning Lamp"]
    end

    CKP --> INTF
    CMP --> INTF
    MAP --> ADC
    MAF --> ADC
    TPS --> ADC
    CTS --> ADC
    IAT --> ADC
    O2 --> ADC
    KNOCK --> INTF
    APP --> ADC
    VSS --> INTF

    INTF --> ADC
    ADC --> CPU
    CPU <--> MEM
    CPU --> DRV
    CPU --> DIAG

    DRV --> INJ
    DRV --> COIL
    DRV --> TB
    DRV --> IAC
    DRV --> VVT
    DRV --> FAN
    DRV --> FP
    DRV --> MIL
```

---

## 4.2 Signal Flow Diagram: Closed-Loop Air-Fuel Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRV[Driver Demand\nAPP Sensor] --> ECU
    MAP[MAP Sensor] --> ECU
    MAF[MAF Sensor] --> ECU
    CTS[CTS Sensor] --> ECU
    O2[O2 Lambda Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU{ECU - Compute\nBase Inj Time}
    ECU --> INJ[Fuel Injector]
    INJ --> CYL[Engine Cylinder]
    CYL --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXH --> O2
    O2 -. Lambda Feedback .-> ECU
    CYL --> KNK[Knock Sensor]
    KNK -. Knock Feedback .-> ECU
    ECU -. Trim Correction .-> INJ
```

---

## 4.3 Output Driver Topology Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DRIVERS["ECU Output Driver Topologies"]
        LSS["LOW SIDE SWITCH\n- NPN BJT or N-MOSFET\n- Load tied to +12 V\n- Used for: injectors, relays"]
        HSS["HIGH SIDE SWITCH\n- P-MOSFET or N-MOSFET + charge pump\n- Load tied to GND\n- Used for: fuel pump, lamp"]
        HBR["H BRIDGE\n- 4 switches 2 N P pairs\n- Bi directional DC motor\n- Used for: DBW throttle stepper"]
    end
    PIN1[ECU GPIO Pin] --> LSS
    PIN2[ECU PWM Pin] --> HSS
    PIN3[ECU PWM Pin Pair] --> HBR
    LSS --> L[Inductive Load - Injector]
    HSS --> H[Inductive Load - Pump]
    HBR --> M[DC Motor - Throttle]
```

---

## 4.4 Power Supply & Reference Voltage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BATT[12 V Battery] --> KL30[KL30 - Battery Direct]
    BATT --> KL15[KL15 - Ignition Switched]
    KL30 --> REG5[5 V LDO Regulator]
    KL15 --> ECU_C[ECU Core Supply]
    REG5 --> VREF[5 V Sensor Reference]
    REG5 --> ADCREF[ADC Vref]
    VREF --> SENS1[MAP / TPS / APP Pull up]
    VREF --> SENS2[CTS / IAT Pull up]
    ECU_C --> MCU[Microcontroller 3.3 V Core]
    KL30 --> INJDRV[12 V Injector Driver Supply]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always label your diagrams clearly. Use **KL30** (Battery +12 V direct, unswitched), **KL15** (Ignition +12 V, switched), and **KL31** (Chassis ground). Examiners reward these industry-standard terminologies.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any six major input sensors used in a modern Electronic Engine Control System. Classify them as active or passive with justification.**

**Model Answer (6 key points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| # | Sensor | Type | Justification |
|---|--------|------|---------------|
| 1 | **CKP Sensor (VR type)** | Active | Generates its own AC voltage via magnetic induction; no excitation needed |
| 2 | **CMP Sensor (Hall type)** | Passive (requires 5 V supply) | Hall IC needs external power to produce output |
| 3 | **MAP Sensor** | Passive (ratiometric) | Piezoresistive bridge needs 5 V excitation |
| 4 | **MAF Sensor** | Passive | Hot-film needs power to maintain temperature |
| 5 | **CTS (NTC)** | Passive | NTC resistance changes only when measured via external circuit |
| 6 | **Knock Sensor (Piezo)** | Active | Piezo crystal self-generates voltage on vibration |
