> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the role of an O₂ (Lambda) sensor in the engine control system. How does the ECU use its output for fuel control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** The O₂ sensor is installed in the exhaust manifold and measures the **residual oxygen content** in the exhaust gases. A **narrow-band sensor** produces a voltage that jumps sharply around stoichiometric AFR (λ = 1): **< 0.2 V** for lean and **> 0.8 V** for rich.
- **(1 mark)** The ECU operates the fuel injectors in a **closed-loop mode** using a **proportional-integral (PI) controller** that adds or subtracts from the base injection time (called the **short-term fuel trim, STFT**, and **long-term fuel trim, LTFT**).
- **(1 mark)** This keeps the catalytic converter at its optimum operating point (λ = 1 ± 0.01) for maximum **three-way catalyst (TWC)** efficiency, achieving the lowest possible emissions of CO, HC, and NOx.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

> ### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2023 pattern]
>
> **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the major **input sensors and output actuators** used in a modern Electronic Engine Control System (ECS). Categorise the sensors into active and passive types with two examples each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the working of a **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** and a **Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor**. For a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine with displacement 1.6 L running at 3000 RPM and volumetric efficiency 85%, compute the theoretical air mass flow rate in kg/h. Assume intake air density = 1.2 kg/m³ and AFR = 14.7. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the **block diagram of an ECS** as shown in SECTION 4.1 (Master Block Diagram). Clearly label three regions: **Sensors (Input)**, **ECU (Processing)**, and **Actuators (Output)**. The examiner awards 3 marks for a complete, labelled, and neat diagram.

**Active Sensors (2 marks, 1 each example):**
- **Definition:** Sensors that generate their own electrical signal without external excitation.
- **Examples:**
  - **Variable Reluctance CKP sensor** — induces an AC voltage when a tooth passes the magnetic core.
  - **Piezoelectric Knock Sensor** — generates voltage when subjected to mechanical vibration.

**Passive Sensors (2 marks, 1 each example):**
- **Definition:** Sensors requiring an external excitation voltage (typically 5 V) from the ECU to produce a measurable output.
- **Examples:**
  - **NTC Coolant Temperature Sensor** — changes resistance with temperature; needs a pull-up to 5 V.
  - **Potentiometric TPS** — requires 5 V reference to produce a divided voltage.

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks

**TPS Working (2 marks):**
The TPS is a **potentiometer** mechanically coupled to the throttle shaft. As the throttle plate rotates, a wiper moves along a resistive track. A **5 V reference** is applied across the track, and the wiper outputs a **ratiometric voltage** (typically 0.5 V at idle, 4.5 V at WOT). A **redundant second track** is used (dual-track TPS) for safety and fault detection — if the two signals disagree, the ECU enters **limp-home mode**.

**MAF Working (1 mark):**
A hot-film anemometer type MAF contains a **heated sensing element** and a **temperature-compensating element**. As air flows past, it cools the film; the ECU varies the current to maintain a constant temperature differential. The current (or voltage) is proportional to the mass of air flowing per second.

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the displaced volume per second.**

For a 4-stroke engine, the working stroke is once every 2 revolutions, but displacement $V_d$ is the swept volume of the engine:

$$V_d = 1.6 \text{ L} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply volumetric efficiency.**

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{displaced}} = \frac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_d}$$

At 3000 RPM, the engine ingests 3000/2 = 1500 working cycles per minute (for 4-stroke), so the volume ingested per minute:

$$\dot{V}_{actual} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{RPM}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{60} \text{ (m}^3/\text{s)}$$

Wait — let me redo this cleanly using the formula:

$$\dot{V}_{actual} = V_d \cdot \frac{RPM}{2 \cdot 60} \cdot \eta_{vol}$$

$$\dot{V}_{actual} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{3000}{120} \cdot 0.85 = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 25 \cdot 0.85$$

$$\dot{V}_{actual} = 0.0340 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass flow.**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{actual} = 1.2 \cdot 0.0340 = 0.0408 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/h.**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.0408 \cdot 3600 = 146.88 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} \approx 147 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step 5 — (Optional bonus point) — Fuel mass flow.**

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR} = \frac{146.88}{14.7} = 9.99 \text{ kg/h} \approx 10 \text{ kg/h}$$

---

> ### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 internal choice]
>
> **(a)** Describe the **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** and **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)**. Explain how the ECU uses their combined signals to perform **sequential fuel injection** and **coil-on-plug (COP) ignition**. A 60-tooth crank wheel is used; if the time between two consecutive CKP pulses is 1.5 ms, find the engine RPM. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the working of the **Drive-by-Wire (DBW) electronic throttle** as a closed-loop system, identifying the input sensor, the ECU logic, the output actuator, and the **redundant safety architecture** (two APP sensors, two TPS tracks, watchdog). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks

**CKP Sensor (1.5 marks):**
A **Variable Reluctance (VR)** or **Hall-effect** sensor mounted near a toothed reluctor wheel on the crankshaft. Each tooth passing the sensor produces one voltage pulse. The 60-tooth wheel generates **60 pulses per crank revolution**, giving 6° of resolution per pulse. Two missing teeth create a **reference gap** identifying TDC of cylinder #1.

**CMP Sensor (1 mark):**
A **Hall-effect** sensor on the camshaft. A **shutter wheel with one or more vanes** interrupts the magnetic field. The ECU determines **cam phase** and which cylinder is in the **intake stroke**.

**Sequential Injection Working (2 marks):**
- The ECU uses the CKP signal to determine **crank angle** in real-time.
- The CMP signal confirms the **intake stroke** of cylinder #1 (cam vane signal high only when cyl #1 is on intake stroke).
- With cylinder #1 identified, the ECU then triggers each injector in the **correct firing order** (1-3-4-2 for many 4-cyl engines), opening the injector for that specific cylinder's **intake stroke only**.
- This is called **"sequential" or "cylinder-individual" injection** — replacing older **batch-fire** or **throttle-body injection (TBI)**.

**COP Ignition (1.5 marks):**
- Each cylinder has its own dedicated **ignition coil mounted on the spark plug** (coil-on-plug).
- The ECU triggers each coil's **IGBT driver** at the precise **ignition advance angle** (typically 10° BTDC at idle to 35° BTDC at cruise).
- Synchronisation is via the CKP + CMP combined signal.

**Numerical (1 mark):**

$$RPM = \frac{60}{n_{teeth} \cdot T_{pulse}} = \frac{60}{60 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{60}{0.09} = 666.67 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\boxed{RPM \approx 667 \text{ RPM}}$$

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks

**System Overview (1 mark):**
Drive-by-Wire (DBW) or Electronic Throttle Control (ETC) **eliminates the mechanical throttle cable**. The driver presses the pedal; the ECU commands the throttle motor.

**Input — APP Sensor (1.5 marks):**
Two independent **Hall-effect** or **potentiometric** sensors measure pedal position.
- **Track 1:** 0% → 4.5 V (rises with pedal).
- **Track 2:** 0.5 V → 4.0 V (offset, redundant).
- The ECU continuously cross-checks both signals. If they disagree by more than 10%, the system enters **fail-safe mode** (defaults to idle).

**ECU Logic (1.5 marks):**
The ECU runs a **PID control algorithm**:
- **Error** = desired throttle position (from APP, after arbitration) − actual throttle position (from TPS).
- Computes a **PWM duty cycle** for the throttle motor H-bridge driver.
- Typical PID gains: Kp = 0.8, Ki = 0.05, Kd = 0.02 (example values for illustration).

**Output — Throttle Body Motor (1.5 marks):**
A **12 V DC brushed motor** (or brushless in modern systems) drives the throttle plate via a reduction gear. An **H-bridge driver** allows forward and reverse rotation. A **spring return** forces the throttle to the default (limp-home) position if power fails.

**Redundant Safety Architecture (1.5 marks):**
- **Two APP sensors** (redundant, disagreement detection).
- **Two TPS tracks** (redundant, integrated in throttle body).
- **Watchdog timer** (a separate microcontroller in the ECU supervises the main CPU).
- **Limp-home mode:** if any fault is detected, throttle defaults to a spring-restored ~7° angle (idle) and engine runs at ~1500 RPM for "limp to workshop."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical!):**
> When asked to "draw the ECU block diagram," students often confuse **input devices** and **output devices**. Common mistakes that cost 2–3 marks:
> 1. Drawing the **fuel pump** as an input (it is an **actuator / output**).
> 2. Confusing **MAP** and **MAF** — MAP measures *pressure* (P), MAF measures *mass flow* ($\dot{m}$). Speed-density uses MAP; mass-flow method uses MAF.
> 3. Forgetting the **flyback diode** when drawing the injector driver circuit (always include it!).
> 4. Labelling the O₂ sensor output as a *current* signal — narrow-band O₂ is a *voltage* signal (0–1 V); only **wideband UEGO** uses a *current* signal.
> 5. Forgetting units in numerical problems — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam:**

- 🔹 **Three pillars of ECS:** Sensing → Processing → Actuation.
- 🔹 **CKP** uses a 60-tooth (or 60-minus-2) wheel; **60 pulses per revolution**; one missing tooth for TDC reference.
- 🔹 **CMP** uses a 1-tooth (or 4-tooth for VVT) shutter wheel; identifies **intake stroke of cyl #1**.
- 🔹 **TPS** is a **potentiometric sensor** with **two redundant tracks**; 0.5 V (idle) to 4.5 V (WOT).
- 🔹 **MAP** measures **manifold pressure in kPa**; used in **speed-density** air metering method.
- 🔹 **MAF** measures **air mass flow in kg/h**; used in **mass-flow** air metering method.
- 🔹 **NTC thermistor** (CTS, IAT) — resistance **decreases** with temperature (negative coefficient).
- 🔹 **PTC thermistor** — resistance **increases** with temperature (used in electric coolant pumps).
- 🔹 **O₂ sensor** (narrow-band) — voltage output, 0.1 V (lean) ↔ 0.9 V (rich); light-off temp ~315 °C.
- 🔹 **Wideband O₂** (UEGO) — current output, measures λ from 0.7 to infinity.
- 🔹 **Knock sensor** — **piezoelectric**, mounted on engine block; ECU retards spark on knock.
- 🔹 **Stoichiometric AFR (petrol)** = **14.7:1**; **Diesel** ≈ **14.5:1**; **CNG** ≈ **17.2:1**.
- 🔹 **Lambda (λ)**: λ = 1 stoich; < 1 rich; > 1 lean.
- 🔹 **Power enrichment** at WOT: λ ≈ 0.85; AFR ≈ 12.5:1.
- 🔹 **Fuel cut (DFCO — Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off)** at closed throttle + RPM > 1500.
- 🔹 **Fuel injectors** are **low-side driven** with a **flyback diode**; **peak-and-hold** current waveform.
- 🔹 **Ignition coil** driven by **IGBT** low-side; **dwell time** pre-charges the coil.
- 🔹 **DBW throttle** uses **H-bridge driver** with **two APP sensors** + **two TPS tracks** + **watchdog**.
- 🔹 **ECU reference voltages:** 5 V sensor excitation; 12 V actuator supply; 3.3 V logic.
- 🔹 **ECU operating temperature:** **−40 °C to +125 °C**.
- 🔹 **CAN bus speed for powertrain:** **500 kbps**.
- 🔹 **Engine displacement formula:** $V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} b^2 \cdot s \cdot N_{cyl}$.
- 🔹 **Engine RPM from CKP:** $RPM = \frac{60}{n_{teeth} \cdot T_{pulse}}$.
- 🔹 **NTC thermistor β-equation:** $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$.
- 🔹 **Voltage divider (sensor interface):** $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}}$.
- 🔹 **Always draw flyback diodes** across all inductive actuators in driver circuits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Fuel Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electronic Fuel Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Fuel Control System (EFCS)** is a closed-loop / open-loop microprocessor-based automotive subsystem that precisely meters and delivers pressurized liquid fuel into the internal combustion engine's intake manifold or directly into the combustion chamber, by continuously computing the required fuel quantity from real-time measurements of engine operating parameters (air mass, engine speed, coolant temperature, throttle position, lambda value, etc.).

The system replaces the legacy mechanical-carburettor fuel metering arrangement with electro-mechanical actuators driven by an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** to satisfy the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio requirement of **14.7 : 1** (for gasoline) under all operating regimes.

### 1.2 Key Performance Parameters (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F):** **14.7 : 1** by mass for petrol/gasoline engines.
- **Excess Air Factor (λ – Lambda):** Ratio of actual A/F to stoichiometric A/F. **λ = 1 → Stoichiometric**, λ > 1 → Lean, λ < 1 → Rich.
- **Injection Pressure:** Typically **2.5 – 3.5 bar** for port injection, **100 – 200 bar** for GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection).
- **Injector Opening Time:** **1.5 – 3.0 ms** at idle (cold start may go up to **10 ms**).
- **Operating Voltage:** **12 V DC** (nominal automotive battery).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Automatic Pourer":**
> Imagine a chef who must pour exactly the right amount of water into a pot of rice. A traditional carburettor is like a clumsy waiter who roughly estimates using a fixed measuring cup—often overcooking or undercooking. An **Electronic Fuel Control System** is like a smart, sensor-equipped automatic pourer: it weighs the rice (air-mass sensor), checks the pot's temperature (coolant sensor), tastes the steam coming out (oxygen sensor), and adjusts the pour in **milliseconds**. The result? Perfectly cooked rice *every* time, whether the stove is cold, simmering, or blazing hot.

### 1.4 Need for Electronic Fuel Control

- **Legislative compliance** (Euro 6 / BS-VI emission norms → demands precise A/F control).
- **Better fuel economy** (3–10 % improvement over carburettor systems).
- **Improved drivability** (smoother idle, throttle response, cold-start).
- **Adaptation to altitude and ambient conditions** automatically.
- **Self-diagnostics** through OBD (On-Board Diagnostics).
- **Compatibility with catalytic converters** (three-way catalyst requires λ = 1 ± 0.01).

### 1.5 Block Diagram – Top-Level View

The Electronic Fuel Control System can be functionally partitioned into four sub-blocks:

1. **Sensing Module** (inputs)
2. **ECU / Microcontroller** (decision)
3. **Actuation Module** (outputs)
4. **Feedback Loop** (Lambda / O2 sensor)

### 1.6 System Classification (KTU Table)

| Generation | Type | Feedback? | Era |
|------------|------|-----------|-----|
| **D-Jetronic** (1967) | Speed-Density | No (Open Loop) | Bosch, 1st Gen |
| **L-Jetronic** (1973) | Mass Airflow (vane) | No (Open Loop) | 2nd Gen |
| **LH-Jetronic** (1981) | Hot-Wire Mass Airflow | Yes (Closed Loop) | 3rd Gen |
| **Motronic** (1979) | Integrated Ignition + Injection | Yes (Closed Loop) | 4th Gen |
| **ME-Motronic** (1990s+) | Torque-Based, CAN-enabled | Yes (Closed Loop, Adaptive) | Modern |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Modes of the Electronic Fuel Control System

The ECU operates the fuel system in several distinct modes, each tailored to a specific engine operating regime:

#### (a) Starting Mode (Cranking Enrichment)
- During starter cranking, manifold air velocity is low and the engine is cold.
- Injectors are pulsed asynchronously (independent of camshaft position) to deliver a **rich mixture** (λ ≈ 0.6 – 0.8).
- Injection pulse width is calculated as a function of **coolant temperature (T_c)** and **cranking RPM**.

#### (b) Warm-Up / After-Start Enrichment
- The engine runs rich for a few seconds (or minutes) after a cold start to prevent stalling.
- Enrichment factor decreases gradually as coolant temperature rises.

#### (c) Open-Loop Operation
- During **cold engine**, **wide-open throttle (WOT)**, or **O2 sensor not yet warm**, the ECU ignores lambda feedback and calculates pulse width from a pre-calibrated **3-D map** indexed by (RPM, Load, T_c).

#### (d) Closed-Loop Operation (Normal Cruising)
- Lambda sensor (heated, narrow-band or wide-band) provides feedback.
- ECU uses a **PI (Proportional-Integral) controller** to maintain λ ≈ 1.000 ± 0.01.
- Switching frequency: **~1 Hz** for narrow-band, **> 100 Hz** for wide-band sensors.

#### (e) Power Enrichment (Full Load / WOT)
- During wide-open throttle, ECU enriches the mixture (λ ≈ 0.85) to **protect the engine from detonation** and to maximize power output (air-cooled exhaust valves).

#### (f) Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO)
- When throttle is closed and RPM > cranking RPM, fuel injection is **fully cut off** (λ → ∞) to save fuel and cool the catalyst.
- Injection resumes when RPM drops below a calibrated threshold (~1100 rpm).

#### (g) Acceleration Enrichment
- During rapid throttle opening, the **air** entering the manifold increases faster than the **fuel** delivered (due to fuel wall-wetting).
- ECU adds an **acceleration-enrichment pulse** based on the rate of change of throttle position (dθ/dt) or MAP (dP/dT).

### 2.2 Fuel Quantity Calculation (Speed-Density Method – D-Jetronic)

The D-Jetronic system uses the **Ideal Gas Law** to compute the air mass per cylinder:

$$m_{air} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{man}}{R \cdot T_{man} \cdot N_{cyl}}$$

Where:
- $m_{air}$ = mass of air per intake stroke (kg)
- $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency (0.85 – 0.95)
- $V_d$ = engine displacement (m³)
- $P_{man}$ = manifold absolute pressure (Pa)
- $T_{man}$ = manifold air temperature (K)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- $N_{cyl}$ = number of cylinders

### 2.3 Injector Pulse Width Equation (Master Equation)

The fundamental ECU output variable is the **injector pulse width** $T_i$:

$$T_i = \frac{m_{air} \cdot K_{stoich}}{n_{inj} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}} \cdot \left( F_{cold} \cdot F_{warm} \cdot F_{acc} \cdot F_{bat} \right) + T_{dead}$$

Where:
- $T_i$ = injection time (ms)
- $m_{air}$ = air mass per stroke (kg)
- $K_{stoich}$ = stoichiometric factor (1/14.7 for petrol)
- $n_{inj}$ = number of injectors
- $\dot{m}_{inj}$ = injector mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $F_{cold}$ = cold-start enrichment factor (1.0 – 5.0)
- $F_{warm}$ = warm-up correction (1.0 – 1.5)
- $F_{acc}$ = acceleration enrichment (1.0 – 2.0)
- $F_{bat}$ = battery voltage correction (1.0 – 1.3)
- $T_{dead}$ = injector dead time / opening delay (typically **0.8 – 1.2 ms** at 14 V)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A/F_{stoich} = 14.7$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol | dimensionless | Baseline calibration |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Lambda / Excess air factor | 0.8 – 1.2 | Closed-loop target |
| 3 | $m_{air} = \dfrac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{man}}{R \cdot T_{man}}$ | Air mass per cycle (Speed-Density) | kg | D-Jetronic |
| 4 | $T_i \propto \dfrac{m_{air}}{V_{bat}}$ | Injector pulse width (ideal) | ms | Output stage |
| 5 | $T_{dead} = f(V_{bat})$ | Injector dead time (voltage-dependent) | 0.8 – 1.5 ms | Low-voltage correction |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Injector flow rate (Bernoulli) | kg/s | Injector sizing |
| 7 | $n_{stoich} = 14.7 \times 23 \approx 338$ | Air-fuel ratio in molecular terms | — | Combustion stoichiometry |
| 8 | $P_{inj} = P_{rail}$ (constant) | Port injection rail pressure | 2.5 – 4.0 bar | Multi-point injection |
| 9 | $P_{inj}$ variable, 50 – 200 bar | GDI rail pressure | bar | Direct injection |
| 10 | $F_{bat} = \dfrac{T_{inj,nom}}{T_{inj,actual}}$ | Battery voltage compensation factor | 1.0 – 1.3 | Low-battery correction |
| 11 | $\eta_{vol} \approx 0.85 + 0.045 \cdot \lambda_{m}$ | Empirical volumetric efficiency | — | Naturally aspirated engines |
| 12 | $Q_{inj} = \dot{m}_{inj} \cdot T_i$ | Fuel quantity per injection | kg | Total metering |

### 2.5 Lambda (Oxygen) Sensor – Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Lambda Sensor = Exhaust-Gas Taster**
> It is a galvanic (voltaic) cell based on a solid zirconia-ceramic electrolyte with porous platinum electrodes exposed to exhaust gas on one side and ambient air on the other.

- **Output voltage (narrow-band):** **0.1 V** (lean, λ > 1) to **0.9 V** (rich, λ < 1).
- **Switching point:** ~0.45 V at λ = 1.000.
- **Operating temperature:** 350 – 850 °C (modern sensors have integrated heaters).
- **Wide-band sensors** output a current proportional to λ over the range 0.7 – ∞.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Three-way Catalytic Converter (TWC):** Reduces CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously only when λ ≈ 1. The closed-loop EFCS keeps the catalyst in its "window of conversion efficiency" (> 95 % for all three pollutants).
- **Drive-by-Wire Integration:** Modern EFCS works in tandem with electronic throttle control (ETC) to enable torque-based engine management.
- **Hybrid Vehicles:** EFCS enables cylinder deactivation and coasting fuel cut-off, contributing 5–10 % fuel savings.
- **Turbocharged Engines:** EFCS prevents lean spikes under boost, protecting the turbine from lean-burn overheating.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Injector Pulse Width from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation bridges the air-mass sensing (input) to the injector actuation (output) and is a **high-yield KTU exam question**.

#### Step 1: Air Mass per Engine Cycle

For a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine, the number of intake events per second is:

$$N_{intake} = \frac{N_{rpm}}{2} \cdot \frac{N_{cyl}}{2} = \frac{N_{rpm} \cdot N_{cyl}}{4}$$

Total air mass flow rate (kg/s) entering the engine:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N_{rpm} \cdot N_{cyl}}{4 \cdot 2}$$

Wait — let us derive carefully. For one complete 4-stroke cycle, each cylinder draws one intake charge (720°). Therefore the intake frequency per cylinder is $N_{rpm}/2$ per minute $= N_{rpm}/120$ per second. So total intake strokes per second:

$$f_{intake} = \frac{N_{rpm}}{120} \cdot N_{cyl}$$

Air mass per intake stroke (kg):

$$m_{air,stroke} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{man}}{R \cdot T_{man}}$$

Total air mass per second:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = m_{air,stroke} \cdot f_{intake} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{man} \cdot N_{rpm} \cdot N_{cyl}}{120 \cdot R \cdot T_{man}}$$

#### Step 2: Required Fuel Mass

For stoichiometric combustion:

$$m_{fuel,total} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{A/F_{stoich}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{14.7}$$

#### Step 3: Fuel Mass per Injector

If $N_{cyl}$ injectors fire in batches, mass per injection event (over one full engine cycle):

$$m_{fuel,inj} = \frac{m_{fuel,total}}{N_{cyl} \cdot f_{intake}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{14.7 \cdot N_{cyl} \cdot f_{intake}}$$

#### Step 4: Convert to Pulse Width

Using the injector flow model:

$$\dot{m}_{inj,static} = C_d \cdot A_{or} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_{fuel} \cdot (P_{rail} - P_{man})}$$

$$T_i = \frac{m_{fuel,inj}}{\dot{m}_{inj,static}} + T_{dead}$$

Putting it all together:

$$\boxed{\, T_i = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{man}}{14.7 \cdot R \cdot T_{man} \cdot C_d \cdot A_{or} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}} + T_{dead} \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pulse width is **linearly proportional to manifold pressure** $P_{man}$ — this is why the D-Jetronic is called the **Speed-Density** system (Speed = RPM, Density = derived from P/T).

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example – KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem [Sample Numerical — KTU Pattern]:**
> A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has $V_d = 2.0 \,\text{L}$, runs at 3000 RPM, with manifold pressure $P_{man} = 0.6 \,\text{bar}$ (gauge) and manifold temperature $T_{man} = 320 \,\text{K}$. The volumetric efficiency is 0.90, the injector static flow rate is 250 g/min, and $T_{dead} = 1.0 \,\text{ms}$. Compute the injector pulse width at stoichiometric ratio.

**Given:**
- $V_d = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3$
- $N_{rpm} = 3000$
- $P_{man} = 0.6 \times 10^5 = 6 \times 10^4 \,\text{Pa}$ (gauge, above atmospheric)
- $T_{man} = 320 \,\text{K}$
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.90$
- $R = 287 \,\text{J/(kg·K)}$
- $\dot{m}_{inj} = 250 \,\text{g/min} = 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{kg/s}$
- $A/F = 14.7$
- $T_{dead} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{s}$

**Step 1: Air mass per intake stroke**

$$m_{air} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{man}}{R \cdot T_{man}}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{0.90 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.0 \times 10^4}{287 \times 320}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{108.0}{91840} = 1.176 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{kg/cycle}$$

**Step 2: Fuel mass per cycle (stoichiometric)**

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{14.7} = \frac{1.176 \times 10^{-3}}{14.7} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{kg/cycle}$$

**Step 3: Fuel mass per cylinder per cycle**

$$m_{fuel,cyl} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{N_{cyl}} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-5}}{4} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{kg/injection}$$

**Step 4: Theoretical pulse width**

$$T_i = \frac{m_{fuel,cyl}}{\dot{m}_{inj}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{4.167 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{s} = 4.8 \,\text{ms}$$

**Step 5: Add dead time**

$$T_{i,total} = 4.8 + 1.0 = 5.8 \,\text{ms}$$

**Answer: $T_{i,total} \approx 5.8 \,\text{ms}$**

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix – KTU Question Pattern

The following table shows how the ECU chooses between modes. This is a frequently asked KTU Part-A question.

| Sensor Input / State | Operating Mode | Lambda Target | Injector Triggering |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cranking (T_c < 40 °C) | Cranking | λ ≈ 0.6 – 0.8 | Asynchronous batch |
| T_c < 70 °C, post-start | Warm-Up | λ ≈ 0.9 – 1.0 | Synchronous + offset |
| T_c > 70 °C, O2 warm | Closed-Loop | λ = 1.000 ± 0.01 | Synchronous, PI-controlled |
| Throttle > 80 % (WOT) | Power Enrichment | λ ≈ 0.85 | Synchronous, batched |
| Throttle = 0 %, RPM > 1100 | DFCO Cut-Off | λ → ∞ (no fuel) | Injection disabled |
| Throttle dθ/dt > threshold | Acceleration | λ ≈ 0.92 + extra pulse | Async wall-wetting pulse |
| Battery < 11 V | Voltage Compensation | $T_i$ increased by $F_{bat}$ | Longer pulse width |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Electronic Fuel Control System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Air Mass Flow Sensor] --> ECU1
    A2[Manifold Absolute Pressure MAP Sensor] --> ECU1
    A3[Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS] --> ECU1
    A4[Throttle Position Sensor TPS] --> ECU1
    A5[Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP] --> ECU1
    A6[Lambda Oxygen Sensor] --> ECU1
    A7[Cam Position Sensor CMP] --> ECU1
    A8[Vehicle Speed Sensor VSS] --> ECU1

    ECU1{ECU Microcontroller}

    ECU1 --> B1[Fuel Pump Relay]
    ECU1 --> B2[Fuel Injector Drivers]
    ECU1 --> B3[Fuel Pressure Regulator Control]
    ECU1 --> B4[Idle Air Control Valve IACV]
    ECU1 --> B5[Malfunction Indicator Lamp MIL]
    ECU1 --> B6[CAN Bus Output]

    B1 --> C1[Electric Fuel Pump]
    C1 --> C2[Fuel Rail]
    B2 --> C2
    C2 --> C3[Engine Cylinders]
    A6 -. feedback loop .-> ECU1
    B3 --> C4[Vacuum Line]
    C4 --> C3
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Lambda Control Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP[Target Lambda 1.000] --> SUM
    FB[Measured Lambda from O2 Sensor] --> SUM
    SUM[Error Summation Junction] --> PI[PI Controller in ECU]
    PI --> PWM[PWM Driver to Injector]
    PWM --> INJ[Fuel Injector]
    INJ --> ENG[Engine Combustion]
    ENG --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXH --> O2[Lambda Sensor]
    O2 --> FB
```

### 4.3 Fuel Injection Timing Modes (Synchronous vs. Asynchronous)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Engine Running] --> Q1{O2 Sensor Warmed Up?}
    Q1 -- No --> OL[Open Loop Mode]
    Q1 -- Yes --> CL[Closed Loop Mode]
    OL --> AS1[Asynchronous Injection during Crank]
    CL --> SY1[Synchronous Injection every CKP tooth]
    AS1 --> ENR[Enrichment Factors Active]
    SY1 --> NORM[Normal Stoichiometric Control]
    ENR --> OUTPUT[Injector Pulse Output]
    NORM --> OUTPUT
```

### 4.4 Architecture Flow – Inputs to Outputs

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[INPUT SUBSYSTEM]
    IN --> S1[Air Mass Sensor]
    IN --> S2[Engine Speed Sensor]
    IN --> S3[Coolant Temperature Sensor]
    IN --> S4[Throttle Position Sensor]
    IN --> S5[Lambda Sensor Feedback]

    S1 --> PROC[ECU PROCESSING CORE]
    S2 --> PROC
    S3 --> PROC
    S4 --> PROC
    S5 --> PROC

    PROC --> MAP1[3D Fuel Map Lookup]
    MAP1 --> CALC[Pulse Width Calculator]
    CALC --> ENR1[Apply Enrichment Corrections]
    ENR1 --> OUT1[OUTPUT ACTUATION]

    OUT1 --> O1[Fuel Injector Driver]
    OUT1 --> O2[Fuel Pump Relay]
    OUT1 --> O3[Idle Air Control]
    OUT1 --> O4[Engine MIL Lamp]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **3D Fuel Map** is the heart of the ECU's "pre-learned" knowledge. It is a two-dimensional table indexed by **Engine RPM** and **Engine Load** (often expressed as MAP or mass-airflow), with a third dimension (corrective enrichment) based on **coolant temperature**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

> *Define the term "Excess Air Factor (λ)" and explain its significance in Electronic Fuel Control Systems. List the typical lambda targets for cranking, normal cruising, and wide-open-throttle conditions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Excess Air Factor (λ) is defined as the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of the fuel:

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

For petrol, $A/F_{stoich} = 14.7 : 1$.

**Significance:**
- λ = 1 → Stoichiometric mixture (required for three-way catalytic converter operation)
- λ < 1 → Rich mixture (more fuel, less air)
- λ > 1 → Lean mixture (less fuel, more air)

**Typical Lambda Targets:**

| Operating Condition | Lambda Target |
|---|---|
| Cranking (cold start) | λ ≈ 0.6 – 0.8 (rich) |
| Normal cruising (closed loop) | λ = 1.000 ± 0.01 |
| Wide-Open Throttle (WOT) | λ ≈ 0.85 (power enrichment) |
| Deceleration fuel cut-off | λ → ∞ (no fuel) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

> *With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of the closed-loop Electronic Fuel Control System using a Lambda (O2) sensor.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In a closed-loop system, the **Lambda sensor** is placed in the exhaust manifold, **before** the catalytic converter. It measures the residual oxygen in the exhaust gases and outputs a voltage between **0.1 V** (lean) and **0.9 V** (rich).

**Block Diagram (Text Description):**

```
Target λ → [Σ] → [PI Controller] → [Injector Driver] → [Engine] → [Exhaust] → [λ Sensor] → Feedback to [Σ]
```

**Operation:**
1. The ECU compares actual λ (from O2 sensor) with target λ (1.000).
2. The PI controller computes an error and adjusts injector pulse width.
3. If the mixture is **lean** (λ > 1, voltage < 0.45 V), ECU **increases** $T_i$.
4. If the mixture is **rich** (λ < 1, voltage > 0.45 V), ECU **decreases** $T_i$.
5. This creates a rapid **oscillation around λ = 1**, ensuring the three-way catalyst operates in its high-efficiency window.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) system** and explain the functions of the following components: (i) ECU, (ii) Fuel Injector, (iii) Fuel Pressure Regulator, (iv) Lambda Sensor, (v) Coolant Temperature Sensor. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the various **operating modes** of the Electronic Fuel Control System. State clearly which sensors are dominant in each mode. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Block Diagram of EFI System (4 Marks)

```
         [Air Mass Sensor]    [CTS]      [TPS]
                |               |          |
                v               v          v
   [CKP] --> [ECU] <-- [MAP]   [λ-Sensor] [CMP]
                |
                |  PWM signals
                v
   [Fuel Pump] [Injectors] [IACV] [MIL] [CAN]
        |          |
        v          v
   [Fuel Tank]->[Fuel Rail]->[Engine]
```

**Component Functions (3 Marks):**

1. **ECU (Electronic Control Unit):** Microcontroller that executes the fuel-calculation algorithm using inputs from all sensors, determines pulse width, and drives injectors via PWM. (0.5 Mark)
2. **Fuel Injector:** Solenoid-actuated valve that sprays atomized fuel into the intake port or directly into the cylinder when energized. Pulse width determines the fuel quantity. (0.5 Mark)
3. **Fuel Pressure Regulator:** Maintains a constant pressure differential (~ 2.5 – 3.5 bar) across the injector nozzle, ensuring flow rate is independent of manifold vacuum. (0.5 Mark)
4. **Lambda (O2) Sensor:** Solid-state galvanic cell that produces voltage proportional to the oxygen content of exhaust gas. Used for closed-loop feedback. (0.5 Mark)
5. **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS):** NTC thermistor that informs the ECU of engine temperature, used to apply cold-start and warm-up enrichment. (0.5 Mark)
6. (Bonus context) Air mass sensor, MAP, TPS, CKP, CMP – briefly mentioned. (0.5 Mark)

#### (b) Operating Modes (7 Marks)

| Mode | Dominant Sensor(s) | Lambda Target | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cranking** | CTS, CKP | 0.6 – 0.8 | Engine cold, manifold walls wet with fuel |
| **Warm-Up** | CTS, MAP | 0.9 – 1.0 | Gradual lean-out as engine warms |
| **Open-Loop** | MAP / MAF, RPM, T_c | 3-D map value | O2 sensor not warm or fault detected |
| **Closed-Loop** | O2 (Lambda) sensor | 1.000 ± 0.01 | Normal cruising – catalyst efficiency |
| **Power Enrichment** | TPS, MAP | 0.85 | WOT – protect engine, increase power |
| **DFCO** | TPS, CKP | ∞ (no fuel) | Closed throttle, RPM > 1100 |
| **Acceleration** | TPS dθ/dt, MAP dP/dt | 0.92 + pulse | Compensate fuel wall-wetting |

**Explanation (3 Marks):**
- During cranking, asynchronous injection is used (independent of camshaft) to provide extra fuel.
- Open-loop uses pre-calibrated 3-D maps.
- Closed-loop uses PI control on lambda signal.
- Power enrichment enriches the mixture to ~ 0.85 lambda for detonation protection and maximum torque.
- DFCO cuts off fuel completely when decelerating to save fuel and reduce emissions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks – Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Derive the **injector pulse width equation** for a Speed-Density based Electronic Fuel Control System starting from the **Ideal Gas Law**. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With reference to a specific numerical example, compute the injector pulse width for the following data: 4-cylinder 4-stroke engine, $V_d = 1.6 \,\text{L}$, $N = 2500 \,\text{RPM}$, $P_{man} = 0.5 \,\text{bar gauge}$, $T_{man} = 330 \,\text{K}$, $\eta_{vol} = 0.88$, $A/F = 14.7$, injector flow rate = 200 g/min, dead time = 1.2 ms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Air behaves as an ideal gas (Ideal Gas Law valid).
2. Volumetric efficiency is constant over the cycle.
3. Fuel injection is batched — one pulse per cycle.
4. Injector flow is steady and turbulence-free.
5. Battery voltage is nominal (no $F_{bat}$ correction needed initially).

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Air mass per intake stroke from the Ideal Gas Law:
$$P_{man} \cdot V_{d,cyl} = m_{air} \cdot R \cdot T_{man}$$
$$m_{air} = \frac{P_{man} \cdot V_{d,cyl}}{R \cdot T_{man}}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Apply volumetric efficiency:
$$m_{air,actual} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \frac{P_{man} \cdot V_{d,cyl}}{R \cdot T_{man}}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Stoichiometric fuel mass:
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air,actual}}{A/F_{stoich}} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot P_{man} \cdot V_{d,cyl}}{14.7 \cdot R \cdot T_{man}}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Injector flow rate model:
$$\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{or} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \cdot (P_{rail} - P_{man})}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Theoretical pulse width:
$$T_{i,theor} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{inj}} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot P_{man} \cdot V_{d,cyl}}{14.7 \cdot R \cdot T_{man} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Add dead time and battery correction:
$$\boxed{\, T_{i,actual} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot P_{man} \cdot V_{d,cyl}}{14.7 \cdot R \cdot T_{man} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}} \cdot F_{bat} + T_{dead} \,}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Note the proportionalities:
- $T_i \propto P_{man}$ (hence "Density" — derived from P/T)
- $T_i \propto 1/T_{man}$ (hot air → less dense → less fuel)
- $T_i \propto 1/\dot{m}_{inj}$ (weaker injector → longer pulse)

#### (b) Numerical Example (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_d = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3$, $N = 2500 \,\text{RPM}$, $P_{man} = 0.5 \times 10^5 = 5 \times 10^4 \,\text{Pa}$
- $T_{man} = 330 \,\text{K}$, $\eta_{vol} = 0.88$, $R = 287$
- $\dot{m}_{inj} = 200 \,\text{g/min} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{kg/s}$
- $T_{dead} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{s}$, $A/F = 14.7$

**Step 1: Air mass per cycle** (1 Mark)
$$m_{air} = \frac{0.88 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^4}{287 \times 330} = \frac{70.4}{94710} = 7.434 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{kg/cycle}$$

**Step 2: Fuel mass per cycle** (1 Mark)
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{7.434 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 5.057 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{kg/cycle}$$

**Step 3: Fuel per cylinder per cycle** (1 Mark)
$$m_{fuel,cyl} = \frac{5.057 \times 10^{-5}}{4} = 1.264 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{kg/injection}$$

**Step 4: Theoretical pulse width** (2 Marks)
$$T_{i,theor} = \frac{1.264 \times 10^{-5}}{3.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.79 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{s} = 3.79 \,\text{ms}$$

**Step 5: Add dead time** (1 Mark)
$$T_{i,actual} = 3.79 + 1.2 = 4.99 \,\text{ms} \approx 5.0 \,\text{ms}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion** (1 Mark)
> The injector must be energized for **approximately 5.0 ms** per injection event. In one second, the engine completes $2500/60/2 \times 4 = 83.3$ injection events, drawing $83.3 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.333 \times 10^{-3} = 1.388 \,\text{g/s}$ of fuel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert gauge pressure to **absolute** pressure. If $P_{man} = 0.5 \,\text{bar gauge}$, then absolute pressure is $0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5 \,\text{bar} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \,\text{Pa}$. (–2 Marks)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $V_d$ (total displacement) directly without dividing by the number of cylinders in the ideal-gas equation. The formula uses $V_{d,cyl} = V_d / N_{cyl}$. (–1 Mark)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to add injector dead time $T_{dead}$. This is a 0.5 – 1.0 ms error but is the most common reason for ECU calibration mismatch. (–1 Mark)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **Speed-Density** (P, T based) with **Mass Airflow** (direct vane/hot-wire) systems. (–1 Mark)
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing $R = 8.314$ (universal gas constant) instead of $R = 287 \,\text{J/(kg·K)}$ (specific gas constant for air). (–0.5 Mark)
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Not specifying the units of $T_i$ (must be in **milliseconds** at the final answer, not seconds).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bulletins:**

- **Stoichiometric A/F for petrol = 14.7 : 1** (by mass). Memorize this value.
- **Excess Air Factor λ = (A/F)_actual / 14.7.** λ = 1 is the holy grail for TWC efficiency.
- **Speed-Density** uses $P_{man}$ and $T_{man}$ (D-Jetronic). **Mass-Flow** uses a vane or hot-wire sensor (L-Jetronic / LH-Jetronic).
- **Three operating modes**: Cranking (rich), Open-Loop (3-D map), Closed-Loop (λ feedback).
- **Power enrichment (WOT): λ ≈ 0.85** — used for detonation protection, not just for power.
- **Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO):** Throttle closed + RPM > 1100 → fuel OFF.
- **Injectors** are of two types: **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)** at 2.5 – 4.0 bar and **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** at 100 – 200 bar.
- **Lambda Sensor** is a **galvanic cell** based on **zirconia-ceramic (ZrO₂)** electrolyte with platinum electrodes. Output: **0.1 V (lean) to 0.9 V (rich)**.
- **PI Controller** is the standard closed-loop compensator for λ regulation.
- **Injector dead time** $T_{dead}$ is **0.8 – 1.5 ms** at 14 V battery and must be added to theoretical pulse width.
- **Battery voltage correction factor $F_{bat}$** — for $V_{bat} < 14\,\text{V}$, the solenoid pulls slower, requiring longer pulse width.
- **Ideal Gas Law constants:** $R_{air} = 287 \,\text{J/(kg·K)}$; $P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$.
- **Cold-start enrichment factor** can be as high as **5×** at –20 °C.
- **Catalytic converter** requires λ = 1 ± 0.01 — this narrow window mandates closed-loop control.
- **OBD-II** monitors the EFI system and triggers MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) on faults.
- **CAN bus** carries EFI parameters to other ECUs (TCU, BCM, ABS).
- **D-Jetronic** (1967) was the first production EFI — uses MAP sensor only.
- **LH-Jetronic** (1981) introduced **hot-wire mass airflow sensor** and **closed-loop λ control**.
- **Motronic** integrates **ignition + injection** into a single ECU.
- **Wall-wetting effect** causes transient lean spikes during acceleration → corrected by **acceleration enrichment pulse**.
- **The master equation:** $T_i \propto \dfrac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{man}}{R \cdot T_{man}}$ (linear in $P_{man}$, inverse in $T_{man}$).
- **Closed-loop frequency:** ~1 Hz for narrow-band sensor, > 100 Hz for wide-band sensor.
- **Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$** for a naturally aspirated engine lies between **0.80 and 0.95** at peak torque.
- **MAP sensor** output: **0.5 V (low load) to 4.5 V (WOT)** for a typical 5 V supply.
- **TPS** output: **~ 0.5 V (idle) to ~ 4.5 V (WOT)**, with a separate idle-switch contact.
- **CTS** is an NTC thermistor: resistance **decreases** as temperature rises.
- **CKP sensor** (inductive or Hall-effect) provides **60-2 tooth pattern** for crank angle and RPM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed Loop Electronic Fuel Control System and Electronic Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Closed Loop Electronic Fuel Control System and Electronic Ignition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Closed Loop Electronic Fuel Control System (CL-FI)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **Closed Loop Electronic Fuel Control System** is a real-time feedback control architecture in which an electronic control unit (ECU) continuously monitors the exhaust gas oxygen content using a lambda (λ) / Oxygen (O₂) sensor mounted in the exhaust manifold, compares the actual Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) against the chemically ideal **stoichiometric ratio of 14.7:1** for gasoline (petrol) engines, and dynamically modulates the injector pulse-width to maintain λ = 1.000 ± 0.01.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Definition
> Closed loop operation is mathematically defined as the condition when the ECU uses the post-combustion feedback signal from the O₂ sensor (voltage output ~0.1 V to 0.9 V) to "close" the control loop, thereby overriding the pre-programmed open-loop fuel map.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Shower Heater
Imagine you are taking a shower with a **manually mixed** hot/cold tap. The "open loop" mode is you guessing the knob position — sometimes too hot, sometimes too cold. The "closed loop" mode is when a **temperature sensor on the showerhead** tells the mixing valve: *"Water is 2°C too cold"*, and the valve automatically rotates a few degrees to compensate. The **O₂ sensor is the showerhead thermometer**; the **ECU is the mixing valve brain**; and the **fuel injectors are the hot/cold taps**. The system never stops adjusting because the "water" (exhaust gas) keeps changing.

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics
- **Stoichiometric AFR (Gasoline):** 14.7 : 1
- **Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel):** 14.5 : 1 (mostly runs lean, λ > 1.5)
- **Stoichiometric AFR (CNG / LPG):** 17.2 : 1 and 15.5 : 1 respectively
- **O₂ Sensor Output Voltage Range:** 100 mV (lean) ↔ 900 mV (rich)
- **Stoichiometric Lambda Point:** **λ = 1.000** (the "switching point" of the O₂ sensor)
- **Closed-Loop Activation Temperature:** O₂ sensor must be > 350 °C (heated sensors reach this in < 30 s)
- **Catalytic Converter Efficiency Window:** 0.99 < λ < 1.01 (the entire reason closed-loop exists)

> [!NOTE] Why λ = 1.000?
> The three-way catalytic converter (TWC) can only simultaneously reduce NOₓ, oxidize CO, and oxidize unburnt HC when the exhaust gas oscillates very tightly around the stoichiometric point. Any deviation > 1% destroys the TWC efficiency — this is the engineering driver for closed loop.

---

## 1.2 Electronic Ignition System (EIS)

### Formal Definition
An **Electronic Ignition System** is a microprocessor-controlled ignition architecture that replaces mechanical contact-breaker points with a solid-state switching transistor (typically an **IGBT — Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor**) triggered by crankshaft and camshaft position sensors, providing precise control over **ignition timing (spark advance)**, **dwell angle**, and **firing order** for each cylinder in real time.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor
Think of a 4-cylinder engine as a 4-piece band. Each musician (spark plug) must play their note at **exactly the right millisecond**, or the music (combustion) collapses. A **mechanical distributor** is like a tired conductor waving a baton — sometimes a fraction early, sometimes late. An **electronic ignition system** is a **metronome wired to a computer** — it watches the crankshaft (the tempo) and tells each spark plug precisely when to fire, even adjusting for the room temperature (knock) and the engine's "energy level" (load and RPM). The **knock sensor** is the audience member shouting *"You're off-key!"* — the ECU listens and corrects.

### Subsystem Components
| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **CKP Sensor** (Crankshaft Position) | Measures RPM and piston TDC position (typically variable-reluctance / Hall-effect) |
| **CMP Sensor** (Camshaft Position) | Identifies the compression stroke vs exhaust stroke (sequential injection needs this) |
| **ECU** | Computes ignition advance from 3D maps: (RPM, Load) |
| **Ignition Coil** | Steps up 12 V to 25–40 kV |
| **IGBT Switch** | Electronically breaks primary current; controls dwell time |
| **Knock Sensor** (Piezoelectric) | Detects detonation (3–7 kHz vibration) and requests retard |
| **Spark Plug** | Creates arc across the gap to ignite the air-fuel mixture |

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Highlight
> Modern EIS architectures are **Distributor-Less Ignition Systems (DIS)** with either **wasted spark** (one coil per pair of cylinders) or **Coil-on-Plug (COP)** (one coil per cylinder) topology. Distributors have been completely phased out of petrol passenger cars since ~1995.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Closed Loop Fuel Control — Operating Logic (Step-by-Step)

### 2.1.1 The Three Operating Modes
1. **Cold Start / Open-Loop Mode** — Engine coolant temperature (ECT) < ~ 40 °C. ECU uses a pre-calibrated "cold enrichment" map: AFR ≈ 9:1 (very rich) to assist evaporation. The O₂ sensor is **cold** (output ≈ 0 V, undefined), so no feedback is used.
2. **Closed-Loop Mode** — ECT > ~ 40 °C AND O₂ sensor > 350 °C. ECU reads the O₂ sensor voltage 1–10 times per second and modulates the injector pulse-width.
3. **Open-Loop Override (Fault / Wide-Open Throttle)** — If MAF sensor fails, O₂ sensor heater fails, or WOT is commanded (λ deliberately goes to ~0.85 for maximum power), the ECU returns to **open-loop** map-based control.

### 2.1.2 Closed Loop Feedback Logic — Why "Oscillation" is Normal
A narrow-band O₂ sensor (most common, zirconia) behaves like a **threshold switch**, not a linear sensor. It outputs:
- ≈ 0.1 V if λ > 1 (lean)
- ≈ 0.9 V if λ < 1 (rich)

Because the sensor only knows "lean" or "rich" but **not "how much"**, the ECU is forced to **integrate** the error using a **PI (Proportional-Integral) controller**. The result: AFR **oscillates** between ~14.4 and ~15.0 at roughly **0.5–2 Hz**. This oscillation is *designed-in* and is the signature of correct closed-loop behavior.

### 2.1.3 Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT) vs Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT)
- **STFT** — Immediate correction (updates every 10–100 ms). Range: **−100 % to +99.2 %** (binary 8-bit, 1 LSB = 0.78 %).
- **LTFT** — Long-term learning value (updates every few minutes). Stored in non-volatile KAM (Keep-Alive Memory). Range: **−100 % to +99.2 %**.

**Final Injector Pulse-Width Equation:**

$$PW_{final} = PW_{base} \times \left(1 + \frac{STFT + LTFT}{100}\right) \times \text{Compensations}$$

Compensations include: coolant temperature, air temperature, barometric pressure, battery voltage (injector dead-time), and transient acceleration enrichment.

### 2.1.4 The "Lambda" Mathematical Framework
Lambda is a **dimensionless equivalence ratio** — universal across all fuels:

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}$$

| λ value | Meaning | ECU Strategy |
|---------|---------|--------------|
| λ < 1 | Rich (excess fuel) | Power enrichment, cold start |
| λ = 1.000 | Stoichiometric (chemically perfect) | Closed loop, cruise, idle |
| λ > 1 | Lean (excess air) | Lean burn (GDI), part-throttle cruise |

---

## 2.2 Electronic Ignition — Operating Logic

### 2.2.1 The Three Variables the ECU Computes in Real Time
1. **Ignition Timing Advance** (degrees BTDC)
2. **Dwell Time** (ms — how long primary coil current flows)
3. **Firing Order / Trigger Routing** (which coil fires when)

### 2.2.2 Total Ignition Advance Equation
The ECU uses a **3-D lookup table** indexed by (RPM, Load):

$$T_{adv} = T_{initial} + T_{centrifugal} + T_{vacuum} - T_{knock\,retard} - T_{warmup}$$

In the **electronic** system, all three "mechanical" terms (initial, centrifugal, vacuum) are **synthesized in software** — there are no flyweights or vacuum diaphragms. The ECU does this with a **16×16 map** typically.

### 2.2.3 Dwell Control
Modern systems use **"current-controlled dwell"**:
- ECU applies battery voltage to the coil primary.
- A current-sense resistor detects I_primary.
- When I_primary reaches ~ 7 A, the IGBT turns OFF.
- Energy stored in the coil: $E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$
- Higher RPM → less available time → ECU extends dwell earlier (adaptive).

> [!NOTE] Engineering Insight
> At 6000 RPM with a 4-stroke engine, each cylinder fires once every **20 ms**. Half of that (10 ms) is the "available window" for dwell + spark + recovery. Older systems with fixed dwell would simply **dwell-out** (saturate) at high RPM. Current-controlled dwell solves this elegantly.

### 2.2.4 Knock Control Sub-Loop
The piezoelectric knock sensor produces a voltage spike when abnormal combustion (detonation, 3–7 kHz) is detected. The ECU:
1. Filters the signal in the **3–7 kHz band**.
2. Compares amplitude against a **background noise map** (RPM + load dependent).
3. If knock is confirmed, **retards timing in 1–3° steps** (typically 1° per event).
4. Slowly **re-advances** at ~ 0.1° per combustion cycle until knock reappears or normal advance is restored.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units / Range | KTU Use |
|---------|---------|---------------|---------|
| $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless; 0.5 ≤ λ ≤ 1.5 | Open vs closed loop decision |
| $AFR_{stoich,\,gasoline} = 14.7$ | Stoichiometric AFR (petrol) | mass ratio | λ = 1 reference |
| $PW_{final} = PW_{base} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{STFT + LTFT}{100}\right)$ | Final injector pulse-width | ms | Closed loop correction |
| $V_{O_2} \approx 0.1\,V \leftrightarrow 0.9\,V$ | O₂ sensor output | volts, narrow-band | Rich/lean detection |
| $T_{adv} = T_{initial} + T_{cent} + T_{vac} - T_{knock}$ | Total spark advance | degrees BTDC | Ignition map |
| $E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Energy stored in ignition coil | Joules | Coil sizing |
| $V_{secondary} = V_{primary} \times \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Coil step-up ratio | volts | 12 V → 25 kV |
| $f_{knock} \approx 3\text{–}7\,kHz$ | Knock sensor resonant band | Hz | Filter design |
| $\theta_{dwell} = \frac{t_{dwell} \cdot RPM \cdot N_{cyl}}{1200}$ | Dwell angle | degrees | Ignition timing |
| $\eta_{TWC} \approx f(\lambda) \big\vert_{\lambda=1}$ | 3-way catalyst efficiency | % (peaks at λ=1) | Motivation for closed loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All LaTeX tables use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the bare pipe `|` to prevent markdown table-breaking in KTU-Premium rendering.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric AFR for Gasoline (C₈H₁₈)

The complete combustion reaction for iso-octane (gasoline surrogate):

$$C_8H_{18} + x \cdot O_2 + 3.76 x \cdot N_2 \rightarrow 8 CO_2 + 9 H_2O + 3.76 x \cdot N_2$$

**Step 1:** Balance oxygen atoms.

LHS oxygen atoms = $2x$

RHS oxygen atoms = $(8 \times 2) + (9 \times 1) = 16 + 9 = 25$

Equating: $2x = 25 \Rightarrow x = 12.5$

**Step 2:** Mass of air required (using air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂):
- Mass of O₂: $12.5 \times 32 = 400$ g
- Mass of N₂: $12.5 \times 3.76 \times 28 = 1316$ g
- Total mass of air: $400 + 1316 = 1716$ g
- Mass of fuel: $1 \times (8 \times 12) + (18 \times 1) = 96 + 18 = 114$ g

**Step 3:** Compute mass AFR:

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05$$

> [!NOTE]
> The theoretical 15.05:1 is the chemistry-based value. Industry uses the empirically measured **14.7:1** because real gasoline is a mixture of many hydrocarbons (paraffins, aromatics, olefins), not pure iso-octane. KTU board exam answers must quote **14.7:1** for gasoline.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Final Injector Pulse-Width Under Closed-Loop Correction

**Given (sample problem data):**
- Base pulse-width (from map): $PW_{base} = 2.50$ ms
- Short-term fuel trim: $STFT = +6.25\,\%$ (ECU richened slightly)
- Long-term fuel trim: $LTFT = -3.125\,\%$
- Coolant temperature compensation: $+2.0\,\%$
- Battery voltage compensation: $+1.5\,\%$

**Step 1:** Combine STFT and LTFT.

$$\Delta_{trim} = \frac{6.25 + (-3.125)}{100} = \frac{3.125}{100} = 0.03125$$

**Step 2:** Apply trim factor.

$$PW_{trimmed} = 2.50 \times (1 + 0.03125) = 2.50 \times 1.03125 = 2.5781\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 3:** Apply temperature compensation.

$$PW_{temp} = 2.5781 \times (1 + 0.020) = 2.5781 \times 1.020 = 2.6297\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 4:** Apply battery voltage compensation.

$$PW_{final} = 2.6297 \times (1 + 0.015) = 2.6297 \times 1.015 = 2.6691\,\text{ms}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$PW_{final} \approx 2.67\,\text{ms}$$

The injector stays open for an additional ~0.17 ms compared to the base map — enough to add roughly 4% more fuel mass per cycle, which brings the O₂ sensor voltage back into the switching band.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dwell Angle from Dwell Time

**Given:**
- Dwell time: $t_{dwell} = 4.0$ ms
- Engine speed: $N = 3000$ RPM
- 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine

**Step 1:** Time per crankshaft revolution.

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} = \frac{60}{3000} = 0.020\,\text{s/rev} = 20\,\text{ms/rev}$$

**Step 2:** Degrees per millisecond.

$$\frac{360°}{20\,\text{ms}} = 18\,°\text{/ms}$$

**Step 3:** Convert dwell time to dwell angle.

$$\theta_{dwell} = t_{dwell} \times 18 = 4.0 \times 18 = 72°$$

**Step 4:** Cross-check with general formula.

$$\theta_{dwell} = \frac{t_{dwell} \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl}}{1200} = \frac{4.0 \times 3000 \times 4}{1200} = \frac{48000}{1200} = 40°$$

> [!NOTE] Why the discrepancy?
> The first method (72°) is degrees of *crankshaft rotation* during dwell. The second (40°) is the equivalent *per-cylinder firing-event share*. KTU expects you to clearly state which convention you are using. For distributor / wasted-spark systems, use the first; for per-cylinder coil analysis, use the second.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ignition Energy Required for Reliable Combustion

**Given:** Spark plug gap = 1.1 mm; required breakdown voltage ≈ 25 kV; secondary coil turns ratio $N_s/N_p = 200$.

**Step 1:** Required primary current to generate 25 kV.

The transformer equation gives the *voltage*; the *energy* depends on the coil's primary inductance $L_p$ and the current at IGBT turn-off.

$$V_{secondary} = V_{primary} \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

$$V_{primary} = \frac{V_{secondary}}{N_s/N_p} = \frac{25{,}000}{200} = 125\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Assume a typical primary inductance $L_p = 6$ mH and that the ECU commands a peak current $I_p = 7$ A at turn-off.

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 7^2 = 0.147\,\text{J}$$

**Step 3:** Energy actually delivered to the spark (assume 60 % transfer efficiency).

$$E_{spark} = 0.60 \times 0.147 = 0.088\,\text{J} \approx 88\,\text{mJ}$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** This is comfortably above the minimum ignition energy of ~ 1 mJ required for a stoichiometric mixture, and provides a healthy margin for lean or EGR-diluted mixtures that need ~ 5–20 mJ.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Closed Loop + Electronic Ignition Are Inseparable

| Subsystem | Open-Loop Behaviour | Closed-Loop Behaviour | Catalyst Linkage |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|------------------|
| **Fuel Control** | AFR can drift 12:1 to 17:1 | AFR pinned to 14.4–15.0 | TWC requires λ = 1 ± 0.01 |
| **Ignition Timing** | Fixed mechanical advance | Adaptive, 3-D mapped | Misfire → HC → TWC damage |
| **Knock Control** | Cannot respond | Retard in 1° steps | Knock → piston damage |
| **Idle Quality** | Rough, stalls | ECU adjusts spark + fuel | Smooth idle, low emissions |
| **Cold Start** | Choke required | ECU enrichment map | Pre-catalyst cold enrichment |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Closed Loop Fuel Control — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Pedal] --> B[Throttle Body / MAF Sensor]
    B --> C[Engine Intake Manifold]
    C --> D[Combustion Chamber]
    D --> E[Exhaust Manifold]
    E --> F[Oxygen Lambda Sensor]
    F --> G[ECU - PI Controller]
    G --> H[Short Term Fuel Trim]
    H --> I[Long Term Fuel Trim Store]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Injector Driver]
    J --> K[Fuel Injector]
    K --> D
    G --> L[Check Engine Lamp MIL]
    F -->|Out of range| M[Fault Code P0130-P0167]
    M --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **two feedback arrows** from the O₂ sensor: one fast (STFT) and one slow (LTFT). The O₂ sensor in the middle of the diagram is the *only* sensor that "closes" the loop — without it, the system would be open-loop.

---

```mermaid
    MAP --> CALC[Advance Calculator]
    CALC --> IGBT[IGBT Driver - Dwell Control]
    IGBT --> C1[Coil Cyl 1]
    IGBT --> C2[Coil Cyl 2]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[O2 Output 0.1V - Lean]

    D --> E[O2 Output 0.9V - Rich]
```
