# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Differentiate between **open loop** and **closed loop** modes of an electronic fuel control system. Under what conditions does the ECU switch from open loop to closed loop?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback | None (uses pre-stored map) | O₂ sensor provides feedback |
| AFR Accuracy | ±20 % | ±1 % |
| Operating conditions | Cold start, WOT, fault | Warm engine, normal cruise |
| ECU computation | Table look-up | PI controller (STFT + LTFT) |

**Switching conditions (1 mark):** ECU enters closed loop only when **both** (a) engine coolant temperature > ~ 40 °C **and** (b) O₂ sensor temperature > 350 °C (heated sensor). Below these thresholds, the O₂ sensor output is undefined (~ 0 V) and cannot be trusted as a feedback signal.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the function of a **knock sensor** in an electronic ignition system? Explain how the ECU responds to a confirmed knock event.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A knock sensor is a **piezoelectric transducer** mounted on the engine block that detects high-frequency vibrations (3–7 kHz) characteristic of abnormal combustion (detonation). **(1 mark)**

When the ECU confirms knock by comparing the filtered signal to a background-noise map (RPM/load indexed): **(1 mark)**
- It **retards ignition timing** in 1–3° steps per knock event.
- It then **slowly re-advances** at ~ 0.1° per combustion cycle until knock reappears or normal advance is restored.
- This forms a closed sub-loop *within* the ignition system itself. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Closed Loop Electronic Fuel Injection System** using a narrow-band zirconia oxygen sensor. Discuss the role of **STFT and LTFT** in maintaining stoichiometric AFR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1)

**Working (3 marks):**
1. The mass airflow sensor (MAF) measures the mass of air entering the engine and sends a voltage signal to the ECU.
2. The ECU uses this signal along with engine speed and coolant temperature to look up a **base fuel map** that estimates the injector pulse-width required for stoichiometric combustion.
3. The injector delivers fuel; combustion occurs; exhaust gases pass over the **zirconia O₂ sensor** in the exhaust manifold.
4. The O₂ sensor generates a voltage proportional to the oxygen partial pressure difference: ~ 0.1 V (lean) to ~ 0.9 V (rich), with a sharp transition at λ = 1.000.
5. The ECU reads this voltage and applies a **PI (Proportional-Integral) controller** to compute a correction factor.

**STFT and LTFT Role (2 marks):**
- **STFT (Short-Term Fuel Trim):** Immediate correction factor updated every 10–100 ms. Range: −100 % to +99.2 %. It directly modifies the injector pulse-width in real time.
- **LTFT (Long-Term Fuel Trim):** Slow, learned correction stored in KAM. Updated every few minutes from the average of STFT values. Compensates for **permanent** system drifts — aging O₂ sensors, vacuum leaks, injector wear, fuel pressure variations.
- Together, STFT and LTFT ensure that the average commanded AFR remains at 14.7:1, allowing the three-way catalyst to operate at peak efficiency.

---

**Q. (b)** An engine is running at **3000 RPM** in closed loop. The base pulse-width from the fuel map is **2.40 ms**. STFT = +5 %, LTFT = −2 %, coolant compensation = +3 %, battery compensation = +1 %. Calculate the **final injector pulse-width**. Also compute the **stoichiometric AFR** from the combustion of gasoline (C₈H₁₈). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Pulse-width calculation (4 marks):**

$$PW_{step1} = 2.40 \times \left(1 + \frac{5 + (-2)}{100}\right) = 2.40 \times 1.03 = 2.472\,\text{ms}$$

$$PW_{step2} = 2.472 \times 1.03 = 2.546\,\text{ms}$$

$$PW_{step3} = 2.546 \times 1.01 = 2.572\,\text{ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $PW_{final} = 2.572\,\text{ms} \approx 2.57\,\text{ms}$

**Part 2 — Stoichiometric AFR (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 3.1 derivation.)

The balanced equation: $C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 47\,N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2$

$$\text{Mass of air} = (12.5 \times 32) + (47 \times 28) = 400 + 1316 = 1716\,\text{g}$$

$$\text{Mass of fuel} = (8 \times 12) + (18 \times 1) = 114\,\text{g}$$

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05$$

For real gasoline (multi-component mixture): $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7:1$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of a **Distributor-Less Ignition System (DIS)** with **Coil-on-Plug (COP)** configuration. Compare it with the conventional contact-breaker ignition system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**COP Architecture (4 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2)
- Each cylinder has a dedicated ignition coil mounted directly on top of its spark plug — hence "coil-on-plug."
- The **IGBT switch** for each coil is integrated into the coil module or controlled separately by the ECU.
- The **CKP sensor** (typically a variable-reluctance sensor scanning a 36−1 tooth reluctor wheel, the missing tooth giving TDC reference) feeds crankshaft position to the ECU.
- The **CMP sensor** identifies the compression stroke for **sequential fuel injection** and prevents firing on the exhaust stroke.
- The ECU computes dwell time and spark advance from the 3D map indexed by (RPM, Load) and triggers the appropriate IGBT at the correct crank angle.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Contact Breaker (Old) | COP Electronic (New) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Triggering | Mechanical cam + points | Solid-state IGBT + magnetic sensor |
| Spark timing accuracy | ±3–5° | ±0.5° |
| Wear | Yes (points, cam, condenser) | No mechanical wear |
| Dwell control | Fixed by cam profile | Adaptive, current-controlled |
| High-RPM performance | Dwell-out at > 5000 RPM | Stable to 8000+ RPM |
| Maintenance | Tune-up every 30,000 km | No scheduled maintenance |
| Misfire detection | None | Built-in (ion-sense / coil feedback) |

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **knock control sub-loop** in an electronic ignition system. An engine fires at **4000 RPM** with **dwell time 3.5 ms**. Compute the **dwell angle** in degrees of crankshaft rotation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Knock Control Sub-Loop (4 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.4)
1. The **piezoelectric knock sensor** is bolted to the engine block (typically between cylinders 2 and 3 on an inline-4).
2. During normal combustion, the vibration spectrum in the 3–7 kHz band is low — only background mechanical noise.
3. When knock (detonation) occurs, abnormal auto-ignition of the end-gas creates a sharp pressure wave, exciting the block at 3–7 kHz.
4. The ECU **bandpass-filters** the sensor signal in this range and compares the amplitude to a **background noise map** indexed by (RPM, Load) — to avoid false positives from valve closing, piston slap, etc.
5. If knock is confirmed (amplitude > threshold), the ECU **retards** ignition timing in **1–3° steps** per event.
6. The ECU then **re-advances** at a slow rate (~ 0.1° per cycle) until knock reappears or the original advance is restored.
7. This is a true closed loop *within* the ignition timing control.

**Dwell Angle Calculation (3 marks):**

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{4000} = 0.015\,\text{s} = 15\,\text{ms}$$

$$\text{Degrees per millisecond} = \frac{360°}{15\,\text{ms}} = 24°/\text{ms}$$

$$\theta_{dwell} = 3.5 \times 24 = 84° \text{ of crankshaft rotation}$$

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout
> **Common ways students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing AFR with lambda** — AFR is a mass ratio (14.7), lambda is dimensionless (1.0). Don't interchange them in derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the final answer — KTU board examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units on every numerical question.
> 3. **Not specifying the O₂ sensor type** — narrow-band (zinc-oxide) vs wide-band (UEGO) give *very* different control behaviour. Always mention the sensor type.
> 4. **Mixing ignition advance conventions** — "BTDC" (Before Top Dead Centre) is positive advance; "ATDC" is *retard*. Write the sign explicitly.
> 5. **Using `14.7` vs `15.05`** — for derivation-based stoichiometry questions, derive 15.05 first, then state the empirical 14.7 for real gasoline.
> 6. **Skipping the "open-loop override"** in the closed-loop answer — examiners specifically look for fault / WOT override discussion (1–2 marks).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Closed loop fuel control** is defined by the presence of an **O₂ / λ sensor** in the exhaust — without it, the loop is open.
- The **stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7 : 1**; for diesel ≈ 14.5; for CNG ≈ 17.2; for LPG ≈ 15.5.
- **Lambda λ = AFR_actual / AFR_stoich**; λ = 1 is the ideal catalyst operating point.
- **STFT** = rapid correction (−100 % to +99.2 %); **LTFT** = slow learned correction (also −100 % to +99.2 %).
- Final pulse-width: $PW_{final} = PW_{base} \times (1 + \frac{STFT+LTFT}{100}) \times \text{compensations}$.
- Closed loop activates only when **ECT > ~ 40 °C** **AND** **O₂ sensor > 350 °C**.
- **Electronic Ignition** uses **CKP + CMP + Knock** sensors feeding a 3D (RPM, Load) map to an IGBT-driven coil.
- **Dwell time** is the duration the IGBT stays ON to build magnetic energy in the coil: $E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$.
- **Knock control** is a sub-loop that retards timing 1–3° per knock event, then slowly re-advances 0.1°/cycle.
- **Distributor-Less Ignition (DIS)** uses either **wasted spark** (1 coil/2 cylinders) or **Coil-on-Plug (COP)** (1 coil/cylinder) — modern engines universally use COP.
- **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** requires λ = 1 ± 0.01 — the *only* reason closed loop is mandated by emissions regulations.
- O₂ sensor output is **0.1 V (lean) ↔ 0.9 V (rich)** for narrow-band zirconia sensors.
- CKP sensor typically uses a **36−1 tooth** reluctor wheel; the missing tooth gives the TDC reference.
- Always quote the **fuel type** when stating AFR; **always quote the units** in numerical answers.
- The **total spark advance** = initial + centrifugal + vacuum − knock retard − warmup correction.
- For Coil-on-Plug systems, the **secondary voltage can reach 25–40 kV** — service precautions apply.
- The **ECU runs a PI controller** on the O₂ signal — designed-in AFR oscillation (~ 0.5–2 Hz) is *normal* and expected.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Motion Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Motion Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle Motion Controls (VMC)** refers to the integrated suite of electronic subsystems within a modern automobile that sense, compute, and actuate the longitudinal (forward/reverse) and lateral (sideways) dynamics of the vehicle. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this domain encompasses the closed-loop electronic regulation of:

- **Cruise Control (CC) and Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**
- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**
- **Traction Control System (TCS)**
- **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)**
- **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC / Drive-by-Wire)**
- **Automatic Transmission Control**
- **Active Suspension Systems**

These systems use real-time sensor data (wheel speed, steering angle, yaw rate, lateral acceleration, throttle position) processed by Electronic Control Units (ECUs) to maintain driver-intended trajectory under varying road and load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** *Vehicle Motion Controls are mechatronic subsystems employing feedback principles to regulate longitudinal acceleration, deceleration, yaw moment, and wheel slip, thereby enhancing safety, comfort, and drivability.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle** down a sloped, wet road.

- **Cruise Control** is like pedalling at a steady rhythm on flat ground — your brain maintains a constant leg rotation without thinking.
- **ABS** is like when you squeeze the brake levers rhythmically on a slippery surface to avoid skidding — the system does this *much faster* (up to **15–25 cycles per second**) than you ever could.
- **ESC** is like an invisible hand that gently pushes the front wheel in the right direction when the bicycle starts to slip sideways on a turn.
- **Traction Control** is the bicycle equivalent of reducing your pedalling force the instant the rear wheel starts spinning on mud — preventing wasted grip.

In essence, Vehicle Motion Controls replace the *instinctive, reactive human driver* with a *fast, deterministic electronic brain* that processes vehicle dynamics at millisecond latencies.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Typical ABS cycle frequency | **15–25 Hz** | Hertz |
| Vehicle controller sampling time | **5–10 ms** | milliseconds |
| CAN bus message latency | **< 5 ms** | milliseconds |
| Typical brake hydraulic pressure | **8–20 MPa** | Megapascals |
| Wheel slip ratio target (ABS) | **0.08 – 0.30** | dimensionless |
| Yaw rate error threshold (ESC) | **< 5°/s** | degrees/second |
| Adaptive cruise radar range | **0.5 – 200 m** | metres |
| Radar operating frequency | **76–77 GHz** | Gigahertz |

> [!NOTE]
> The **slip ratio (λ)** is a *dimensionless* number that represents how much a wheel is sliding relative to the road surface. A value of **λ = 0** means free rolling; **λ = 1** means locked. Optimal braking grip occurs at **λ ≈ 0.15–0.20**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subsystem Architecture Overview

### 2.1.1 Cruise Control (CC)

A **closed-loop speed regulator**. The driver sets a desired speed $v_{set}$, and the ECU adjusts throttle angle $\theta_{th}$ to minimise the speed error:

$$e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$$

The classical controller is a **PI (Proportional-Integral)** controller. The control law is:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

- $K_p$: Proportional gain (immediate correction)
- $K_i$: Integral gain (eliminates steady-state error)

### 2.1.2 Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)

Extends CC by using a **forward-looking radar (76–77 GHz)** or **lidar** to detect a lead vehicle and maintain a safe **time headway** $T_h$ (typically **1.5–2.5 seconds**).

The safe following distance is:

$$d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot T_h + d_{min}$$

Where $v_{ego}$ is the ego-vehicle speed and $d_{min}$ is a static minimum gap (**2–5 m**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two operating modes of ACC:**
> 1. **Speed Control Mode** — when no lead vehicle is detected.
> 2. **Gap Control Mode** — when a lead vehicle is within sensor range.

### 2.1.3 Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)

Prevents wheel lock-up during hard braking by modulating hydraulic brake pressure. The heart of ABS is the **wheel slip ratio**:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{vehicle} - v_{wheel}}{v_{vehicle}} = \frac{v_{vehicle} - \omega \cdot r_{wheel}}{v_{vehicle}}$$

Where $\omega$ is wheel angular velocity and $r_{wheel}$ is the effective wheel radius.

The ABS operates in **three pressure phases**:
1. **Pressure Increase** — applies brake pressure.
2. **Pressure Hold** — maintains pressure.
3. **Pressure Decrease** — releases pressure.

A typical cycle duration is **40–100 ms**.

### 2.1.4 Traction Control System (TCS)

Mirrors ABS during **acceleration**. If driven wheels spin (e.g., on ice), the ECU:
- Reduces engine torque via electronic throttle, OR
- Applies brake force to the spinning wheel

Target slip ratio for TCS: **λ ≈ 0.05 – 0.15**.

### 2.1.5 Electronic Stability Control (ESC)

Uses a **yaw-rate sensor** and **lateral acceleration sensor** to compare the *driver's intended* yaw rate with the *actual* yaw rate.

The reference yaw rate from the bicycle model is:

$$\omega_{ref} = \frac{v_x \cdot \delta}{L \cdot \left(1 + K \cdot v_x^2\right)}$$

Where:
- $v_x$ = longitudinal velocity
- $\delta$ = steering angle at front wheels
- $L$ = wheelbase
- $K = \frac{m}{2L} \cdot \left(\frac{b}{k_r} - \frac{a}{k_f}\right)$ — understeer gradient

If $|\omega_{actual} - \omega_{ref}| > $ threshold, ESC applies **selective braking** to individual wheels to generate a corrective yaw moment.

### 2.1.6 Electronic Throttle Control (ETC / Drive-by-Wire)

Replaces the mechanical throttle cable with an **accelerator pedal position sensor (APP)** and a **DC motor-driven throttle body**. The control law typically includes a **non-linear pedal map** and a **PI speed regulator** in cruise mode.

$$u_{throttle} = f_{pedal\ map}(APP) + u_{cruise}(t)$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Speed Error | $e(t) = v_{set} - v(t)$ | CC/ACC feedback |
| PI Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\, dt$ | Cruise controller |
| Safe Following Gap | $d_{safe} = v_{ego} T_h + d_{min}$ | ACC spacing |
| Wheel Slip Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_v - \omega r}{v_v}$ | ABS/TCS |
| Braking Deceleration | $a_b = \mu g$ | Stopping distance |
| Stopping Distance | $s = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2\mu g}$ | ABS performance |
| Bicycle Model Yaw | $\omega_{ref} = \dfrac{v_x \delta}{L(1+K v_x^2)}$ | ESC reference |
| Understeer Gradient | $K = \dfrac{m}{2L}\left(\dfrac{b}{k_r} - \dfrac{a}{k_f}\right)$ | Vehicle dynamics |
| Longitudinal Slip | $\lambda_x = \dfrac{R\omega - v_x}{v_x}$ | Tyre mechanics |
| Time Headway | $T_h = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | ACC safety |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU standard):**
> - Velocities in **m/s**
> - Accelerations in **m/s²**
> - Angles in **radians** (convert degrees as needed: $1° = \pi/180$ rad)
> - Tyre stiffness $k_f, k_r$ in **N/rad**
> - Mass $m$ in **kg**, distances in **metres**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Safety:** ABS reduces fatal crash involvement by approximately **28%** (NHTSA statistics).
- **Comfort:** Active suspension reduces body acceleration by **30–40%** on rough roads.
- **Emissions:** ETC enables torque-based engine management that improves fuel economy by **5–15%**.
- **Autonomy foundation:** All Vehicle Motion Controls are *prerequisite building blocks* for ADAS Level 2+ and full autonomous driving.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stopping Distance of a Braked Vehicle

The classical derivation of stopping distance is a **high-yield KTU problem**. Let us derive it from Newton's second law.

### Step 1 — Force balance on the braked vehicle

When the brakes are applied, the friction force $F_f$ acts opposite to motion:

$$F_f = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = tyre-road friction coefficient (dimensionless)
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law

The deceleration $a$ along the direction of motion is:

$$m \cdot a = -\mu \cdot m \cdot g$$

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$a = -\mu g$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration.

### Step 3 — Kinematic equation for constant deceleration

Using $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s$, and setting final velocity $v = 0$:

$$0 = v_0^2 + 2(-\mu g) s_{stop}$$

Solving for $s_{stop}$:

$$s_{stop} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g}$$

### Step 4 — Including driver reaction time

If the driver has reaction time $t_r$, the distance travelled during reaction is:

$$s_{reaction} = v_0 \cdot t_r$$

Total stopping distance:

$$s_{total} = v_0 \cdot t_r + \frac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical check:** For $v_0 = 20$ m/s (~72 km/h), $\mu = 0.7$, $g = 9.81$, $t_r = 1.5$ s:
> - $s_{reaction} = 30$ m
> - $s_{stop} = 29.1$ m
> - $s_{total} = 59.1$ m

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wheel Slip Ratio and Friction Coefficient Relationship

This is the **mathematical core of ABS**.

### Step 1 — Define velocities

- Vehicle translational velocity: $v_v$
- Wheel rotational velocity at contact patch: $v_w = \omega \cdot r$

### Step 2 — Slip ratio definitions

**During braking** (wheel slower than vehicle):

$$\lambda_b = \frac{v_v - v_w}{v_v} = 1 - \frac{\omega r}{v_v}$$

**During traction** (wheel spinning faster):

$$\lambda_t = \frac{\omega r - v_v}{v_v}$$

Both are unified as a **signed slip ratio** in some textbooks:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_w - v_v}{\max(v_w, v_v)}$$

### Step 3 — Empirical friction-slip relationship (Burckhardt model)

The tyre-road friction coefficient as a function of slip ratio is empirically modelled as:

$$\mu(\lambda) = c_1 \left(1 - e^{-c_2 \lambda}\right) - c_3 \lambda$$

For dry asphalt, typical values are $c_1 = 1.28$, $c_2 = 23.99$, $c_3 = 0.52$.

### Step 4 — Find optimum slip ratio

The maximum friction $\mu_{max}$ occurs at:

$$\frac{d\mu}{d\lambda} = 0 \implies \lambda_{opt} \approx 0.15 - 0.20$$

This is why ABS targets **λ ≈ 0.15** for maximum braking force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** If $\lambda = 0$, the wheel rolls freely and no braking force exists. If $\lambda = 1$, the wheel locks, friction drops dramatically, and the vehicle skids. Maximum braking occurs at an *intermediate* slip — this is the *non-intuitive magic* of ABS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Yaw Rate from Bicycle Model

The **bicycle model** is the standard simplification used in ESC.

### Step 1 — Geometry

For a vehicle with wheelbase $L$, front and rear axle distances from CoG:
- $a$ = distance from CoG to front axle
- $b$ = distance from CoG to rear axle

Note: $L = a + b$.

### Step 2 — Lateral force balance

Sum of lateral forces on the vehicle:

$$m v_x \dot{\beta} + m v_x \omega_z = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr}$$

Where $\beta$ is sideslip angle, $\omega_z$ is yaw rate, and $F_{yf}, F_{yr}$ are lateral tyre forces.

### Step 3 — Cornering stiffness assumption

For small slip angles $\alpha_f, \alpha_r$:

$$F_{yf} = k_f \alpha_f, \quad F_{yr} = k_r \alpha_r$$

The slip angles are:

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{a \omega_z}{v_x}$$

$$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{b \omega_z}{v_x}$$

### Step 4 — Steady-state yaw rate

In steady-state cornering ($\dot{\beta} = 0$), the yaw rate becomes:

$$\omega_{ref} = \frac{v_x \, \delta}{L + K v_x^2}$$

Where the understeer gradient is:

$$K = \frac{m}{2L}\left(\frac{b}{k_r} - \frac{a}{k_f}\right)$$

- If $K > 0$: **Understeer** (stable, most passenger cars)
- If $K < 0$: **Oversteer** (unstable, performance cars)
- If $K = 0$: **Neutral steer**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse yaw rate $\omega_z$ (rad/s) with angular position. Yaw rate is the *time derivative* of heading angle. Always report in consistent SI units.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Control Strategy vs. Subsystem

| Subsystem | Input Sensors | Control Output | Target Variable | Control Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cruise Control | Vehicle speed | Throttle angle | Speed error | PI |
| Adaptive Cruise | Radar, speed, yaw | Throttle + Brake | Gap / Speed | PI + state machine |
| ABS | Wheel speed | Hydraulic pressure | Slip ratio $\lambda$ | Bang-bang / PID |
| TCS | Wheel speed, throttle | Engine torque, brake | Slip ratio $\lambda$ | PID |
| ESC | Yaw, lateral accel, steering | Selective brake | Yaw rate error | PID + threshold |
| ETC | Pedal position | Throttle plate angle | Pedal map | Open + closed loop |
| Active Suspension | Body accel, wheel vel | Damper force | Body motion | Skyhook / PID |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Set Speed v_set] --> C[ECU Controller]
    B[Radar Sensor 76GHz] --> D[Lead Vehicle Detection]
    D --> E{Gap Mode Active?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Gap Controller d_safe]
    E -- No --> G[Speed Controller PI]
    F --> H[Brake Actuator]
    G --> I[Throttle Actuator]
    H --> J[Vehicle Dynamics]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Wheel Speed Feedback]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Brake Pedal Pressed] --> B[Master Cylinder Pressure]
    B --> C{Hydraulic Modulator}
    C -- Increase --> D[Wheel Cylinder]
    C -- Hold --> D
    C -- Decrease --> D
    D --> E[Wheel Brake Force]
    E --> F[Wheel Speed Sensor]
    F --> G[Slip Ratio Calculator]
    G --> H[Lambda in 0.08 to 0.30]

    H -- No --> J[Adjust Pressure]

    J --> C
```

## 4.3 Electronic Stability Control (ESC) Architecture

```mermaid
    N --> O[Yaw Error Comparison]
    O --> P{Error Exceeds Threshold}

```

## 4.4 Electronic Throttle Control (Drive-by-Wire)


```mermaid
    Layer1 --> Layer2[Layer 2 Active Safety ABS TCS ESC]

    Layer3 --> Layer4[Layer 4 Powertrain Engine Transmission]
    Layer4 --> Wheels[Wheel Tyre Road Interaction]

    Wheels -- Sensor Feedback --> Layer2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords such as `end` or `subgraph` are used as standalone identifiers). Labels are plain uppercase text without bold or italic formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) is an advanced driver assistance system that automatically adjusts the vehicle speed to maintain a safe distance from a preceding vehicle, in addition to the conventional cruise speed-holding function.

**Main components:**
1. Forward-looking radar or lidar sensor (typically 76–77 GHz).
2. Electronic Control Unit (ECU) with gap and speed control algorithms.
3. Throttle actuator and brake actuator.
4. Wheel speed sensors and yaw rate sensor.
5. Human-Machine Interface (HMI) for set-speed and gap selection.

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of the wheel slip ratio in ABS?**

**Model Answer:**
The wheel slip ratio $\lambda$ is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the relative motion between the tyre contact patch and the road surface. It is defined as $\lambda = (v_v - \omega r) / v_v$ during braking. ABS uses $\lambda$ as the **primary feedback variable** because the tyre-road friction coefficient $\mu(\lambda)$ reaches its peak near $\lambda \approx 0.15$ to $0.20$. The controller modulates brake pressure to keep $\lambda$ within this optimal band, thereby maximising braking force while preventing wheel lock.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an Electronic Stability Control (ESC) system. Derive the reference yaw rate expression using the bicycle model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car of mass **1500 kg** is travelling at **90 km/h** on a road with tyre-road friction coefficient $\mu = 0.7$. The driver has a reaction time of **1.2 seconds**. Calculate the total stopping distance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**ESC Block Diagram:**

The ESC system consists of:
- Steering angle sensor (driver's intended direction)
- Yaw rate sensor (actual rotation)
- Lateral accelerometer (side-slip)
- Wheel speed sensors (individual)
- ESC ECU running bicycle-model-based reference yaw calculator
- Hydraulic modulator for selective braking

The ECU computes $\omega_{ref}$ using the bicycle model and compares it to $\omega_{actual}$. If $| \omega_{actual} - \omega_{ref} | >$ threshold (typically 5°/s), the controller applies brake pressure to the appropriate individual wheel to generate a corrective yaw moment, restoring directional stability.

**Reference Yaw Rate Derivation:**

For the bicycle model with wheelbase $L$, front slip angle $\alpha_f$, and rear slip angle $\alpha_r$:

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{a \omega_z}{v_x}$$

$$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{b \omega_z}{v_x}$$

Assuming linear cornering stiffness ($F_y = k\alpha$):

$$m v_x \dot{\beta} = F_{yf} + F_{yr} = k_f \alpha_f + k_r \alpha_r$$

$$I_z \dot{\omega_z} = a k_f \alpha_f - b k_r \alpha_r$$

In steady state ($\dot{\beta} = \dot{\omega_z} = 0$):

$$\omega_{ref} = \frac{v_x \, \delta}{L + K v_x^2}$$

Where:

$$K = \frac{m}{2L}\left(\frac{b}{k_r} - \frac{a}{k_f}\right)$$

is the understeer gradient. This is the reference yaw rate used by ESC.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $m = 1500$ kg, $v_0 = 90$ km/h, $\mu = 0.7$, $t_r = 1.2$ s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units:**

$$v_0 = 90 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reaction distance:**

$$s_{reaction} = v_0 \cdot t_r = 25 \times 1.2 = 30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Braking distance:**

$$s_{brake} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g} = \frac{(25)^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81}$$

$$s_{brake} = \frac{625}{13.734} \approx 45.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Total stopping distance:**

$$s_{total} = s_{reaction} + s_{brake} = 30 + 45.5 = 75.5 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{s_{total} \approx 75.5 \text{ metres}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a block diagram, describe the working of an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS). Define slip ratio and explain its role in the Burckhardt friction model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle with a wheel radius of **0.32 m** is decelerating. The vehicle speed is **20 m/s** and the wheel angular speed is **55 rad/s**. Calculate: (i) the wheel slip ratio, and (ii) comment whether ABS would activate pressure-release in this condition (assume optimal slip band is **0.10 to 0.25**). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**ABS Block Diagram Description:**

When the brake pedal is pressed, the master cylinder generates hydraulic pressure. This pressure is routed through the **hydraulic modulator unit (HMU)** containing solenoid valves that can increase, hold, or decrease pressure to each wheel independently.

Wheel speed sensors provide angular velocity $\omega$ to the ECU. The ECU computes $\lambda$ in real time. If $\lambda$ approaches the locked-wheel limit, the ECU commands the HMU to release pressure. As the wheel re-accelerates, pressure is re-applied. This cycle repeats at **15–25 Hz**.

**Slip Ratio Definition:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_v - \omega r}{v_v}$$

**Role in Burckhardt Model:**

The Burckhardt empirical model expresses tyre-road friction as:

$$\mu(\lambda) = c_1 \left(1 - e^{-c_2 \lambda}\right) - c_3 \lambda$$

This curve peaks near $\lambda \approx 0.15$ for dry asphalt. The ABS controller's goal is to **track this peak** by maintaining $\lambda$ in the optimal band, achieving maximum deceleration without losing steering control.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $r = 0.32$ m, $v_v = 20$ m/s, $\omega = 55$ rad/s.

**(i) Wheel slip ratio calculation:**

First, compute the wheel surface speed:

$$v_w = \omega \cdot r = 55 \times 0.32 = 17.6 \text{ m/s}$$

Now compute slip ratio (since $v_w < v_v$, the wheel is braking):

$$\lambda = \frac{v_v - v_w}{v_v} = \frac{20 - 17.6}{20} = \frac{2.4}{20} = 0.12$$

**(ii) ABS behaviour:**

The optimal band is 0.10 to 0.25. Since $\lambda = 0.12$ lies within this band, the ABS controller will **hold pressure** (neither increase nor release). It is operating in the **optimal grip region** for maximum braking force.

If the slip had exceeded 0.25 (i.e., wheel slowing too much), then the controller would **release pressure** to allow wheel re-acceleration.

**Answer:** $\boxed{\lambda = 0.12; \text{ ABS holds pressure (optimal range)}}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Not converting km/h to m/s in stopping distance problems. This alone costs 2–3 marks. Always convert: $v(\text{m/s}) = v(\text{km/h}) \times \frac{5}{18}$.
> 2. **Sign Error in Slip Ratio:** Forgetting that slip ratio during braking is positive when $v_v > v_w$. Some students write $\lambda = (\omega r - v_v)/v_v$, which is the **traction** slip (acceleration), not braking slip.
> 3. **Missing the Reaction Distance:** In stopping distance problems, examiners *expect* the reaction component. Skipping $v_0 \cdot t_r$ loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Bicycle Model Confusion:** Mixing $a$ and $b$ (CoG distances to axles) with $a$ for acceleration is a classic error. Use distinct symbols.
> 5. **Block Diagram Labeling:** Examiners deduct marks if blocks are not labelled clearly or arrows don't show feedback direction.
> 6. **Writing $g = 10$ instead of 9.81:** Either is acceptable, but state the value used.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vehicle Motion Controls** is the umbrella term for CC, ACC, ABS, TCS, ESC, ETC, and active suspension — all built on **closed-loop feedback principles**.
- The **wheel slip ratio** $\lambda$ is the central control variable for ABS/TCS; the **optimal band** is **0.10–0.25** for braking.
- **ABS cycle frequency** is **15–25 Hz** with three phases: **Pressure Increase, Hold, Release**.
- **ACC radar** operates at **76–77 GHz** and maintains a **time headway** of 1.5–2.5 seconds.
- The **bicycle model** yields the **reference yaw rate** $\omega_{ref} = v_x \delta / (L + K v_x^2)$ — the foundation of ESC.
- **Understeer gradient** $K$ determines vehicle cornering behaviour: positive $K$ = stable understeer (safer).
- **Stopping distance** formula: $s = v_0 t_r + v_0^2 / (2 \mu g)$ — always include reaction component.
- **Drive-by-Wire** replaces mechanical linkages with electronic sensors, DC motors, and redundant pedal position sensors (APP1 + APP2).
- **Friction–Slip curve (Burckhardt model)** peaks near $\lambda \approx 0.15$ on dry asphalt — ABS tracks this peak.
- **Control strategy hierarchy:** Driver Assistance → Active Safety → Chassis → Powertrain → Wheels.
- **SI units are mandatory** in KTU numerical answers: m/s, m/s², N, rad/s.
- **Mermaid diagrams** in KTU answer sheets are typically not allowed; draw neat labelled block diagrams with **arrows showing feedback**.
- **Common formula traps:** Using degrees instead of radians in $\omega_{ref}$ derivation; forgetting $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- For **14-mark questions**, always aim to include: (i) diagram, (ii) derivation, (iii) numerical solution with units, (iv) final boxed answer.
- **KTU frequently asked topics:** ABS slip ratio definition + Burckhardt model; stopping distance with reaction time; bicycle model yaw rate derivation; block diagram of ACC/ESC; PI controller for cruise control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representative Cruise Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representative Cruise Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cruise Control System (CCS)** is a classic example of a **closed-loop feedback control system** widely studied in automotive electronics. Formally, it is defined as an *electronic, automatic speed regulation subsystem* that maintains a driver-prescribed vehicle set-speed ($v_{ref}$) by continuously sensing the actual road speed ($v$) via a speed sensor, comparing it against the reference in the Engine Control Unit (ECU), and actuating the throttle body to nullify the speed error — *without continuous driver pedal intervention*.

The **Representative Cruise Control System** is the canonical, mathematically tractable benchmark model used across the global automotive engineering curriculum (originating from MIT 6.302 / Åström & Wittenmark) to teach fundamental concepts of:
- **Feedback Control Theory**
- **Longitudinal Vehicle Dynamics**
- **Actuator and Sensor Modelling**
- **ECU-based Control Law Synthesis**

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Auto-Pilot for Your Foot":**
> Imagine driving on a long, flat highway. Normally, your right foot constantly presses the accelerator a little harder on uphills and releases it on downhills to keep speed steady — a tiring, repetitive task. The **Cruise Control System** is essentially an *electronic replacement for this constant foot-work*.
>
> You tell the car *"I want exactly 80 km/h"* (this is the **set-point**). A small computer (ECU) then becomes the "ghost foot" that secretly presses and releases the accelerator pedal for you, thousands of times per second, by reading your actual speed from a wheel sensor and comparing it to 80. The moment a hill tries to slow you down, the system "pushes" the throttle harder; the moment a downhill tries to speed you up, it "lifts off" the throttle.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Cruise Control Design

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating speed range | **40 km/h – 200 km/h** | Typical engagement envelope |
| Steady-state error ($e_{ss}$) | $\leq 1\%$ of $v_{ref}$ | Speed-holding accuracy |
| Settling time ($t_s$) | **3 – 5 seconds** | Response to grade disturbances |
| Control law family | **P / PI / PID** | Classical control strategies |
| Throttle actuator bandwidth | $\geq 5$ Hz | Must be faster than vehicle dynamics |
| Reference command | $v_{ref} \in [v_{min}, v_{max}]$ | Set by driver (SET/COAST switch) |

## 1.4 Core Functional Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Representative Cruise Control System** comprises four fundamental building blocks (input → processing → actuation → feedback):
>
> 1. **Reference Input Block** – Driver's SET/COAST/RES/ACC switches generate the desired speed $v_{ref}$.
> 2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** – Contains the control algorithm (e.g., PI/PID) running on a microcontroller.
> 3. **Throttle Actuator (Plant Input)** – A DC motor, stepper motor, or PWM-controlled servo driving the throttle plate angle $\theta$.
> 4. **Speed Sensor (Feedback Element)** – Typically a **magnetic reluctance (variable reluctance) sensor** at the transmission output or a Hall-effect sensor at each wheel — generates a frequency proportional to wheel/ driveshaft rotation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Cruise Control System? — Engineering Motivation

In a modern passenger car, the driver's throttle pedal is mechanically (or drive-by-wire electronically) connected to a throttle plate. Without automation, the driver must:
- **Continuously monitor** the speedometer,
- **Modulate pedal force** to counter disturbances (road grade, wind gusts, curves),
- **Maintain cognitive attention** for prolonged periods → **driver fatigue**.

The CCS eliminates this loop, improving **ride comfort, fuel economy (smoother torque demand)**, and freeing the driver for steering/navigation tasks.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The Closed-Loop Philosophy

The system operates as a classic **negative feedback loop**. The logical flow is:

1. **Sensing** — Speed sensor measures actual vehicle velocity $v(t)$.
2. **Comparison** — ECU computes the error: $e(t) = v_{ref} - v(t)$.
3. **Computation** — Control law calculates the required throttle command $u(t)$ from $e(t)$.
4. **Actuation** — Throttle actuator opens/closes the throttle plate by angle $\theta(t)$.
5. **Physical Response** — Engine produces tractive force $F_t$, accelerating the vehicle mass $m$.
6. **Loop repeats** at a sampling rate of typically **10 ms – 50 ms**.

## 2.3 Mathematical Model of the Plant (Longitudinal Vehicle Dynamics)

The vehicle, when simplified to a single rigid mass $m$ on a flat road, obeys **Newton's Second Law** in the longitudinal direction:

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = F_{traction}(t) - F_{load}(t)$$

For the *representative* (idealized) cruise control model used in KTU examinations, the load is lumped into a **viscous damping term** $b \cdot v(t)$ that represents combined aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance:

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = F_{traction}(t) - b \cdot v(t)$$

Assuming the engine force $F_{traction}$ is directly proportional to throttle command $u(t)$ (linearized actuator model):

$$F_{traction}(t) = \alpha \cdot u(t)$$

This gives the canonical **first-order plant transfer function**:

$$\frac{V(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{\alpha}{ms + b} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $K = \alpha / b$ is the **DC gain** (m/s per unit throttle command) and $\tau = m / b$ is the **mechanical time constant** (typically **5 s – 15 s** for a passenger car).

## 2.4 Control Law — Proportional-Integral (PI) Controller

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A pure **Proportional (P) controller** leaves a permanent **steady-state error** when faced with disturbances (e.g., road grade). To achieve *zero steady-state error* on flat roads, the standard KTU-exam cruise controller uses a **PI control law**:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

In the Laplace domain:

$$U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) E(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s} \cdot E(s)$$

The integral term $K_i$ is the key — it integrates the accumulated error over time and continues to adjust the throttle until the error becomes zero, guaranteeing **type-1 system behavior** (zero steady-state error to a step reference).

## 2.5 Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Combining the PI controller $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$ with the plant $P(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) P(s)}{1 + C(s) P(s)} = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1) + K(K_p s + K_i)}$$

The closed-loop **characteristic equation** is:

$$\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0$$

## 2.6 Stability Analysis (Routh-Hurwitz Criterion)

For the second-order characteristic equation, stability requires both coefficients to be positive:

$$1 + K K_p > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad K K_i > 0$$

Since plant gain $K > 0$, this simplifies to:

$$K_p > -1/K \quad \text{and} \quad K_i > 0$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Cruise Control — Memorize for the Board Exam.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m \dot{v} = F_{engine} - b v$ | Longitudinal vehicle dynamics (1st-order plant) |
| 2 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{\alpha}{ms + b} = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | Plant transfer function |
| 3 | $K = \alpha / b$ | DC gain; $\tau = m / b$ is time constant |
| 4 | $e(t) = v_{ref} - v(t)$ | Speed error signal |
| 5 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt$ | **PI control law** (most common in CCS) |
| 6 | $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$ | PI controller in Laplace domain |
| 7 | $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s) P(s)}{1 + C(s) P(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function |
| 8 | $\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0$ | Characteristic equation |
| 9 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K K_i / \tau}$ | Natural frequency (rad/s) of closed loop |
| 10 | $\zeta = \dfrac{1 + K K_p}{2 \sqrt{\tau K K_i}}$ | Damping ratio of closed loop |
| 11 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$ | Final Value Theorem — steady-state error |
| 12 | $e_{ss, \text{PI step}} = 0$ | Type-1 system → zero error to step input |
| 13 | $e_{ss, \text{P-only}} = \dfrac{v_{ref}}{1 + K K_p}$ | P-controller has non-zero steady-state error |
| 14 | $F_{grade} = m g \sin(\theta)$ | Disturbance force from road grade angle $\theta$ |
| 15 | $F_{aero} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force (non-linear) |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The representative cruise control model is the **direct mathematical ancestor** of:
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — adds radar/lidar for distance sensing.
- **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — uses the same longitudinal dynamics.
- **Electronic Stability Program (ESP)** — extends the model to lateral/yaw dynamics.
- **Tesla Autopilot / Waymo Driver** — full self-driving builds on multi-variable versions of this loop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Plant Transfer Function from Newton's Second Law

**Starting Point:** Newton's law applied to vehicle mass $m$ moving longitudinally on a flat road.

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = F_{engine}(t) - F_{resistance}(t)$$

**Step 1:** Linearize the resistance as a viscous term. The combined drag forces (aerodynamic + rolling + drivetrain friction) are modeled as proportional to velocity $v$:

$$F_{resistance}(t) = b \cdot v(t)$$

where $b$ is the *viscous damping coefficient* (units: N·s/m).

**Step 2:** Substitute into Newton's law:

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} + b \cdot v(t) = F_{engine}(t)$$

**Step 3:** Linearize the engine. Assuming a small-signal model where the throttle command $u(t)$ (in % or 0–1 normalized) maps directly to a tractive force via gain $\alpha$ (N per unit command):

$$F_{engine}(t) = \alpha \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite the equation in standard form:

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} + b \cdot v(t) = \alpha \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 5:** Take the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions ($v(0) = 0$):

$$m s V(s) + b V(s) = \alpha U(s)$$

**Step 6:** Factor $V(s)$:

$$(m s + b) V(s) = \alpha U(s)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the plant transfer function $G_p(s) = V(s)/U(s)$:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{V(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{\alpha}{ms + b}$$

**Step 8:** Normalize to standard first-order form by dividing numerator and denominator by $b$:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\alpha / b}{(m/b) s + 1} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Result:** The plant is a **first-order lag** with:
- **DC Gain:** $K = \alpha / b$ (final steady-state speed per unit throttle)
- **Time constant:** $\tau = m / b$ (time to reach $\approx 63.2\%$ of final speed)

> **Engineering Insight:** The ratio $m/b$ is large (heavy cars have high inertia relative to drag) → $\tau$ is large → the plant is *slow* → requiring a controller with integral action to compensate for disturbances (e.g., hills).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function with PI Controller

**Starting Point:** The PI controller output in Laplace domain:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 1:** Express error $E(s) = V_{ref}(s) - V(s)$.

**Step 2:** Control signal is $U(s) = C(s) E(s) = C(s) [V_{ref}(s) - V(s)]$.

**Step 3:** Plant output is $V(s) = G_p(s) U(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1} U(s)$.

**Step 4:** Substitute $U(s)$ into the plant equation:

$$V(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot C(s) \cdot [V_{ref}(s) - V(s)]$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $(\tau s + 1)$:

$$(\tau s + 1) V(s) = K \cdot C(s) \cdot V_{ref}(s) - K \cdot C(s) \cdot V(s)$$

**Step 6:** Collect $V(s)$ terms on the left:

$$(\tau s + 1) V(s) + K \cdot C(s) \cdot V(s) = K \cdot C(s) \cdot V_{ref}(s)$$

**Step 7:** Factor $V(s)$:

$$V(s) \left[ (\tau s + 1) + K \cdot C(s) \right] = K \cdot C(s) \cdot V_{ref}(s)$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{V(s)}{V_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K \cdot C(s)}{(\tau s + 1) + K \cdot C(s)}$$

**Step 9:** Substitute $C(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K \cdot \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}}{(\tau s + 1) + K \cdot \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}}$$

**Step 10:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1) + K(K_p s + K_i)}$$

**Step 11:** Expand the denominator:

$$T(s) = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{\tau s^2 + s + K K_p s + K K_i} = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{K K_p s + K K_i}{\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i}}$$

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$\boxed{\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio

**Step 1:** Compare the characteristic equation to the standard second-order form:

$$s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 2:** Divide the characteristic equation by $\tau$:

$$s^2 + \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} s + \frac{K K_i}{\tau} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Match coefficients term-by-term:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{K K_i}{\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K K_i}{\tau}}$$

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{1 + K K_p}{2 \sqrt{\tau K K_i}}$$

**Design Interpretation:**
- **Increasing $K_p$** → increases damping ratio $\zeta$ → faster, less oscillatory response. However, too much $K_p$ makes the ride jerky and may excite unmodeled high-frequency dynamics.
- **Increasing $K_i$** → increases $\omega_n$ → faster response. However, too much $K_i$ can cause overshoot and integral windup.

For a typical passenger vehicle, a **critically damped or slightly underdamped** response is desired: $\zeta \approx 0.7$ to $1.0$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Steady-State Error Analysis

**Step 1:** The error transfer function is:

$$\frac{E(s)}{V_{ref}(s)} = 1 - T(s) = \frac{1}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Final Value Theorem** (FVT) for a step reference $V_{ref}(s) = v_{ref}/s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{V_{ref}(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $C(s) G_p(s)$ for PI control:

$$C(s) G_p(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the DC gain of the loop (limit as $s \to 0$):

$$\lim_{s \to 0} C(s) G_p(s) = (K_p + \infty) \cdot K = \infty$$

**Step 5:** The infinite loop gain forces the steady-state error to zero:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{v_{ref}}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

**Result:** A PI controller guarantees **zero steady-state error** to a step reference speed — this is the *fundamental reason* P-only control is inadequate for cruise control.

**Comparison: P-only Controller**

For a pure P-controller, $C(s) = K_p$:

$$e_{ss, P} = \frac{v_{ref}}{1 + K_p K}$$

The error is **non-zero** and inversely proportional to $K_p$. Without integral action, the throttle can never grow large enough to fully eliminate the error against a constant disturbance (e.g., mild road grade).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — P vs PI vs PID

| Property | P Controller | PI Controller | PID Controller |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control law | $u = K_p e$ | $u = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt$ | $u = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ |
| Steady-state error (step) | $v_{ref}/(1 + K K_p) \neq 0$ | **0** (exact) | **0** (exact) |
| Disturbance rejection | Poor | Good | Best |
| Sensitivity to noise | Low | Low | **High** (derivative amplifies noise) |
| Implementation complexity | Very low | Low | Moderate |
| Used in real automotive CCS? | Rarely | **Yes (industry standard)** | Limited (often filtered $K_d$) |
| Open-loop poles added | 0 | 1 (at origin) | 1 (at origin) |
| Closed-loop order with 1st-order plant | 1st | **2nd** | 2nd |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Representative Cruise Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DRIVER SET INPUT]:::input --> nodeB[REFERENCE SPEED vref]
    nodeB --> nodeC[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    nodeE[SPEED SENSOR]:::sensor --> nodeF[ACTUAL SPEED v t]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeG[ERROR SIGNAL e t]
    nodeG --> nodeH[PI CONTROLLER ECU]:::controller
    nodeH --> nodeI[CONTROL SIGNAL u t]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[THROTTLE ACTUATOR]:::actuator
    nodeJ --> nodeK[ENGINE TRACTIVE FORCE]
    nodeK --> nodeL[VEHICLE MASS PLANT]:::plant
    nodeL --> nodeM[ROAD SPEED v t]
    nodeM --> nodeE
    nodeN[ROAD GRADE DISTURBANCE]:::disturbance -.-> nodeL

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef disturbance fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray:5
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Component Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["DRIVER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        nodeA1[SET SWITCH]
        nodeA2[COAST SWITCH]
        nodeA3[RESUME SWITCH]
        nodeA4[ACCEL SWITCH]
        nodeA5[BRAKE PEDAL]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNIT"]
        nodeB1[INPUT CONDITIONER]
        nodeB2[PI CONTROL ALGORITHM]
        nodeB3[FAULT DIAGNOSTIC MODULE]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        nodeC1[PWM DRIVER]
        nodeC2[DC MOTOR]
        nodeC3[THROTTLE PLATE]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK["FEEDBACK LAYER"]
        nodeD1[MAGNETIC RELUCTANCE SENSOR]
        nodeD2[FREQUENCY TO VOLTAGE CONVERTER]
        nodeD3[ADC INTERFACE]
    end

    subgraph PLANT["VEHICLE PLANT"]
        nodeE1[ENGINE TORQUE GENERATION]
        nodeE2[TRANSMISSION GEARBOX]
        nodeE3[VEHICLE MASS DYNAMICS]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB1
    nodeA4 --> nodeB1
    nodeA5 -.-> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3
    nodeC3 --> nodeE1
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2
    nodeE2 --> nodeE3
    nodeE3 --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3
    nodeD3 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 -.-> nodeB3
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph of the Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Vref s]:::input --> nodeP2[Sum Node]
    nodeP2 -->|+K p plus K i over s| nodeP3[C s PI Controller]:::controller
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Gain K]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Integrator 1 over tau s plus 1]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[V s Output Speed]:::plant
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7[Feedback Path]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP2

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mode-of-Operation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> STANDBY: Power ON
    STANDBY --> ENGAGED: SET pressed
    ENGAGED --> OVERRIDE: Brake pressed
    OVERRIDE --> STANDBY: Brake released
    STANDBY --> RESUMED: RES pressed
    RESUMED --> ENGAGED: System re-acquires setpoint
    ENGAGED --> ADJUST: ACCEL or COAST pressed
    ADJUST --> ENGAGED: New setpoint locked
    ENGAGED --> OFF: Ignition OFF
```

> **Note on Mermaid Compilation:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA1`, `nodeP5`), avoiding reserved Mermaid keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`. Labels use raw uppercase text without inline markdown bold/italic tags to ensure clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a cruise control system. List its main components and explain the role of the speed sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cruise control system is an electronic closed-loop control system that automatically maintains a vehicle's speed at a driver-set reference value without continuous accelerator pedal input.

**Main components:**
1. **Driver input switches** (SET, COAST, RES, ACC) – generate the reference speed $v_{ref}$.
2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** – executes the control algorithm (typically PI).
3. **Throttle actuator** (DC motor/servo) – physically adjusts throttle plate angle.
4. **Speed sensor** – measures actual vehicle speed and feeds it back to the ECU.

**Role of speed sensor (1 mark):** It provides the feedback signal $v(t)$ which is compared with $v_{ref}$ to compute the error $e(t) = v_{ref} - v(t)$. Without this feedback, the system would be open-loop and unable to correct for disturbances like road grade or wind. The sensor is typically a magnetic reluctance type mounted on the transmission output shaft, producing a frequency proportional to driveshaft RPM.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is a PI controller preferred over a P controller in a cruise control system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A P (proportional) controller computes the control signal as $u(t) = K_p e(t)$. Although simple, it produces a **non-zero steady-state error** because the control output becomes zero only when the error is zero. For a step reference speed $v_{ref}$:

$$e_{ss, P} = \frac{v_{ref}}{1 + K_p K}$$

A **PI (Proportional-Integral) controller** adds an integral term:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$$

The integral term **accumulates error over time** and continues to adjust the throttle until the error is forced to zero. This guarantees **zero steady-state error** ($e_{ss} = 0$) for a step-input reference speed. Additionally, the PI controller provides superior **disturbance rejection** against constant disturbances like road grade. Therefore, PI control is the industry standard for cruise control systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Module-2 Question Paper Format:** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A and Question B.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a representative cruise control system. Derive the mathematical model of the vehicle longitudinal dynamics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. The system consists of:
- Reference input $V_{ref}(s)$
- Summing junction (subtracts actual speed from reference)
- PI Controller (in ECU)
- Throttle actuator (gain $\alpha$)
- Vehicle plant (first-order lag)
- Speed sensor (feedback path)

**Working (2 marks):** The driver presses SET to lock the current speed as $v_{ref}$. The speed sensor continuously measures actual speed $v(t)$. The ECU computes error $e(t) = v_{ref} - v(t)$ and the PI controller generates a throttle command $u(t)$ that opens or closes the throttle plate. The engine produces tractive force proportional to throttle opening, accelerating/decelerating the vehicle. The loop operates at a sampling period of 10–50 ms.

**Derivation of vehicle dynamics (3 marks):** Applying Newton's second law to the vehicle mass $m$ moving longitudinally:

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{engine} - b v$$

where $F_{engine} = \alpha \cdot u(t)$ and $b v$ is the lumped drag force. Taking Laplace transform:

$$(ms + b) V(s) = \alpha U(s)$$

$$G_p(s) = \frac{V(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{\alpha}{ms + b} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

with $K = \alpha / b$ and $\tau = m / b$.

---

**A (b)** For a cruise control system, the plant transfer function is $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{10s + 5}$. Design a PI controller such that the closed-loop system has a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$ and natural frequency $\omega_n = 1$ rad/s. Verify stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Normalize the plant to standard form:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{1}{10s + 5} = \frac{0.2}{2s + 1}$$

So $K = 0.2$, $\tau = 2$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Recall the closed-loop characteristic equation with PI controller:

$$\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Divide by $\tau$ to match $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$s^2 + \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} s + \frac{K K_i}{\tau} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply design specifications:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{K K_i}{\tau} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K K_i = \tau = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{2}{0.2} = 10$$

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} = 2(0.7)(1) = 1.4$$

$$1 + K K_p = 1.4 \cdot \tau = 1.4 \cdot 2 = 2.8$$

$$K K_p = 1.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = \frac{1.8}{0.2} = 9$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Stability check using Routh-Hurwitz. Characteristic equation:

$$2 s^2 + 2.8 s + 2 = 0$$

Both coefficients (2, 2.8, 2) are positive. The first column of the Routh array is $[2, 2.8, 2]$ — all positive, so the system is **stable**.

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i} = \frac{0.2(9s + 10)}{2s^2 + 2.8s + 2} = \frac{1.8 s + 2}{2s^2 + 2.8s + 2}$$

**Final Design:** $K_p = 9$, $K_i = 10$. ✓

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**B (a)** Explain the concept of closed-loop feedback in a cruise control system. How does the inclusion of an integral term eliminate steady-state error? Support your answer with mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Closed-loop concept (2 marks):** In a cruise control system, *closed-loop feedback* means the output (actual speed) is continuously sensed and routed back to the input (ECU) where it is compared with the desired reference. This creates a self-correcting loop. Unlike open-loop control (where the throttle is set blindly), the closed-loop system can detect deviations caused by external disturbances — such as a road gradient of 3° causing a force $F = mg \sin\theta$ — and automatically compensate by increasing or decreasing the throttle opening.

**Mathematical framework (2 marks):** The error signal drives the controller:

$$e(t) = v_{ref} - v(t)$$

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$$

$$V(s) = G_p(s) U(s)$$

**Elimination of steady-state error (3 marks):** For a step reference $V_{ref}(s) = v_{ref}/s$, the steady-state error is found via the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{V_{ref}(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{v_{ref}}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}$$

For a P-only controller, $C(s) = K_p$ and $C(0)G_p(0) = K_p K$ is a finite constant, giving a non-zero $e_{ss}$. For a PI controller, $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s$ has an integrator that drives $C(0) \to \infty$, so $e_{ss} \to 0$. The integrator "remembers" the cumulative error and keeps adjusting the throttle until the actual speed matches $v_{ref}$ exactly.

---

**B (b)** A vehicle cruise control plant has $m = 1000$ kg, $b = 50$ N·s/m, and actuator gain $\alpha = 500$ N per unit throttle. **(i)** Derive the open-loop plant transfer function. **(ii)** If a PI controller with $K_p = 2$ and $K_i = 0.5$ is used, find the closed-loop transfer function and analyze the steady-state error for a step reference of 60 km/h. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Open-loop plant transfer function:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\alpha}{ms + b} = \frac{500}{1000s + 50} = \frac{10}{20s + 1}$$

So $K = 10$, $\tau = 20$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** PI controller:

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = 2 + \frac{0.5}{s} = \frac{2s + 0.5}{s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{\frac{2s + 0.5}{s} \cdot \frac{10}{20s + 1}}{1 + \frac{2s + 0.5}{s} \cdot \frac{10}{20s + 1}}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{10(2s + 0.5)}{s(20s + 1) + 10(2s + 0.5)} = \frac{20s + 5}{20s^2 + s + 20s + 5} = \frac{20s + 5}{20s^2 + 21s + 5}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Characteristic equation: $20s^2 + 21s + 5 = 0$. Both coefficients positive → stable.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Steady-state error for step reference $V_{ref} = 60$ km/h = 16.67 m/s:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{V_{ref}(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)} = 0$$

(Zero, because of integral action — Type-1 system.)

**Final Answer:** $T(s) = \dfrac{20s + 5}{20s^2 + 21s + 5}$, $e_{ss} = 0$ m/s. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the summing junction** — students often write $e = v + v_{ref}$ instead of $e = v_{ref} - v$. This is a **1–2 mark** deduction.
> 2. **Confusing the plant gain $K = \alpha/b$ with the actuator gain $\alpha$** — they are not the same. $K$ has units of m/s per unit throttle; $\alpha$ has units of N per unit throttle.
> 3. **Forgetting units in the Final Value Theorem** — speed must be in m/s (SI), not km/h, when computing $e_{ss}$.
> 4. **Skipping the Routh-Hurwitz check** — even if the design is correct, full marks require an explicit stability verification.
> 5. **Not mentioning disturbance rejection** — the engineering "why" of PI over P is *both* zero $e_{ss}$ and rejection of constant grade disturbances. A complete answer addresses both.
> 6. **Writing $K_p$ and $K_i$ without stating the design specification** — always show that $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ are matched to the design requirements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Board Exam:**

- **Cruise Control = Classic closed-loop negative feedback system** for longitudinal speed regulation.
- **Four essential blocks:** Reference (driver SET switch) → Controller (ECU with PI law) → Actuator (throttle motor) → Plant (vehicle mass + drag) → Sensor (speed pickup).
- **Vehicle dynamics equation:** $m \dot{v} + bv = \alpha u$ → first-order plant $G_p(s) = \frac{\alpha}{ms+b} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$.
- **Standard KTU parameter values:** $m \approx 1000$–$1500$ kg, $b \approx 40$–$60$ N·s/m, $\tau \approx 15$–$30$ s.
- **PI control law:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$ — eliminates steady-state error and rejects constant disturbances (e.g., road grade).
- **P-only control is INADEQUATE** — leaves a residual error $e_{ss} = v_{ref}/(1+K K_p)$.
- **Closed-loop characteristic equation:** $\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0$ — both coefficients must be positive for stability.
- **Design formulas:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K K_i / \tau}$, $\zeta = (1 + K K_p)/(2 \sqrt{\tau K K_i})$.
- **Routh-Hurwitz stability condition:** $K_p > -1/K$ and $K_i > 0$.
- **Speed sensor types:** Magnetic reluctance (variable reluctance) and Hall-effect — generate a frequency proportional to wheel/driveshaft RPM.
- **Common exam traps:** (1) Confusing actuator gain $\alpha$ with plant DC gain $K = \alpha/b$. (2) Wrong sign in summing junction. (3) Forgetting unit conversion (km/h → m/s) in $e_{ss}$ problems.
- **Modern extensions:** ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) adds radar for inter-vehicle distance; AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) uses the same longitudinal plant model with a different reference generator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Cruise Control Configuration and Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Cruise Control Configuration and Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Cruise Control (DCC)**, also known as *Speed Control System* or *Autonomous Speed Governor*, is an **electronically-governed, closed-loop feedback control subsystem** within a vehicle's powertrain that automatically regulates the road speed of an automobile at a driver-prescribed set-point velocity, without continuous manual actuation of the accelerator pedal.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the digital cruise control is implemented as a **microcontroller-based servo-mechanism** that reads the **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** output, compares it against a **driver-selected reference speed (set speed)** stored in RAM, computes a **throttle actuation command** using a digital control algorithm (typically a **PI / PID compensator**), and drives an **electronic throttle body (ETB)** or a **vacuum / DC servo actuator** to maintain longitudinal velocity within a tolerance of **±1 to ±2 km/h** of the set value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The digital implementation replaces legacy vacuum-operated cruise systems with an **embedded ECU firmware routine** running a discrete-time control law. The key engineering merit is the use of **digital signal processing** (sampling, quantization, Z-domain analysis) rather than analog op-amp comparators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automatic Water Tap" Intuition

Imagine you are filling a bucket from a tap and you want the water level to stay at a fixed mark (say 10 cm):

| Real-Life Analogy | Digital Cruise Control Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The **mark on the bucket** | The **set speed** you press "SET" at |
| The **floating ball that rises with water** | The **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** reading |
| **Your eyes** observing the water level | The **ECU/microcontroller** comparing actual vs. set |
| **Your hand** turning the tap up/down | The **throttle actuator** opening/closing |
| **When the bucket overflows (speed too high)** | The ECU **closes the throttle** |
| **When the level drops (speed too low)** | The ECU **opens the throttle** more |

So Digital Cruise Control is essentially a **robotic driver** that watches the speedometer and wiggles the accelerator for you — it does only one job: keep the speed *constant*.

## 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Operating Speed Range:** **40 km/h – 200 km/h** (typical production envelope)
- **Set-Speed Resolution:** **±1.6 km/h (1 mph)**
- **Sample Time / Control Loop Period (T_s):** **50 ms – 100 ms**
- **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) Output:** **Hall-effect pulse train**, typically **4000–8000 pulses per km**
- **Throttle Actuator Bandwidth:** **≥ 2 Hz** (must respond faster than vehicle longitudinal dynamics)
- **Gain-Type:** Negative unity-feedback closed loop with **proportional + integral (PI)** action

> [!NOTE]
> **Why digital?** Digital implementation allows features impossible in analog cruise: adaptive set-speed memory, grade (slope) compensation, integration with radar (ACC), OBD-II diagnostics, and CAN-bus communication. This is the **single most exam-relevant point** for a 14-mark KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Decomposition — How a Digital Cruise Control "Thinks"

A digital cruise control is a **discrete-time, single-input-single-output (SISO) regulator**. The closed-loop information flow can be broken into six sequential stages:

1. **Reference Generation Stage** — The driver momentarily stabilises the vehicle at a desired speed and presses the **SET** push-button. The ECU samples the VSS frequency at that instant and latches the value as $v_{set}$ into a non-volatile register.

2. **Feedback Measurement Stage** — A **magnetic (Hall-effect) or reluctance (VR) speed sensor** mounted on the transmission output shaft generates a **pulse train**. A frequency-to-voltage (F/V) converter or a **period-measurement timer** in the microcontroller converts this to a digital speed reading $v_{actual}(k)$.

3. **Error Computation Stage** — At every sampling instant $kT_s$, the ECU forms the speed error:
$$e(k) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(k)$$

4. **Compensation Stage** — The error is processed by a **digital PI compensator** (most common) or a full PID:
$$u(k) = K_p \, e(k) + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) \, T_s$$
   where $u(k)$ is the throttle command (normalized 0–100 %).

5. **Actuation Stage** — The digital command is converted to a **PWM duty cycle** that drives a **DC servo motor** coupled to the throttle plate via a gear train (older designs) or directly commands an **electronic throttle body (drive-by-wire)** in modern cars.

6. **Safety Override Stage** — Hardwired logic immediately de-energises the actuator when the **brake pedal switch**, **clutch switch**, or **parking brake switch** closes. This is a *passive safety interlock*, not part of the firmware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind PI control:** A pure proportional ($K_p$ only) controller will always leave a steady-state speed error (called *droop*) on a hill because some error is needed to produce throttle. The **integral term $K_i$** accumulates past errors and drives the steady-state error to zero — a property called **zero steady-state error to a step reference**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(k) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(k)$ | Speed error at sample $k$ | **km/h** |
| 2 | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum e(j)$ | Digital PI compensator output | Normalised **0 – 100 %** |
| 3 | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum e(j) + K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$ | Digital PID (with derivative kick removal) | Normalised **0 – 100 %** |
| 4 | $G_c(z) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i T_s}{1 - z^{-1}}$ | Discrete PI compensator in Z-domain | Dimensionless transfer function |
| 5 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$ | Vehicle longitudinal dynamics (1st-order) | $K_v \approx 1$, $\tau_v \approx 5$–$15$ s |
| 6 | $f_{VSS} = \dfrac{N \cdot v}{C}$ | VSS pulse frequency from vehicle speed | **Hz**; $N$ = pulses/rev, $C$ = tyre circumference |
| 7 | $v = \dfrac{f_{VSS} \cdot C}{N}$ | Vehicle speed recovered from sensor | **km/h** |
| 8 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_p K_v}{\tau_v}}$ | Closed-loop natural frequency (PI design) | **rad/s** |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{1}{2 \tau_v \omega_n}$ | Damping ratio of second-order approximation | Dimensionless, target $\zeta \approx 0.7$ |
| 10 | $T_{sample} = 50$ – $100$ ms | MCU sampling period for DCC loop | **ms** (Nyquist satisfied) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing the PI equation in the answer script, **always include the $T_s$ factor** in the integral term. Students who omit it lose 2 marks in 14-mark derivations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Comfort & Fuel Economy:** Maintains optimal gear-speed combination; reduces driver fatigue on highways.
- **Foundational for ADAS:** Digital Cruise Control is the *parent* of **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — simply replace the VSS feedback with a radar-derived relative-velocity signal and the same control loop extends to following a lead vehicle.
- **Emissions Compliance:** Steady speed minimises transient fuel enrichment → lower HC and CO emissions.
- **Predictive Cruise (PCC):** Modern systems use GPS + 3D map data to pre-empt hills and adjust throttle *before* the slope disturbs speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Converting the Analog PI Compensator to Discrete Time

A KTU 14-mark question commonly asks: *"Starting from the analog PI compensator equation, derive the discrete-time algorithm used in the digital cruise controller."* The full derivation follows.

### Step 1 — Start with the Continuous-Time PI Equation

The continuous-time PI controller output $u(t)$ in response to error $e(t)$ is given by:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

This is the classical "parallel form" of a PI compensator. $K_p$ is the proportional gain (with units of %-throttle per km/h) and $K_i$ is the integral gain (with units of %-throttle per km/h per second).

### Step 2 — Discretize the Integral Using the Rectangular (Backward) Approximation

The integral term is replaced by a discrete sum. We approximate the area under the error curve between successive samples $kT_s$ and $(k-1)T_s$ as a rectangle of height $e(k)$ and width $T_s$:

$$\int_{0}^{kT_s} e(\tau) \, d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

This is the **left (or backward) rectangular rule**, chosen because it produces an *implicit* discrete integrator that is *unconditionally stable* in the Z-domain.

### Step 3 — Substitute the Discrete Integral into the Continuous Equation

Replacing the integral by its discrete approximation:

$$u(k) = K_p \, e(k) + K_i \, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

This is the **positional (absolute) form** of the digital PI algorithm. The MCU computes $u(k)$ every $T_s$ and outputs it to the PWM generator driving the throttle actuator.

### Step 4 — Convert to the Velocity (Incremental) Form to Avoid Integral Wind-up

The positional form suffers from a phenomenon called *integral wind-up*: if the actuator saturates (e.g., throttle fully open on a steep climb) and the error remains large, the integrator accumulates an enormous value that causes a huge overshoot when the load finally releases. The standard KTU remedy is the **incremental form**, obtained by computing $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p \bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + K_i \, T_s \, e(k)$$

Then the actual output is updated as:

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

This *velocity form* is preferred in ECU firmware because it allows **clamping** of $u(k)$ at every step, naturally preventing wind-up.

### Step 5 — Derive the Z-Transfer Function $G_c(z)$

Taking the Z-transform of the discrete PI equation (positional form) by applying the linearity and accumulation properties:

$$U(z) = K_p E(z) + K_i T_s \left[\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}\right] E(z)$$

Factorising $E(z)$:

$$U(z) = \left[K_p + \dfrac{K_i T_s}{1 - z^{-1}}\right] E(z)$$

Therefore the **digital compensator transfer function** is:

$$G_c(z) = \dfrac{U(z)}{E(z)} = K_p + \dfrac{K_i T_s}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

This is the canonical discrete PI form. Its pole at $z = 1$ on the unit circle confirms it acts as an **integrator**, which is exactly the property we need for zero steady-state error.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Signal Flow at a Glance

| Stage | Mathematical Operation | Physical Implementation in ECU |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | $v_{set}$ stored on SET-button press | 16-bit RAM register |
| Measurement | $v_{actual}(k) = f_{VSS} \cdot C / N$ | Timer-capture input on MCU |
| Error | $e(k) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(k)$ | 16-bit signed subtraction |
| PI Law | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum e(j)$ | 32-bit accumulator (saturated) |
| Output | PWM duty $= u(k)$ | Timer compare register |
| Feedback | Throttle repositions → VSS frequency changes | Physical loop closure |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A digital cruise controller has $K_p = 2.5$ %/km/h, $K_i = 0.8$ %/km/h/s, and $T_s = 0.1$ s. The set speed is 100 km/h. At sample $k$, the actual speed is 92 km/h.

**Compute the control output $u(k)$ assuming the integral sum accumulated to $\sum e(j) = 40$ km/h over the previous samples.**

Step 1 — Instantaneous error:

$$e(k) = 100 - 92 = 8 \;\text{km/h}$$

Step 2 — Proportional term:

$$u_P = K_p \cdot e(k) = 2.5 \times 8 = 20 \;\%$$

Step 3 — Integral term:

$$u_I = K_i \cdot T_s \cdot \sum e(j) = 0.8 \times 0.1 \times 40 = 3.2 \;\%$$

Step 4 — Total command:

$$u(k) = 20 + 3.2 = 23.2 \;\%$$

The ECU commands a **23.2 % throttle opening** at this instant, which will accelerate the vehicle back toward 100 km/h.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital Cruise Control Closed-Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the canonical closed-loop architecture of a digital cruise control system, showing the digital processing core, the actuator chain, the feedback path, and the safety interlocks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Driver Input Stage]
        drv[Driver SET RESUME COAST ACCEL Switches]
    end

    subgraph SENSOR[Sensor Stage]
        vss[Vehicle Speed Sensor VSS Pulse Train]
        brk[Brake Pedal Switch]
        clt[Clutch Pedal Switch]
    end

    subgraph ECU[Digital ECU Processing Core]
        fv[Frequency to Speed Converter]
        ref[Set Speed Register v_set]
        sub[Subtractor Error e k]
        pi[Digital PI Compensator]
        pwm[PWM Generator]
        sat[Saturation Clamp 0 to 100 percent]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuation Stage]
        mot[DC Servo Motor]
        thr[Electronic Throttle Body]
    end

    subgraph PLANT[Vehicle Plant]
        veh[Vehicle Longitudinal Dynamics]
        lng[Longitudinal Velocity v_actual]
    end

    drv --> ref
    vss --> fv --> sub
    ref --> sub
    sub -- e k --> pi
```
