## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Cruise Control System

The cruise control system operates as a **closed-loop negative feedback controller**. The following structured sequence describes the system behavior during a typical "Set" operation:

1. **Driver Activation:** The driver brings the vehicle to the desired speed using the accelerator pedal.
2. **Set Command:** The driver presses the **SET** switch on the steering-wheel-mounted control stalk.
3. **Signal Acquisition:** The ECU reads the instantaneous vehicle speed $v_{actual}$ from the **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)**, which is typically a Hall-effect or magneto-resistive sensor mounted on the transmission output shaft.
4. **Setpoint Latch:** The ECU stores $v_{set} = v_{actual}$ in non-volatile memory.
5. **Error Generation:** The control algorithm computes the speed error:
   $$e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$$
6. **Control Law Execution:** The ECU applies a PID control law to compute the required throttle command $u(t)$.
7. **Actuation:** The throttle actuator (vacuum servo or DC motor on electronic throttle body) adjusts the throttle plate angle $\theta$ to regulate engine torque.
8. **Continuous Monitoring:** The ECU continuously samples brake and clutch switches; any activation immediately disengages cruise control (safety override).
9. **Steady State:** The vehicle stabilises at $v_{set}$ with errors typically bounded within $\pm 2$ km/h under nominal conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PID?** Pure proportional control leads to a steady-state offset (the car never quite reaches 100 km/h). The **integral term** eliminates this offset, while the **derivative term** damps oscillations caused by sudden load changes (e.g., entering a hill). PID is the industry-standard compromise between response speed, accuracy, and stability.

### 2.2 Key Components — Roles and Signal Flow

| Component | Function | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver Control Switch** | Inputs SET, COAST, RESUME, ACCEL commands | Steering column stalk / steering wheel buttons |
| **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** | Provides real-time speed feedback | Transmission output shaft / wheel hub |
| **Cruise Control ECU (or BCM)** | Runs the control algorithm | Behind dashboard / engine bay ECU box |
| **Throttle Actuator** | Physically modulates the throttle plate | On the throttle body / intake manifold |
| **Brake Switch** | Safety disengagement | Brake pedal assembly |
| **Clutch Switch** | Safety disengagement (manual transmission) | Clutch pedal assembly |
| **Stop Lamp Fuse / Relay** | Cuts power to vacuum servo on braking | Under-hood fuse box |
| **Vacuum Servo (legacy systems)** | Converts ECU PWM signal to throttle motion | Engine bay, near throttle body |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $e(t)$ | Speed error | $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$ | m/s or km/h |
| $u(t)$ | Control output (throttle command) | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | % throttle or V |
| $K_p$ | Proportional gain | Tuned empirically | dimensionless |
| $K_i$ | Integral gain | Tuned empirically | s$^{-1}$ |
| $K_d$ | Derivative gain | Tuned empirically | s |
| $F_{trac}$ | Tractive force at wheels | $F_{trac} = \dfrac{T_{engine} \cdot \tau_{trans} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{r_{wheel}}$ | N |
| $F_{resist}$ | Total resistive force | $F_{resist} = F_{aero} + F_{roll} + F_{grade} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + \mu_r m g \cos\alpha + m g \sin\alpha$ | N |
| $m \dot{v}$ | Equation of motion | $m \dot{v} = F_{trac} - F_{resist}$ | N |
| $G_v(s)$ | Vehicle speed transfer function (simplified) | $G_v(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{(\tau_v s + 1)}$ | m/s per unit input |
| $\Delta v_{tap}$ | Step size per Tap-Up / Tap-Down | $\Delta v_{tap} = 1.6$ km/h (typical) | km/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Formatting Note:** In mathematical expressions, absolute value is written using $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ (e.g., $\lvert e(t) \rvert$) and never with the vertical pipe character $\vert$ used in markdown tables, to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cruise control electronics is the foundational technology behind:
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** Adds radar/lidar to maintain gap to preceding vehicle.
- **Stop-and-Go ACC:** Extends the concept to urban traffic with full braking to zero.
- **Predictive Cruise Control:** Uses GPS and map data to pre-adjust speed for upcoming hills, curves, and speed limits.
- **Platooning (V2V):** Multiple trucks in convoy use coordinated cruise control for fuel savings of up to **10%**.
- **Autonomous Driving (Level 2+):** Longitudinal control (speed + gap) is the first sub-problem solved in any self-driving stack.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Vehicle Dynamics to the Cruise Control Loop

We start from Newton's second law applied to a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v(t)$:

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = F_{trac}(t) - F_{resist}(t)$$

The total resistive force is composed of three physically distinct terms:

$$F_{resist}(t) = F_{aero}(t) + F_{roll}(t) + F_{grade}(t)$$

Expanding each resistive term with its standard automotive engineering expression:

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, v^2(t)$$

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r\, m\, g\, \cos\alpha$$

$$F_{grade} = m\, g\, \sin\alpha$$

Substituting back into Newton's law:

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = F_{trac}(t) - \left[\frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2(t) + \mu_r m g \cos\alpha + m g \sin\alpha\right]$$

For cruise control analysis, we **linearise** around an operating speed $v_0$. Letting $v(t) = v_0 + \tilde{v}(t)$ with $\tilde{v} \ll v_0$ and neglecting higher-order terms, the linearised equation of motion becomes:

$$m \frac{d\tilde{v}(t)}{dt} = \tilde{F}_{trac}(t) - \rho C_d A v_0\, \tilde{v}(t)$$

Rearranging by collecting the $\tilde{v}$ terms on the left side:

$$m \frac{d\tilde{v}(t)}{dt} + \rho C_d A v_0\, \tilde{v}(t) = \tilde{F}_{trac}(t)$$

Dividing through by $m$ and defining the **vehicle time constant** $\tau_v = \dfrac{m}{\rho C_d A v_0}$ and the **steady-state gain** $K_v = \dfrac{1}{\rho C_d A v_0}$:

$$\tau_v \frac{d\tilde{v}(t)}{dt} + \tilde{v}(t) = K_v\, \tilde{F}_{trac}(t)$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions) yields the **vehicle speed transfer function**:

$$G_v(s) = \frac{\tilde{V}(s)}{\tilde{F}_{trac}(s)} = \frac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$$

This is a **first-order lag**, which is the canonical model used in KTU board problems for cruise control analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation: The PID Control Law in the Time Domain

The general form of a PID controller acting on the speed error $e(t)$ is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Interpretation of each term:**

- The **proportional term** $K_p e(t)$ produces an immediate corrective action proportional to the current error. Larger $K_p$ means faster response, but excessive $K_p$ causes overshoot and oscillation.
- The **integral term** $K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$ accumulates past errors. This guarantees that in steady state, $\lim_{t\to\infty} e(t) = 0$ (zero steady-state error).
- The **derivative term** $K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ anticipates future error by reacting to the rate of change. It provides a damping effect, reducing overshoot.

Taking the Laplace transform of the control law:

$$U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s) = C(s)\, E(s)$$

The **PID transfer function** is therefore:

$$C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

### 3.3 Closed-Loop Transfer Function and Stability

The full closed-loop block diagram has:
- Forward path: $C(s) \cdot G_v(s)$
- Feedback path: unity (since VSS measures actual speed directly)
- Reference: $V_{set}(s)$
- Error: $E(s) = V_{set}(s) - V(s)$

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{V(s)}{V_{set}(s)} = \frac{C(s)\, G_v(s)}{1 + C(s)\, G_v(s)}$$

Substituting the first-order plant and PID controller:

$$\frac{V(s)}{V_{set}(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K_v(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_v s + 1)}}{1 + \dfrac{K_v(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_v s + 1)}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s(\tau_v s + 1)$:

$$\frac{V(s)}{V_{set}(s)} = \frac{K_v(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_v s + 1) + K_v(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}$$

The **characteristic polynomial** (denominator) governs stability:

$$s(\tau_v s + 1) + K_v(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\tau_v s^2 + s + K_v K_d s^2 + K_v K_p s + K_v K_i = 0$$

Grouping by powers of $s$:

$$(\tau_v + K_v K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_v K_p) s + K_v K_i = 0$$

For stability, by the **Routh–Hurwitz criterion** applied to a 2nd-order polynomial, all coefficients must be positive:

$$(\tau_v + K_v K_d) > 0, \quad (1 + K_v K_p) > 0, \quad K_v K_i > 0$$

Since $\tau_v > 0$ and $K_v > 0$ (physical plant parameters), stability reduces to:

$$K_d > 0, \quad K_p > -\frac{1}{K_v}, \quad K_i > 0$$

In practice, all three gains are positive, satisfying stability automatically.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg cruises at $v_0 = 30$ m/s. The aerodynamic drag coefficient product is $\rho C_d A = 0.65$ kg/m. A cruise controller with gains $K_p = 1.2$, $K_i = 0.25$ s$^{-1}$, $K_d = 0.08$ s is installed. Find the vehicle time constant and the closed-loop characteristic equation.

**Step 1 — Compute the vehicle time constant:**

$$\tau_v = \frac{m}{\rho C_d A v_0} = \frac{1500}{0.65 \times 30} = \frac{1500}{19.5} \approx 76.92 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the steady-state gain:**

$$K_v = \frac{1}{\rho C_d A v_0} = \frac{1}{0.65 \times 30} = \frac{1}{19.5} \approx 0.0513$$

**Step 3 — Form the closed-loop characteristic polynomial:**

$$(\tau_v + K_v K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_v K_p) s + K_v K_i = 0$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$(76.92 + 0.0513 \times 0.08) s^2 + (1 + 0.0513 \times 1.2) s + 0.0513 \times 0.25 = 0$$

$$(76.92 + 0.0041) s^2 + (1 + 0.0616) s + 0.01283 = 0$$

$$76.924\, s^2 + 1.0616\, s + 0.01283 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Check stability:** All three coefficients are positive → system is stable.

**Step 5 — Compute natural frequency and damping ratio** (useful for board problems):

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_v K_i}{\tau_v + K_v K_d}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.01283}{76.924}} \approx 0.0129 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{1 + K_v K_p}{\tau_v + K_v K_d} = \frac{1.0616}{76.924} \approx 0.0138$$

$$\zeta = \frac{0.0138}{2 \times 0.0129} \approx 0.535$$

Since $\zeta \approx 0.535$ (between 0 and 1), the system is **underdamped** — a desirable behaviour for cruise control: fast response with controlled overshoot.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** A damping ratio $\zeta$ in the range **0.4 to 0.7** is the standard KTU-recommended target for a well-tuned cruise controller. Below 0.4, the vehicle oscillates unacceptably; above 1.0, the response becomes sluggish.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Cruise Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional signal flow** of a cruise control system, from the driver's command to the final throttle actuation, including the feedback loop and all safety overrides.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driverA[Driver Control Switch] --> ecmA[ECU Control Module]
    vssA[Vehicle Speed Sensor VSS] --> ecmA
    brakeA[Brake Pedal Switch] --> ecmA
    clutchA[Clutch Pedal Switch] --> ecmA
    ecmA --> compA[Comparator Error Generator]
    spA[Setpoint Memory] --> compA
    compA --> pidA[PID Control Algorithm]
    pidA --> pwmA[PWM Signal Generator]
    pwmA --> actA[Throttle Actuator]
    actA --> thrA[Throttle Plate Angle]
    thrA --> engA[Engine Torque Output]
    engA --> whlA[Vehicle Wheels]
    whlA --> vehA[Vehicle Speed]
    vehA --> vssA
```

### 4.2 Detailed State-Flow Diagram — Operating Modes

The following Mermaid state diagram maps the transitions between the six canonical cruise control operating modes, including the safety disengagement triggers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stateA[OFF State] --> stateB[Ready State]
    stateB --> stateC[Set State Active] 
    stateC --> stateD[Coast Mode]
    stateC --> stateE[Accel Mode]
    stateC --> stateF[Resume Mode]
    stateC --> stateG[Tap Up Mode]
    stateC --> stateH[Tap Down Mode]
    stateD --> stateC
    stateE --> stateC
    stateG --> stateC
    stateH --> stateC
    stateF --> stateC
    stateC --> stateI[Disengaged Brake or Clutch Pressed]
    stateI --> stateJ[Temporary Suspend]
    stateJ --> stateF
    stateI --> stateB
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Wiring / Signal Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how each physical sensor and actuator is wired (electrically/logically) to the central ECU, which is essential for KTU board questions asking students to "draw the cruise control system."

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    swA[SET Switch] --> ecu1[ECU Pin 1]
    swB[COAST Switch] --> ecu1
    swC[RESUME Switch] --> ecu1
    swD[ACCEL Switch] --> ecu1
    vss1[VSS Hall Sensor] --> ecu1
    brk1[Brake Switch NO Contact] --> ecu1
    clt1[Clutch Switch NO Contact] --> ecu1
    ecu1 --> pwm1[PWM Output Stage]
    pwm1 --> vac1[Vacuum Servo Solenoid OR DC Motor Driver]
    vac1 --> thr1[Throttle Body]
    ecu1 --> ind1[Indicator Lamp Driver]
    ind1 --> lamp1[Dash CRUISE Lamp]
    ecu1 --> can1[CAN Bus Interface]
    can1 --> bcu1[Body Control Module BCM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In all Mermaid block diagrams, arrows represent the **direction of signal or command flow**, not physical wiring layout. The feedback arrow from Vehicle Speed back to VSS represents the *physical* relationship (the sensor measures the wheel rotation that produces speed), not an electrical signal.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip (Mermaid on Paper):** During the KTU exam, students are expected to draw this on paper using rectangular blocks. The key marks are awarded for: (1) the feedback loop, (2) all safety switches shown, (3) the actuator as a separate block, and (4) the SET/RESUME inputs clearly shown entering the ECU.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**  
Define cruise control system. List any four operating modes of cruise control.

**Model Answer:**  
Cruise control is an electronic system that automatically maintains a vehicle's road speed at a driver-selected setpoint without continuous accelerator pedal input, by closed-loop control of the throttle.  
Four operating modes are: (i) **Set**, (ii) **Coast**, (iii) **Resume**, and (iv) **Accel**.  
*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four modes = 3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
What is the role of the Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) and the brake switch in a cruise control system?

**Model Answer:**  
The **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** provides real-time feedback of the actual road speed to the ECU, enabling the closed-loop regulation. It is typically a Hall-effect sensor mounted on the transmission output shaft.  
The **brake switch** acts as a safety disengagement input — when the driver presses the brake pedal, the cruise control is immediately released, returning throttle control to the driver.  
*(Marks: 1.5 for VSS role + 1.5 for brake switch role = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**  
**(a)** Draw the block diagram of an electronic cruise control system and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a vehicle under cruise control, treating the vehicle as a first-order plant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3(A):

**(a) Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The block diagram consists of the following elements (refer to the Mermaid architecture in Section 4.1 for the visual):

1. **Setpoint input** $V_{set}$ — entered by driver via SET switch, stored in ECU memory.
2. **Comparator** — computes error $e(t) = V_{set} - V_{actual}$.
3. **PID controller** $C(s)$ — processes error to produce control output $u(t)$.
4. **Throttle actuator** (vacuum servo / electronic throttle motor) — converts $u(t)$ into throttle plate angle $\theta$.
5. **Engine and drivetrain** — converts $\theta$ into tractive force $F_{trac}$.
6. **Vehicle dynamics** (plant $G_v(s)$) — converts force into vehicle speed $v(t)$.
7. **Feedback sensor (VSS)** — measures $v(t)$ and feeds it back to the comparator.
8. **Safety override inputs** — brake and clutch switches that immediately disable the controller.

**Marks distribution:** 4 marks for a clean labelled diagram; 3 marks for explanation of each block's function.

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from Newton's second law for the vehicle:

$$m \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = F_{trac}(t) - F_{resist}(t)$$

The dominant resistive force at cruise speed is aerodynamic drag (rolling resistance and grade are secondary at steady state):

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$

Linearising about operating speed $v_0$ by substituting $v = v_0 + \tilde{v}$ and dropping higher-order terms:

$$m \frac{d\tilde{v}}{dt} = \tilde{F}_{trac} - \rho C_d A v_0\, \tilde{v}$$

Rearranging:

$$m \frac{d\tilde{v}}{dt} + \rho C_d A v_0\, \tilde{v} = \tilde{F}_{trac}$$

Dividing by $m$ and defining $\tau_v = \dfrac{m}{\rho C_d A v_0}$, $K_v = \dfrac{1}{\rho C_d A v_0}$:

$$\tau_v \frac{d\tilde{v}}{dt} + \tilde{v} = K_v\, \tilde{F}_{trac}$$

Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$\tau_v s\, \tilde{V}(s) + \tilde{V}(s) = K_v\, \tilde{F}_{trac}(s)$$

$$G_v(s) = \frac{\tilde{V}(s)}{\tilde{F}_{trac}(s)} = \frac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$$

The **closed-loop transfer function** with unity feedback and a controller $C(s)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G_v(s)}{1 + C(s) G_v(s)}$$

**Marks distribution:** 2 marks for setting up the equation of motion; 2 marks for linearisation; 2 marks for transfer function derivation; 1 mark for final closed-loop form.

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative Choice, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**  
**(a)** Explain the PID control law used in cruise control electronics. State the role of each term (P, I, D) and the units of $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** A vehicle of mass $m = 1200$ kg cruises at $v_0 = 25$ m/s with $\rho C_d A = 0.6$ kg/m. Compute the vehicle time constant $\tau_v$ and steady-state gain $K_v$. If $K_p = 1.5$, $K_i = 0.3$ s$^{-1}$, $K_d = 0.05$ s, find the closed-loop characteristic equation and determine the damping ratio. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3(B):

**(a) PID Control Law (7 marks):**

The PID control law in the time domain is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **Proportional term** $K_p e(t)$: Produces a corrective action proportional to the current error. Higher $K_p$ → faster response but possible overshoot. **Unit of $K_p$:** dimensionless (output unit per error unit).
- **Integral term** $K_i \int e(\tau)\, d\tau$: Accumulates past errors to eliminate steady-state offset. **Unit of $K_i$:** s$^{-1}$ (output unit per (error · second)).
- **Derivative term** $K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$: Reacts to the rate of change of error, providing predictive damping. **Unit of $K_d$:** s (output unit per (error/second)).

**Marks distribution:** 2 marks for writing the law; 3 marks for term-by-term role; 2 marks for units.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Vehicle time constant:**

$$\tau_v = \frac{m}{\rho C_d A v_0} = \frac{1200}{0.6 \times 25} = \frac{1200}{15} = 80 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state gain:**

$$K_v = \frac{1}{\rho C_d A v_0} = \frac{1}{0.6 \times 25} = \frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop characteristic polynomial** (from derivation in Section 3.3):

$$(\tau_v + K_v K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_v K_p) s + K_v K_i = 0$$

Computing each coefficient:

- $s^2$ coefficient: $80 + 0.0667 \times 0.05 = 80 + 0.00333 = 80.0033$
- $s^1$ coefficient: $1 + 0.0667 \times 1.5 = 1 + 0.1 = 1.1$
- $s^0$ coefficient: $0.0667 \times 0.3 = 0.02$

Characteristic equation:

$$80.0033\, s^2 + 1.1\, s + 0.02 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Natural frequency and damping ratio:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{0.02}{80.0033}} \approx 0.0158 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_n(\tau_v + K_v K_d) = 1 + K_v K_p$$

$$\zeta = \frac{1 + K_v K_p}{2\omega_n(\tau_v + K_v K_d)} = \frac{1.1}{2 \times 0.0158 \times 80.0033} = \frac{1.1}{2.528} \approx 0.435$$

Since $0 < \zeta \approx 0.435 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — acceptable for cruise control.

**Marks distribution:** 2 marks for $\tau_v$ and $K_v$; 3 marks for characteristic equation; 2 marks for $\zeta$ computation and conclusion.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cruise Control Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the feedback loop** in the block diagram — examiners deduct **2 marks** if the diagram is open-loop or if the VSS feedback arrow is missing.
> 2. **Confusing $K_p$ and $K_i$ units** — writing $K_p$ in s$^{-1}$ or $K_i$ as dimensionless is a **1-mark penalty**. Memorise: $K_p$ is dimensionless, $K_i$ is s$^{-1}$, $K_d$ is s.
> 3. **Skipping linearisation** — the equation $m\dot v = F_{trac} - F_{resist}$ is non-linear due to the $v^2$ drag term. Examiners expect a linearisation step yielding the first-order form. Jumping directly to $G_v(s) = K_v/(\tau_v s + 1)$ without justification loses **1 to 2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the operating range** of cruise control (typically 40–200 km/h) — if the question asks for "typical values," omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting safety switches** — brake and clutch disengagement switches are mandatory components. A block diagram without them is incomplete.
> 6. **Using $\zeta > 1$** in design — over-damped cruise control is sluggish and considered poor design. Examiners want $\zeta \in [0.4, 0.7]$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Cruise control electronics is a **closed-loop speed regulation system** that maintains driver-selected speed by automatic throttle modulation.
- **Core components:** ECU, VSS, throttle actuator, brake switch, clutch switch, and driver control switches (SET / COAST / RESUME / ACCEL).
- **Six operating modes:** Set, Coast, Resume, Accel, Tap-Up, Tap-Down (memorise in this exact order for KTU).
- **Speed error formula:** $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$.
- **PID control law:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot e$.
- **Vehicle plant (first-order):** $G_v(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$ with $\tau_v = \dfrac{m}{\rho C_d A v_0}$.
- **Closed-loop characteristic equation (with PID):** $(\tau_v + K_v K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_v K_p) s + K_v K_i = 0$.
- **Stability condition:** All three gains must be **positive**.
- **Design target:** Damping ratio $\zeta \in [0.4,\, 0.7]$ for fast, well-damped response.
- **Operating speed range:** **40 to 200 km/h** for typical automotive cruise control.
- **Safety:** Brake and clutch pedal switches must **always** override the system, regardless of ECU logic.
- **Modern extension:** Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) adds **radar/lidar** for gap maintenance, but the underlying longitudinal PID/lead-lag control is identical in principle.
- **Communication:** Modern ECUs interface with the Body Control Module (BCM) and engine ECU via the **CAN bus** for switch status, setpoint storage, and fault diagnostics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Stepper Motor Based and Vacuum Operated Actuator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Stepper Motor Based and Vacuum Operated Actuator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Stepper Motor — Formal Definition

A **stepper motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that converts a train of input digital pulses (from an Electronic Control Unit / ECU) into discrete angular mechanical displacements, known as **steps**. Each electrical pulse advances the rotor by a precise, fixed angular increment — typically **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.72°** (500 steps/rev) in automotive-grade units.

In the automotive context, stepper motors function as **digital-to-mechanical transducers**. The ECU commands a known number of pulses, and the motor produces an exact, repeatable, open-loop mechanical position without requiring positional feedback sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAET634, Module 2):** Stepper motors are classified as *actuators* in the ECU-driven mechatronic chain. They are the workhorses of **idle air control (IAC)**, **throttle position modulation** (older systems), **instrument cluster pointers**, **EGR valve positioning**, and **headlamp leveling**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Ratchet-Wheel & Pawl" Picture

Imagine a clock's second hand. With each tick (pulse), the hand rotates a precise, identical angle. There is no continuous sweeping motion — the hand jumps from one fixed position to the next and locks. A stepper motor behaves identically:

- **Pulse from ECU** → "tick"
- **Step angle** → fixed angular jump
- **Holding torque** → the hand stays put between ticks (positional lock)

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike a DC motor, a stepper motor's accuracy is **open-loop** — there is no encoder telling the ECU "I have arrived." The ECU simply *trusts* the pulse count. This is why steppers are cheap, simple, and dominant in low-power automotive positioning tasks.

### 1.3 Vacuum Operated Actuator — Formal Definition

A **vacuum operated actuator** (also called a *vacuum motor* or *vacuum diaphragm actuator*) is a pneumatic-mechanical device that converts a **negative gauge pressure** (vacuum) sourced from the engine intake manifold or a dedicated vacuum pump into a **linear or rotary mechanical force**. The actuator contains a flexible rubber diaphragm (or a piston) coupled to a pushrod, and the pressure differential across this diaphragm produces the actuation stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Source of Automotive Vacuum:** In a gasoline port-injection engine, the intake manifold at idle produces a vacuum of approximately **-18 to -22 inHg** (≈ **-60 kPa to -75 kPa** gauge), or **40-50 kPa absolute** pressure. This manifold vacuum is the free energy reservoir that drives actuators in older and many modern vehicles.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Suction Cup & Plunger" Picture

Place a suction cup on a wall, press it, and let it "stick" — atmospheric pressure is pushing the cup toward the wall with hundreds of Newtons of force. A vacuum actuator works on the **same principle but in reverse**:

- A **rubber diaphragm** sits between two chambers.
- One side is connected to manifold vacuum (low pressure).
- The other side is exposed to atmospheric pressure.
- Atmospheric pressure pushes the diaphragm toward the vacuum side → **the pushrod moves**.

The engine's intake manifold literally *sucks* the actuator's diaphragm, and atmospheric pressure does the work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why vacuum?** Vacuum is "free" — the engine creates it as a byproduct of the intake stroke. No electrical power, no solenoids, no wiring harness is needed. This is why vacuum actuators dominated emissions control (EGR, EVAP), HVAC blend doors, and brake boosters for decades.

---

### 1.5 Fundamental Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Automotive Value |
|---|---|
| **Stepper step angle** | **1.8°** (200 steps/revolution) — most common |
| **Stepper step angle (high-res)** | **0.72°** (500 steps/revolution) |
| **Stepper holding torque** | **0.05 – 0.5 N·m** (typical automotive IAC class) |
| **Stepper supply voltage** | **12 V DC** (battery rail of the vehicle) |
| **Manifold vacuum at idle** | **-18 to -22 inHg** (≈ **-61 to -75 kPa** gauge) |
| **Atmospheric pressure (sea level, ISA)** | **101.325 kPa** |
| **Vacuum actuator diaphragm diameter** | **50 – 150 mm** (varies by application) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stepper Motor — Construction & Operating Principle

A stepper motor consists of two main parts:
1. **Stator** — a stack of laminated silicon-steel sheets carrying wound field coils, typically configured as two phase windings (A and A', B and B') producing **N-S pole pairs**.
2. **Rotor** — either a soft-iron toothed structure (Variable Reluctance type) or a permanently magnetized cylinder (PM type), or a combination of both (Hybrid type).

The **operational sequence** proceeds as follows:

- The ECU energizes one stator phase (say Phase A), creating N-S poles on the stator teeth.
- The rotor — being either magnetically pulled or seeking the path of minimum reluctance — rotates until its poles align with the energized stator poles. This is one **stable detent position**.
- The ECU then de-energizes Phase A and energizes Phase B. The rotor advances by one **step angle θ_s** to align with the new pole pattern.
- Subsequent pulse sequence continues this stepping motion.

### 2.2 Step Angle — The Defining Equation

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}}$$

where:
- $\theta_s$ = **step angle** in **degrees**,
- $N_s$ = number of stator teeth (or stator pole pairs × teeth per pole),
- $m$ = **number of stator phases** (commonly $m = 2$ for two-phase bipolar or four-phase unipolar drive).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this formula matters:** A common KTU numerical asks — *"For a stepper motor with 50 stator teeth and 2 phases, find the step angle and the number of steps per revolution."* The answer drops out directly from this expression.

### 2.3 Stepping Modes of Operation

| Mode | Description | Step Magnitude | Torque |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-step (Wave drive)** | Only one phase energized at a time | $1 \times \theta_s$ | Lowest holding torque |
| **Full-step (Two-phase-on)** | Two adjacent phases always energized | $1 \times \theta_s$ | Highest holding torque |
| **Half-step** | Alternates one-phase-on and two-phase-on | $0.5 \times \theta_s$ | Smooth motion, moderate torque |
| **Microstepping** | Phases driven with sinusoidal currents | $\theta_s / N$ (N = microsteps) | Lowest, but ultra-smooth |

### 2.4 Stepper Motor Speed — Pulse Frequency Relation

The rotational speed of the stepper is directly tied to the pulse-train frequency $f_p$ delivered by the ECU:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_{steps/rev}} \text{ [rpm]}}$$

where:
- $n$ = rotational speed in revolutions per minute,
- $f_p$ = pulse frequency in pulses per second (**Hz**),
- $N_{steps/rev}$ = total steps per revolution = $\dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance & Pull-out:** If $f_p$ enters a mechanical resonance band of the rotor-load system (typically **70-200 Hz** for small automotive steppers), the rotor can lose synchronism and stall. The ECU must **ramp** (accel/decel) the pulse frequency to traverse resonance bands safely.

### 2.5 Stepper Holding Torque (Static Torque)

The torque resisting external disturbance while the rotor is held at a detent position is given by:

$$\boxed{T_{hold} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}$$

where:
- $T_{hold}$ = holding torque in **N·m**,
- $i$ = phase current in **A**,
- $L(\theta)$ = inductance as a function of rotor angle (Henry),
- $\theta$ = rotor angular position (radians).

The **inductance variation with angle** is the key to producing torque — the change in magnetic stored energy as the rotor moves creates a force to align with the minimum-reluctance (or maximum-flux-linkage) position.

### 2.6 Vacuum Actuator — Operating Principle

A vacuum actuator has the following essential components:

- A **rigid housing** divided into two chambers by a flexible **diaphragm** (typically fabric-reinforced neoprene or silicone).
- A **pushrod** mechanically connected to the diaphragm center, exiting the housing.
- A **vacuum port** (nipple) connected via hose to the intake manifold (or a vacuum reservoir).
- A **spring** providing a return force on the diaphragm opposite to the vacuum-induced stroke.

**Operating logic:**

1. With both sides of the diaphragm at atmospheric pressure, the **return spring** keeps the pushrod extended (rest position).
2. When the vacuum port is opened to manifold vacuum, the **spring-side chamber** drops to low pressure.
3. The **atmospheric-side chamber** remains at $\approx 101.3 \text{ kPa}$.
4. The **net pressure differential** pushes the diaphragm toward the vacuum side, retracting the pushrod against the spring.
5. When vacuum is vented (or a vacuum solenoid valve closes), the spring returns the pushrod.

### 2.7 Vacuum Actuator Force — The Master Equation

The force generated by a vacuum actuator on its pushrod is:

$$\boxed{F_{vac} = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} = \left(P_{atm} - P_{vac}\right) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \eta_{dia}}$$

where:
- $F_{vac}$ = actuation force in **Newtons (N)**,
- $\Delta P$ = pressure differential across the diaphragm in **Pa**,
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure ($\approx 101{,}325$ **Pa**),
- $P_{vac}$ = absolute pressure on the vacuum side in **Pa**,
- $D$ = effective diaphragm diameter in **m**,
- $A_{eff} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ = effective diaphragm area in **m²**,
- $\eta_{dia}$ = diaphragm effective-area coefficient (typically **0.7 – 0.85** to account for clamped edge effects).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The spring force opposes the diaphragm motion. The *usable* force is $F_{usable} = F_{vac} - F_{spring}$. For the actuator to operate, $\Delta P$ must be large enough that $F_{vac} > F_{spring}$. This sets the minimum vacuum level required.

### 2.8 Stroke Length of the Pushrod

$$\boxed{L_{stroke} = \int_{P_{vac,\,min}}^{P_{vac,\,max}} \frac{A_{eff}}{k_{spring}} \, dP = \frac{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{k_{spring}}}$$

where:
- $L_{stroke}$ = pushrod travel in **m**,
- $k_{spring}$ = spring constant of the return spring in **N/m**.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$ | $\theta_s$, $N_s$, $m$ | degrees, count, count | Step angle calculation |
| 2 | $N_{steps/rev} = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | steps/revolution | count | Resolution check |
| 3 | $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_{steps/rev}}$ | $n$, $f_p$ | rpm, Hz | Stepper speed control |
| 4 | $T_{hold} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | torque, current, inductance | N·m, A, H/rad | Static torque |
| 5 | $F_{vac} = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \eta_{dia}$ | force, pressure, area | N, Pa, m² | Vacuum actuator force |
| 6 | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | area, diameter | m², m | Diaphragm area |
| 7 | $L_{stroke} = \dfrac{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{k_{spring}}$ | stroke, area, pressure, k | m, m², Pa, N/m | Pushrod travel |
| 8 | $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ | differential, abs pressures | Pa | Vacuum differential |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Idle Air Control (IAC):** A stepper motor positions a pintle valve bypass-air screw, modulating the air quantity bypassing the closed throttle plate. ECU commands **0-125 steps** to control idle RPM.
- **EGR Valve (older designs):** A vacuum actuator opens the EGR valve, with a vacuum solenoid valve (VSV) PWM-modulated by the ECU to meter the vacuum.
- **Brake Booster:** A large vacuum diaphragm (≈ **200-250 mm** diameter) amplifies the driver's brake-pedal force by a factor of **4-8×**.
- **HVAC Blend/Defrost Doors:** Vacuum actuators rotate HVAC doors between modes (face/feet/defrost) when the driver changes settings.
- **Cruise Control (older systems):** A vacuum actuator on the throttle linkage holds the set speed, with a bi-directional solenoid regulating vacuum and atmospheric venting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1 — Step Angle from Stator Tooth Count and Phase Count

> **Problem Statement:** A bipolar stepper motor used in an idle-air-control valve has **50 stator teeth** and operates in **2 phases**. Compute (a) the step angle, (b) the number of steps per revolution, and (c) the rotor position after **47 pulses**.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**

From the problem: $N_s = 50$ stator teeth, $m = 2$ phases.

**Step 2 — Apply the step-angle formula.**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$$

Substituting values:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{50 \cdot 2}$$

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{100}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_s = 3.6° \text{ per step}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute steps per revolution.**

$$N_{steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{3.6°}$$

$$\boxed{N_{steps/rev} = 100 \text{ steps/revolution}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute rotor position after 47 pulses.**

$$\theta_{47} = 47 \cdot \theta_s = 47 \cdot 3.6°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{47} = 169.2°}$$

This corresponds to a fractional revolution of:

$$\frac{169.2°}{360°} = 0.47 \text{ revolution} \quad (\approx 47\% \text{ of a full turn})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** A Part A 3-mark question often gives a value like 50 teeth, 4 phases and asks for $\theta_s$ and $N_{steps/rev}$ — the answer here would be $\theta_s = 1.8°$, the canonical automotive value.

---

### 3.2 Derivative 2 — Stepper Speed from Pulse Frequency

> **Problem Statement:** The ECU drives the IAC stepper at a pulse frequency of **150 Hz**. The motor has **200 steps/rev**. Find (a) the rotational speed in **rpm**, and (b) the linear speed of the pintle if the lead screw has a pitch of **0.5 mm/turn**.

**Step 1 — Apply the speed formula.**

$$n = \frac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_{steps/rev}}$$

Substituting $f_p = 150 \text{ Hz}$ and $N_{steps/rev} = 200$:

$$n = \frac{60 \cdot 150}{200}$$

$$n = \frac{9000}{200}$$

$$\boxed{n = 45 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to linear speed.**

The linear speed $v$ of the pintle is given by:

$$v = n \cdot \text{pitch} \quad \text{[in consistent units]}$$

$$v = 45 \text{ rev/min} \cdot 0.5 \text{ mm/rev}$$

$$\boxed{v = 22.5 \text{ mm/min} = 0.375 \text{ mm/s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivative 3 — Vacuum Actuator Force and Stroke

> **Problem Statement:** A vacuum actuator used in an HVAC blend door system has a diaphragm of effective diameter **D = 80 mm**, an effective-area coefficient of $\eta_{dia} = 0.8$, and a return spring constant of $k_{spring} = 1500 \text{ N/m}$. The engine manifold supplies a vacuum corresponding to an absolute pressure of **30 kPa** on the vacuum side. Atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. Calculate: (a) the actuation force, (b) the pushrod stroke, and (c) the required spring preload for the actuator to be in equilibrium at half-stroke.

**Step 1 — Convert all units to SI.**

- $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- $P_{atm} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{vac} = 30{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta_{dia} = 0.8$
- $k_{spring} = 1500 \text{ N/m}$

**Step 2 — Compute effective diaphragm area.**

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.08)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0064}{4}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{0.02011}{4}$$

$$\boxed{A_{eff} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \quad (\approx 50.27 \text{ cm}^2)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pressure differential.**

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

$$\Delta P = 101{,}325 - 30{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = 71{,}325 \text{ Pa} \quad (\approx 71.3 \text{ kPa})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the actuation force.**

$$F_{vac} = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \eta_{dia}$$

$$F_{vac} = 71{,}325 \cdot 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.8$$

$$F_{vac} = 71{,}325 \cdot 4.021 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{vac} \approx 286.8 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the pushrod stroke.**

$$L_{stroke} = \frac{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{k_{spring}}$$

$$L_{stroke} = \frac{5.027 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 71{,}325}{1500}$$

$$L_{stroke} = \frac{358.6}{1500}$$

$$\boxed{L_{stroke} \approx 0.239 \text{ m} = 239 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** An HVAC blend door stroke of 20-25 cm is reasonable for a large diaphragm actuator.

**Step 6 — Compute spring preload for equilibrium at half-stroke.**

At half-stroke, the spring is compressed by:

$$x_{half} = \frac{L_{stroke}}{2} = 0.1195 \text{ m}$$

For equilibrium, the spring force must equal the vacuum force:

$$F_{spring} = k_{spring} \cdot (x_0 + x_{half}) = F_{vac}$$

where $x_0$ is the spring **preload** (initial compression at rest, against atmospheric pressure).

The atmospheric pressure also acts on the spring-side diaphragm at rest, contributing to a baseline force. For simplicity, assume the spring preload is set so that at rest, the net force is zero (atmospheric pressure on both sides), and at full vacuum the spring deflects to $L_{stroke}$:

$$F_{vac} = k_{spring} \cdot L_{stroke}$$

The equilibrium at half-stroke is automatically satisfied if we linearize:

$$F_{vac,\,half} = \Delta P_{half} \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \eta_{dia}$$

For half-stroke, the spring is compressed by $x_0 + 0.1195$ m, so:

$$x_0 = \frac{F_{vac}}{k_{spring}} - L_{stroke} = 0.239 - 0.239 = 0$$

This indicates that with $L_{stroke}$ already designed to balance $F_{vac}$ at full vacuum, the preload is zero. If we want the actuator to *return* to rest when vacuum is removed, the spring must be preloaded such that at $\Delta P = 0$ the spring force exceeds the residual atmospheric differential (which is zero in this idealized case).

In practice, **preload $F_0 \approx 5-10\% \text{ of } F_{vac}$**, so:

$$F_0 \approx 0.075 \times 286.8 \approx 21.5 \text{ N}$$

This corresponds to a preload deflection of:

$$x_0 = \frac{F_0}{k_{spring}} = \frac{21.5}{1500} \approx 0.0143 \text{ m} = 14.3 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{F_0 \approx 21.5 \text{ N}, \quad x_0 \approx 14.3 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stepper vs. Vacuum Actuator Comparison

| Feature | Stepper Motor Actuator | Vacuum Operated Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Electrical (12 V DC + pulse train) | Pneumatic (intake manifold vacuum) |
| **ECU interface** | Discrete digital pulses, two H-bridge outputs | Solenoid valve PWM modulation |
| **Position feedback** | Open-loop (no sensor) | Open-loop (vacuum level $\propto$ force) |
| **Response time** | **10-50 ms** (full travel) | **100-300 ms** (vacuum fill time) |
| **Force/torque range** | **0.05 – 0.5 N·m** (low) | **20 – 500 N** (high) |
| **Stroke range** | Continuous (rotary) | **10 – 100 mm** linear |
| **Cost** | Higher (driver IC + motor) | Very low (hose + can) |
| **Reliability** | Excellent (brushless) | Degrades with diaphragm aging/vacuum leaks |
| **Modern usage** | Idle, throttle, instrument cluster, headlamp leveling | HVAC doors, brake booster, EGR (older) |
| **Failure mode** | Open winding, driver IC fault | Diaphragm tear, vacuum hose leak |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Drive Architecture (Idle Air Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECU1[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    DRV1[H-Bridge Driver IC]
    STP1[Stepper Motor 2-Phase]
    VLV1[IAC Pintle Valve]
    AIR1[Air Bypass Passage]
    THROT1[Main Throttle Plate]
    INTAKE1[Engine Intake Manifold]

    ECU1 -->|Pulse Train A| DRV1
    ECU1 -->|Pulse Train B| DRV1
    DRV1 -->|Phase A| STP1
    DRV1 -->|Phase B| STP1
    STP1 -->|Rotation| VLV1
    VLV1 -->|Position 0-125 steps| AIR1
    AIR1 -->|Bypass Air| THROT1
    THROT1 --> INTAKE1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The ECU emits two pulse trains (A and B), phase-shifted by 90° electrically (quadrature). The H-bridge IC amplifies these to ~1 A currents, energizing the bipolar windings. The motor shaft turns the pintle, which opens/closes the bypass-air passage around the closed throttle plate at idle.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Vacuum Actuator with ECU-Controlled Solenoid (EGR System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MANI1[Intake Manifold Vacuum Source]
    SOL1[Vacuum Solenoid Valve VSV]
    RES1[Vacuum Reservoir]
    VAC1[Vacuum Actuator Diaphragm]
    SPR1[Return Spring]
    PUSH1[Pushrod to EGR Valve]
    EGRA1[EGR Valve]
    EXH1[Exhaust Gas]
    ECUA1[ECU PWM Command]

    MANI1 -->|Vacuum Hose| SOL1
    RES1 -->|Hose| SOL1
    SOL1 -->|Modulated Vacuum| VAC1
    VAC1 -->|Diaphragm Deflection| PUSH1
    SPR1 -->|Opposes| VAC1
    PUSH1 --> EGRA1
    EXH1 --> EGRA1
    ECUA1 -->|PWM Duty Cycle| SOL1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating principle:** The ECU sends a **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) signal** to the vacuum solenoid valve. The **duty cycle** controls the *average* vacuum reaching the actuator diaphragm. Higher duty cycle = more vacuum = larger EGR valve opening. The return spring closes the EGR valve when vacuum is removed.

### 4.3 Stepper Motor Phasing — Half-Step Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ECU as Engine Control Unit
    participant DRV as H-Bridge Driver
    participant PHASE_A as Phase A Winding
    participant PHASE_B as Phase B Winding
    participant ROT as Rotor Shaft

    Note over ECU,ROT: Full-step wave drive (1 phase on)
    ECU->>DRV: Pulse 1 - A only
    DRV->>PHASE_A: Energize +A
    PHASE_A->>ROT: Aligns to 0 deg
    ECU->>DRV: Pulse 2 - B only
    DRV->>PHASE_A: De-energize A
    DRV->>PHASE_B: Energize +B
    PHASE_B->>ROT: Advances to 90 deg
    ECU->>DRV: Pulse 3 - A reverse
    DRV->>PHASE_B: De-energize B
    DRV->>PHASE_A: Energize -A
    PHASE_A->>ROT: Advances to 180 deg
    ECU->>DRV: Pulse 4 - B reverse
    DRV->>PHASE_A: De-energize A
    DRV->>PHASE_B: Energize -B
    PHASE_B->>ROT: Advances to 270 deg
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For half-step mode:** the ECU inserts intermediate steps where **both phases are on** (e.g., +A and +B together), producing 8 distinct rotor positions per 4-pulse electrical cycle — halving the effective step angle to $\theta_s / 2$.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Brake Booster (Vacuum-Powered)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PED1[Brake Pedal Input]
    ROD1[Brake Pedal Pushrod]
    BST1[Brake Booster Diaphragm Housing]
    DIAPH1[Large Diaphragm Diameter 200 mm]
    VACIN1[Manifold Vacuum Port]
    ATM1[Atmospheric Valve]
    MSTR1[Master Cylinder Piston]
    HYD1[Hydraulic Brake Lines]
    WHEEL1[Wheel Brake Assemblies]

    PED1 --> ROD1
    ROD1 --> BST1
    BST1 --> DIAPH1
    DIAPH1 --> MSTR1
    MSTR1 --> HYD1
    HYD1 --> WHEEL1
    VACIN1 --> BST1
    ATM1 --> BST1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operation:** At rest, both sides of the booster diaphragm are at vacuum, the atmospheric valve is closed, and the master cylinder is at its rest position. When the driver presses the pedal, the rod opens the atmospheric valve, admitting air to the rear chamber. The pressure differential across the diaphragm assists the pedal force, multiplying it by a typical **booster ratio of 4:1 to 8:1**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET634]**
*Define a stepper motor. List the two principal types of stepper motors used in automotive applications and state one example application for each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **stepper motor** is a brushless synchronous electric motor that rotates in discrete angular increments called *steps*, with each step corresponding to one electrical pulse applied to its stator windings. The rotor's angular position is therefore directly determined by the number of input pulses, without requiring a position feedback sensor (open-loop control).

**Two principal types used in automobiles:**

1. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** — Rotor carries permanent magnets. Used in *instrument cluster pointer drives* (speedometer, tachometer) and *headlamp leveling* systems. Provides good holding torque and detent position memory.

2. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** — Rotor is a soft-iron toothed structure with no permanent magnetization. Used in *idle air control (IAC) valves* for its high speed and moderate cost. Stalls gracefully if overloaded.

*(Half-mark reserved for example accuracy and one-line reasoning.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET634]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a vacuum operated actuator. State two automotive applications.*

**Model Answer:**

A **vacuum operated actuator** is a pneumatic device that uses the pressure differential between **atmospheric pressure** ($\approx 101.3 \text{ kPa}$) and **intake manifold vacuum** ($\approx 30-40 \text{ kPa absolute at idle}$) to produce a mechanical force on a pushrod.

**Working principle:**
- The actuator has a **rigid housing** divided into two chambers by a flexible **diaphragm**.
- One chamber is connected via a hose to the **intake manifold** (vacuum source).
- The other chamber is exposed to **atmospheric pressure**.
- A **return spring** keeps the pushrod extended when no vacuum is applied.
- When the vacuum solenoid valve (VSV) opens, the manifold-side chamber pressure drops. The **higher atmospheric pressure** on the other side pushes the diaphragm inward, retracting the pushrod against the spring force.
- When vacuum is vented, the spring returns the pushrod to its rest position.

**Two automotive applications:**
1. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) valve actuation** (older, non-electronic EGR systems).
2. **HVAC blend / mode door actuation** (face / feet / defrost selector).

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks for working principle with sketch mention, 1 mark for each application × 2, with 0.5 mark for clarity of language.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET634 Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **two-phase bipolar stepper motor** used in an automotive idle air control (IAC) system. Derive the expression for the **step angle** in terms of stator teeth and number of phases.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An IAC stepper motor has **200 steps per revolution** and is driven by the ECU at a pulse frequency of **200 Hz**. The pintle valve is connected to the motor shaft via a lead screw of **0.4 mm pitch**.
Compute:
(i) The rotational speed of the motor in **rpm**.
(ii) The **linear speed** of the pintle in **mm/s**.
(iii) The **angular displacement** of the rotor in degrees, if the ECU issues **35 pulses** in succession.

---

#### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Construction and Working Principle of Two-Phase Bipolar Stepper Motor** [7 Marks]

**Construction:**

A two-phase bipolar stepper motor has the following key components:

1. **Stator:** Built from laminated silicon-steel sheets to reduce eddy current losses. The stator carries **two phase windings (Phase A and Phase B)**, each wound as a *bipolar* coil (i.e., current can flow in either direction through each winding). The stator has a number of salient poles, each carrying small teeth on the inner surface.

2. **Rotor:** A cylindrical structure of permanently magnetized ferrite (or rare-earth) material with two pole pairs, OR a toothed soft-iron cylinder (variable reluctance design). The rotor is mounted on a shaft supported by bearings at both ends.

3. **Shaft and bearings:** The shaft couples the rotor to the external load (in this case, the pintle valve screw).

4. **Lead wires and connector:** Four leads (A+, A−, B+, B−) exit the housing and connect to the ECU's H-bridge driver IC.

**Working Principle:**

The ECU energizes the two phase windings in a programmed sequence. With each pulse, the magnetic field orientation in the stator changes by a fixed electrical angle, and the rotor follows by aligning its permanent-magnet poles (or minimum-reluctance path) to this new field. The sequence for one full electrical cycle is:

| Step | Phase A | Phase B | Rotor Position (electrical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +A | OFF | 0° |
| 2 | +A | +B | 45° |
| 3 | OFF | +B | 90° |
| 4 | −A | +B | 135° |
| 5 | −A | OFF | 180° |
| 6 | −A | −B | 225° |
| 7 | OFF | −B | 270° |
| 8 | +A | −B | 315° |

Each row corresponds to a **half-step** of $\theta_s/2$. The rotor advances by one mechanical step per pulse.

**Derivation of Step Angle:**

The step angle is the mechanical angle traversed by the rotor per pulse. For a motor with $N_s$ stator teeth and $m$ phases operating in full-step mode, the rotor aligns to the nearest tooth once per phase excitation. In one complete electrical cycle (one full mechanical revolution of the rotor's magnetic field pattern), the rotor must move through $360°$, and the number of distinct steps in that cycle is $N_s \cdot m$. Therefore:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$$

For a typical automotive IAC stepper with $N_s = 50$ and $m = 2$:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{50 \times 2} = 3.6°$$

For a higher-resolution motor with $N_s = 50$ and $m = 4$:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{50 \times 4} = 1.8°$$

**In the IAC application:** The rotor's rotation is geared down (or directly coupled via a lead screw) to linearly move a **pintle valve** that controls the air bypassing the closed throttle plate. The ECU modulates the idle RPM by commanding the stepper to retract the pintle (more bypass air = higher idle) or extend it (less bypass air = lower idle).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** [7 Marks]

**Given:** $N_{steps/rev} = 200$, $f_p = 200 \text{ Hz}$, lead screw pitch = $0.4 \text{ mm/rev}$, $N = 35$ pulses.

**(i) Rotational speed in rpm:**

$$n = \frac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_{steps/rev}}$$

$$n = \frac{60 \times 200}{200}$$

$$\boxed{n = 60 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Linear speed of the pintle in mm/s:**

The motor rotates at 60 rev/min = 1 rev/s. The lead screw converts this to linear motion:

$$v = n_{rev/s} \times \text{pitch}$$

$$v = 1 \text{ rev/s} \times 0.4 \text{ mm/rev}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.4 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**(iii) Angular displacement for 35 pulses:**

The step angle is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_{steps/rev}} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8° \text{ per step}$$

For 35 pulses:

$$\theta_{35} = 35 \times 1.8°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{35} = 63°}$$

This corresponds to a fractional revolution of:

$$\frac{63°}{360°} = 0.175 \text{ revolution (17.5\% of a full turn)}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET634 Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **construction and working of a vacuum operated actuator** as used in the EGR system of a passenger car. With the help of a block diagram, describe how the **ECU modulates the EGR valve opening** using a vacuum solenoid valve. State the role of the **return spring**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A vacuum actuator has a diaphragm of effective diameter **100 mm**, an effective-area coefficient $\eta_{dia} = 0.85$, and a return spring constant of $k_{spring} = 2000 \text{ N/m}$. The vacuum side of the diaphragm is exposed to a manifold absolute pressure of **35 kPa**, while the other side is at atmospheric pressure. The atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**.
Calculate:
(i) The **effective diaphragm area**.
(ii) The **actuation force** on the pushrod.
(iii) The **stroke length** of the pushrod.

---

#### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Construction, Working, and ECU Modulation of a Vacuum EGR Actuator** [7 Marks]

**Construction:**

A vacuum operated actuator for EGR application consists of:

1. **Two-chamber housing:** A rigid metal can divided into two airtight chambers by a flexible **diaphragm** (typically fabric-reinforced neoprene) clamped at its periphery.
2. **Pushrod:** A metal rod mechanically connected to the center of the diaphragm, exiting the housing through a sealed bushing on the spring side.
3. **Vacuum port (nipple):** A hose barb on the spring-side (or vacuum-side) chamber for connecting the manifold vacuum hose.
4. **Atmospheric vent:** A filtered breather on the other chamber allowing free movement of air at atmospheric pressure.
5. **Return spring:** A helical compression spring surrounding the pushrod, preloaded to push the diaphragm outward when vacuum is removed.

**Working:**

At rest, the spring keeps the diaphragm pushed to one extreme and the EGR valve is held closed. When the ECU energizes the **vacuum solenoid valve (VSV)**, the manifold vacuum port opens to the actuator chamber, and the pressure inside drops. The atmospheric pressure on the other side of the diaphragm is now higher than the vacuum side, creating a pressure differential $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$. This pushes the diaphragm toward the vacuum side, moving the pushrod and **opening the EGR valve** to allow exhaust gas recirculation.

**ECU Modulation (Block Diagram):**

```
ECU PWM Signal → VSV (Vacuum Solenoid Valve) → Actuator Chamber → Diaphragm → Pushrod → EGR Valve
```

- The ECU sends a **PWM signal** (typically **20-200 Hz, 0-100% duty cycle**) to the VSV.
- At **low duty cycle**, the VSV is mostly closed → little vacuum reaches the actuator → small EGR opening.
- At **high duty cycle**, the VSV is mostly open → full manifold vacuum reaches the actuator → maximum EGR opening.
- The *average* vacuum level at the actuator is approximately **$\text{Duty Cycle} \times (P_{atm} - P_{manifold,vac})$**, allowing fine proportional control.

**Role of the Return Spring:**

The spring serves three critical functions:
1. **Default closed position:** With no vacuum, the spring holds the EGR valve closed, which is the safe state for engine starting and idle.
2. **Linearizing the force-stroke relationship:** The spring's restoring force is proportional to its deflection, balancing the vacuum force to produce a defined stroke for each vacuum level.
3. **Dynamic response:** When the VSV closes, the spring rapidly retracts the diaphragm, closing the EGR valve within ~100-200 ms.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $\eta_{dia} = 0.85$
- $k_{spring} = 2000 \text{ N/m}$
- $P_{vac} = 35 \text{ kPa} = 35{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Effective Diaphragm Area:**

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{0.031416}{4}$$

$$\boxed{A_{eff} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \quad (\approx 78.54 \text{ cm}^2)}$$

**(ii) Actuation Force:**

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

$$\Delta P = 101{,}325 - 35{,}000$$

$$\Delta P = 66{,}325 \text{ Pa}$$

$$F_{vac} = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \eta_{dia}$$

$$F_{vac} = 66{,}325 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.85$$

$$F_{vac} = 66{,}325 \times 6.676 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{vac} \approx 442.8 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Stroke Length:**

$$L_{stroke} = \frac{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{k_{spring}}$$

$$L_{stroke} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 66{,}325}{2000}$$

$$L_{stroke} = \frac{520.96}{2000}$$

$$\boxed{L_{stroke} \approx 0.2605 \text{ m} = 26.05 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** A very common error is mixing **kPa** and **Pa**. The formula demands SI units (**Pa**). Always convert: $1 \text{ kPa} = 1000 \text{ Pa}$. Losing 1-2 marks for not converting.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta_{dia}$:** Students often write $A_{eff} = \pi D^2/4$ correctly but forget to multiply by the effective-area coefficient $\eta_{dia}$. The clamped-edge effect reduces the *true* active area by 15-30%. Examiner **will deduct 1 mark** for this omission.
> 3. **Confusing gauge vs. absolute pressure:** The problem states "manifold absolute pressure of 35 kPa." Some students incorrectly treat this as gauge pressure and compute $\Delta P = 101.325 - 35 \text{ (in kPa same as Pa)} = 66.325 \text{ kPa} = 66{,}325 \text{ Pa}$ — but a few will mistakenly compute $\Delta P = 101.325 - (-35) = 136.325 \text{ kPa}$, which is wrong. Always use **absolute** values directly in the difference.
> 4. **Stroke formula misuse:** The stroke formula $L = A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P / k_{spring}$ assumes a linear spring and ignores the spring preload $x_0$. In a precise KTU Part B, mention this assumption explicitly to gain full marks.
> 5. **Stepper: forgetting to convert Hz to rev:** The speed formula $n = 60 f_p / N$ uses $f_p$ in Hz. A common slip is writing $n = f_p / N$ directly, which gives **steps per second** instead of rpm. Examiner looks for the explicit "×60" factor.
> 6. **Half-step confusion:** In Part A or viva questions, students sometimes incorrectly say "the half-step mode doubles the rotor speed." It does **not** — the rotor speed depends on the pulse rate. Half-step mode **halves the step angle** while the rotor speed remains the same for a given pulse frequency. The ECU must issue *twice as many pulses* per revolution in half-step mode.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stepper Motor Core Definition:** Brushless synchronous motor; rotor advances by fixed **step angle** per pulse; open-loop positioning.
- **Step Angle Formula:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$ — derive it for $N_s = 50, m = 4$ to get the canonical $1.8°$ per step.
- **Stepper Speed:** $n = \dfrac{60 f_p}{N_{steps/rev}}$ — pulse frequency directly controls rpm; watch for **mechanical resonance** at 70-200 Hz.
- **Driving Modes:** Wave (1-phase), Full (2-phase), Half-step, Microstepping — know the trade-off (torque vs. smoothness vs. resolution).
- **Automotive Applications:** IAC valve, instrument cluster, headlamp leveling, EGR valve (older), throttle body (older).
- **Vacuum Actuator Core Definition:** Uses **pressure differential** between atmospheric and manifold vacuum across a **diaphragm** to produce **linear force** on a pushrod.
- **Vacuum Source:** Intake manifold at idle provides **-18 to -22 inHg** vacuum ($\approx 30-40 \text{ kPa absolute}$).
- **Vacuum Force Formula:** $F_{vac} = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \eta_{dia}$ — never forget $\eta_{dia} \approx 0.7-0.85$.
- **Stroke Formula:** $L_{stroke} = \dfrac{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{k_{spring}}$ — assumes linear return spring.
- **Automotive Applications:** EGR valve, HVAC mode/blend doors, brake booster, cruise control throttle actuator, wastegate (turbo).
- **ECU Modulation of Vacuum:** Vacuum Solenoid Valve (VSV) driven by **PWM** — duty cycle controls average vacuum level.
- **Comparison Insight:** Steppers offer **precise digital positioning** but low force; vacuum actuators offer **high force** but coarse, slower response. Modern systems increasingly use **DC motors with position sensors** (closed-loop) for both.
- **Failure Modes:** Stepper — driver IC open/short, open winding, dry bearing. Vacuum actuator — diaphragm tear, hose leak, stuck VSV.
- **Energy Audit:** Steppers consume battery current continuously (holding torque); vacuum actuators consume **no electrical energy** during steady-state actuation (vacuum is free).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Antilock Braking System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Antilock Braking System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Antilock Braking System (ABS)** is a closed-loop, electro-hydraulic vehicle safety subsystem that continuously monitors the rotational speed of each driven wheel during braking and **modulates hydraulic brake-line pressure** to prevent wheel lock-up, thereby maintaining **directional stability** and **steerability** of the vehicle on low-friction (μ-low) surfaces.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, ABS belongs to the family of **Chassis Electronics / Active Safety Systems** and is classified as a **Vehicle Dynamics Control (VDC)** subsystem governed by real-time feedback from wheel-speed sensors to a microcontroller-based **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**.

The governing performance objectives of an ABS are:

1. **Prevent wheel lock-up** — i.e., wheel angular velocity must remain > 0 throughout braking.
2. **Maintain lateral tire force** so that the driver retains steering control.
3. **Optimize longitudinal tire force** so that braking distance is minimized.
4. **Adapt to road surface friction (μ)** — wet asphalt, dry concrete, snow, ice.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Metric:** The driver controls **steering**; the ABS controls **brake slip**. Together, they prevent the vehicle from becoming an uncontrolled skidding mass.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ice-Walking & Ice-Skating Story"

Imagine walking on a frozen lake in two different shoes:

- **Ordinary shoe (no ABS):** You press your foot hard on the ice. The shoe **locks** against the ice — you slip and cannot steer. The kinetic friction is much less than the static friction you had.
- **ABS-equipped shoe (smart brake):** A tiny sensor detects that your foot is sliding. It **instantly releases**, then **re-applies** pressure — many times per second — so your foot never fully locks, but still transfers maximum force to the ice.

The vehicle's wheel behaves identically. **ABS** acts as a high-frequency (typically **8–15 Hz**), electro-mechanical "anti-skid clutch" that pulses the brake pressure so the tire stays at the **peak of the friction curve**.

## 1.3 Why ABS is Needed — The Physics of Locked Wheels

When a wheel locks (skids), the tire-road contact patch transitions from **static friction** to **kinetic friction**. The kinetic friction coefficient (μ_k) is typically **20–30% lower** than the static friction coefficient (μ_s). This causes two simultaneous failures:

| Failure Mode | Physical Consequence |
|--------------|---------------------|
| Loss of lateral grip | Vehicle cannot be steered; spins on low-μ surfaces |
| Reduced longitudinal grip | Braking distance **increases** instead of decreasing |

ABS keeps the wheel operating in the **slip range of 10%–30%**, where the longitudinal friction coefficient is at its **peak value** (μ_peak).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Standard Reference Slip Target:** Most production ABS controllers target a longitudinal slip ratio of approximately **λ ≈ 0.10 to 0.20 (10%–20%)** during peak braking.

## 1.4 The Slip Ratio — Fundamental Variable of ABS

The single most important physical quantity in any ABS algorithm is the **slip ratio (λ)**:

$$\lambda = \frac{V_{\text{vehicle}} - V_{\text{wheel}}}{V_{\text{vehicle}}}$$

where:
- **V_vehicle** = true forward speed of the vehicle body (m/s)
- **V_wheel** = linear speed of the tire contact patch, equal to ω·r (rad/s × m)

Special cases:

| Condition | V_wheel | λ value | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|---------|---------|------------------|
| Free rolling | V_wheel = V_vehicle | **0** | No braking demand |
| Light braking | V_wheel slightly < V_vehicle | **0 < λ < 0.1** | Within stable region |
| Optimal ABS | V_wheel ≈ 0.8·V_vehicle | **≈ 0.2** | Peak friction point |
| Wheel lock-up | V_wheel ≈ 0 | **≈ 1** | Tire skidding |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A question asking "What is the controlled variable in ABS?" expects the answer **slip ratio (λ)** — *not* brake pressure, *not* wheel speed.

## 1.5 Historical & Regulatory Context

- **First patent:** 1929 (Gabriel Talbot) for aircraft use.
- **First automotive implementation:** 1966, Jensen FF (four-wheel drive + ABS).
- **Bosch ABS 2** (1978) — first electronic-digital production ABS.
- **Regulatory mandate:** ABS became **mandatory in the European Union** for all new passenger cars from **2004**, and in **India** for two-wheelers above 125 cc from **2019** (AIS-146).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Friction Coefficient–Slip Curve (μ–λ Curve)

The friction coefficient between tire and road is **not constant** — it is a strong function of slip ratio. This curve is the **theoretical foundation** of every ABS algorithm.

The empirical **Burckhardt / Pacejka "Magic Formula"** approximation:

$$\mu(\lambda) = a \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-b \cdot \lambda} \right) - c \cdot \lambda$$

where **a, b, c** are surface-dependent empirical constants.

A simpler, analytically tractable engineering approximation used in textbooks:

$$\mu(\lambda) = \mu_{\text{peak}} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot \lambda \cdot \lambda_{\text{peak}}}{\lambda^2 + \lambda_{\text{peak}}^2}$$

The curve has three operating regions:

| Region | Slip Range | Behaviour | Consequence |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Stable** | 0 ≤ λ < λ_peak | dμ/dλ > 0 | Pressure increase → more braking force |
| **Peak** | λ = λ_peak | μ maximum | Optimal target for ABS |
| **Unstable** | λ > λ_peak | dμ/dλ < 0 | Pressure increase → *less* braking force → lock-up |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this matters for ABS design:** Once the wheel enters the **unstable region**, simply pressing harder on the brake pedal is **counter-productive** — friction actually drops. ABS exists to keep the wheel *near* λ_peak, never beyond it.

## 2.2 Decomposition of the ABS Control Problem

The ABS controller solves a real-time **non-linear feedback problem** that can be broken down into five logical steps:

**Step 1 — Sense:** Read wheel angular velocity ω_i(t) from each wheel-speed sensor (typically at 48-tooth tone wheels, producing ~**48 pulses per revolution**).

**Step 2 — Estimate:** Compute vehicle reference speed V_ref from the **maximum wheel speed** plus a known deceleration floor (typically **–0.85 g** for passenger cars).

**Step 3 — Calculate slip:** Determine λ_i(t) for each wheel.

**Step 4 — Decide:** Apply a control law (hysteresis, PID, fuzzy-logic, or model-based) to determine whether each wheel needs pressure **Increase (I), Hold (H), or Decrease (D)**.

**Step 5 — Actuate:** Drive the **solenoid valves** in the hydraulic modulator (response time ≈ **2–5 ms**).

## 2.3 The Three-Phase ABS Pressure Modulation Cycle

At the heart of every ABS algorithm — whether classical Bosch 8 Hz, modern 15 Hz, or regenerative EV — lies the same three-state pressure modulator cycle:

| Phase | Driver Pedal | Inlet Valve | Outlet Valve | Pump | Effect on Wheel Speed |
|-------|-------------|-------------|--------------|------|-----------------------|
| **Pressure Build-up (I)** | Pressed | **OPEN** | CLOSED | Idle | Wheel decelerates → slip increases |
| **Pressure Hold (H)** | Pressed | CLOSED | CLOSED | Idle | Wheel decelerates freely; ECU evaluates λ |
| **Pressure Release (D)** | Pressed | CLOSED | **OPEN** | **ACTIVE** | Brake fluid dumped → wheel re-accelerates |

A modern ABS ECU executes this cycle at **8–15 Hz** (one full I–H–D–I cycle every **65–125 ms**). The driver, meanwhile, applies steady pedal pressure — they feel only a **characteristic vibration / pulsation** through the brake pedal.

## 2.4 Architecture of the ABS Hydraulic Modulator

A modern **Bosch ABS 8.x / Continental MK100** modulator integrates:

- **2/2-way solenoid valves** per wheel (one inlet, one outlet)
- A **return pump** (often dual-piston for fast refill)
- A **low-pressure accumulator** to receive released fluid
- An **ECU** mounted on the modulator body itself

The ECU uses **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM)** at typically **100 Hz–1 kHz** on each solenoid to fine-tune pressure between the discrete I/H/D states — this is called the **"active pressure buildup"** technique used in modern ABS.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{V_v - V_w}{V_v}$ | Slip ratio (definition) | dimensionless, $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ |
| 2 | $V_w = \omega \cdot r_{\text{eff}}$ | Wheel linear speed from angular speed | m/s, rad/s, m |
| 3 | $a_w = \dfrac{\omega(t) - \omega(t - \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$ | Wheel angular deceleration | rad/s² (negative while braking) |
| 4 | $V_{\text{ref}}(t) = \max\bigl(\omega_i(t)\bigr) \cdot r$ | Vehicle reference speed | m/s (computed each cycle) |
| 5 | $V_{\text{ref}}(t) = V_{\text{ref}}(t_0) - \int a_{\text{floor}}\, dt$ | Reference speed decay during full lock | $a_{\text{floor}} \approx -8.5 \, \text{m/s}^2$ |
| 6 | $\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 0.10\text{–}0.20$ | Optimal slip target | empirical, surface-dependent |
| 7 | $f_{\text{cycle}} \approx 8\text{–}15 \, \text{Hz}$ | ABS pressure modulation frequency | Hertz |
| 8 | $\mu(\lambda) = \mu_{\text{peak}} \cdot \dfrac{2\lambda\lambda_p}{\lambda^2 + \lambda_p^2}$ | Friction–slip approximation | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_{\text{brake}} = P_{\text{hydraulic}} \cdot A_{\text{caliper}} \cdot \mu_{\text{pad}}$ | Brake torque at caliper | Newton-metres |
| 10 | $T_{\text{brake}} = F_{\text{brake}} \cdot r_{\text{disc}} \cdot \eta$ | Net braking torque at wheel | Nm, η = mechanical efficiency |
| 11 | $F_{\text{long}} = \mu(\lambda) \cdot N$ | Longitudinal tire force at contact patch | Newton |
| 12 | $F_{\text{lat}} = \mu_{\text{lat}}(\lambda) \cdot N$ | Lateral tire force at contact patch | Newton — falls sharply as λ → 1 |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked to "derive" or "explain" ABS operation, you **must** show the slip ratio formula and reference the μ–λ curve. Omitting these earns only partial marks.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ABS architecture is the **foundation layer** for several modern active-safety systems:

- **EBD** (Electronic Brake-force Distribution) — uses ABS valves to vary front/rear pressure.
- **EBA / BAS** (Emergency Brake Assist) — detects panic brake application and applies maximum pressure.
- **ESC / ESP** (Electronic Stability Control) — adds **yaw-rate** and **steering-angle** sensors; uses ABS valves to brake individual wheels to correct skids.
- **AEB** (Autonomous Emergency Braking) — adds radar/lidar; pre-charges the ABS modulator before driver reacts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Slip Ratio λ from First Principles

We start from the definition of wheel linear velocity at the tire contact patch:

$$V_w(t) = \omega(t) \cdot r_{\text{eff}}$$

where $r_{\text{eff}}$ is the **effective rolling radius** under load (typically 95–97% of the unloaded tyre radius).

The **vehicle body speed** $V_v$ is taken as the speed of the vehicle's centre of mass. Under pure rolling without slip:

$$V_w = V_v \quad \text{(free rolling)}$$

When the brake is applied, the wheel decelerates faster than the vehicle body, because brake torque opposes wheel rotation. The wheel linear speed becomes:

$$V_w < V_v$$

The **relative slip** is therefore:

$$\Delta V = V_v - V_w$$

Dividing by the vehicle speed to obtain a normalized, dimensionless ratio:

$$\lambda = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v} = \frac{\Delta V}{V_v}$$

Special-case verification:

**Case 1 — Free rolling (no brake):**
$V_w = V_v \Rightarrow \Delta V = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0$ ✓

**Case 2 — Wheel fully locked:**
$V_w = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta V = V_v \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$ ✓

**Case 3 — Wheel spinning in air (ABS fault / burnout):**
$V_w > V_v \Rightarrow \Delta V < 0 \Rightarrow \lambda < 0$ (negative slip = traction-control regime)

## 3.2 Derivation of the Wheel-Deceleration Threshold Logic

The ABS controller cannot instantaneously compute V_v — it must be estimated. The classical approach uses **wheel deceleration** as a proxy for the onset of lock-up.

From the sampled wheel-speed signal, the ECU computes:

$$a_w(t_k) = \frac{\omega(t_k) - \omega(t_{k-1})}{t_k - t_{k-1}}$$

where $t_k - t_{k-1} = \Delta t \approx 5\text{–}10 \, \text{ms}$.

The **deceleration threshold** is typically set to:

$$a_{w,\text{threshold}} \approx -1.5 \, g \quad \approx -14.7 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

When $a_w \le a_{w,\text{threshold}}$, the controller concludes that the wheel is approaching lock-up and initiates the **Pressure Hold** state.

The corresponding **slip threshold** logic uses the integral of $a_w$:

$$V_{\text{ref}}(t_k) = V_{\text{ref}}(t_{k-1}) + a_{\text{floor}} \cdot \Delta t$$

with $a_{\text{floor}} \approx -8.5 \, \text{m/s}^2$. Once a wheel's $V_w$ falls more than a calibrated margin below $V_{\text{ref}}$, the controller forces a **Pressure Release**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Pressure-Modulation Duty Cycle

Consider one wheel during steady-state ABS operation. In each modulation cycle of period $T_c$, the controller spends:

- $t_I$ seconds in **Pressure Build-up (I)**
- $t_H$ seconds in **Pressure Hold (H)**
- $t_D$ seconds in **Pressure Release (D)**

By definition of period:

$$T_c = t_I + t_H + t_D$$

The **effective duty cycle** for the inlet valve is:

$$D_I = \frac{t_I}{T_c}$$

The **average hydraulic pressure** delivered to the wheel during the cycle is approximately:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{T_c} \int_0^{T_c} P(t) \, dt \approx P_{\text{max}} \cdot \frac{t_I - t_D + \alpha \, t_H}{T_c}$$

where $\alpha$ is a small pressure-rise coefficient during Hold due to residual compliance. The ECU adjusts $t_I, t_H, t_D$ each cycle (typically every **5–15 ms**) so that $\bar{P}$ drives the wheel slip toward $\lambda_{\text{peak}}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ABS Phases vs. Vehicle Dynamics

| Phase | Valve State | Wheel Behaviour | Vehicle Behaviour | Slip Trend |
|-------|------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Build-up (I)** | Inlet OPEN, Outlet CLOSED | Decelerates rapidly | Body decelerates | λ ↑ (increasing) |
| **Hold (H)** | Inlet CLOSED, Outlet CLOSED | Decelerates freely (no added brake torque) | Body continues to slow | λ ↑ (still increasing, but slower) |
| **Release (D)** | Inlet CLOSED, Outlet OPEN, Pump ON | Re-accelerates (less brake torque) | Body still slowing | λ ↓ (decreasing) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Energy-balance note:** During Release, kinetic energy of the vehicle body is dissipated primarily as heat in the brake disc. The pump returns brake fluid to the master cylinder reservoir at high pressure — this is why ABS pumps are **loud and powerful** (~ 30 W hydraulic power).

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A vehicle is braking on dry asphalt from $V_v = 30 \, \text{m/s}$. The wheel radius is $r = 0.32 \, \text{m}$, and the wheel has decelerated to $\omega = 70 \, \text{rad/s}$. Compute the slip ratio.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute wheel linear speed:

$$V_w = \omega \cdot r = 70 \times 0.32 = 22.4 \, \text{m/s}$$

Step 2 — Apply slip formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v} = \frac{30 - 22.4}{30} = \frac{7.6}{30}$$

$$\lambda \approx 0.253 \;\;\; (\approx 25.3\%)$$

Step 3 — Interpretation: This slip value is just **above** the typical μ-peak region (10–20%) and is approaching the unstable shoulder of the μ–λ curve. The ABS ECU must command a **Pressure Hold or Release** to bring λ back to ~ 0.15.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
