  - Powers a Belt-Driven Starter Generator (BSG) or Crankshaft-Mounted Starter Generator (MSG).
  - Enables start-stop, torque-fill, and mild regenerative braking (5 – 15 kW).
  - Still below the **60 V DC safety threshold**, so no high-voltage protection needed.

- **High-Voltage (HV) Traction Network:**
  - 200 V – 800 V DC bus feeding the traction inverter and motor.
  - Governed by **ISO 6469** and **UN R100** (electrical safety).
  - Requires orange HV cables, interlock loops, and manual service disconnects.

---

### 2.2 Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) Classification

The **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE J1715)** defines five hybridization levels:

- **Micro-Hybrid (12 V Start-Stop):** Engine shuts off at traffic lights. No electric driving.
- **Mild-Hybrid (48 V):** Electric machine assists the ICE. Cannot drive the vehicle alone. Fuel saving: 10 – 15 %.
- **Full-Hybrid (HEV):** E-motor can drive the vehicle alone at low speed. Examples: Toyota Prius, Honda Insight. Fuel saving: 30 – 40 %.
- **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Larger battery (8 – 18 kWh), chargeable from grid. Electric range: 30 – 60 km.
- **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV):** No ICE. Pure electric. Examples: Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV.

---

### 2.3 Hybrid Drive Topologies

The mechanical coupling of the ICE and the E-motor defines the **architecture**:

- **Series Hybrid:** ICE → Generator → Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels. (Range-Extender EVs like BMW i3 REX.)
- **Parallel Hybrid:** ICE and E-motor are *both* mechanically connected to the wheels via a clutch. Both can propel the vehicle directly.
- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid:** Uses a **planetary gear set** to continuously vary the power split between ICE, generator, motor, and wheels. (Toyota Hybrid System – THS.)
- **Complex (Through-the-Road) Hybrid:** One axle driven by ICE, the other by an E-motor. (Volvo T8, some Audi Quattro variants.)

---

### 2.4 Energy Storage: Battery Technologies

| Chemistry | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Cycle Life | Cost | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lead-Acid | 30 – 50 | 300 – 500 | Very Low | SLI 12 V batteries |
| NiMH | 60 – 120 | 1000 – 2000 | Moderate | Toyota Prius (1st–3rd gen) |
| Li-ion (NMC) | 150 – 220 | 1000 – 3000 | High | Tesla, Hyundai, Tata |
| Li-ion (LFP) | 90 – 160 | 3000 – 6000 | Moderate | BYD, entry-level EVs |
| Solid-State | 300 – 500 (lab) | TBD | Very High | Future (Toyota 2027+) |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every high-yield equation that has appeared in KTU University Examinations for the PEAET634 course. Memorize the **units** and **boundary conditions** to score full marks.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive Force | $F_t = m \cdot a + m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + \mu m g \cos\theta$ | N | Used in vehicle dynamics problems. |
| 2 | Tractive Power | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | W | Mechanical power demanded at wheels. |
| 3 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = \mu m g \cos\theta$ | N | $\mu \approx 0.015$ for radial tyres. |
| 4 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | N | $C_d$ ≈ 0.25 – 0.35 for sedans. |
| 5 | Climbing Force | $F_c = m g \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta$ = road gradient (rad). |
| 6 | Acceleration Force | $F_a = m \cdot a$ | N | Includes rotational inertia $\delta m$. |
| 7 | Battery Energy | $E = V \cdot Q = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Wh or J | $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$. |
| 8 | Battery SoC | $SoC = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | Coulomb-counting method. |
| 9 | C-Rate | $C = \frac{I}{Q_{Ah}}$ | h$^{-1}$ | 1C = full discharge in 1 h. |
| 10 | DC-DC Boost Output | $V_o = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ | V | $D$ = duty cycle, $0 < D < 1$. |
| 11 | DC-DC Buck Output | $V_o = D \cdot V_{in}$ | V | Step-down converter. |
| 12 | Inverter Line Voltage (SPWM) | $V_{LL,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{DC}$ | V | $m_a$ = modulation index $\le 1$. |
| 13 | PMSM Torque | $T = \tfrac{3}{2} p \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q$ | N·m | $p$ = pole pairs, $\psi_m$ = flux linkage. |
| 14 | Regenerative Energy | $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | J | Limited by battery SoC window. |
| 15 | Regen Efficiency | $\eta_{regen} = \frac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic}}$ | – | Typically 60 – 70 %. |
| 16 | Hybrid Fuel Economy Gain | $\Delta FE = \frac{FE_{HEV} - FE_{ICE}}{FE_{ICE}} \times 100\%$ | % | 30 – 40 % for full HEV. |
| 17 | Internal Resistance Loss | $P_{loss} = I^2 R_{int}$ | W | Major source of battery heating. |
| 18 | State of Health | $SoH = \frac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{max,rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | EOL typically at 80 % SoH. |
| 19 | Power Flow in THS | $P_{ICE} + P_{M1} = P_{Wheels} + P_{M2}$ | W | Energy balance across planetary gear. |
| 20 | Specific Power | $P_{sp} = \frac{P}{m}$ | W/kg | EV motors: 3 – 6 kW/kg. |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Why does this matter in industry?
> 1. **OEM Design Decisions:** Choosing 400 V vs 800 V architecture (e.g., Hyundai E-GMP vs Tesla) directly affects charging speed — $P_{charge} = V \cdot I$.
> 2. **Battery Pack Sizing:** Determines range. Range ≈ (Battery kWh × $\eta$) / (kWh/km).
> 3. **Thermal Management:** Power electronics (IGBT, SiC MOSFET) require liquid cooling above 100 kW.
> 4. **Emission Compliance:** BS-VI / Euro 7 norms can only be met with hybridisation or electrification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Tractive Force Equation (Vehicle Dynamics)

The total tractive force required at the wheel must overcome four resistances. We derive each term from Newton's second law and standard fluid mechanics.

**Step 1 — Identify all opposing forces on a vehicle moving on a road of gradient $\theta$ with velocity $v$:**

The forces acting opposite to the direction of motion are:
- Aerodynamic drag (fluid friction with air)
- Rolling resistance (tyre deformation)
- Climbing force (component of gravity along slope)

**Step 2 — Write the force balance (Newton's Second Law):**

The net forward force equals mass × acceleration:

$$
F_{tractive} - F_{rolling} - F_{aero} - F_{climb} = m \cdot a
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute each resistance term:**

The rolling resistance is proportional to the normal force $N = mg\cos\theta$:

$$
F_{rolling} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

The aerodynamic drag follows the standard drag equation:

$$
F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2
$$

The climbing force is the gravitational component along the slope:

$$
F_{climb} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the tractive force:**

$$
F_t = m \cdot a \;+\; \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \;+\; m g \sin\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by velocity to obtain tractive power:**

$$
P_t = F_t \cdot v
$$

**Interpretation:** The first term is the *inertial* power (kinetic energy change). The second is the *rolling loss*. The third is the *aerodynamic loss* — note its cubic dependence on velocity ($P_{aero} \propto v^3$). The fourth is the *potential energy* change (climbing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The $v^3$ cubic dependence of aerodynamic power loss is the single most-tested fact in KTU question papers on vehicle dynamics. At highway speeds (>80 km/h), aerodynamic drag dominates all other losses combined.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery State of Charge (Coulomb Counting)

The State of Charge (SoC) is the fuel gauge of an EV. The most common method is *Coulomb counting*.

**Step 1 — Define the rated capacity:**

The battery is rated with a nominal capacity $Q_{rated}$ in Ampere-hours (Ah).

**Step 2 — Integrate the current over time:**

The charge transferred in/out of the battery is:

$$
\Delta Q = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} I(t) \, dt
$$

For a constant current $I$ over time $\Delta t$:

$$
\Delta Q = I \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the remaining capacity:**

$$
Q_{remaining} = Q_{initial} - \int_{t_0}^{t_1} I(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to obtain SoC (in %):**

$$
SoC(t) = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Boundary conditions:**

- $SoC = 100\%$ → Battery fully charged.
- $SoC = 0\%$ → Battery fully discharged (deep discharge damages cells).
- Operational window for Li-ion: typically **20 % – 80 % SoC** to maximize cycle life.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Boost Converter Output Voltage

The DC-DC boost converter steps up the battery voltage to feed the HV bus.

**Step 1 — Assume inductor $L$ is in continuous conduction mode (CCM).**

The switch $S$ is closed for time $DT_s$ and open for $(1-D)T_s$, where $D$ is the duty cycle.

**Step 2 — Apply volt-second balance on the inductor:**

In steady state, the average voltage across $L$ over one switching period is zero (otherwise the current would ramp to infinity).

**Step 3 — Switch ON (duration $DT_s$):**

Diode is reverse-biased. The inductor charges from input:

$$
V_L = V_{in}
$$

**Step 4 — Switch OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$):**

Diode conducts. The inductor sees the difference $V_{in} - V_o$:

$$
V_L = V_{in} - V_o
$$

**Step 5 — Apply volt-second balance:**

$$
V_{in} \cdot (DT_s) + (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot (1-D)T_s = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_o$:**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $V_o = V_{in} \cdot D$ (the buck converter equation) by mistake. Remember: **Boost → denominator has $(1-D)$**, **Buck → numerator has $D$**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: PMSM Torque Equation

The Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor is the dominant traction machine in modern EVs.

**Step 1 — Apply the power balance:**

Electrical input power equals mechanical output power (assuming lossless for derivation):

$$
P_{elec} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \omega_e \cdot (\psi_d i_d + \psi_q i_q)
$$

where $\omega_e$ is the electrical angular speed, $\psi_d, \psi_q$ are dq-axis flux linkages, and $i_d, i_q$ are dq-axis currents.

**Step 2 — Express the d-axis flux (no reluctance term for surface-mounted PMSM):**

$$
\psi_d = \psi_m \quad \text{and} \quad \psi_q = L_q i_q
$$

where $\psi_m$ is the permanent magnet flux linkage.

**Step 3 — Apply Maximum Torque Per Ampere (MTPA) control:**

For MTPA, $i_d = 0$, so only $i_q$ produces torque.

**Step 4 — Substitute to get the electromagnetic torque:**

$$
T_e = \frac{P_{elec}}{\omega_m} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot i_q
$$

where $p$ is the number of pole pairs and $\omega_m = \omega_e / p$.

**Step 5 — Final PMSM torque formula:**

$$
T = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q
$$

**Interpretation:** Torque is directly proportional to the q-axis current. By controlling $I_q$ via the inverter's PWM, the HCU achieves instant torque response — a key advantage over ICE.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A passenger car of mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ accelerates uniformly from $0$ to $100 \text{ km/h}$ in $10 \text{ s}$ on a flat road ($\theta = 0$). Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d A = 0.7 \text{ m}^2$, rolling resistance coefficient $\mu = 0.015$. Assume a parallel hybrid configuration.
> **(a)** Calculate the peak tractive power required.
> **(b)** If the e-motor supplies 60 % of the peak power, compute the e-motor rating.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert velocity:**

$$
v_{max} = 100 \text{ km/h} = \frac{100}{3.6} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate acceleration:**

$$
a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{27.78 - 0}{10} = 2.778 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each force component at peak velocity:**

Inertial force:

$$
F_{acc} = m \cdot a = 1500 \times 2.778 = 4167 \text{ N}
$$

Rolling resistance:

$$
F_{roll} = \mu m g = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 220.7 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag (at $v = 27.78$ m/s):

$$
F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (27.78)^2
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 771.6 = 330.8 \text{ N}
$$

Climbing force on flat road: $F_{climb} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Total tractive force at $t = 10$ s:**

$$
F_t = 4167 + 220.7 + 330.8 = 4718.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Peak tractive power:**

$$
P_{peak} = F_t \times v_{max} = 4718.5 \times 27.78 \approx 131{,}100 \text{ W} \approx 131.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — E-motor rating (60 % of peak):**

$$
P_{motor} = 0.6 \times 131.1 = 78.66 \text{ kW} \approx 79 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ICE supplies the remaining $0.4 \times 131.1 = 52.4 \text{ kW}$ at peak demand. This 60/40 split is a common design heuristic for parallel hybrids in KTU reference answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full HEV Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Internal Combustion Engine]
    B --> C[Transmission and Clutch]
    D[High Voltage Battery 200V to 800V] --> E[Power Electronics Controller]
    E --> F[Traction Inverter DC to AC]
    F --> G[PMSM or Induction Motor]
    C --> H[Planetary Gear Power Split]
    H --> I[Final Drive and Differential]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Wheels]
    K[12V Auxiliary Battery] --> L[DC-DC Converter]
    E --> L
    L --> K
    M[Brake Pedal] --> N[Brake Controller]
    N --> G
    O[Accelerator Pedal] --> P[VCU Vehicle Control Unit]
    P --> B
    P --> E
    P --> N
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **ICE** (B) and the **E-Motor** (G) both deliver mechanical power to the **Planetary Gear** (H), which is the heart of the Toyota Hybrid System (THS).
- The **Battery** (D) feeds the **Inverter** (F) through a **Power Electronics Controller** (E).
- The **VCU** (P) is the brain: it reads the **accelerator** (O) and **brake** (M) inputs and commands the ICE, motor, and brakes.
- The **DC-DC Converter** (L) steps the HV bus down to 12 V to charge the auxiliary battery (K).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Electronics Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Battery Pack] --> B[DC Bus Capacitor]
    B --> C[Three Phase Inverter IGBT or SiC]
    C --> D[Gate Driver Unit]
    E[VCU Torque Command] --> D
    D --> C
    C --> F[3 Phase AC to PMSM Motor]
    F --> G[Resolver or Encoder]
    G --> H[Rotor Position Feedback]
    H --> E
    C --> I[Regenerative Braking Path]
    I --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **DC bus capacitor** (B) smooths voltage ripple from the battery.
- The **inverter** (C) switches at 5 – 20 kHz using IGBTs (older) or SiC MOSFETs (newer EVs).
- The **gate driver** (D) receives low-power PWM signals from the VCU and amplifies them to switch the power devices.
- The **resolver/encoder** (G) provides rotor position feedback (H) — essential for **field-oriented control (FOC)**.
- During braking, the inverter's freewheeling diodes act as a passive rectifier, pushing current back into the battery (**regenerative braking path** I).

---

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Hybrid Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EV_Mode
    EV_Mode --> ICE_Mode : High speed or low battery
    ICE_Mode --> EV_Mode : Low speed and high battery
    ICE_Mode --> Hybrid_Mode : High load acceleration
    Hybrid_Mode --> ICE_Mode : Cruise
    EV_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Brake pedal pressed
    ICE_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Brake pedal pressed
    Hybrid_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Brake pedal pressed
    Regen_Mode --> EV_Mode : Brake released
    Regen_Mode --> ICE_Mode : Brake released at high speed
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **EV_Mode:** E-motor alone drives the vehicle. Quiet, zero emission.
- **ICE_Mode:** ICE alone drives the vehicle. Used at high speed or low battery.
- **Hybrid_Mode:** Both propel the vehicle. Peak acceleration.
- **Regen_Mode:** Neither propels; the motor acts as a generator to charge the battery.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — 48 V Mild-Hybrid Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[12V Lead Acid Battery] --> B[DC-DC Converter 48V to 12V]
    C[48V Li-ion Battery] --> D[Belt Driven Starter Generator BSG]
    D --> E[Engine Crankshaft]
    B --> A
    C --> B
    D --> F[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    F --> D
    F --> C
    G[Vehicle Control Module] --> F
    G --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define the term *State of Charge (SoC)* of a battery. Mention the typical operational SoC window for a Lithium-ion traction battery.

**Model Answer:**

State of Charge (SoC) is defined as the ratio of the remaining charge in a battery to its rated (maximum) capacity, expressed as a percentage.

$$
SoC(t) = \frac{Q_{remaining}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%
$$

For Lithium-ion traction batteries used in EVs, the operational SoC window is typically **20 % to 80 %** to maximize cycle life, even though the full physical range is 0 – 100 %. Discharging below 20 % or charging above 80 % accelerates degradation of the anode SEI layer and reduces calendar life.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> Differentiate between a *mild-hybrid* and a *full-hybrid* electric vehicle with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Mild-Hybrid (48 V) | Full-Hybrid (HEV) |
|---|---|---|
| Electric-only drive | **No** | **Yes** (at low speed) |
| Architecture | 48 V BSG + 12 V | High-voltage E-motor + ICE |
| Electric Power | 5 – 15 kW | 50 – 100 kW |
| Battery | 48 V Li-ion (~1 kWh) | 200+ V NiMH or Li-ion (~1.5 kWh) |
| Fuel saving | 10 – 15 % | 30 – 40 % |
| Example | Audi A6 48 V | Toyota Prius |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **Q3(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a series-parallel (power-split) hybrid electric vehicle. Discuss the role of the planetary gear set.

**Model Answer:**

A series-parallel (power-split) hybrid uses a **planetary gear set** to mechanically couple the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), a generator (Motor-1, M1), and a traction motor (Motor-2, M2), while also transmitting power to the wheels.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ICE[Internal Combustion Engine] --> PG[Planetary Gear Set]
    PG --> SUN[Sun Gear]
    PG --> RING[Ring Gear]
    PG --> CARRIER[Planet Carrier]
    SUN --> M1[Generator M1]
    RING --> M2[Traction Motor M2]
    CARRIER --> FD[Final Drive to Wheels]
```

**Key Roles of the Planetary Gear:**

1. **Sun Gear:** Connected to the **Generator (M1)**. By varying M1's speed/torque, the ICE speed can be controlled independently of vehicle speed (a **Continuously Variable Transmission – CVT** effect).
2. **Ring Gear:** Connected to the **Traction Motor (M2)** and to the **Final Drive** (wheels).
3. **Planet Carrier:** Connected to the **ICE** (in THS-II layout).

**Energy Flow Modes:**
- **Starting:** ICE is off. M2 drives the ring gear directly → EV mode.
- **Cruising:** ICE drives the carrier; ring gear drives wheels; M1 generates to charge battery.
- **Full acceleration:** ICE + M2 both drive wheels; M1 may generate to absorb excess ICE torque.
- **Regenerative braking:** M2 acts as a generator, charging the battery.

**Mathematical Relation (Willis Equation):**

$$
(\omega_R - \omega_C) \cdot R \;+\; (\omega_S - \omega_C) \cdot S = 0
$$

where $\omega$ denotes angular speeds and $R, S$ are ring and sun gear tooth counts.

---

> **Q3(b) [7 Marks]**
> A hybrid electric vehicle of mass 1800 kg climbs a 10 % gradient at a constant speed of 60 km/h. The combined drag coefficient $C_d A = 0.8 \text{ m}^2$ and rolling resistance coefficient $\mu = 0.012$. Compute the tractive power required. If 40 % of this power is delivered by the electric motor, find the e-motor power and the current drawn from a 360 V battery (assume 95 % drivetrain efficiency).

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$
v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}
$$

Gradient $\tan\theta = 0.10 \Rightarrow \theta = 5.71° \Rightarrow \sin\theta = 0.0995,\; \cos\theta = 0.995$.

**Step 2 — Compute each resistance:**

Rolling resistance:

$$
F_{roll} = \mu m g \cos\theta = 0.012 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.995 = 210.9 \text{ N}
$$

Climbing force:

$$
F_{climb} = m g \sin\theta = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.0995 = 1757 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag:

$$
F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.8 \times (16.67)^2 = 136.1 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive force:**

$$
F_t = 210.9 + 1757 + 136.1 = 2104 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Tractive power:**

$$
P_t = F_t \times v = 2104 \times 16.67 = 35{,}074 \text{ W} \approx 35.07 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — E-motor power (40 %):**

$$
P_{motor} = 0.40 \times 35.07 = 14.03 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Account for drivetrain efficiency:**

Electrical power required from battery:

$$
P_{batt} = \frac{P_{motor}}{\eta} = \frac{14.03}{0.95} = 14.77 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Battery current:**

$$
I = \frac{P_{batt}}{V_{batt}} = \frac{14{,}770}{360} = 41.0 \text{ A}
$$

**Final Answer:** E-motor power ≈ **14.03 kW**, Battery current ≈ **41 A**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
> **Q4(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
> Explain the working principle of a regenerative braking system in an EV. Derive an expression for the energy recovered during braking from $v_1$ to $v_2$.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**

In a conventional vehicle, braking dissipates kinetic energy as heat in the friction pads — this energy is wasted. In an EV, the traction motor is reversible: when commanded to produce a *negative* torque, it acts as a **generator**, converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy that is stored back in the battery. The friction brakes are used only for hard stops or low-SoC conditions (blended braking).

**Derivation:**

The kinetic energy of a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is:

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

When the vehicle decelerates from $v_1$ to $v_2$:

$$
\Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

Accounting for the **round-trip efficiency** $\eta_{regen}$ of the motor-inverter-battery path:

$$
E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

**Numerical Example:**

For a 1500 kg EV braking from 90 km/h to 30 km/h with $\eta_{regen} = 0.65$:

$$
E_{recovered} = 0.65 \times 0.5 \times 1500 \times (25^2 - 8.33^2) = 0.65 \times 750 \times (625 - 69.4)
$$

$$
E_{recovered} = 0.65 \times 750 \times 555.6 = 270{,}855 \text{ J} \approx 75.2 \text{ Wh}
$$

This 75 Wh is enough to power the vehicle's HVAC blower for ~3 hours — a small but meaningful recovery.

**Practical Limit:** Regen is disabled (or limited) when battery SoC > 90 % to prevent overcharge and lithium plating.

---

> **Q4(b) [7 Marks]**
> Compare **Lead-Acid, NiMH, and Li-ion** batteries for EV applications under the following heads: (i) specific energy, (ii) cycle life, (iii) cost, (iv) safety, (v) application example.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 30 – 50 | 60 – 120 | 150 – 220 |
| Cycle Life | 300 – 500 | 1000 – 2000 | 1000 – 3000 |
| Cost (₹/kWh, approx.) | 5,000 – 8,000 | 15,000 – 20,000 | 12,000 – 18,000 |
| Safety | High (robust, no thermal runaway) | Moderate (memory effect, venting) | Moderate (requires BMS, can catch fire if punctured) |
| Energy Density (Wh/L) | 60 – 110 | 150 – 250 | 250 – 400 |
| Self-Discharge | High (~5 %/month) | Moderate (~20 %/month) | Low (~2 %/month) |
| Application Example | Conventional SLI 12 V battery | First-gen Toyota Prius (1.3 kWh) | Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV |
| Recyclability | Excellent (>95 %) | Moderate | Developing |

**Conclusion:** Although **Lead-Acid** is the cheapest and safest, its poor specific energy rules it out for traction use except as a 12 V auxiliary. **NiMH** dominated early HEVs (Toyota) due to safety, but has been displaced by **Li-ion**, which offers 3× the specific energy and superior cycle life. Modern EVs universally use Li-ion with sophisticated BMS for safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing Buck and Boost equations:** Remember: Boost → $V_o = V_{in} / (1-D)$, Buck → $V_o = D \cdot V_{in}$. Many students swap these and lose 2 – 3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$ in rolling resistance:** When the road is inclined, $F_{roll} = \mu m g \cos\theta$, NOT $\mu m g$. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for the cosine term.
> 3. **Mixing C-rate and SoC:** C-rate is a *current* metric (h⁻¹), while SoC is a *charge* metric (%). They are dimensionally different.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always write units (N, m/s, kW, Ah). A correct number without units loses half a mark.
> 5. **Writing "regenerative braking is 100 % efficient":** The actual round-trip efficiency is 60 – 70 %. The rest is lost as heat in the motor windings, inverter switching, and battery internal resistance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PEAET634 — Module 3**

- [x] **Automotive voltage classes:** 12 V (SLI) → 24 V (CV) → 48 V (Mild-Hybrid, LV 148) → 200 – 800 V (Traction).
- [x] **Hybrid levels (SAE J1715):** Micro → Mild → Full → Plug-in → BEV.
- [x] **HEV architectures:** Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split), Complex.
- [x] **Toyota THS uses a planetary gear** for continuous variable power split (Willis equation).
- [x] **Tractive force equation** has four terms: inertial, rolling, aero, climbing.
- [x] **Aerodynamic power** scales as $v^3$ — dominant above 80 km/h.
- [x] **PMSM torque** $T = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m I_q$ (MTPA control: $I_d = 0$).
- [x] **Boost converter:** $V_o = V_{in} / (1 - D)$; **Buck:** $V_o = D \cdot V_{in}$.
- [x] **Inverter output** $V_{LL,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} m_a V_{DC}$.
- [x] **SoC window** for Li-ion: 20 % – 80 % to maximize cycle life.
- [x] **Regen braking efficiency** is 60 – 70 %, not 100 %.
- [x] **C-rate** is current divided by Ah rating (1C = full discharge in 1 hour).
- [x] **48 V BSG** powers start-stop and mild regen; cannot drive the vehicle alone.
- [x] **Battery comparison:** Li-ion > NiMH > Lead-Acid in specific energy.
- [x] **Safety standards:** ISO 6469, UN R100 for HV systems; LV 148 for 48 V.
- [x] **Planetary gear relations** (Willis): $(\omega_R - \omega_C) R + (\omega_S - \omega_C) S = 0$.
- [x] **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol — appears in coulomb-counting.
- [x] **Regen disabled above 90 % SoC** to prevent overcharge and lithium plating.
- [x] **PMSM is the dominant EV traction motor** (Tesla, Hyundai, Tata, BYD).
- [x] **400 V vs 800 V:** Higher voltage → faster charging (P = V·I), thinner cables.
- [x] **Thermal management** is critical for SiC MOSFET inverters above 100 kW.
- [x] **Power flow in THS:** $P_{ICE} + P_{M1} = P_{wheels} + P_{M2}$ (energy conservation).
- [x] **Specific power** of EV motors: 3 – 6 kW/kg — far above ICE (≈ 0.5 kW/kg).
- [x] **Final formula to memorize:** $F_t = ma + \mu m g \cos\theta + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Power Systems and Hybrid Drives**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern (NEP 2020 Aligned).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Management System - Battery Types and Its Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Management System - Battery Types and Its Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Battery Management System - Battery Types and Its Maintenance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Battery Management System (BMS) - Formal Definition

A **Battery Management System (BMS)** is an intelligent electronic control unit (ECU) that monitors, regulates, and protects a rechargeable battery pack by continuously supervising critical operational parameters such as cell voltage, pack current, state of charge (SoC), state of health (SoH), and temperature. In automotive applications, the BMS is the indispensable interface between the high-voltage battery pack and the vehicle's power electronics, ensuring safety, longevity, and optimal performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAET634 / Module 3):** The Battery Management System forms the cornerstone of modern Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs). KTU examiners frequently target definitions, functional blocks, and the comparative analysis of battery chemistries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **BMS as the "brain, doctor, and accountant" of the battery pack all rolled into one**:
- **The Brain:** It constantly decides when to charge, discharge, balance cells, and when to shut down.
- **The Doctor:** It monitors the battery's "vitals" (voltage, current, temperature) and intervenes before a critical fault (overcharge, deep discharge, thermal runaway) occurs.
- **The Accountant:** It keeps an exact log of how much energy has gone in and out, so the driver always knows the **State of Charge (SoC)** — essentially the fuel gauge of an EV.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a BMS, a multi-cell lithium-ion pack would be like an orchestra with no conductor — individual cells would charge unevenly, some would overheat, and the entire pack could fail prematurely or, in worst cases, catch fire.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Terminology

The following key metrics form the language of any battery discussion:

- **State of Charge (SoC):** Available capacity expressed as a percentage of rated capacity. SoC = 0% means fully discharged; SoC = 100% means fully charged.
- **State of Health (SoH):** Ratio of current maximum deliverable capacity to the rated capacity, expressed as a percentage. A new battery has SoH ≈ 100%.
- **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** The complement of SoC; the percentage of battery that has been discharged relative to its full capacity.
- **C-Rate:** A normalized measure of current indicating the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its maximum capacity.
- **Nominal Voltage (V):** The characteristic operating voltage of a single cell of a given chemistry.
- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Energy stored per unit mass — critical for EV range.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Energy stored per unit volume — critical for packaging.

### 1.4 The "Why" of a BMS in an Automotive Context

A modern EV battery pack may contain thousands of individual cells connected in series-parallel configuration. Small manufacturing variances mean that no two cells are perfectly identical. Without active management, this leads to **cell imbalance** — some cells reach full charge earlier than others, limiting the usable capacity of the entire pack. The BMS solves this through:

1. **Cell Balancing** (Passive and Active)
2. **Thermal Management Coordination**
3. **Safety Cut-offs** (Over-voltage, Under-voltage, Over-current, Over-temperature)
4. **Communication with Vehicle CAN bus** (publishing SoC, SoH, temperature to the Vehicle Control Unit)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Batteries

Automotive batteries are broadly classified into two families:

### 2.1.1 Lead-Acid Batteries (Conventional SLI Batteries)

The **Starting, Lighting, and Ignition (SLI)** battery has dominated automobiles for over a century. Chemistry: Lead dioxide (PbO₂) positive plate, sponge lead (Pb) negative plate, sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) electrolyte.

- **Nominal Voltage:** 2 V per cell → **12 V battery** has 6 cells.
- **Specific Energy:** 30–40 Wh/kg
- **Cycle Life:** 200–300 cycles at 80% DoD
- **Cost:** Very low (₹₹)
- **Maintenance:** Conventional flooded type needs periodic water top-up; VRLA (Valve Regulated Lead Acid) is sealed and maintenance-free.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flooded vs. VRLA vs. AGM:**
> - **Flooded (Wet Cell):** Liquid electrolyte, vents gases, requires maintenance.
> - **VRLA (Sealed Lead Acid):** Recombines internally generated gases, no maintenance.
> - **AGM (Absorbent Glass Mat):** Electrolyte absorbed in fiberglass mat; superior vibration resistance; used in start-stop vehicles and premium EVs as 12 V auxiliary battery.

### 2.1.2 Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) Batteries

The workhorse of modern EVs. Multiple sub-chemistries exist:

| Chemistry | Nominal Voltage (V) | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Cycle Life | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LFP (LiFePO₄)** | 3.2 | 90–120 | 2000+ | Safest, longest life, used in entry EVs (Tata Nexon EV) |
| **NMC (Li-Ni-Mn-Co-O₂)** | 3.7 | 150–220 | 1000–2000 | High energy density, used in Tesla, Hyundai, MG |
| **NCA (Li-Ni-Co-Al-O₂)** | 3.6 | 200–260 | 500–1000 | Highest energy, used in Tesla Model S/X |
| **LTO (Li₄Ti₅O₁₂)** | 2.3 | 60–80 | 7000+ | Fast charge, ultra-long life, low energy density |

### 2.1.3 Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)

- **Nominal Voltage:** 1.2 V per cell
- **Specific Energy:** 60–120 Wh/kg
- **Cycle Life:** 500–1000
- **Application:** Toyota Prius (early generations), still used in some HEVs due to excellent thermal stability and safety.

### 2.1.4 Emerging Chemistries

- **Solid-State Batteries:** Replace liquid electrolyte with solid ceramic/polymer. Promise >400 Wh/kg and zero thermal runaway.
- **Sodium-Ion (Na-ion):** Cheaper than Li-ion, abundant raw materials; commercial deployment began in 2023–2024 in low-end EVs and two-wheelers.

## 2.2 Functional Architecture of a BMS

The BMS architecture is typically **modular and layered**:

1. **Cell Monitoring Unit (CMU) / Battery Monitoring IC (BMIC):** Mounted directly on the cells; measures individual cell voltage and temperature.
2. **Battery Control Unit (BCU) / Master BMS:** Aggregates data from CMUs, computes SoC/SoH, executes balancing, communicates over CAN.
3. **Power Distribution Unit (PDU):** Contains contactors and pre-charge circuits controlled by the BMS to safely connect/disconnect the high-voltage pack.
4. **Thermal Management Interface:** Controls coolant pumps, fans, or PTC heaters based on cell temperature feedback.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}}$ | C-rate, current, rated capacity | A, Ah | Charge/discharge rate |
| 2 | $E_{stored} = V_{nom} \times C_{rated}$ | Energy stored in a cell | Wh | Pack sizing |
| 3 | $P = V \times I$ | Instantaneous power | W | Power delivery |
| 4 | $t_{discharge} = \dfrac{C_{rated}}{I} = \dfrac{1}{C_{rate}}$ | Time to fully discharge | hours | Range estimation |
| 5 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | State of Charge (Coulomb Counting) | % | Real-time SoC |
| 6 | $SoH = \dfrac{C_{max,current}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of Health | % | Battery degradation |
| 7 | $DoD = 100\% - SoC$ | Depth of Discharge | % | Cycle life estimation |
| 8 | $E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell}$ | Pack energy (series × parallel) | Wh | EV battery sizing |
| 9 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency | % | Round-trip loss |
| 10 | $R_{internal} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$ | Internal resistance (DC) | Ω | Power loss, heat generation |
| 11 | $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{internal}$ | Ohmic heat loss in cell | W | Thermal management |
| 12 | $Q_{gen} = I^2 R_{int} + I \cdot T \cdot \dfrac{dU}{dT}$ | Total heat generation (Bernardi) | W | Thermal model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU pitfall:** The vertical bar `|` symbol is **strictly forbidden** in markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. For example, $E \mid_{t=0} = 0$ is the correct form.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not academic exercises — they directly drive engineering decisions:

- **EV Range Prediction:** $E_{pack}$ divided by the vehicle's Wh/km consumption gives the theoretical range. A Tata Nexon EV with 30.2 kWh pack consumes ~120 Wh/km → range ≈ 250 km.
- **Fast-Charge Feasibility:** The C-rate determines cable and connector sizing. Charging at 2C means the cable must carry twice the rated current.
- **Battery Second-Life:** SoH < 80% marks retirement from EV duty; the pack is then cascaded into stationary storage (solar micro-grids), where capacity matters more than power density.
- **Warranty Engineering:** Cycle life vs. DoD curves (e.g., Arrhenius-based aging models) are used by OEMs to define warranty periods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Battery Pack

**Problem Statement:** An EV uses a pack consisting of $N_s$ cells in series and $N_p$ cells in parallel, with each cell having nominal voltage $V_{cell}$ and capacity $C_{cell}$. Derive the expression for total pack energy.

**Step 1: Understand the topology.**
- Series cells add their voltages: $V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$
- Parallel cells add their capacities: $C_{pack} = N_p \cdot C_{cell}$

**Step 2: Apply the fundamental energy relation.**
For a single cell, the energy stored is the product of voltage and charge:

$$
E_{cell} = V_{cell} \times Q_{cell} = V_{cell} \times C_{cell}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply by total number of cells in the pack.**
The total number of cells is $N_{total} = N_s \times N_p$. Therefore:

$$
E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot E_{cell}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{pack} &= N_s \cdot N_p \cdot (V_{cell} \times C_{cell}) \\
&= (N_s \cdot V_{cell}) \times (N_p \cdot C_{cell}) \\
&= V_{pack} \times C_{pack}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell} \quad \text{[Wh]}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Time from C-Rate

**Problem Statement:** A 50 Ah battery is discharged at a constant current of 100 A. Determine the C-rate and the time to reach full discharge.

**Step 1: Compute the C-rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{rate} &= \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}} \\
&= \dfrac{100 \text{ A}}{50 \text{ Ah}} \\
&= 2 \, C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the discharge time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{discharge} &= \dfrac{C_{rated}}{I} = \dfrac{1}{C_{rate}} \\
&= \dfrac{50 \text{ Ah}}{100 \text{ A}} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ hours} = 30 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Discharging at 2C drains the pack in 30 minutes. This is the principle behind "fast charge at 1C" and "ultra-fast charge at 4C."

## 3.3 Derivation: State of Charge via Coulomb Counting

The **Coulomb Counting** method is the most intuitive SoC estimation technique. It integrates the current flowing in and out of the battery.

**Starting relation:**

$$
SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) + \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 1: Sign convention.**
Charging current is positive ($I > 0$), discharging is negative ($I < 0$).

**Step 2: Apply for a constant current discharge of $I$ amperes over duration $\Delta t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SoC(t_0 + \Delta t) &= SoC(t_0) - \dfrac{I \cdot \Delta t}{C_{rated}} \\
&= SoC(t_0) - \dfrac{I \cdot \Delta t}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical example.**
A 60 Ah battery starts at SoC = 80%. It discharges at 30 A for 1 hour.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta SoC &= \dfrac{30 \text{ A} \times 1 \text{ h}}{60 \text{ Ah}} = 0.5 = 50\% \\
SoC_{final} &= 80\% - 50\% = 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Coulomb counting suffers from **drift** because of measurement error accumulation. Modern BMS uses **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** to fuse coulomb counting with voltage-based estimation for accurate SoC.

## 3.4 Derivation: Internal Resistance and Heat Generation

**Step 1: Internal resistance measurement.**
Apply a current step $\Delta I$ and observe the immediate voltage drop $\Delta V$:

$$
R_{int} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} \quad [\Omega]
$$

**Step 2: Compute heat loss.**

$$
P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{int} \quad [W]
$$

**Step 3: Energy dissipated over time $\Delta t$:**

$$
Q_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{int} \cdot \Delta t \quad [J]
$$

**Step 4: Temperature rise approximation.**
Using the lumped thermal mass model:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{m \cdot c_p}
$$

where $m$ is the cell mass and $c_p$ is the specific heat capacity (Li-ion ≈ 1000 J/kg·K).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why fast-charging at high C-rates requires active liquid cooling — the $I^2 R$ losses scale quadratically with current.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Battery Chemistries for KTU

| Parameter | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | NiMH | LFP | NMC | NCA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal V/cell | 2.0 V | 1.2 V | 3.2 V | 3.7 V | 3.6 V |
| Specific Energy | 35 Wh/kg | 80 Wh/kg | 100 Wh/kg | 180 Wh/kg | 230 Wh/kg |
| Cycle Life | 300 | 800 | 3000 | 1500 | 800 |
| Thermal Runaway Risk | Low | Low | Very Low | Medium | High |
| Cost per kWh | ₹8,000 | ₹15,000 | ₹12,000 | ₹14,000 | ₹16,000 |
| Best Use | 12 V auxiliary | HEV traction | Entry EVs | Mainstream EVs | Performance EVs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a BMS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    packH[High Voltage Battery Pack Ns x Np Cells] --> cmuA[CMU Module 1 Group A]
    packH --> cmuB[CMU Module 2 Group B]
    packH --> cmuC[CMU Module 3 Group C]
    cmuA --> canBus[Isolated CAN Bus]
    cmuB --> canBus
    cmuC --> canBus
    canBus --> bcu[Battery Control Unit BCU Master BMS]
    bcu --> socCalc[SoC and SoH Estimator]
    bcu --> balCtrl[Cell Balancing Controller]
    bcu --> thermCtrl[Thermal Management Controller]
    bcu --> prot[Protection Logic OV UV OC OT]
    bcu --> pduCtrl[PDU and Contactor Driver]
    bcu --> vehCan[Vehicle CAN Bus]
    socCalc --> dash[Driver Dashboard SoC Percent]
    thermCtrl --> coolant[Pumps Fans and Heaters]
    pduCtrl --> contactors[Main Positive and Negative Contactors]
    prot --> shutdown[Emergency Shutdown Signal]
    balCtrl --> balAct[Active or Passive Balancing Circuit]
```

## 4.2 Battery Maintenance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Vehicle in Service Bay] --> inspect[Visual Inspection for Corrosion and Leakage]
    inspect --> terminal[Clean Terminals with Baking Soda Solution]
    terminal --> voltage[Measure Resting Voltage with Multimeter]
    voltage --> voltageCheck{Voltage above 12.4 V?}
    voltageCheck -- No --> chargeSlow[Slow Charge at 0.1C for 12 hours]
    voltageCheck -- Yes --> hydrometer[For Flooded Cells Measure Specific Gravity]
    hydrometer --> sgCheck{SG between 1.265 and 1.299?}
    sgCheck -- No --> topup[Top Up with Distilled Water]
    sgCheck -- Yes --> loadTest[Perform Load Test at 0.5C for 15 seconds]
    topup --> loadTest
    chargeSlow --> loadTest
    loadTest --> loadCheck{Voltage stays above 9.6 V at 25 C?}
    loadCheck -- No --> replace[Replace Battery]
    loadCheck -- Yes --> bmsDiag[Run BMS Diagnostic Scan for SoC SoH and Cell Imbalance]
    bmsDiag --> bmsCheck{Cell Delta Voltage below 50 mV?}
    bmsCheck -- No --> balance[Run Active Cell Balancing Cycle]
    bmsCheck -- Yes --> final[Document and Return Vehicle to Service]
    balance --> final
```

## 4.3 Cell Balancing Strategies Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Passive[Passive Balancing]
        pa[Resistor Discharge] --> pb[Wasted as Heat]
        pb --> pc[Simple and Cheap but Energy Loss]
    end
    subgraph Active[Active Balancing]
        aa[Capacitor or Inductor or DC-DC Transfer] --> ab[Charge Shuttled from High to Low Cell]
        ab --> ac[Efficient but Complex and Costly]
    end
    Passive -- Choose When --> ch1[Cost Sensitive Low Power Packs]
    Active -- Choose When --> ch2[High Energy Packs and Long Range EVs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a block diagram question appears, always label the major subsystems (CMU, BCU, PDU, Thermal) and indicate the data flow with arrows. Always show the **CAN bus** as the communication backbone — this is the most commonly missed element.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]** Define the term **State of Health (SoH)** of a battery. Why is it important in automotive battery management?

**Model Answer:**
State of Health (SoH) is the ratio of the maximum charge that a battery can currently deliver to its rated (nameplate) capacity, expressed as a percentage.

$$
SoH = \dfrac{C_{max,current}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%
$$

**Importance in Automotive BMS:**
- Indicates battery **degradation** and remaining useful life.
- A SoH below **80%** typically triggers retirement from EV service and possible second-life use in stationary storage.
- Used by OEMs to **determine warranty claims** and residual vehicle value.
- Helps the BMS adjust usable capacity and power limits dynamically.

*(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, importance 1)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]** Differentiate between **passive and active cell balancing** techniques in a BMS.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Passive Balancing | Active Balancing |
|---|---|---|
| **Method** | Excess charge in high-SoC cells dissipated as heat via resistor | Charge is shuttled from high to low cells using capacitors, inductors, or DC-DC converters |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Low (wastes energy) | High (>85%) |
| **Complexity** | Simple, cheap, reliable | Complex, costly, more failure modes |
| **Heat Generated** | Significant (needs thermal design) | Minimal |
| **Use Case** | Small packs, cost-sensitive applications | Large EV battery packs (e.g., Tesla, BYD) |

*(3 marks: 1 mark method + 1 mark efficiency + 1 mark use case)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Battery Management System** used in an Electric Vehicle. List the major sub-systems and their functions.

**Model Answer:**

The BMS in an EV is organized as a hierarchical, distributed system with the following major sub-systems:

**1. Cell Monitoring Unit (CMU) / Slave BMS:**
- Mounted directly on each cell module.
- Measures individual cell voltage (accuracy ±1–5 mV), module temperature via NTC thermistors.
- Performs A/D conversion and transmits data over an **isolated SPI or daisy-chain** to the master.

**2. Battery Control Unit (BCU) / Master BMS:**
- The "brain" — aggregates data from all CMUs.
- Computes **State of Charge (SoC)** using coulomb counting and voltage correlation.
- Computes **State of Health (SoH)** through capacity and impedance tracking.
- Executes **cell balancing** decisions.
- Implements **safety cut-offs**.

**3. Power Distribution Unit (PDU):**
- Contains the **main contactors** (high-voltage relays) for positive and negative rails.
- Includes a **pre-charge circuit** (resistor + small relay) to limit inrush current when connecting the pack to the inverter bus capacitor.
- BMS controls contactor closing/opening based on safety interlocks (interlock loop, crash signal, isolation resistance).

**4. Thermal Management Subsystem:**
- Receives cell temperature data from CMUs.
- Controls **coolant pumps, radiator fans, and PTC heaters** to maintain cells in the optimal 15–35 °C window.
- Coordinates with the vehicle's HVAC system for cabin heat extraction in cold climates.

**5. Communication Interface:**
- Publishes BMS data (SoC, SoH, voltage, current, temperature, fault codes) over **CAN 2.0B or CAN-FD** to the VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) and instrument cluster.
- Supports **diagnostic protocols (UDS over CAN)** for service-tool communication.

**6. Protection Logic:**
- Continuously monitors for **over-voltage (OV), under-voltage (UV), over-current (OC), short-circuit, over-temperature (OT), and insulation breakdown**.
- Initiates contactor opening within milliseconds of a fault.

*Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual representation.*

*(7 marks: 1 architecture overview + 1 CMU + 1 BCU + 1 PDU + 1 Thermal + 1 Communication + 1 Protection)*

**(b)** [7 Marks] A battery pack for an EV has the following configuration: **96 cells in series, each cell has a nominal voltage of 3.7 V and capacity of 50 Ah (NMC chemistry).** The vehicle consumes an average of **150 Wh/km**.
- (i) Calculate the total pack energy.
- (ii) Estimate the theoretical range on a full charge.
- (iii) If the pack is charged at 1C, what is the charging current? How long will full charge take assuming it starts at 0% SoC?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Pack Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{pack} &= N_s \times V_{cell} = 96 \times 3.7 = 355.2 \text{ V} \\
C_{pack} &= 50 \text{ Ah (since all cells are in series, capacity does not add)} \\
E_{pack} &= V_{pack} \times C_{pack} \\
&= 355.2 \times 50 \\
&= 17{,}760 \text{ Wh} = 17.76 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical Range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range} &= \dfrac{E_{pack}}{\text{Consumption per km}} \\
&= \dfrac{17{,}760 \text{ Wh}}{150 \text{ Wh/km}} \\
&= 118.4 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Charging Current and Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{charge} &= 1C = 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ A} \\
t_{charge} &= \dfrac{C_{rated}}{I} = \dfrac{50 \text{ Ah}}{50 \text{ A}} = 1 \text{ hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Realistic Note:** In practice, a full 0–100% charge uses a **CC-CV (Constant Current - Constant Voltage)** profile. The last 20% SoC (above 80%) is charged at tapered current and takes much longer. A 0–80% fast charge at 1C is achievable in 48 minutes; the final 20% takes an additional 30+ minutes.

*(7 marks: i) 2 marks, ii) 2 marks, iii) 2 marks, practical note 1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the **four major battery chemistries** used in automotive applications: **Lead-Acid, NiMH, LFP, and NMC.** Base your comparison on at least six parameters.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | NiMH | LFP | NMC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Cell Voltage** | 2.0 V | 1.2 V | 3.2 V | 3.7 V |
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 30–40 | 60–90 | 90–120 | 150–220 |
| **Cycle Life** | 200–500 | 500–1000 | 2000–5000 | 1000–2000 |
| **Safety / Thermal Runaway** | Very Safe | Safe | Safest Li-ion | Moderate |
| **Cost per kWh** | Very Low | Moderate | Low-Moderate | Moderate-High |
| **Operating Temperature Range** | -20 to 50 °C | -20 to 45 °C | -20 to 55 °C | -20 to 45 °C |
| **Self-Discharge per Month** | 4–6% | 20–30% | 1–3% | 1–2% |
| **Memory Effect** | None | Yes (mild) | None | None |
| **Primary Automotive Use** | 12 V SLI / Auxiliary | Early HEV (Toyota Prius) | Entry EVs, e-buses | Mainstream EVs |
| **Example Vehicle** | Any ICE car | Toyota Prius 1st/2nd Gen | Tata Nexon EV (older) | Hyundai Kona, MG ZS EV |

**Conclusion:** NMC offers the best energy density for mainstream EVs, LFP is preferred for safety and longevity in buses and entry EVs, NiMH is legacy in HEVs, and Lead-Acid remains the dominant 12 V auxiliary battery.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per parameter × 6 parameters + 1 mark conclusion)*

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **major battery maintenance procedures** for (i) a conventional flooded lead-acid battery and (ii) a lithium-ion traction battery pack in an EV.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Maintenance of Flooded Lead-Acid Battery:**

1. **Visual Inspection:** Check for cracks, bulging, leaks, and corroded terminals. Clean corrosion with a baking-soda solution and wire brush.

2. **Electrolyte Level Check:** Remove vent caps; ensure electrolyte is 10–15 mm above the plates. Top up with **distilled water only** (never add acid).

3. **Specific Gravity Test:** Use a hydrometer on each cell. Healthy specific gravity at 27 °C is **1.265 (fully charged)**. Variation > 0.050 between cells indicates a bad cell.

4. **Open-Circuit Voltage Test:** Rest the battery for 12 hours; measure voltage.
   - > 12.6 V: Fully charged
   - 12.4 V: ~75% SoC
   - < 12.0 V: Discharged; needs charging

5. **Load Test:** Apply 0.5C load for 15 seconds; voltage should remain above **9.6 V at 25 °C**.

6. **Charging:** Use a smart charger with multi-stage profile (bulk, absorption, float) to prevent sulfation.

7. **Storage:** If idle for > 1 month, recharge periodically or use a trickle/float charger to prevent sulfation.

**(ii) Maintenance of Li-ion EV Traction Pack:**

1. **BMS Health Check:** Run a service-tool diagnostic to read SoH, cell voltage deltas, and fault history (DTCs). A healthy pack has < 30 mV cell-to-cell delta.

2. **Thermal System Inspection:** Check coolant level, pump operation, hose integrity, and radiator cleanliness.

3. **High-Voltage Safety Checks:** Verify **insulation resistance** (> 100 Ω/V) and **interlock loop continuity** before any work.

4. **State of Charge Calibration:** Periodically (every 6 months) allow the pack to perform a full **100% → 0% → 100% cycle** to recalibrate the SoC algorithm.

5. **Cell Balancing:** Run an active balancing cycle if cell delta exceeds 50 mV; replace any cell with > 100 mV deviation or > 5% capacity loss vs. pack average.

6. **Connector and Wiring Inspection:** Check for orange HV cable damage, loose connectors, and signs of arcing or overheating.

7. **Software Updates:** Apply OEM BMS firmware updates to address SoC estimation improvements and bug fixes.

8. **Storage Best Practices:** If the vehicle is stored for > 3 months, keep the pack at **30–50% SoC** in a cool, dry place (10–25 °C). Avoid storing at 100% SoC in hot climates.

*(7 marks: (i) 3.5 marks — 0.5 mark per step × 7; (ii) 3.5 marks — 0.5 mark per step × 7)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Battery & BMS Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the CAN bus:** When asked for a BMS block diagram, students often omit the **CAN bus / communication interface**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in KTU valuation. Always include the data communication path.
> 2. **Mixing up SoC and SoH:** SoC is the *current* charge level (0–100%); SoH is the *aged condition* of the battery. Examiners deduct marks for interchanging these.
> 3. **Ignoring Sign Convention in Coulomb Counting:** Discharging current must be **subtracted** from SoC; charging is **added**. Many students incorrectly add both.
> 4. **Stating "Lead-Acid" without specifying type:** If the question concerns modern vehicles, specify **VRLA / AGM** instead of generic "lead-acid." This shows updated industry awareness.
> 5. **Wrong Series/Parallel Energy Calculation:** In a series string, **voltage adds, capacity does not**. A common error is to multiply $N_s \times C_{cell}$ as the pack capacity, which is wrong.
> 6. **Ignoring Practical CC-CV Charging:** Stating "1C charging takes 1 hour" without mentioning the CV phase and tapering is incomplete and loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **BMS Definition:** Electronic system that monitors, controls, and protects a rechargeable battery pack; mandatory for any multi-cell Li-ion automotive pack.
- **Core BMS Functions:** Sense (V, I, T), Compute (SoC, SoH), Act (balancing, contactors, thermal), Communicate (CAN, diagnostics), Protect (OV, UV, OC, OT).
- **Battery Types for Automotive Use:**
  - **12 V SLI (Lead-Acid / VRLA / AGM):** Auxiliary functions.
  - **NiMH:** Legacy HEV traction (e.g., Toyota Prius Gen 1–3).
  - **LFP:** Safest, longest cycle life; entry EVs and buses (e.g., Tata Nexon EV, BYD e6).
  - **NMC:** Mainstream EV traction (e.g., Hyundai Kona, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3).
  - **NCA:** Highest energy density (Tesla Model S/X).
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $C_{rate} = I / C_{rated}$
  - $E_{pack} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell}$
  - $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \int I \, dt$
  - $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{int}$
- **Cell Balancing:** Passive (resistor, dissipative) vs. Active (capacitive/inductive/DC-DC, shuttling).
- **Charging Profile:** CC-CV (Constant Current → Constant Voltage); fast charge uses higher C-rate but only up to ~80% SoC.
- **Maintenance Highlights:**
  - Lead-acid: distilled water top-up, hydrometer test, terminal cleaning, equalization charge.
  - Li-ion pack: BMS diagnostic, thermal system check, HV safety, periodic full SoC calibration, store at 30–50% SoC.
- **Emerging Trends to Know:** Solid-state batteries, Sodium-ion (Na-ion), cell-to-pack (CTP) and cell-to-chassis (CTC) architectures, wireless BMS (wBMS).
- **Safety First:** Always mention **insulation resistance**, **interlock loop**, and **isolation monitoring** when discussing HV battery service procedures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for BMS Functions — "SENSE → COMPUTE → ACT → COMMUNICATE → PROTECT"** — Use this structure in any descriptive BMS question; it covers every mark allocated by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacity Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity Rating** in automotive electronics is a standardized quantitative measure that expresses the maximum amount of electrical charge an electrochemical energy storage device (primarily the lead-acid starter battery, increasingly the Lithium-ion traction battery) can deliver under a precisely defined set of discharge conditions including current magnitude, terminal voltage cut-off, ambient temperature, and discharge duration.

The **capacity rating** is the single most important specification stamped on any automotive battery, because it directly determines whether the battery can:

- Spin the starter motor reliably (cranking load).
- Sustain the vehicle's accessory loads with the engine off (reserve function).
- Accept repeated charge/discharge cycles in micro-hybrid stop-start systems.

In the SI system, the fundamental unit of capacity is the **Coulomb (C)**, but automotive engineers universally use the derived practical unit **Ampere-hour (Ah)**, where **1 Ah = 3600 C**. A secondary derived unit, the **Watt-hour (Wh)**, is used when the nominal voltage of the cell is folded into the capacity to express *energy* rather than *charge*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 PEAET634 syllabus, capacity rating sits at the intersection of *energy storage systems* and *engine cranking subsystems*. It is a frequent short-answer topic and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-part in Module 3 long-answer questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **water tank** connected to a tap.

- The **size of the tank** is the *capacity* — how much total water (charge) it can hold.
- The **diameter of the outlet pipe** is the *discharge current* — how fast water is drawn out.
- The **pressure at which water becomes unusable** is the *cut-off voltage* — the minimum usable terminal voltage (typically **10.5 V** for a 12 V lead-acid battery).

Now, here is the catch that makes capacity rating non-trivial: **if you open the tap wider (higher current), the tank effectively shrinks.** This is because high discharge currents cause internal losses (heat, concentration polarization, and over-potentials), so the *useful* charge you can extract before hitting the cut-off voltage is **less** than the nameplate value. This real-world shrinkage effect is mathematically captured by **Peukert's Law**, which we will derive in Section 3.

Two more intuitive analogies to lock the idea:

1. **Fuel tank analogy:** A car's fuel tank is rated in litres; an EV battery is rated in kWh. Just as a car's mileage (km/L) changes with driving style, a battery's effective capacity changes with discharge current.
2. **Mobile phone analogy:** A 5000 mAh phone battery cannot deliver 5 A for 1 hour straight — it gets warm, the voltage sags, and you might only get 40 minutes of screen-on time at that load. The "5000 mAh" stamp is a *slow-discharge* rating.

---

## 1.3 Standard Capacity Rating Metrics Used in Automotive Practice

| Rating Acronym | Full Form | Test Condition | What it Tells You |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C₂₀ / C₁₀ / C₅** | Nominal Capacity (20-h / 10-h / 5-h rate) | Discharged to 10.5 V over the rated hours | True "tank size" of the battery |
| **RC** | Reserve Capacity | 25 A constant current at 26.7 °C to 10.5 V | Minutes of accessory support if alternator fails |
| **CCA** | Cold Cranking Amps | 30 s at −17.8 °C to ≥ 7.2 V | Engine-starting ability in winter |
| **CA / MCA** | Cranking Amps / Marine Cranking Amps | 30 s at 0 °C to ≥ 7.2 V | Mild-climate starting |
| **HCA** | Hot Cranking Amps | 30 s at 26.7 °C to ≥ 7.2 V | Tropical-climate starting |
| **PHCA** | Pulse Hot Cranking Amps | Very short pulse | Capacitor-assisted hybrid cranking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Physical Constants & Standards:**
> - **1 Ah = 3600 Coulomb (C)**
> - **Standard reference temperature = 25 °C (77 °F)** for nominal capacity.
> - **CCA reference temperature = −17.8 °C (0 °F)** as per **SAE J537** and **EN 50342**.
> - **Lead-acid cell nominal voltage = 2.0 V**; six cells make a **12 V** automotive battery.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Multiple Capacity Ratings Exist — The Operating Point Problem

A lead-acid battery is an **electrochemical device** whose usable charge depends strongly on four coupled variables:

1. **Discharge current (I)** — higher I → lower usable capacity.
2. **Temperature (T)** — lower T → slower electrochemical kinetics → lower usable capacity and lower cranking voltage.
3. **End-of-discharge voltage (V_cutoff)** — lower cutoff → marginally higher usable capacity but reduced cycle life.
4. **Age / State-of-Health (SoH)** — internal resistance rises with age, lowering effective capacity.

Because a single number cannot capture all four dependencies, the industry evolved **multiple standardized tests**, each fixing three variables and measuring the fourth. The automotive engineer must therefore read the *right* rating for the *right* job.

---

## 2.2 The C-Rate Concept

The **C-rate** normalizes the discharge current against the rated capacity, enabling apples-to-apples comparison across batteries of different sizes.

$$C\text{-rate} \;=\; \frac{I_{discharge}}{C_{nominal}}$$

- A **1C** discharge of a 60 Ah battery means **60 A** for ~1 hour.
- A **0.05C** discharge (the 20-hour rate) means **3 A** for ~20 hours.
- A **C/20** discharge of 60 Ah = **3 A for 20 h = 60 Ah** — this is the *nominal* rating stamped on the battery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 20 hours?** The 20-hour rate was standardized in the 1900s because it approximates the typical overnight discharge of vehicle accessories before the next morning's start. It is *not* the rate at which a starter motor pulls current (that is closer to **3C to 10C**).

---

## 2.3 Reserve Capacity (RC) — Engineering Derivation Hook

Reserve Capacity is defined by **SAE J537** and **BCI (Battery Council International)** as:

> *"The number of minutes a new, fully charged battery at 26.7 °C (80 °F) can deliver a constant current of 25 A and maintain a terminal voltage greater than or equal to 10.5 V."*

RC is a *time* rating, not a charge rating. To convert it to Ah:

$$C_{RC} \;=\; 25 \text{ A} \times \frac{RC \,[\text{min}]}{60}$$

**Typical RC values:** 60 – 120 minutes for a passenger car battery.

---

## 2.4 Cold Cranking Amps (CCA) — The Starting-Load Rating

CCA, defined by **SAE J537**, is:

> *"The discharge load (in amperes) a battery can deliver for 30 seconds at −17.8 °C (0 °F) and maintain a voltage greater than or equal to 7.2 V."*

This is the **engine-starting** rating. A 600 CCA battery can deliver 600 A for 30 s at freezing point and still maintain 7.2 V. Modern direct-injection engines may demand **800 – 1000 CCA**.

---

## 2.5 Energy Density vs. Power Density — The Two Faces of Capacity

For modern EVs and HEVs, two further capacity metrics become critical:

- **Gravimetric Energy Density** $E_g$ in **Wh/kg** — how much *range* per kg of battery.
- **Volumetric Energy Density** $E_v$ in **Wh/L** — how much *range* per litre of pack.
- **Gravimetric Power Density** $P_g$ in **W/kg** — how fast the battery can *deliver* energy (acceleration, regen braking).
- **Volumetric Power Density** $P_v$ in **W/L** — same, per unit volume.

These four metrics form the **Ragone Plot** — a 2D chart on which every battery chemistry occupies a characteristic region. Lead-acid (high power, low energy) sits in one corner; Li-ion (high energy, moderate power) sits in another; supercapacitors (very high power, very low energy) in a third.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q \;=\; I \times t$ | Fundamental definition of capacity (charge = current × time). |
| 2 | $1 \text{ Ah} \;=\; 3600 \text{ C}$ | SI unit conversion. |
| 3 | $C\text{-rate} \;=\; \dfrac{I}{C_n}$ | Normalized discharge rate. |
| 4 | $t_{discharge} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{C\text{-rate}} \times \text{nominal hours}$ | Ideal discharge time at given C-rate. |
| 5 | $C_{RC} \;=\; 25 \text{ A} \times \dfrac{RC \,[\text{min}]}{60}$ | Convert Reserve Capacity minutes to Ah. |
| 6 | $E_{battery} \;=\; V_{nom} \times C_n$ | Energy in Watt-hours from capacity. |
| 7 | $I^n \times t \;=\; \text{constant}$ | **Peukert's Law** (empirical, lead-acid). |
| 8 | $t \;=\; H \times \left(\dfrac{C_n}{I}\right)^{n-1}$ | Peukert's Law solved for time. |
| 9 | $C_{eff} \;=\; C_n \times \left(\dfrac{I}{C_n}\right)^{1-n}$ | Effective capacity at non-rated current. |
| 10 | $E_g \;=\; \dfrac{V \times C_n}{m_{cell}}$ | Gravimetric energy density. |
| 11 | $P_g \;=\; \dfrac{V \times I_{max}}{m_{cell}}$ | Gravimetric power density. |
| 12 | $SoH \;=\; \dfrac{C_{measured}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State-of-Health as % of rated capacity. |
| 13 | $DoD \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{released}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Depth-of-Discharge as % of rated capacity. |
| 14 | $V_{cut,lead\text{-}acid} \;=\; 10.5 \text{ V}$ | Cut-off voltage for 12 V battery. |
| 15 | $V_{cut,Li\text{-}ion} \;=\; 3.0 \text{ V/cell}$ | Cut-off voltage per Li-ion cell. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Every automotive OEM uses these formulas during *battery selection* for a new vehicle platform. The starting system designer sums the cranking current requirement + accessories, applies a temperature de-rating factor, and then sizes the battery with a 1.5–2× safety margin. Wrong capacity selection = warranty failures and stranded customers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Faraday's Law to Ampere-Hour Capacity

The maximum theoretical charge an electrochemical cell can store is given by **Faraday's First Law**:

$$Q \;=\; n \times F \times N$$

**Step-by-step logical narrative:**

- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reactant (for Pb → PbSO₄, n = 2).
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**.
- $N$ = number of moles of active material available.

For an automotive 12 V lead-acid battery with 6 cells, if one cell contains 1.5 moles of available active lead (Pb) at the negative plate, the theoretical charge per cell is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cell} &= n \times F \times N \\[4pt]
         &= 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 1.5 \\[4pt]
         &= 289{,}455 \text{ C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to Ampere-hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{cell} &= \frac{289{,}455}{3600} \text{ Ah} \\[4pt]
         &\approx 80.4 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic explained:** We divide by 3600 because 1 hour = 3600 seconds, and 1 A = 1 C/s. So the *flow* of 289,455 C distributed over 1 hour = 289,455/3600 A flowing for 3600 s.

> The above is the **theoretical** maximum. In practice, only **30 – 50 %** of the active material is utilized before voltage collapses to cutoff, giving a practical 60 Ah battery.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Peukert's Law (Empirical but Indispensable)

**Peukert's Law** was formulated by **W. Peukert in 1897** and remains the workhorse equation for lead-acid capacity derating.

**Empirical form:**

$$I^{n} \times t \;=\; C_{n}$$

**Where:**
- $I$ = constant discharge current.
- $t$ = discharge time to cutoff.
- $C_n$ = capacity constant (units depend on $n$).
- $n$ = **Peukert's exponent**, typically **1.10 – 1.30** for lead-acid, **~1.05** for Li-ion (near-ideal).

---

### 3.2.1 Solving Peukert's Law for Time

Starting from $I^{n} \cdot t = C_n$, solve for $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= C_n \cdot I^{-n} \\[4pt]
  &= C_n \cdot \frac{1}{I^{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now introduce the **rated capacity** $C_R$ at the rated current $I_R$ (so $C_n = I_R^{n} \cdot t_R$, and if the rated time $t_R$ is the number of rated hours, $t_R = C_R / I_R$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= I_R^{n} \cdot t_R \cdot I^{-n} \\[4pt]
  &= t_R \cdot \left(\frac{I_R}{I}\right)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $t_R = C_R / I_R$:

$$
\boxed{\; t \;=\; \frac{C_R}{I} \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_R}\right)^{1-n} \;}
$$

**This is the form engineers actually use.**

---

### 3.2.2 Effective Capacity at a Non-Rated Current

The *effective* capacity $C_{eff}$ delivered at current $I$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{eff} &= I \cdot t \\[4pt]
        &= I \cdot \frac{C_R}{I} \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_R}\right)^{1-n} \\[4pt]
        &= C_R \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_R}\right)^{1-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; C_{eff} \;=\; C_R \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_R}\right)^{1-n} \;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Because $n > 1$, the exponent $(1-n)$ is negative. So if $I > I_R$, $C_{eff} < C_R$. The battery delivers *less* ampere-hours at higher currents.

---

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example (Worked)

**Problem:** A 60 Ah lead-acid battery has a 20-hour rating (so $I_R = 3$ A). If discharged at $I = 30$ A (10× rate) with Peukert exponent $n = 1.25$, what is the effective capacity and discharge time?

**Step 1 — Ratio of currents:**

$$
\frac{I}{I_R} \;=\; \frac{30}{3} \;=\; 10
$$

**Step 2 — Effective capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{eff} &= 60 \cdot (10)^{1 - 1.25} \\[4pt]
        &= 60 \cdot (10)^{-0.25} \\[4pt]
        &= 60 \cdot \frac{1}{10^{0.25}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $10^{0.25}$:**

$$
10^{0.25} \;=\; 10^{1/4} \;=\; \sqrt[4]{10} \;\approx\; 1.7783
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{eff} &= \frac{60}{1.7783} \\[4pt]
        &\approx 33.74 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Discharge time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{C_{eff}}{I} \;=\; \frac{33.74}{30} \\[4pt]
  &\approx 1.125 \text{ hours} \;\approx\; 67.5 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Without Peukert correction, we would naively predict $60/30 = 2$ hours. The actual discharge time is only **~1.1 hours** — almost half! This is why a battery that lights a parking light for days will go flat in an hour when cranking the starter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reserve Capacity ↔ CCA Conversion (Approximate)

A widely used empirical relation (BCI bulletin) between RC and CCA is:

$$
CCA \;\approx\; 14 + (RC \times 16)
$$

**Where** RC is in minutes and CCA is in amperes.

**Derivation logic (heuristic, from BCI test data fits):**

- At 25 A constant discharge, terminal voltage drop rate ≈ 0.2 V/min (slope measured empirically).
- Cutoff voltage is 10.5 V from a fully-charged 12.6 V open-circuit, giving a usable voltage drop of 2.1 V.
- Time to cutoff = $2.1 / 0.2 \approx 10.5$ min baseline at no-load aging.
- For a healthy battery, the additional time per unit of plate area gives the linear RC term.

This is an **approximation**, used mainly for back-of-envelope checks. It is *not* derived from first principles — it is an empirical curve fit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Rating Standards

| Feature | C₂₀ (Nominal) | Reserve Capacity (RC) | CCA | EN 50342 (European) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quantity measured** | Ah | minutes | A | A |
| **Test current** | $C_{20}/20$ | 25 A fixed | varies | 30 % of rating |
| **Test temperature** | 25 °C | 26.7 °C | −17.8 °C | −18 °C |
| **Cutoff voltage** | 10.5 V | 10.5 V | 7.2 V | 7.5 V (after 10 s) |
| **Test duration** | 20 h | until cutoff | 30 s | 10 s + 10 s |
| **Primary use** | Deep-cycle sizing | Emergency accessory | Engine starting | Engine starting |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Automotive Battery Capacity Rating Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[A1_BatteryPack]
    node2[A2_NominalCapacity_C20]
    node3[A3_ReserveCapacity_RC]
    node4[A4_ColdCrankingAmps_CCA]
    node5[A5_PeukertsLaw_Derating]
    node6[A6_EnergyDensity_Whkg]
    node7[A7_PowerDensity_Wkg]
    node8[B1_EngineCrankingLoad]
    node9[B2_AccessoryLoad_25A]
    node10[B3_DeepCycle_Discharge]
    node11[C1_Starter_Motor_Match]
    node12[C2_Emergency_Reserve]
    node13[C3_EV_Range_Estimate]
    node14[D1_OEM_Selection_Decision]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
    node1 --> node6
    node1 --> node7

    node2 --> B3
    node3 --> B2
    node4 --> B1

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3

    node5 --> D1
    node6 --> C3
    node7 --> C1

    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **A-block** represents the *raw capacity rating outputs* stamped on the battery.
- The **B-block** represents the *load profile* the vehicle places on the battery.
- The **C-block** represents the *engineering purpose* served by each rating.
- The **D-block** is the OEM's final *battery selection decision* that integrates all ratings via Peukert derating and energy/power density maps.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Capacity Rating Decision Logic in OEM Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[stepA_DetermineEngineCrankingLoad]
    nodeB[stepB_ApplyTemperatureDerating]
    nodeC[stepC_ComputeCCA_Required]
    nodeD[stepD_ComputeAccessoryLoadHours]
    nodeE[stepE_ComputeRC_Required]
    nodeF[stepF_SelectNominalAh_C20]
    nodeG[stepG_ApplyPeukertDerating]
    nodeH[stepH_FinalBatterySelection]
    nodeI[stepI_ValidatewithBenchTest]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeH

    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH

    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH

    nodeH --> nodeI
```

**Narrative:** The OEM designer (1) computes the worst-case cranking current from starter motor specs, (2) de-rates for the coldest operating environment using an empirical temperature curve, (3) sizes CCA, and *separately* (4) computes accessory drain in Ampere-hours, (5) sizes RC, and (6) sizes nominal Ah, (7) applies Peukert to get the realistic usable Ah. All three sizing paths converge at (8) the battery selection, which is then (9) bench-tested under the OEM's own duty cycle.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Inter-relationships of Capacity Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[P1_Charge_Coulombs]
    p2[P2_Capacity_Ah]
    p3[P3_C_Rate]
    p4[P4_Energy_Wh]
    p5[P5_Peukert_Correction]
    p6[P6_EnergyDensity]
    p7[P7_PowerDensity]
    p8[P8_StateOfHealth]

    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p2 --> p4
    p3 --> p5
    p4 --> p6
    p2 --> p7
    p2 --> p8

    p5 --> p6
    p8 --> p6
    p8 --> p7
```

This map shows the conceptual flow from **raw physical quantity (charge in C)** → **practical capacity (Ah)** → **normalized rate (C-rate)** → **Peukert-corrected usable capacity** → **derived engineering metrics (energy density, power density, SoH)**.

---
