<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Cold Cranking Amps (CCA) and Reserve Capacity (RC) of an automotive battery. State the standard test conditions for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Cold Cranking Amps (CCA):** It is the discharge current, in amperes, that a new fully-charged 12 V battery can deliver for **30 seconds** at a temperature of **−17.8 °C (0 °F)** while maintaining a terminal voltage **≥ 7.2 V**. (2 marks)
- **Reserve Capacity (RC):** It is the time, in minutes, that a new fully-charged battery can deliver a constant current of **25 A** at **26.7 °C (80 °F)** before its terminal voltage drops to **10.5 V**. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is C-rate? Why is the C₂₀ rating commonly used as the nominal capacity of an automotive battery?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **C-rate definition:** The C-rate is the ratio of the discharge current to the rated capacity of the battery. A 1C rate discharges the full rated capacity in 1 hour. (2 marks)
- **Why C₂₀:** The 20-hour rate approximates the typical overnight accessory discharge profile of a passenger car, gives a near-fully-utilized capacity (low Peukert losses), and is the standardized reference rate in **SAE J537** and **BCI** specifications. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Capacity Rating Questions**
> - Students **commonly lose 1 – 2 marks** by quoting CCA test conditions as "0 °C" instead of the correct "**−17.8 °C**". Memorize the exact figure.
> - Another common error: confusing **Reserve Capacity (a *time* rating in minutes)** with **Ampere-hour capacity**. RC is converted to Ah only via $25 \text{ A} \times RC/60$.
> - In Peukert problems, students often forget to state **the assumption of constant current** and lose 1 mark on "Assumptions" line.
> - Always include **units** in the final numerical answer; a dimensionless Ah is **not** accepted.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various capacity rating standards used for automotive batteries, including **Nominal Capacity (C₂₀)**, **Reserve Capacity (RC)**, **Cold Cranking Amps (CCA)**, **Cranking Amps (CA)** and **Hot Cranking Amps (HCA)**. Tabulate the test conditions for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12 V lead-acid automotive battery is rated **60 Ah at the C₂₀ rate**. Using **Peukert's Law** with exponent **n = 1.25**, calculate (i) the effective capacity when discharged at a constant current of **12 A**, and (ii) the discharge time to a 10.5 V cutoff. Comment on the difference between the rated and effective capacities. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Capacity Rating Standards (7 marks):**

| Rating | Test Current | Test Temperature | Cutoff Voltage | Test Duration | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **C₂₀ Nominal** | $C_R / 20$ | 25 °C | 10.5 V | 20 h | Energy tank size |
| **Reserve Capacity** | 25 A | 26.7 °C | 10.5 V | to cutoff | Emergency accessory runtime |
| **CCA** | varies | −17.8 °C | 7.2 V | 30 s | Cold-engine starting |
| **CA** | varies | 0 °C | 7.2 V | 30 s | Mild-climate starting |
| **HCA** | varies | 26.7 °C | 7.2 V | 30 s | Tropical starting |

> The cut-off voltages differ because **engine starting** is a *voltage-limited* application (starter motor needs at least 7.2 V to develop enough torque), while **deep discharge** is a *capacity-limited* application.

(7 marks — table 4 marks + 3 marks explanation of why each rating exists)

---

**Part (b) — Peukert's Law Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Rated capacity $C_R = 60$ Ah
- Rated time $t_R = 20$ h, so rated current $I_R = 60/20 = 3$ A
- Peukert exponent $n = 1.25$
- Discharge current $I = 12$ A

**Step 1 — Compute effective capacity using Peukert:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{eff} &= C_R \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_R}\right)^{1-n} \\[4pt]
        &= 60 \cdot \left(\frac{12}{3}\right)^{1-1.25} \\[4pt]
        &= 60 \cdot (4)^{-0.25}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $4^{-0.25}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4^{0.25} &= \sqrt[4]{4} \;\approx\; 1.4142 \\[4pt]
4^{-0.25} &= \frac{1}{1.4142} \;\approx\; 0.7071
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{eff} &= 60 \times 0.7071 \\[4pt]
        &\approx 42.43 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Effective capacity ≈ 42.4 Ah (3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Compute discharge time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{C_{eff}}{I} \;=\; \frac{42.43}{12} \\[4pt]
  &\approx 3.54 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Discharge time ≈ 3.54 hours (2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Comment (2 marks):**

The rated capacity of 60 Ah is achieved only at the low C/20 rate of 3 A. At 12 A (which is still a modest 0.2C rate), Peukert's Law predicts only ~70.7% of the rated capacity is deliverable, i.e., **17.6 Ah less** than the nameplate. This loss arises from internal resistance, concentration polarization, and acid stratification at higher discharge currents. For accurate sizing, the OEM must always apply Peukert derating rather than rely on the nominal Ah stamp alone.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Define **Energy Density** and **Power Density** in the context of automotive batteries. Explain the significance of the **Ragone Plot** for selecting a battery for a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12 V, **70 Ah** automotive battery has a Reserve Capacity rating of **120 minutes**. Calculate (i) the usable charge in Ah corresponding to the RC rating, (ii) the energy stored in Watt-hours assuming nominal voltage of 12 V, and (iii) the gravimetric energy density in Wh/kg if the battery mass is **18 kg**. Comment on whether this battery is suitable for an EV traction application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Energy & Power Density (7 marks):**

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Gravimetric Energy Density ($E_g$):** Total energy stored per unit mass, in **Wh/kg**.
$$E_g \;=\; \frac{V_{nom} \times C_n}{m_{cell}}$$
- **Gravimetric Power Density ($P_g$):** Maximum deliverable power per unit mass, in **W/kg**.
$$P_g \;=\; \frac{V_{nom} \times I_{max}}{m_{cell}}$$

**Ragone Plot significance (4 marks):**
The **Ragone Plot** is a log-log chart of energy density (Wh/kg) on the x-axis versus power density (W/kg) on the y-axis, on which every energy-storage technology occupies a unique region.

For an **HEV** application, the battery must simultaneously deliver:
- **High energy** for EV-mode cruising (favors Li-ion).
- **High power** for acceleration and regenerative braking pulses (favors supercapacitors and high-power Li-ion).

Lead-acid, despite its high power density at short pulses, has very **low energy density (~30 – 40 Wh/kg)**, which is why modern HEVs universally select **Li-ion or NiMH** chemistries that sit in the upper-right region of the Ragone plot.

---

**Part (b) — RC-to-Energy Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{nom} = 12$ V, $C_n = 70$ Ah, $RC = 120$ min, $m = 18$ kg.

**(i) Usable charge from RC (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{RC} &= 25 \text{ A} \times \frac{120 \text{ min}}{60} \\[4pt]
       &= 25 \times 2 \;=\; 50 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Stored energy (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V_{nom} \times C_n \;=\; 12 \times 70 \\[4pt]
  &= 840 \text{ Wh} \;\approx\; 0.84 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Gravimetric energy density (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \frac{840 \text{ Wh}}{18 \text{ kg}} \\[4pt]
    &\approx 46.7 \text{ Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
An energy density of **~47 Wh/kg** is characteristic of a **lead-acid battery** — perfectly adequate for a 12 V starter/auxiliary role but **far too low for EV traction**. A typical EV needs **150 – 250 Wh/kg** at the cell level, which only Li-ion chemistries can provide. Hence this battery is **not suitable for EV traction**; it is correctly classified as a **SLI (Starting-Lighting-Ignition) battery**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Capacity Rating**

- ☐ **1 Ah = 3600 C** — memorize this conversion; it appears in nearly every numerical.
- ☐ **C-rate = I / C_n** — dimensionless; a battery rated 50 Ah discharged at 50 A is at **1C**.
- ☐ **Nominal capacity (C₂₀)** = discharge over **20 hours** at **25 °C** to **10.5 V**.
- ☐ **Reserve Capacity** = minutes at **25 A**, **26.7 °C**, to **10.5 V**.
- ☐ **CCA** = amperes for **30 s** at **−17.8 °C** maintaining **≥ 7.2 V**.
- ☐ **CA / MCA** = amperes for 30 s at **0 °C** to 7.2 V.
- ☐ **HCA** = amperes for 30 s at **26.7 °C** to 7.2 V.
- ☐ **Peukert's Law:** $I^n \cdot t = \text{constant}$; $n = 1.1 – 1.3$ for lead-acid, $\approx 1.05$ for Li-ion.
- ☐ **Effective capacity:** $C_{eff} = C_R \cdot (I/I_R)^{1-n}$ — always less than $C_R$ at high discharge currents.
- ☐ **Energy density** of lead-acid ≈ **30 – 50 Wh/kg**; Li-ion ≈ **150 – 250 Wh/kg** — a ~5× difference that drives EV battery selection.
- ☐ **Cut-off voltages:** **10.5 V** for 12 V lead-acid; **3.0 V/cell** for Li-ion.
- ☐ Always **apply Peukert derating** when sizing batteries for high-discharge applications; the nominal Ah is *not* the usable Ah.
- ☐ RC and CCA are *not* independent ratings — they are coupled via the BCI empirical relation $CCA \approx 14 + 16 \cdot RC$.
- ☐ In KTU answers, **tabulate** the test conditions (current, temperature, cutoff, duration) — examiners award marks for the structured comparison.
- ☐ Always state the **assumptions** (constant current, steady temperature, new battery) before applying Peukert's Law.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Charging Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Charging Methods

# 🔋 Battery Charging Methods — Automotive Electronics (KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (Module 3 – KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic falls under *Energy Storage & Power Management in Automobiles*, covering charging strategies for SLI (Starting, Lighting, Ignition) batteries used in modern vehicles. Mastery of charging profiles is **mandatory** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) ESE questions.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Battery Charging Method** is defined as the controlled process of restoring electrical energy into a secondary (rechargeable) electrochemical cell by forcing a unidirectional current through it, reversing the discharge chemical reaction, while maintaining voltage, current, and temperature within manufacturer-specified limits to ensure longevity, safety, and full state-of-charge (SOC) recovery.

In an automotive context, the **12 V / 24 V lead-acid battery** is recharged by the vehicle's **alternator (AC generator) + rectifier + voltage regulator** assembly, governed by the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** or **Body Control Module (BCM)** in modern systems.

### 1.2 Key Battery Parameters (Recall)

- **Nominal Voltage:** 12 V (passenger cars), **24 V** (heavy commercial vehicles, defence).
- **Capacity (C):** Rated in **Ampere-hours (Ah)** — typically **35 Ah to 100 Ah** for cars, **100–200 Ah** for trucks.
- **Cold Cranking Amps (CCA):** Current deliverable at **−18 °C** for 30 s while maintaining ≥ **7.2 V**.
- **State of Charge (SOC):** Percentage of usable energy remaining (0 % empty → 100 % full).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Bucket & Tap"

Imagine a **leaky water bucket** (the battery) that loses water (charge) as you drive. Every time the engine runs, a **smart tap (alternator + regulator)** opens to refill it — but you cannot pour water in too fast (overcharge → battery boils) or too slow (undercharge → battery starves). The **charging method** is essentially the *strategy* the tap uses:
- *Constant Current* = tap flows at fixed rate regardless of bucket fullness.
- *Constant Voltage* = tap adjusts flow to keep pressure constant.
- *Trickle Charge* = a tiny drip to compensate for natural leakage.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "C-BAT":** **C**onstant current, **B**ulk, **A**bsorption, **T**rickle — the four classic automotive charging stages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Battery Charging Methods

#### A. By Electrical Profile

| # | Method | Profile | Used In |
|---|--------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Constant Current (CC)** | Current held fixed; voltage rises | Workshops, bench chargers |
| 2 | **Constant Voltage (CV)** | Voltage held fixed; current tapers | Automotive alternators |
| 3 | **Modified Constant Voltage (Taper)** | Voltage rises in steps | Older vehicles, simple chargers |
| 4 | **Float / Trickle** | Low constant voltage compensates self-discharge | Standby vehicles, storage |
| 5 | **Pulse Charging** | Current pulses with rest intervals | Smart chargers, AGM/EFB |
| 6 | **Multi-Stage (IUoU / IUIa)** | Bulk → Absorption → Equalization → Float | Modern ECU-controlled systems |
| 7 | **Fast / Boost Charging** | High current (C/3 to 1C) for short duration | Emergency, hybrid vehicles |

#### B. By On-Vehicle System

- **Alternator-driven charging** (engine running).
- **External / stationary charger** (garage, EV depot).
- **Regenerative braking charging** (HEV/EV — module 3 extension).

---

### 2.2 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

#### (i) Constant Current Charging
- Current **I** is held constant (e.g., **10 A** for a 60 Ah battery → C/6 rate).
- Terminal voltage rises from **~11.5 V** (discharged) to **~14.4 V** (full).
- **Drawback:** Risk of gassing and overheating near full charge.

#### (ii) Constant Voltage Charging
- Voltage clamped at **14.4 V ± 0.2 V** (typical automotive regulation).
- Current is high initially (bulk) → decays exponentially as battery fills.
- **Self-regulating** — naturally tapers off → ideal for SLI batteries.

#### (iii) Modified Constant Voltage (Taper Charging)
- Two-stage: starts at higher voltage (**14.8 V**), drops to **13.8 V** at the end.
- Compromise between CC and CV — used in older cars without electronic regulators.

#### (iv) Multi-Stage (IUIa / IUoU) — The Modern Standard
Modern vehicles use the **IUoU characteristic** (German notation):

1. **I-Stage (Bulk):** Constant current (I_max ≈ C/3) until voltage = 14.4 V.
2. **Uo-Stage (Absorption / Overcharge):** Constant voltage held, current decays to **C/100**.
3. **U-Stage (Float / Trickle):** Voltage lowered to **13.2–13.8 V** to compensate self-discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IUoU dominates:** It minimises gassing, prevents sulphation, extends cycle life by **2–3×**, and is fully compatible with ECU-controlled alternators (LIN/BSS controlled).

#### (v) Pulse Charging
- Pulses of **2–5× C-rate** current with **millisecond rest intervals**.
- Rest period allows **ionic equalisation** in the electrolyte, reducing polarisation.
- Used in **AGM, EFB, and start-stop** batteries.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU ESE. Memorise the units, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\displaystyle t \;=\; \frac{C \cdot D}{I \cdot \eta}$ | Charging time | $t$ in h, $C$ in Ah, $D$ = depth of discharge (0–1), $I$ in A, $\eta$ = efficiency (0.7–0.85) |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \eta_{chg} \;=\; \frac{E_{in}}{E_{out(equiv)}}$ | Coulombic / energy efficiency | Lead-acid ≈ 70–85 % |
| 3 | $\displaystyle V_{term} \;=\; E_{emf} \;+\; I(R_{int} + R_{ext})$ | Kirchhoff's law for charging cell | $E_{emf}$ rises with SOC |
| 4 | $\displaystyle I_{chg} \;=\; \frac{V_{reg} - E_{batt}}{R_{int}}$ | Instantaneous charging current (CV mode) | Exponential decay |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \text{CCA}_{min} \;=\; k \cdot C_{20}$ | Cranking amps vs capacity | $k \approx 6$ for petrol, $7$ for diesel |
| 6 | $\displaystyle P_{heat} \;=\; I^2 R_{int}$ | Heat dissipation in cell | Limit < **0.5 W/cell** |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \text{SOC}(t) \;=\; \text{SOC}_0 \;+\; \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | State of charge evolution | Coulometric integration |
| 8 | $\displaystyle C_{p} \;=\; I^{n} \cdot t \;=\; \text{const}$ | Peukert's equation (capacity loss at high rate) | $n \approx 1.1$–$1.3$ for lead-acid |

> Where:
> - $C$ = rated capacity in **Ah**
> - $D$ = depth of discharge (decimal, e.g., 0.5 = 50 % discharged)
> - $\eta$ = charging efficiency
> - $R_{int}$ = internal resistance (mΩ)
> - $V_{reg}$ = regulated alternator voltage (**14.0–14.8 V**)
> - $k$ = engine-type factor
> - $n$ = Peukert exponent

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Context

- **EV / HEV Context:** Multi-stage charging is the basis for **CC-CV protocols** in Li-ion traction batteries (extended to Module 5).
- **Start-Stop Vehicles:** AGM/EFB batteries require **pulse or IUoU** — simple alternators damage them within months.
- **Smart Alternators (LIN/BSS):** Modern alternators receive torque/SOC feedback from ECU and vary output between **12.6 V (idle) and 15.2 V (recovery)** — this is *load-response charging*.
- **48 V Mild-Hybrid Systems:** DC-DC converter charges the 48 V Li-ion pack from 12 V bus using **CC-CV** profile.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Charging Time under Constant Current

**Given:**
- Battery rated capacity $C = 60 \,\text{Ah}$
- Depth of discharge $D = 0.6$ (60 % discharged)
- Charging current $I = 10 \,\text{A}$ (constant)
- Charging efficiency $\eta = 0.80$

**Find:** Charging time $t$

**Step 1 — Identify the Ah deficit**

The battery must regain the discharged portion. The amount of charge (in Ah) to be restored is:

$$
Q_{needed} \;=\; C \cdot D
$$

**Step 2 — Apply efficiency correction**

Because not every coulomb pumped in is stored (some lost to gassing and heat), the *effective* current contributing to SOC is $I \cdot \eta$:

$$
Q_{stored} \;=\; I \cdot \eta \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve**

For the battery to be fully recharged:

$$
C \cdot D \;=\; I \cdot \eta \cdot t
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{C \cdot D}{I \cdot \eta}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values**

$$
t \;=\; \frac{60 \cdot 0.6}{10 \cdot 0.80}
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{36}{8.0} \;=\; 4.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**Answer:** $t = \mathbf{4.5 \text{ hours}}$ to recharge from 60 % DOD at 10 A with 80 % efficiency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Current Decay under Constant Voltage Mode

**Given:**
- Regulated voltage $V_{reg} = 14.4 \text{ V}$
- Battery EMF (rising) $E(t) = E_0 + (E_f - E_0)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$
- Internal resistance $R_{int} = 0.020 \,\Omega$
- $E_0 = 11.6 \text{ V}$ (discharged), $E_f = 13.2 \text{ V}$ (full), time constant $\tau = 2 \text{ h}$

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop**

$$
V_{reg} \;=\; E(t) \;+\; I(t) \cdot R_{int}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $I(t)$**

$$
I(t) \;=\; \frac{V_{reg} - E(t)}{R_{int}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $E(t)$**

$$
I(t) \;=\; \frac{V_{reg} - E_0 - (E_f - E_0)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})}{R_{int}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = 0$ (start)**

$$
I(0) \;=\; \frac{14.4 - 11.6 - (13.2 - 11.6)(0)}{0.020} \;=\; \frac{2.8}{0.020} \;=\; 140 \text{ A}
$$

(Bulk current — alternator-limited to ~80–140 A in practice.)

**Step 5 — Evaluate as $t \to \infty$ (full charge)**

$$
I(\infty) \;=\; \frac{14.4 - 13.2 - 0}{0.020} \;=\; \frac{1.2}{0.020} \;=\; 60 \text{ A}
$$

> Wait — this is **physically wrong**: at full charge current should → 0. The error is that $V_{reg} - E_f = 1.2 \text{ V}$ would still drive current. The fix: $V_{reg}$ must equal $E_f$ at end. In reality, **once $E$ reaches ~14.4 V, gassing sustains voltage and $I$ → 0** through polarisation. The model is a *first-order approximation only*; refined models include Butler-Volmer kinetics.

**Corrected terminal current (with gassing threshold $E_g = 14.4 \text{ V}$):**

$$
I_{term} \;=\; \frac{V_{reg} - E_g}{R_{int}} \;\to\; 0
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | Constant Current | Constant Voltage | Pulse Charging | Multi-Stage (IUoU) |
|-----------|:----------------:|:----------------:|:--------------:|:------------------:|
| Hardware complexity | Low | Medium | High (micro) | High (ECU) |
| Charging time | Long | Medium | **Short** | **Short** |
| Gassing risk | **High** | Low | Very low | Very low |
| Battery life impact | Reduces | Neutral | **Extends** | **Extends** |
| SOC at end | Hard to detect | Self-tapering | Precise | Precise |
| Automotive use | Workshop only | **Standard alternator** | AGM/EFB | **Modern smart alternators** |
| Temperature sensitivity | High | Medium | Low | Low (sensor-controlled) |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Modern Multi-Stage Charging Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    alt[Three-Phase Alternator] --> rect[Three-Phase Full-Wave Rectifier Bridge]
    rect --> reg[Voltage Regulator LIN / BSS Controlled]
    reg --> ecu[Engine Control Unit ECU / BCM]
    ecu -->|SOC Demand Signal| reg
    reg --> bat[12 V SLI Battery]
    bat -->|Voltage Temp Current| bms[Battery Sensor Module IBS]
    bms -->|LIN CAN Bus| ecu
    bat --> load1[Lighting Load]
    bat --> load2[Ignition Load]
    bat --> load3[ECU Infotainment Loads]
    reg -.->|Stage Control| stage1[Bulk Stage I]
    reg -.->|Stage Control| stage2[Absorption Stage Uo]
    reg -.->|Stage Control| stage3[Float Stage U]
    reg -.->|Stage Control| stage4[Equalisation Optional]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Charging Method Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Vehicle Ignition ON]) --> check{SOC Check via IBS Sensor}
    check -->|SOC less than 80 percent| bulk[Bulk Stage: Constant Current I max]
    check -->|SOC between 80 and 95 percent| absorb[Absorption Stage: Constant Voltage 14.4 V]
    check -->|SOC greater than 95 percent| float[Float Stage: 13.2 to 13.8 V]
    bulk --> vreach{Voltage Reached 14.4 V}
    vreach -->|Yes| absorb
    vreach -->|No| bulk
    absorb --> idecay{Current Decayed to C over 100}
    idecay -->|Yes| float
    idecay -->|No| absorb
    float --> ready([Battery Ready Steady State])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Charging Method Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root[Battery Charging Methods] --> A1[Constant Current]
    root --> A2[Constant Voltage]
    root --> A3[Taper Modified CV]
    root --> A4[Pulse Charging]
    root --> A5[Multi Stage IUoU]
    root --> A6[Float Trickle]
    A5 --> S1[Bulk I]
    A5 --> S2[Absorption Uo]
    A5 --> S3[Float U]
    A2 --> auto[Standard Automotive Alternator]
    A5 --> smart[Smart ECU Controlled]
    A4 --> agm[AGM EFB Start Stop]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**Define "State of Charge (SOC)" of an automotive battery. Mention its importance in modern charging systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> SOC is the ratio of the remaining capacity of a battery to its rated capacity, expressed as a percentage.  
> $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{C_{remaining}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\,\%$  
> **Importance:** Modern vehicles use SOC feedback to the ECU to control smart alternators, enabling fuel economy improvement (1.5–3 %), CO₂ reduction, and preventing battery overcharge/undercharge. It also enables **stop-start** and **regenerative braking** functions (1 mark each for definition + 2 × applications).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**What is the difference between "Constant Current" and "Constant Voltage" charging methods?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Constant Current | Constant Voltage |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Controlled parameter | Current held fixed | Voltage held fixed |
| Battery response | Voltage rises with time | Current decays with time |
| Gassing risk | High at end of charge | Low (self-tapering) |
| Use case | Workshop bench charging | On-vehicle alternator charging |
| Termination | Time-based / voltage cutoff | Current threshold cutoff |

(1 mark definition each + 1 mark for proper distinction.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **IUoU multi-stage charging method** used in modern automotive alternators with a neat block diagram. Discuss why it is preferred over simple constant-voltage charging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **60 Ah, 12 V** lead-acid battery is discharged to **40 % SOC**. It is to be recharged at the **C/6 rate** using constant current. Charging efficiency is **75 %**. Calculate:  
  (i) Charging current  
  (ii) Time required for full recharge  
  (iii) Energy lost as heat, if internal resistance is **20 mΩ** (average). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**  
IUoU is a multi-stage charging profile consisting of **I-stage (constant current bulk)**, **Uo-stage (constant voltage absorption)**, and **U-stage (float)**.

**2. Three Stages Explained (3 marks):**

- **I-Stage (Bulk):** Alternator delivers maximum allowable current (limited to ~80 % of rated). Terminal voltage rises from ~12.0 V (discharged) to 14.4 V. SOC reaches ~70–80 %.
- **Uo-Stage (Absorption):** Voltage held at **14.4 V ± 0.1 V**. Current decays exponentially as electrochemical equilibrium establishes. SOC → ~95 %.
- **U-Stage (Float):** Voltage reduced to **13.2–13.8 V**. Compensates only self-discharge. Maintains SOC at 100 %.

**3. Block Diagram (2 marks):**  
*(Refer Section 4.1 of these notes — Mermaid diagram.)*

**4. Advantages over simple CV (1 mark):**
- Prevents prolonged gassing at absorption voltage.
- Extends battery cycle life by 2–3×.
- Compatible with ECU-controlled smart alternators using **LIN/BSS** bus.
- Enables **load-response** charging (alternator output varies with engine load).

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $C = 60\,\text{Ah}$, $V = 12\,\text{V}$, $\text{SOC}_0 = 40\,\%$, $D = 0.60$, $\eta = 0.75$, $R_{int} = 20 \text{ m}\Omega = 0.020 \,\Omega$, rate = C/6.

**(i) Charging current (1 mark):**

$$
I \;=\; \frac{C}{6} \;=\; \frac{60}{6} \;=\; \mathbf{10 \text{ A}}
$$

**(ii) Charging time (3 marks):**

$$
t \;=\; \frac{C \cdot D}{I \cdot \eta} \;=\; \frac{60 \times 0.60}{10 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{36}{7.5} \;=\; \mathbf{4.8 \text{ hours}}
$$

**(iii) Energy lost as heat (3 marks):**

Heat dissipated per unit time:

$$
P_{heat} \;=\; I^2 R_{int} \;=\; (10)^2 \times 0.020 \;=\; 2.0 \text{ W}
$$

Total energy lost:

$$
E_{heat} \;=\; P_{heat} \times t \;=\; 2.0 \times 4.8 \;=\; \mathbf{9.6 \text{ Wh}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With necessary diagrams, describe the operation of a **pulse charging system** for automotive batteries. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with constant current and constant voltage methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **charging time equation** for a lead-acid battery under constant current conditions. A **100 Ah** battery is **80 % discharged** and is charged at **20 A** with **85 % efficiency**. Find the time required and the **ampere-hours returned** to the battery. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Concept (2 marks):**  
Pulse charging delivers current in short pulses (typically 1–3 seconds ON, 1–2 seconds OFF) with controlled amplitude (2C–5C). The rest period allows **ionic diffusion** to equalise electrolyte concentration, reducing **concentration polarisation**.

**2. Working (2 marks):**  
A microcontroller generates PWM control of a MOSFET/IGBT switch. Current sensor (Hall-effect or shunt) provides feedback. Battery voltage is monitored; when it reaches gassing threshold, charging terminates.

**3. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pulse | Constant Current | Constant Voltage |
|-----------|:-----:|:----------------:|:----------------:|
| Charging speed | **Fastest** | Slow | Medium |
| Polarisation control | **Excellent** | Poor | Moderate |
| Gassing | **Minimal** | High | Low |
| Cycle life extension | **Yes (2–3×)** | No | Marginal |
| Complexity | **High** | Low | Medium |
| Cost | **High** | Low | Medium |

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Charge required to restore = $C \times D$ Ah

Effective charge delivered per hour = $I \times \eta$ Ah/h

For full recharge:

$$
C \cdot D \;=\; I \cdot \eta \cdot t
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t \;=\; \frac{C \cdot D}{I \cdot \eta}\,}
$$

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

$$
t \;=\; \frac{100 \times 0.80}{20 \times 0.85} \;=\; \frac{80}{17} \;=\; \mathbf{4.71 \text{ hours}}
$$

Ampere-hours returned to the battery:

$$
Q_{returned} \;=\; I \cdot \eta \cdot t \;=\; 20 \times 0.85 \times 4.71 \;=\; \mathbf{80.07 \text{ Ah}}
$$

(Equivalent to the 80 Ah deficit — confirms conservation of charge.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency** in the charging time formula → loses 1–2 marks immediately. Always multiply denominator by $\eta$.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing minutes and hours in $t$ calculation. **Always express $C$ in Ah and $t$ in hours.**
> 3. **Wrong gassing voltage:** SLI lead-acid gassing starts at **14.4 V** — exceeding this by > 0.2 V causes water loss and plate damage.
> 4. **Confusing "Float" with "Trickle":** Float maintains 100 % SOC at low constant voltage; trickle is a *continuous small current* — different mechanisms.
> 5. **Forgetting the block diagram** in Part B (a) — KTU mandates a neat labelled diagram. Draw **alternator → rectifier → regulator → battery → ECU** with arrows.
> 6. **Not stating CCA relation** in long answers when capacity is mentioned. Examiners award bonus marks for cross-topic integration.

---

### 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Battery charging** = restoring electrical energy by forcing controlled DC into a secondary cell.
- ✅ **Three classic methods:** Constant Current, Constant Voltage, Modified CV (Taper).
- ✅ **Modern automotive standard = IUoU multi-stage** (Bulk → Absorption → Float).
- ✅ **Key formula:** $\displaystyle t = \frac{C \cdot D}{I \cdot \eta}$ — **always include efficiency.**
- ✅ **Alternator regulation voltage = 14.0–14.8 V** (12 V system); 28–29 V for 24 V trucks.
- ✅ **Gassing threshold of lead-acid = 14.4 V** — exceeding it permanently damages the cell.
- ✅ **Pulse charging** is preferred for **AGM, EFB, and start-stop** batteries.
- ✅ **Smart alternators** use **LIN or BSS bus** to receive SOC feedback from **IBS (Intelligent Battery Sensor)**.
- ✅ **CCA** relates to capacity: $CCA \approx 6C$ (petrol), $7C$ (diesel).
- ✅ **Regenerative braking** (HEV/EV) is a charging method with high-current short pulses.
- ✅ **Charging efficiency** of lead-acid = **70–85 %**; rest lost to heat ($I^2R$) and gas evolution.
- ✅ **State of Charge (SOC)** is computed via **coulomb counting** integrated over time: $\text{SOC} = \text{SOC}_0 + \frac{1}{C}\int I\,dt$.
- ✅ **Internal resistance** of a healthy 12 V battery = **4–20 mΩ**; rises with age and sulphation.
- ✅ **Float voltage** for standby/maintenance = **13.2–13.8 V** for 12 V systems.
- ✅ **Peukert's equation** explains why capacity shrinks at high discharge/charge rates ($n = 1.1$–$1.3$).
- ✅ Always draw a **neat block diagram** in Part B answers — it is the single highest ROI step for marks.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Battery Charging Methods | PEAET634 | KTU 2024 Scheme** 🚗⚡
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Electronics (PEAET634)**, **Battery Tests** refer to a systematic suite of diagnostic procedures performed on the vehicle's energy storage unit (predominantly the **12V SLI — Starting, Lighting, and Ignition Lead-Acid Battery**) to evaluate its **State of Charge (SOC)**, **State of Health (SOH)**, **Cranking Performance**, **Internal Resistance**, and ability to sustain onboard electronic loads.

These tests are classified under the broader domain of **automotive electrical system diagnostics** and are essential prerequisites for fault isolation in the **charging system (alternator + voltage regulator)** and **cranking system (starter motor + solenoid)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PEAET634 covers the *Electrical and Electronic Systems in Automobiles* with specific focus on **Battery Construction, Battery Rating (CCA, RC, AH), and Battery Testing Procedures.** Students must be able to describe, perform, and interpret standard battery tests.

## 1.2 Classification of Automotive Battery Tests

The tests are broadly grouped into the following categories:

| Test Category | Purpose | Performed When |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Load Tests** | Static condition diagnostics | Battery at rest (no load) |
| **Load Tests** | Performance under simulated cranking | Engine off, tester applied |
| **Dynamic Tests** | Real-time charging system response | Engine running |
| **Destructive Tests** | Long-term capacity verification | Laboratory / service center |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank and Pump" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (For First-Time Learners):**
> Imagine the car battery is a **water tank**, the alternator is a **water pump** filling it, and the starter motor is a **fire hydrant** that suddenly demands a huge burst of water.
>
> - **Voltage (V)** is the *water pressure*.
> - **Current (A)** is the *flow rate*.
> - **Capacity (Ah)** is the *total water volume*.
> - **Internal Resistance (Rᵢ)** is *pipe friction* — high friction means the tank cannot deliver water fast enough.
>
> **Battery tests are like a doctor's health checkup** — measuring pressure, flow, volume, and pipe condition to predict whether the patient (battery) can survive a sudden emergency (engine cranking).

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Nominal Battery Voltage (Vₙ):** **12.6 V** (6 cells × 2.1 V/cell) for a fully charged SLI battery at 26.7 °C.
- **Electrolyte Specific Gravity (SG) of fully charged cell:** **1.265 to 1.285** at 26.7 °C.
- **Freezing Point of 1.265 SG electrolyte:** **−57 °C** (a key safety standard).
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** **26.7 °C (80 °F)** — all battery ratings are normalized to this.

## 1.5 List of Standard Battery Tests (KTU Module 3 Syllabus)

1. **Visual / Mechanical Inspection**
2. **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) Test**
3. **Specific Gravity (Hydrometer) Test**
4. **High-Rate Discharge (Load) Test**
5. **Capacity Test (Reserve Capacity & Ampere-Hour Test)**
6. **Cranking Voltage Test**
7. **Three-Minute Charge Test**
8. **Charging System Voltage Test**
9. **Parasitic Drain (Leakage) Test**
10. **Insulation / Earth Leakage Test**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Test 1 — Visual / Mechanical Inspection

Before any electrical test, a **preliminary physical inspection** is mandatory.

**Checks Performed:**
- **Case Condition:** Cracks, bulges, leaks — indicate overcharging, freezing damage, or physical impact.
- **Terminal Condition:** Corrosion (white/blue-green deposits of lead sulfate), looseness, or sulfation crust.
- **Electrolyte Level:** Should be **10 mm above the plate tops**. Top up with distilled water only (never sulfuric acid for topping up).
- **Vent Plug Condition:** Must be clean and unclogged.
- **Hold-Down Clamps:** Loose batteries vibrate, causing internal plate damage and short circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A bulging case indicates **thermal runaway** — the battery has generated gas (H₂ and O₂) faster than the vents can release it, often due to overcharging above **14.8 V**.

---

## 2.2 Test 2 — Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) Test

### Theory

When a battery is disconnected from all loads for a **stabilization period of at least 10 minutes (ideally 24 hours)**, the terminal voltage reaches a stable value called the **Open-Circuit Voltage (V_OC)**.

The OCV is directly related to the **Specific Gravity (SG)** of the electrolyte by the **Nernst Equation**, simplified for automotive use as:

$$V_{cell} = SG + 0.84$$

For a **12 V battery with 6 cells**, the terminal voltage is:

$$V_{OC} = 6 \times (SG + 0.84)$$

### Step-by-Step Procedure
1. Ensure the engine and all accessories are **OFF** for at least 10 minutes.
2. Disconnect the **negative (−) terminal first**, then positive (+).
3. Measure terminal voltage using a **digital multimeter** (DMM) across + and − posts.
4. Read the stabilized value and compare with the SOC table.

### Interpretation Table (KTU Reference Data)

| Open-Circuit Voltage (V) | Specific Gravity | State of Charge (%) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **12.65 – 12.75** | 1.265 | **100 %** | Fully charged — OK |
| **12.45** | 1.225 | **75 %** | Good — Recharge advised |
| **12.24** | 1.190 | **50 %** | Marginal — Recharge |
| **12.06** | 1.155 | **25 %** | Poor — Recharge + Load test |
| **11.89 or below** | 1.120 | **0 %** (Discharged) | Replace or deep charge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Correction:** For every **10 °C drop** below 26.7 °C, subtract **0.07 V** from the OCV reading. For every **10 °C rise** above 26.7 °C, add **0.07 V**.

---

## 2.3 Test 3 — Specific Gravity (Hydrometer) Test

### Theory

The **specific gravity** of the electrolyte (a dilute sulfuric acid solution) directly indicates the strength of the acid and thus the SOC. As the battery discharges, sulfuric acid combines with lead plates to form lead sulfate, reducing the SG.

A **hydrometer** (a float-type density meter) is used. The float is calibrated to read SG directly.

### Key Formulas

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{electrolyte}}{\rho_{water}}$$

Where $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 4 °C.

**Temperature-Corrected Specific Gravity:**

$$SG_{corrected} = SG_{measured} + 0.0007 \times (T_{electrolyte} - 26.7)$$

### Interpretation
- **1.265 – 1.285:** Fully charged
- **1.215 – 1.235:** 50 % discharged
- **Below 1.150:** Fully discharged / defective

> [!WARNING]
> **Cell Variation Rule:** The maximum permissible SG difference between any two cells of a healthy battery is **0.050**. A greater variation indicates a **defective cell** (sulfated, shorted, or aged).

---

## 2.4 Test 4 — High-Rate Discharge (Load) Test

### Theory

The **High-Rate Discharge Test** simulates the cranking load applied by the starter motor. It is the **most important battery test** for evaluating the battery's ability to deliver high current.

The battery is discharged at a current equal to **half the Cold Cranking Amps (CCA) rating** (or **3 × the ampere-hour rating**, whichever is higher) for **15 seconds**, at a temperature of **21 °C (70 °F)**.

### Pass / Fail Criteria

| Terminal Voltage at End of 15 s (at 21 °C) | Battery Condition |
|---|---|
| **≥ 9.6 V** | **GOOD** |
| **9.4 – 9.6 V** | Marginal — Recharge and retest |
| **< 9.4 V** | **FAILED** — Replace battery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Correction for Load Test:**
> - Below 21 °C: subtract **0.04 V per 10 °C** drop.
> - Above 21 °C: add **0.04 V per 10 °C** rise.

---

## 2.5 Test 5 — Capacity Test (Ampere-Hour & Reserve Capacity)

### Theory

**Capacity (C)** is the total charge a fully charged battery can deliver at a specified discharge rate before its terminal voltage drops below a cutoff threshold (**10.5 V** for a 12 V battery).

$$C = I_{discharge} \times t_{discharge}$$

Where $C$ is in **Ampere-Hours (Ah)**, $I_{discharge}$ is the constant current in Amperes, and $t$ is the discharge duration in hours.

**Reserve Capacity (RC)** is defined as the number of minutes a fully charged battery at **26.7 °C** can deliver **25 A** until its terminal voltage falls below **10.5 V**.

**Energy Delivered:**

$$E = V_{avg} \times I \times t = V_{avg} \times C \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

---

## 2.6 Test 6 — Cranking Voltage Test (In-Vehicle Test)

The cranking voltage test is performed **with the battery connected in the vehicle**:

1. Connect a voltmeter across battery terminals.
2. Crank the engine for **15 seconds**.
3. Observe the **minimum cranking voltage**.

| Reading | Diagnosis |
|---|---|
| **≥ 9.6 V** | Cranking system OK |
| **< 9.6 V** | Weak battery or high-resistance connections |
| **> 10 V with slow crank** | High internal resistance — replace battery |

---

## 2.7 Test 7 — Three-Minute Charge Test

Performed when OCV is below **12.4 V** to identify deeply discharged vs. sulfated batteries.

1. Charge at **40 A** for 3 minutes.
2. Measure voltage at the end of 3 minutes.

| Voltage Reading | Diagnosis |
|---|---|
| **≥ 15.5 V** | Sulfated battery — Replace |
| **< 15.5 V** | Recoverable — Continue slow charge |

---

## 2.8 Test 8 — Charging System Voltage Test

With engine running at **2000 rpm** and accessories OFF, the **alternator output voltage** must be:

$$V_{charging} = 13.8 \text{ to } 14.8 \text{ V}$$

- **< 13.8 V:** Undercharging (weak alternator / regulator).
- **> 14.8 V:** Overcharging (failed regulator / faulty ECU command).

---

## 2.9 Test 9 — Parasitic Drain (Leakage) Test

Modern vehicles draw **20 – 50 mA** continuously to preserve ECU memory, clock, and alarm. Measure with an **ammeter in series** with the negative terminal.

$$I_{parasitic} \leq 50 \text{ mA (typical)}$$

> **Exceeding 100 mA indicates a parasitic drain fault** (e.g., stuck relay, faulty ECU, aftermarket accessory).

---

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{cell} = SG + 0.84$ | $V_{cell}$ in V, SG dimensionless | Cell EMF from hydrometer reading |
| 2 | $V_{OC} = 6 \times (SG + 0.84)$ | $V_{OC}$ in V | 12 V battery OCV |
| 3 | $SG_{corrected} = SG_{meas} + 0.0007 \times (T - 26.7)$ | $T$ in °C | Temperature-corrected SG |
| 4 | $C = I \times t$ | $C$ in Ah, $I$ in A, $t$ in h | Battery capacity |
| 5 | $E = V_{avg} \times I \times t$ | $E$ in Wh | Energy delivered |
| 6 | $I_{load\,test} = 0.5 \times CCA$ | $I$ in A | Load test current |
| 7 | $V_{min\,load} = 9.6 \text{ V (at 21 °C)}$ | V | Pass threshold |
| 8 | $P_{cranking} = I_{crank} \times V_{crank}$ | P in W | Starter power draw |
| 9 | $R_{internal} = \dfrac{V_{OC} - V_{load}}{I_{load}}$ | R in Ω | Internal resistance |
| 10 | $I_{parasitic} \leq 50 \text{ mA}$ | I in mA | Acceptable key-off drain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin **OBD-II Battery Management System (BMS) algorithms** in modern vehicles. The BMS estimates SOC using the **Nernst equation** integrated with **Coulomb counting** ($SOC = SOC_0 - \int I\,dt / C_{nom}$). Accurate battery tests at the workshop ensure **predictive maintenance** and **prevent roadside breakdowns**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Nernst Equation to $V_{cell} = SG + 0.84$

The Nernst equation for the lead-acid cell electrochemical reaction is:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left( \frac{a_{H^+}^2 \cdot a_{SO_4^{2-}}}{a_{H_2O}} \right)$$

For a lead-acid cell:
- $n = 2$ electrons transferred per mole,
- $E^0 = 1.93$ V (standard cell potential for Pb | PbSO₄ | H₂SO₄ | PbO₂ | Pb),
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K),
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

The activity of $H^+$ and $SO_4^{2-}$ ions is related to the **molarity** of sulfuric acid, which in turn relates to **specific gravity** through the empirical calibration:

$$m_{H_2SO_4} \approx 4.85 \times SG - 4.55 \quad [\text{mol/kg}]$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Nernst equation** at standard temperature (T = 298 K):

$$E = 1.93 + \frac{0.0257}{2} \ln(a_{H^+}^2 \cdot a_{SO_4^{2-}})$$

**Step 2 — Use the empirical relationship** that the logarithmic activity term in a lead-acid battery reduces approximately to:

$$\frac{0.0257}{2} \ln(a_{H^+}^2 \cdot a_{SO_4^{2-}}) \approx SG \cdot 0.84 - 0.84 + 0.84$$

This simplifies to the **engineering approximation** (widely used in KTU syllabus):

$$E = SG + 0.84 \quad [\text{V per cell}]$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 6 for a 12 V battery:**

$$\boxed{V_{OC} = 6 \times (SG + 0.84)}$$

This derivation confirms the academic basis of the OCV test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Internal Resistance from Load Test

**Given:**
- Open-circuit voltage: $V_{OC}$
- Loaded voltage at end of test: $V_L$
- Applied load current: $I_L$

**Using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** for the simplified battery model (EMF + internal resistance):

$$V_{OC} = V_L + I_L \cdot R_{int}$$

**Solving for $R_{int}$:**

$$R_{int} = \frac{V_{OC} - V_L}{I_L}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):**

> A 12 V, 60 Ah battery has OCV = 12.6 V. During a load test at 300 A, the terminal voltage drops to 9.6 V. Calculate the internal resistance.

**Solution:**

$$R_{int} = \frac{12.6 - 9.6}{300}$$

$$R_{int} = \frac{3.0}{300}$$

$$\boxed{R_{int} = 0.01 \, \Omega = 10 \, \text{m}\Omega}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Healthy battery:** $R_{int} < 5$ mΩ. **Aged battery:** $R_{int} > 20$ mΩ. High internal resistance causes **voltage sag** during cranking, leading to dim headlights and ECU resets.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacity Remaining (Peukert's Law — Advanced)

For high discharge rates, the deliverable capacity is reduced due to **diffusion losses**. **Peukert's Law** captures this:

$$C_p = I^n \cdot t$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = Peukert capacity (constant for a given battery),
- $I$ = discharge current (A),
- $t$ = discharge time (h),
- $n$ = **Peukert exponent** (≈ 1.1 – 1.3 for SLI batteries).

**Deriving the Effective Capacity at a given discharge current:**

$$C_{eff} = C_{rated} \times \left( \frac{I_{rated}}{I} \right)^{n-1}$$

**Example:** A 60 Ah battery rated at the 20-hour rate (3 A) has Peukert exponent $n = 1.2$. Find the capacity when discharged at 30 A (starter-like current).

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$C_{eff} = 60 \times \left( \frac{3}{30} \right)^{1.2 - 1}$$

$$C_{eff} = 60 \times (0.1)^{0.2}$$

$$C_{eff} = 60 \times 0.631$$

$$\boxed{C_{eff} \approx 37.9 \, \text{Ah}}$$

This explains why batteries deliver **less usable capacity at high discharge rates** — critical in cranking analysis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Charging System Voltage Drop Analysis

For a complete charging circuit: **Alternator → Positive Cable → Battery + → Battery − → Negative Cable → Chassis → Alternator ground**.

The total voltage drop is:

$$V_{drop} = I_{alt} \times (R_{cable+} + R_{cable-} + R_{contacts})$$

A healthy 12 V system with 14.0 V alternator output should show:

$$V_{battery} = V_{alt} - V_{drop}$$

**Acceptable drop:** $\leq 0.5$ V total (i.e., $V_{battery} \geq 13.5$ V).

**Worked Example:**

> Alternator output: 14.2 V. Positive cable drop: 0.15 V. Negative cable drop: 0.10 V. Contact resistance drop: 0.05 V. Find battery terminal voltage.

$$V_{drop} = 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.30 \, \text{V}$$

$$V_{battery} = 14.2 - 0.30 = 13.9 \, \text{V} \quad (\text{Acceptable})$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Test

| Symptom / Condition | Recommended Test Sequence | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Engine slow to crank, dim lights | OCV → Load Test → Cranking Voltage | $V_L < 9.6$ V = weak battery |
| Battery flat after overnight parking | Parasitic Drain Test → OCV | $I_{para} > 50$ mA = drain fault |
| Vehicle won't start, full charge suspected | Three-Minute Charge Test | $V > 15.5$ V = sulfated |
| Electrolyte level dropping frequently | Charging System Voltage Test | $V > 14.8$ V = overcharge |
| Car works but battery "dies" in winter | SG Test + OCV at low temp | SG < 1.215 = weak |
| Engine warning light + battery symbol | Alternator Ripple Test + OCV | Ripple > 0.5 V = diode failure |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Pattern

> **Question:** A 12 V, 40 Ah automotive battery is tested. The OCV is 12.30 V. During a 5-second cranking attempt, the voltage drops to 9.4 V at 250 A. The specific gravity is 1.225 at 30 °C. Determine: (a) State of Charge, (b) Internal Resistance, (c) Cranking Power, (d) Health verdict.

**Solution:**

**(a) SOC from OCV:**

$$SOC = \frac{12.30 - 11.89}{12.65 - 11.89} \times 100 = \frac{0.41}{0.76} \times 100$$

$$SOC \approx 53.9\%$$

**(b) Internal Resistance:**

$$R_{int} = \frac{12.30 - 9.40}{250} = \frac{2.90}{250}$$

$$R_{int} = 0.0116 \, \Omega = 11.6 \, \text{m}\Omega$$

**(c) Cranking Power:**

$$P_{crank} = V_L \times I = 9.4 \times 250 = 2350 \, \text{W} = 2.35 \, \text{kW}$$

**(d) Health Verdict:**

Voltage dropped below 9.6 V threshold during cranking → **Battery is FAILED**, replace after recharge fails to restore performance.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Automotive Battery Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Arrives at Workshop] --> B[Visual Mechanical Inspection]
    B --> C{Physical Defects Found?}
    C --> C1[Cracks Bulges Leaks] --> C2[Reject Battery Immediately]
    C --> D[Proceed to Electrical Tests]
    D --> E[OCV Test with DMM]
    E --> F{Voc greater than 12.4V?}
    F --> F1[No Battery Discharged] --> F2[Recharge then Retest]
    F --> G[Yes Continue]
    G --> H[Hydrometer SG Test]
    H --> I{Cell Variation within 0.050?}
    I --> I1[No Defective Cell Detected] --> I2[Replace Battery]
    I --> J[Yes SG Balanced]
    J --> K[High Rate Load Test 15 sec at 0.5 CCA]
    K --> L{Vload greater than 9.6V?}
    L --> L1[No Battery Failed] --> L2[Replace Battery]
    L --> M[Yes Battery Passes Load]
    M --> N[Cranking Voltage Test In Vehicle]
    N --> O{Vcrank greater than 9.6V?}
    O --> O1[No Check Cables and Connections] --> O2[Clean Tighten Retest]
    O --> P[Yes Healthy]
    P --> Q[Charging System Voltage Test]
    Q --> R{Vcharge within 13.8 to 14.8V?}
    R --> R1[No Alternator Regulator Fault] --> R2[Service Charging System]
    R --> S[Yes Charging OK]
    S --> T[Parasitic Drain Test]
    T --> U{Ipara less than 50 mA?}
    U --> U1[No Locate and Remove Drain] --> U2[Trace Circuit]
    U --> V[Yes Fully Diagnosed Battery]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Equivalent Circuit of a Battery Under Test

```mermaid
graph LR
    source1[Voc Open Circuit Voltage] --> R1[Int Res Ri]
    R1 --> term1[Positive Terminal]
    term1 --> load1[Load RL Tester]
    load1 --> term2[Negative Terminal]
    term2 --> source1
    meas1[DMM Voltage Measurement] -.-> term1
    meas2[DMM Voltage Measurement] -.-> term2
    meas3[Ammeter IA Current] -.-> load1
```

**Equivalent Circuit Model Explanation:**

- $V_{OC}$ = Open-circuit EMF source (≈ 12.6 V when fully charged).
- $R_i$ = Internal resistance (sum of electrolyte, plate, and contact resistances).
- $R_L$ = External load (tester / starter motor).
- The DMM measures terminal voltage: $V_T = V_{OC} - I_A \cdot R_i$.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Battery Management System (BMS) Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    batt[Battery 12V Lead Acid] --> volt1[Voltage Sensor]
    batt --> curr1[Current Sensor Shunt]
    batt --> temp1[Temperature Sensor NTC]
    volt1 --> bmsc[BMS ECU]
    curr1 --> bmsc
    temp1 --> bmsc
    bmsc --> alt[Alternator Control]
    bmsc --> lcd[Dashboard SOC Display]
    bmsc --> obd[OBD II Port]
    bmsc --> start[Start Stop Decision]
```

**Block Functionality:**

1. **Voltage Sensor** → Monitors terminal voltage in real time.
2. **Current Shunt** → Measures charge / discharge current for **Coulomb counting**.
3. **NTC Thermistor** → Sends electrolyte temperature for **compensation**.
4. **BMS ECU** → Calculates SOC, SOH, and predicts remaining life.
5. **Alternator Control** → Adjusts charging voltage dynamically (smart charging).
6. **Start-Stop Decision** → Disables engine stop if SOC < threshold (e.g., 70 %).

## 4.4 Process Flow — Parasitic Drain Diagnosis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Key OFF Wait 30 min] --> stepA[Disconnect Negative Terminal]
    stepA --> stepB[Connect Ammeter in Series]
    stepB --> stepC{Reading greater than 50 mA}
    stepC --> stepC1[No Drain Within Spec] --> stepC2[System OK]
    stepC --> stepD[Yes Excessive Drain]
    stepD --> stepE[Pull Fuses One by One]
    stepE --> stepF{Drain Current Drops}
    stepF --> stepF1[Yes Identify Circuit] --> stepF2[Repair Faulty Component]
    stepF --> stepG[No Drain Persists] --> stepG1[Check Direct Feed to Battery]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: List any three battery tests and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) Test:** Determines the State of Charge (SOC) by measuring the terminal voltage of the battery after a stabilization period of at least 10 minutes. A fully charged 12 V battery reads **12.65 – 12.75 V**.

2. **Specific Gravity (Hydrometer) Test:** Measures the density of the electrolyte using a float-type hydrometer. A fully charged cell has SG between **1.265 and 1.285**. A cell variation greater than **0.050** indicates a defective cell.

3. **High-Rate Discharge (Load) Test:** Determines the battery's ability to deliver cranking current. A current of **half the CCA rating** is applied for **15 seconds**, and the voltage must remain **above 9.6 V** at 21 °C for the battery to pass.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is Cold Cranking Amps (CCA) rating? How is it different from Reserve Capacity (RC)?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Cold Cranking Amps (CCA):** It is the **maximum current (in Amperes) a fully charged battery can deliver for 30 seconds at −17.8 °C (0 °F)** while maintaining a terminal voltage of at least **7.2 V** (1.2 V/cell). It indicates the battery's ability to start a cold engine.

- **Reserve Capacity (RC):** It is the **number of minutes a fully charged battery at 26.7 °C can deliver 25 A** until terminal voltage falls below **10.5 V**. It indicates how long the vehicle can run on battery alone if the alternator fails.

| Parameter | CCA | RC |
|---|---|---|
| Current | High (e.g., 500 A) | Moderate (25 A) |
| Temperature | −17.8 °C | 26.7 °C |
| End Voltage | 7.2 V | 10.5 V |
| Purpose | Cranking | Backup duration |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Q: (a) Explain the procedure and interpretation of the High-Rate Discharge (Load) Test of an automotive battery. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A 12 V, 60 Ah battery is subjected to a load test. The OCV is 12.55 V. The terminal voltage at the end of 15 seconds while drawing 300 A is 9.5 V. Determine the state of health and internal resistance. Comment on the result. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Procedure and Interpretation:**

**Apparatus Required:** Variable load tester (carbon pile or electronic), digital voltmeter, thermometer, fully charged battery.

**Procedure:**
1. Ensure the battery is **fully charged** (OCV ≥ 12.6 V) before testing.
2. Measure and record the **electrolyte temperature**. Standard reference is **21 °C (70 °F)**.
3. Apply a discharge current equal to **half the CCA rating** (or 3 × AH rating, whichever is greater) of the battery.
4. Maintain the load for **exactly 15 seconds**.
5. Record the terminal voltage at the **end of 15 seconds**.

**Interpretation:**

| Terminal Voltage at 15 s (at 21 °C) | Result |
|---|---|
| ≥ 9.6 V | Battery is GOOD |
| 9.4 – 9.6 V | Marginal — recharge and retest |
| < 9.4 V | Battery is FAILED — replace |

**Temperature Correction:**
- Below 21 °C: subtract **0.04 V per 10 °C** drop.
- Above 21 °C: add **0.04 V per 10 °C** rise.

**Significance:** This test simulates actual cranking stress. It reveals weak cells, high internal resistance, and sulfation that the OCV test may miss.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{OC} = 12.55$ V, $V_L = 9.5$ V, $I = 300$ A, time = 15 s.

**Step 1 — Internal Resistance:**

$$R_{int} = \frac{V_{OC} - V_L}{I} = \frac{12.55 - 9.5}{300}$$

$$R_{int} = \frac{3.05}{300} = 0.01017 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{int} \approx 10.17 \, \text{m}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Pass Criterion:**

The terminal voltage **9.5 V** is **below** the **9.6 V** threshold (but within 9.4 – 9.6 V marginal band).

**Step 3 — State of Health (SOH) Verdict:**

$$SOH \approx 70\% \quad (\text{Marginal})$$

**Step 4 — Comments:**

- The battery shows **elevated internal resistance** (≈ 10 mΩ; healthy < 5 mΩ).
- During the 15 s load, voltage dipped to **9.5 V** → **Marginal** — may fail under cold conditions.
- **Recommendation:** Recharge fully, retest after 24 hours. If still below 9.6 V, **replace the battery**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Q: (a) Describe the Specific Gravity (Hydrometer) Test and explain how the state of charge is determined from it. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A 6-cell lead-acid battery has specific gravity readings of 1.265, 1.270, 1.265, 1.260, 1.265, 1.120 at 26.7 °C. Determine: (i) the average state of charge, (ii) the defective cell number, and (iii) the open-circuit voltage of the healthy cells. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Specific Gravity Test:**

**Principle:** The specific gravity (SG) of the electrolyte (dilute sulfuric acid) is directly proportional to the concentration of acid, which decreases as the battery discharges (since $H_2SO_4$ is consumed and $H_2O$ is produced during discharge).

**Apparatus:** Hydrometer (bulb-type with float and scale), safety goggles, gloves.

**Procedure:**
1. Remove the vent caps of all cells.
2. Squeeze the hydrometer bulb, insert into the cell, and draw electrolyte up until the float is freely suspended.
3. Read the SG at the **meniscus level** of the float.
4. Record SG for **all six cells**.
5. Measure electrolyte temperature with a thermometer.

**Temperature Correction:**

$$SG_{corrected} = SG_{measured} + 0.0007 \times (T - 26.7)$$

**SOC Determination:**

| Specific Gravity (at 26.7 °C) | State of Charge (%) |
|---|---|
| 1.265 – 1.285 | 100 % |
| 1.235 | 75 % |
| 1.205 | 50 % |
| 1.175 | 25 % |
| 1.120 or less | 0 % (Discharged) |

**Cell Variation Rule:** Maximum permissible difference between any two cells is **0.050**. Greater variation indicates a **faulty cell** (sulfated, shorted, or aged).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given SG readings:** 1.265, 1.270, 1.265, 1.260, 1.265, 1.120

**(i) Average SG of healthy cells (excluding defective 6th cell):**

$$SG_{avg} = \frac{1.265 + 1.270 + 1.265 + 1.260 + 1.265}{5} = \frac{6.325}{5} = 1.265$$

**Average SOC ≈ 100 %** (since SG 1.265 corresponds to fully charged).

**(ii) Defective Cell:**

The 6th cell has SG = **1.120**, which is **0.145 lower** than the average — far exceeding the 0.050 limit.

$$\boxed{\text{Defective Cell Number: 6}}$$

**Reason:** Possible internal short circuit, dried-out electrolyte, or permanent sulfation.

**(iii) Open-Circuit Voltage of Healthy Cells:**

Using $V_{cell} = SG + 0.84$:

$$V_{cell} = 1.265 + 0.84 = 2.105 \, \text{V per cell}$$

For 5 healthy cells (the defective one contributes only to internal short):

$$V_{OC\,healthy} = 5 \times 2.105 = 10.525 \, \text{V}$$

**Overall Battery OCV (including the shorted cell contributing ≈ 0 V):**

$$V_{OC\,battery} \approx 10.5 \, \text{V} \quad (\text{Defective — Replace Battery})$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Temperature Correction:** Many students apply SG → SOC tables without correcting for temperature. Examiner deducts **1 mark** if temperature correction formula is not shown.
> 2. **Confusing CCA with CA or MCA:** CCA = −17.8 °C, CA = 0 °C, MCA (Marine Cranking Amps) = 0 °C with different end voltages.
> 3. **Wrong Sign in Internal Resistance Formula:** $R_{int} = (V_{OC} - V_L)/I$, not $(V_L - V_{OC})/I$.
> 4. **Skipping the Peukert Effect:** When asked for "capacity at high current," students blindly write the rated Ah. Examiner expects Peukert correction.
> 5. **Forgetting to State the Pass/Fail Threshold:** Saying "voltage is 9.4 V so battery is bad" without stating "the threshold is 9.6 V at 21 °C" loses half a mark.
> 6. **Mixing Up Load Test Current Rule:** The correct rule is **0.5 × CCA** OR **3 × Ah**, **whichever is higher**, not just one of them.
> 7. **Cell Numbering:** In problems, "Cell 1" is typically the **positive-end** cell; check diagrams carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Battery Tests Module 3:**

- [x] **OCV Test** → 12.65 V (full), 11.89 V (empty). Wait **10 min** after disconnect.
- [x] **Hydrometer Test** → SG 1.265 (full). Max cell variation **0.050**.
- [x] **Load Test** → 0.5 × CCA for 15 s. Pass if **V ≥ 9.6 V at 21 °C**.
- [x] **Cranking Test** → In-vehicle, V_cranking **≥ 9.6 V**.
- [x] **Three-Minute Charge** → If V > 15.5 V, battery is **sulfated — replace**.
- [x] **Charging System** → 13.8 to 14.8 V at 2000 rpm.
- [x] **Parasitic Drain** → ≤ 50 mA acceptable.
- [x] **CCA** = Current at −17.8 °C for 30 s, V ≥ 7.2 V.
- [x] **RC** = Minutes at 25 A, 26.7 °C, until V < 10.5 V.
- [x] **$V_{cell} = SG + 0.84$** — Nernst-derived.
- [x] **$R_{int} = (V_{OC} - V_L)/I$** — for internal resistance.
- [x] **$C = I \times t$** — capacity in Ah.
- [x] **Peukert's Law:** Capacity decreases at high discharge rates.
- [x] **Temperature correction:** ±0.07 V per 10 °C for OCV; ±0.04 V per 10 °C for load test.
- [x] **Healthy $R_{int}$:** < 5 mΩ; **Aged:** > 20 mΩ.
- [x] **BMS** uses OCV + Coulomb counting for SOC estimation in modern cars.
- [x] **Cell numbering:** Cell 1 = positive end; check before answering.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "describe" a test, always structure the answer as **Principle → Apparatus → Procedure → Interpretation → Significance**. This 5-point format guarantees full marks in the 7-mark descriptive parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Battery Types - Lithium-Ion Battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modern Battery Types - Lithium-Ion Battery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lithium-Ion Battery (Li-ion):** A *rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device* in which electrical energy is stored via the **reversible intercalation (insertion) and de-intercalation of lithium ions** between two host electrodes (a carbon-based anode and a lithium transition-metal-oxide or phosphate cathode) through a non-aqueous liquid or gel electrolyte and a porous polymer separator.

In the **automotive context (KTU – Automotive Electronics)**, a Li-ion cell is the fundamental *Energy Storage System (ESS)* building block of Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs), Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), and modern 48 V / 12 V auxiliary networks.

- **Nominal cell voltage:** **3.6 V – 3.7 V**
- **Operating voltage window:** **2.5 V (cut-off) – 4.2 V (upper limit, per cell)**
- **Practical specific energy:** **100 – 265 Wh/kg**
- **Automotive pack voltages:** **400 V (standard)**, **800 V (modern, e.g., Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lithium Shuttles"

Imagine a **multilevel parking garage** with two halves separated by a toll-booth:

- **Left side (Anode)** is fully filled with cars (Li⁺ parked in graphite layers).
- **Right side (Cathode)** is empty (metal-oxide waiting for cars).
- During **discharge**, the cars (Li⁺) drive across the **toll-booth (electrolyte + separator)** to the right side. They pay a *toll* (energy) which turns on the highway lights (external circuit / motor).
- During **charge**, an external **elevator (charger)** mechanically pulls the cars back to the left side, refilling it for the next trip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Lithium" specifically?**
> Lithium is the **lightest metal (atomic mass ≈ 6.94 g/mol)** with the **most negative standard reduction potential (−3.04 V vs SHE)**. This combination gives Li-ion cells the **highest practical energy density** of all commercial rechargeable chemistries — a non-negotiable requirement for automotive range.

---

## 1.3 Position in the KTU 2024 Module-3 Map

| Sub-topic in Module 3 | Role of Li-ion |
|---|---|
| Conventional batteries (Lead-acid, Ni-MH) | Li-ion replaces them due to higher energy density |
| EV architecture | Li-ion is the primary traction energy source |
| BMS & Sensors | Li-ion **demands** an electronic BMS for safety |
| Charging infrastructure | CC-CV profile is the canonical Li-ion charging law |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping (CO3 – Apply):** Students must be able to *identify, compare, and justify* a Li-ion cell chemistry for a given automotive application (range vs. power vs. safety trade-off).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Electrochemistry

**During Discharge (vehicle accelerating / cruising):**

1. **Anode (Graphite, C₆):** Lithium atoms stored between graphene layers are **oxidized**.
   $$\text{LiC}_{6} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_{6} \;+\; \text{Li}^{+} \;+\; e^{-}$$

2. **Cathode (e.g., LiCoO₂):** Li⁺ ions arriving through the electrolyte are **reduced** and inserted back into the cobalt-oxide lattice.
   $$\text{CoO}_{2} \;+\; \text{Li}^{+} \;+\; e^{-} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{LiCoO}_{2}$$

3. **External circuit:** Released electrons flow from anode → load (traction motor inverter) → cathode, doing useful electrical work.

4. **Electrolyte:** A lithium salt (e.g., **LiPF₆**) dissolved in organic carbonates (EC, DMC, EMC). It is an **ionic conductor but electronic insulator** — forces electrons to take the external path.

5. **Separator:** A microporous polyolefin membrane (PE/PP, ~25 µm) that physically prevents anode-cathode contact (short circuit) while letting Li⁺ pass.

**During Charge (plug-in or regen-braking):**
- The above reactions are **reversed** by applying an external EMF greater than the cell's open-circuit voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shuttling of Li⁺ between the two electrodes is called the **"Rocking-Chair" mechanism** — a KTU-favourite one-line definition.

---

## 2.2 Automotive Li-ion Chemistries — Comparative Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask: *"Compare LFP, NMC, and NCA for an EV application."* Memorize the trade-off below.

| Parameter | LFP (LiFePO₄) | NMC (LiNiₓMnᵧCozO₂) | NCA (LiNiCoAlO₂) | LTO (Li₄Ti₅O₁₂) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage (V) | **3.2** | **3.6 – 3.7** | **3.6 – 3.7** | **2.4** |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 90 – 160 | 150 – 220 | 200 – 260 | 50 – 80 |
| Cycle Life (cycles) | **> 3000** | 1000 – 2000 | 500 – 1000 | **> 10000** |
| Thermal Runaway (°C) | **> 270 (safest)** | ~210 | ~150 | **> 280 (very safe)** |
| Cost ($/kWh, 2024) | **Lowest** | Medium | High | Very High |
| Typical EV User | BYD, Tesla SR | VW MEB, Hyundai | Tesla LR, Lucid | Honda, Toshiba SCiB |
| **Best for** | Buses, entry-EV, storage | **General EV (best balance)** | Long-range BEV | Ultra-fast charge, HEV |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU high-yield**. Memorize units and the sign convention.

| # | Quantity / Formula | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cell Energy | $E \;=\; V \cdot Q$ | Wh (or kWh) | $V$ = nominal V, $Q$ = Ah |
| 2 | Pack Energy | $E_{pack} \;=\; n_s \cdot V_{cell} \cdot n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Wh | $n_s$ = series, $n_p$ = parallel |
| 3 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp} \;=\; \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Determines range |
| 4 | Energy Density | $E_{v} \;=\; \dfrac{E}{V}$ | Wh/L | Determines packaging |
| 5 | Specific Power | $P_{sp} \;=\; \dfrac{V^{2}}{4 \cdot R_{int} \cdot m}$ | W/kg | Peukert-adjusted |
| 6 | C-rate Current | $I \;=\; C\text{-rate} \cdot Q_{rated}$ | A | E.g., 2C of 50 Ah = 100 A |
| 7 | Discharge Time | $t \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{I} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\text{C-rate}}$ | h | At constant current |
| 8 | Heat Generation | $P_{heat} \;=\; I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$ | W | Dominant at high C |
| 9 | SOC (Coulomb Counting) | $SOC(t) \;=\; SOC(t_0) \;-\; \dfrac{\int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau}{Q_{rated}}$ | — | (0 to 1 or %) |
| 10 | SOH | $SOH \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{max,new}}$ | — | End-of-life at SOH $\leq$ 80 % |
| 11 | Pack Voltage | $V_{pack} \;=\; n_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | V | $n_s$ typically 96 – 108 for 400 V |
| 12 | Open-Circuit Voltage (Nernst) | $V_{oc} \;=\; V^{0} \;+\; \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}\right)$ | V | Underlies BMS lookup tables |
| 13 | Pack Resistance | $R_{pack} \;=\; n_s \cdot \dfrac{R_{int,cell}}{n_p}$ | Ω | Series-parallel rule |
| 14 | EV Range Estimate | $R \;=\; \dfrac{E_{pack} \cdot \eta_{veh}}{E_{cons}}$ | km | $\eta_{veh}$ includes drivetrain + aux |

**CRITICAL — KTU Pitfall:** Never write absolute value as `|x|` inside any table row — it breaks the markdown renderer. In your exam, hand-write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ correctly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Automotive Utility

- **Range anxiety** in BEVs is directly solved by Li-ion specific energy. The Tesla Model 3 LR (~82 kWh pack) achieves ~$E_{sp} \approx 250$ Wh/kg at pack level.
- **Fast charging** (350 kW DC) is enabled by chemistries like NMC and LTO that tolerate 3 C – 6 C continuous current.
- **Cold-start reliability** (−30 °C) requires a thermal management loop because Li-ion electrolyte viscosity increases and **Li plating** risk on the anode rises sharply below 0 °C.
- **Recycling economy:** Cobalt and nickel recovery makes end-of-life Li-ion packs economically attractive — a circular-economy topic now appearing in KTU's Management/Economics elective.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four derivations below are **frequently-asked KTU numerical / analytical problems**. Work through every step — no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pack Energy and Series-Parallel Configuration

**Problem:** An EV uses a Li-ion pack composed of **$n_s = 100$** cells in series and **$n_p = 2$** cells in parallel. Each cell has a nominal voltage of **3.7 V** and a capacity of **50 Ah**. Find: (a) the pack nominal voltage, (b) the pack capacity, and (c) the pack energy in kWh.

### (a) Pack Nominal Voltage

Cells in **series** add voltages; capacity is unchanged by series connection.

$$V_{pack} \;=\; n_s \cdot V_{cell}$$

$$V_{pack} \;=\; 100 \times 3.7 \;\text{V} \;=\; 370 \;\text{V}$$

### (b) Pack Capacity

Cells in **parallel** add capacities; voltage is unchanged by parallel connection.

$$Q_{pack} \;=\; n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$$

$$Q_{pack} \;=\; 2 \times 50 \;\text{Ah} \;=\; 100 \;\text{Ah}$$

### (c) Pack Energy

$$E_{pack} \;=\; V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack}$$

$$E_{pack} \;=\; 370 \;\text{V} \times 100 \;\text{Ah} \;=\; 37{,}000 \;\text{Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{pack} \;=\; 37 \;\text{kWh}}$$

**Engineering check:** This is realistic — a small-to-mid BEV (e.g., MG ZS EV has a 50.3 kWh pack).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — State of Charge by Coulomb Counting

**Problem:** A 60 Ah Li-ion traction pack starts the journey with **SOC = 90 %**. The vehicle draws a **constant current of 120 A** for **15 minutes**, then enters **regenerative braking** delivering **40 A** for **10 minutes**. The BMS self-discharge coefficient is **k = 0.1 %/h** over the 25-minute window. Find the **final SOC**.

### Step 1 — Time conversions

$$t_1 \;=\; 15 \;\text{min} \;=\; 0.25 \;\text{h}$$

$$t_2 \;=\; 10 \;\text{min} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} \;\text{h} \;\approx\; 0.1667 \;\text{h}$$

$$t_{tot} \;=\; 0.25 \;+\; 0.1667 \;=\; 0.4167 \;\text{h}$$

### Step 2 — Net charge transferred (sign convention: discharge = positive outflow)

$$Q_{out} \;=\; I_1 \cdot t_1 \;-\; I_2 \cdot t_2$$

$$Q_{out} \;=\; 120 \times 0.25 \;-\; 40 \times 0.1667$$

$$Q_{out} \;=\; 30 \;-\; 6.667 \;=\; 23.333 \;\text{Ah}$$

### Step 3 — Self-discharge loss

$$Q_{self} \;=\; k \cdot Q_{rated} \cdot t_{tot}$$

$$Q_{self} \;=\; 0.001 \times 60 \times 0.4167 \;=\; 0.025 \;\text{Ah}$$

(Ne gligible, but KTU loves to test it.)

### Step 4 — Final SOC

$$SOC_{f} \;=\; SOC_0 \;-\; \frac{Q_{out} + Q_{self}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\,\%$$

$$SOC_{f} \;=\; 90 \;-\; \frac{23.333 + 0.025}{60} \times 100$$

$$SOC_{f} \;=\; 90 \;-\; 38.93 \;=\; 51.07\,\%$$

$$\boxed{SOC_{f} \;\approx\; 51.1\,\%}$$

**Engineering check:** A 120 A draw from a 60 Ah pack is 2 C — sustained for 15 min, the pack should drop by ~50 % SOC. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Generation and Temperature Rise

**Problem:** A 400 V, 50 kWh Li-ion pack has an internal resistance of **$R_{int} = 0.1\;\Omega$**. The vehicle is fast-charging at **$I = 125\;\text{A}$** for **30 min**. The pack mass is **300 kg** with specific heat **$c_p = 1100\;\text{J/(kg·K)}$**. Find the (a) total heat generated, (b) adiabatic temperature rise, and (c) the cooling system heat-rejection rate required to keep $\Delta T \le 10\;\text{K}$.

### (a) Total Heat Generated

$$P_{heat} \;=\; I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$$

$$P_{heat} \;=\; (125)^{2} \times 0.1 \;=\; 1562.5 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.56 \;\text{kW}$$

$$Q_{tot} \;=\; P_{heat} \cdot t \;=\; 1562.5 \times (30 \times 60)$$

$$Q_{tot} \;=\; 1562.5 \times 1800 \;=\; 2{,}812{,}500 \;\text{J} \;=\; 2.81 \;\text{MJ}$$

### (b) Adiabatic Temperature Rise (no cooling)

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} \;=\; \frac{Q_{tot}}{m \cdot c_p}$$

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} \;=\; \frac{2.8125 \times 10^{6}}{300 \times 1100}$$

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} \;=\; \frac{2.8125 \times 10^{6}}{3.3 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{adiabatic} \;\approx\; 8.52 \;\text{K}}$$

### (c) Required Heat-Rejection Rate to Limit to 10 K

If the cooling system must absorb all the heat, the **required rate** is:

$$\dot{Q}_{cool} \;\ge\; P_{heat} \;=\; 1562.5 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.56 \;\text{kW}$$

But a **safety margin of 1.5×** is standard automotive practice:

$$\dot{Q}_{cool,design} \;=\; 1.5 \times 1.56 \;\text{kW} \;\approx\; 2.35 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Thermal-runaway threshold** for NMC starts at ~**210 °C** cell temperature. An $8.5\;\text{K}$ adiabatic rise is safe **only if** the cooling loop is active. Without it, a 1 C – 2 C charge cycle for an hour can push cells into thermal runaway.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — EV Range Estimation

**Problem:** A 50 kWh Li-ion BEV has vehicle efficiency **$E_{cons} = 0.15$ kWh/km** (combined WLTP cycle). The drivetrain efficiency is **$\eta_{veh} = 0.85$**. Find the **realistic range**.

### Step 1 — Usable Energy (with 10 % buffer at top, 5 % buffer at bottom)

$$E_{usable} \;=\; 0.90 \times 50 \;\text{kWh} \;=\; 45 \;\text{kWh (top buffer kept)}$$

But the BMS typically allows only **80 – 90 %** of the rated energy:

$$E_{usable} \;=\; 0.85 \times 50 \;\text{kWh} \;=\; 42.5 \;\text{kWh}$$

### Step 2 — Energy Delivered to Wheels

$$E_{wheel} \;=\; E_{usable} \cdot \eta_{veh}$$

$$E_{wheel} \;=\; 42.5 \times 0.85 \;=\; 36.125 \;\text{kWh}$$

### Step 3 — Range

$$R \;=\; \frac{E_{wheel}}{E_{cons}}$$

$$R \;=\; \frac{36.125}{0.15} \;=\; 240.83 \;\text{km}$$

$$\boxed{R \;\approx\; 240 \;\text{km}}$$

**Engineering check:** A 50 kWh pack realistically delivers ~250 – 400 km depending on driving style, aerodynamics, and HVAC load. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Why Li-ion "Wins" Each Trade-off

| Automotive Requirement | Li-ion Metric | Competing Tech (Lead-acid) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range (high $E_{sp}$) | 200 Wh/kg | 35 Wh/kg | **Li-ion ~6× better** |
| Packaging (high $E_v$) | 600 Wh/L | 80 Wh/L | **Li-ion ~7.5× better** |
| Cycle life | 1000 – 3000 | 300 – 500 | **Li-ion ~3 – 6× better** |
| Self-discharge / month | 2 – 3 % | 4 – 6 % | **Li-ion better** |
| Cost (\$/kWh, 2024) | 115 – 140 | 150 – 200 | **Li-ion now cheaper** |
| Operating temperature | −20 °C – 60 °C | −30 °C – 50 °C | Lead-acid slightly better in cold |
| Safety without BMS | **Poor (thermal runaway)** | Robust | Lead-acid safer passively |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single disadvantage — **safety** — is exactly why the **BMS** is *not optional* in a Li-ion automotive pack. Section 4 illustrates the BMS architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All four diagrams use **Mermaid flowchart** syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric with a letter prefix; labels are plain uppercase text to avoid rendering issues.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Li-ion Cell Internal Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CATHODE CURRENT COLLECTOR AL FOIL] --> A2[CATHODE ACTIVE MATERIAL LAYER LICO2 OR NMC]
    A2 --> A3[POROUS POLYMER SEPARATOR PE OR PP 25 UM]
    A3 --> A4[ANODE ACTIVE MATERIAL GRAPHITE OR SI]
    A4 --> A5[ANODE CURRENT COLLECTOR CU FOIL]
    A5 --> A6[ELECTROLYTE LIPF6 IN EC DMC EMC]
    A6 --> A1
    B1[LI PLUS ION FLOW DURING DISCHARGE ANODE TO CATHODE] -.-> A3
    B2[ELECTRON FLOW THROUGH EXTERNAL LOAD] -.-> A5
    B2 -.-> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The outer loop represents the **ionic path** (Li⁺ through the electrolyte + separator).
- The dashed lines represent the **electronic path** (e⁻ through the external load — i.e., the traction motor).
- The separator is the **physical barrier** that prevents direct electron flow inside the cell.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Battery Management System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[CELL MODULE BANK 96S 1P] --> M1[CELL MONITORING IC AFE]
    M1 --> M2[MICROCONTROLLER SOC SOH ESTIMATION]
    M2 --> M3[PROTECTION LOGIC OV UV OC OT]
    M3 --> M4[CONTACTORS AND FUSES]
    M4 --> P2[HIGH VOLTAGE BUS TO INVERTER]
    M2 --> C1[COOLING CONTROL VALVE AND PUMP]
    C1 --> P1
    M2 --> C2[CAN BUS TO VCU AND CHARGER]
    M2 --> C3[STATE OF CHARGE DISPLAY CLUSTER]
    M2 --> C4[CELL BALANCING PASSIVE OR ACTIVE]
    C4 --> P1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **AFE (Analog Front End):** Per-cell voltage and temperature sampling.
- **Microcontroller:** Runs the **Kalman-filter or coulomb-counting** SOC algorithm.
- **Protection logic:** Hard-wired thresholds for **OV (over-voltage), UV (under-voltage), OC (over-current), OT (over-temperature)** — these trip the contactors within milliseconds.
- **Cell balancing:** Either dissipative (resistor) or active (inductor/capacitor shuttling) to keep all $n_s$ cells within $\pm 30\;\text{mV}$.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — CC-CV Charging Profile Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST0[START SOC LESS THAN 20 PERCENT] --> ST1[PRECHARGE CC PHASE I AT 0.3 C]
    ST1 --> ST2[MAIN CC PHASE I AT 1 C UP TO 4.2 V PER CELL]
    ST2 --> ST3{TERMINAL VOLTAGE 4.2 V REACHED}
    ST3 -- NO --> ST2
    ST3 -- YES --> ST4[CV PHASE V HELD AT 4.2 V I TAPERS]
```
