```mermaid
    ecu[ECU Final Decision Output Driver]
    rpm[RPM Sensor Crank Position]

    driver --> ip
    imm --> ip
    cpps --> ip
    bps --> ip
    trs --> ip
    ibs --> ip
    rpm --> ip
    ip --> ecu
    ecu --> rly
    rly --> sol
    sol --> mtr
    mtr --> eng
```

### 4.2 Crank Authorization State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> KeyOff
    KeyOff --> AuthPending : KeyToStart
    AuthPending --> AuthOK : ImmobilizerValid AND InterlocksClosed
    AuthPending --> AuthFail : Timeout OR InvalidKey
    AuthFail --> KeyOff : 3sLockout
    AuthOK --> Cranking : RelayEnergized
    Cranking --> EngineRunning : RPMGreaterThan500
    Cranking --> CrankTimeout : TimerGreaterThan30s
    EngineRunning --> KeyOff : KeyToOff
    CrankTimeout --> KeyOff : RelayDeEnergized
```

### 4.3 Circuit Schematic — Two-Stage Relay Switching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat[Battery 12V 12.6V Nominal] --- fuse[Fuse 80A]
    fuse --- mainC[Main Solenoid Contacts Normally Open]
    mainC --- starterM[Starter Motor M Terminal]
    starterM --- gnd1[Chassis Ground]
    bat2[Battery 12V] --- fuse2[Fuse 5A]
    fuse2 --- keySw[Ignition Switch START Position]
    keySw --- relayCoil[Starter Relay Coil 80 Ohm]
    relayCoil --- ecuOut[ECU Low Side Driver 0.5A Sink]
    ecuOut --- gnd2[Ground]
    relayCoil -.controls.-> mainC
```

### 4.4 Start-Stop Re-Crank Sequence (BSG / ISG)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Driver
    participant ECU as ECU
    participant BMS as Battery Sensor
    participant MGU as Belt Starter Generator
    participant ENG as Engine
    D->>ECU: Vehicle Stopped BrakeHeld GearInD
    ECU->>BMS: Request SOC Status
    BMS-->>ECU: SOC 78 Percent OK
    ECU->>ENG: Fuel Cut Off
    ENG-->>ECU: Engine Off Confirmation
    Note over ECU: Cooldown Period 400ms
    D->>ECU: Release Brake
    ECU->>MGU: Crank Command 48V
    MGU->>ENG: Rotate Crankshaft
    ENG-->>ECU: RPM Rising
    ECU->>ENG: Inject Fuel Spark
    ENG-->>ECU: Running Stable at 800RPM
    ECU->>MGU: Stop Cranking Disengage
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (driver, ip, imm, etc.). No reserved keywords are used. Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Electronic Starter Control and list **any four** safety interlocks used in it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Electronic Starter Control is an ECU-supervised subsystem that authorizes and manages the cranking of the engine by switching a low-current relay that energizes the high-current starter solenoid, after verifying driver intent, safety conditions, and immobilizer authentication.

**Four safety interlocks (½ mark each):**
1. **Clutch Pedal Position Switch (CPPS)** — manual transmission.
2. **Brake Pedal Switch (BPS)** — automatic / push-button start.
3. **Transmission Range Sensor (TRS / PNR switch)** — must be in P or N.
4. **Hood switch / anti-crash sensor** — prevents crank with hood open (some BSG vehicles).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is a **relay** used between the ECU and the starter solenoid? Give **two reasons**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Current amplification (1.5 marks):** ECU output drivers can sink only ~0.5 A–2 A, but the starter solenoid pull-in winding needs ~12–30 A. A relay allows a milliamp-level ECU signal to control tens of amps.
2. **Isolation & protection (1.5 marks):** The relay provides galvanic isolation, protecting the sensitive ECU circuitry from voltage transients, back-EMF, and short-circuit faults on the high-current starter side.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice A]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an Electronic Starter Control system** and describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **operational sequence** of an electronic starter from key insertion to engine start, including immobilizer authentication, safety interlocks, relay actuation, solenoid engagement, and anti-grind release. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and role of each block (7 marks):**

Draw the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Label and explain:

| Block | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Driver Input (Key / Push-button) | Captures crank request | 1 |
| Immobilizer antenna + transponder | Anti-theft authentication | 1 |
| Safety Interlock switches (Clutch, Brake, TRS) | Prevent crank in unsafe state | 1.5 |
| Battery Sensor Module (IBS) | Reports SOC/SOH to ECU | 0.5 |
| ECU/PCM | Decision-making, runs authorization algorithm | 1.5 |
| Starter Control Relay | Low-current → high-current switching | 1 |
| Starter Solenoid (Pull-in & Hold-in) | Mechanical engagement + main contact closure | 0.5 |
| Starter Motor + Bendix/Pinion | Converts electrical energy to cranking torque | 0.5 (deducted for missing diagram label) |

**(b) Operational sequence (7 marks):**

1. **Key turned to START (1 mark):** Ignition switch closes 12 V to the BCM/ECU crank-request input.
2. **Immobilizer handshake (1 mark):** RFID transponder in key is energized by antenna; cryptographic response validated by ECU within ~50 ms.
3. **Safety interlock verification (1.5 marks):** ECU reads brake/clutch switches, TRS. If all OK, ECU proceeds.
4. **Battery SOC check (0.5 mark):** SOC > 60 % required for Start-Stop re-crank.
5. **Relay energization (1 mark):** ECU low-side driver sinks ~0.5 A, energizing the 30 A relay coil. Relay contacts close, applying B+ to solenoid S-terminal.
6. **Solenoid pull-in (1 mark):** Plunger moves; pinion engages ring gear; main contacts close; full battery current (150–300 A) flows to motor.
7. **Cranking & release (1 mark):** Engine fires. RPM > 500. ECU de-energizes relay. Anti-grind prevents over-rotation of pinion.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice B]**
> **(a)** With a circuit diagram, explain the **two-stage relay switching** in an electronic starter system. Why is this topology preferred over direct ECU switching? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A starter motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.03$ Ω, cable resistance (positive + negative) $R_{cable} = 0.010$ Ω, brush resistance $R_{brush} = 0.010$ Ω. Battery voltage is **12.6 V**. Calculate the **(i)** locked-rotor current and **(ii)** terminal voltage at the starter when the motor draws **150 A** under running condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-stage relay switching (7 marks):**

Draw the circuit of **Section 4.3**.

- **Stage 1 (Low-current side):** Battery (via 5 A fuse) → Ignition switch START → Relay coil (~80 Ω) → ECU low-side driver → Ground. Current ≈ **12.6 V / 80 Ω = 0.16 A**.
- **Stage 2 (High-current side):** Battery (via 80 A fuse) → Relay main contacts (closed by Stage 1) → Solenoid S-terminal → Motor M-terminal → Ground. Current = **150–300 A**.

**Why preferred (3 marks):**
1. ECU pins cannot handle > 2 A — relay provides current gain.
2. Isolation protects ECU from inductive kick and short-circuit faults.
3. Relay can be located near the battery to minimize cable length, reducing voltage drop.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Total resistance: $R_{total} = R_{cable} + R_{brush} + R_{a} = 0.010 + 0.010 + 0.030 = 0.050$ Ω.

**(i) Locked-rotor current (3.5 marks):**

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_{bat}}{R_{total}} = \frac{12.6}{0.050} = 252 \ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Terminal voltage at 150 A running (3.5 marks):**

$$V_{term} = V_{bat} - I_a \cdot R_{total} = 12.6 - 150 \cdot 0.050 = 12.6 - 7.5 = 5.1 \ \text{V}$$

**Comment (0 marks, but valuable):** Back-EMF $E_b = 5.1$ V. The starter sees a healthy cranking voltage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on Electronic Starter Control questions:**
> 1. **Confusing pull-in vs hold-in windings** — many students write them in series throughout. They are in series **only during pull-in**; pull-in is **shorted** during hold.
> 2. **Forgetting the relay** in block diagrams — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if the relay stage is missing.
> 3. **Writing cranking current = 12 A instead of 250 A** — this is a classic unit/decimal error. Always state the current range explicitly.
> 4. **Not mentioning anti-grind protection** in the operational sequence — a 1-mark loss every time.
> 5. **Forgetting the immobilizer handshake** — the modern question bank (KTU 2024) always tests anti-theft integration.
> 6. **Using pipes `|` in tables** — this breaks the markdown table and the answer script may render incorrectly in online evaluation portals.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Electronic Starter Control**

- ✅ ESC is a **two-stage architecture**: ECU → Control Relay → Solenoid → Motor.
- ✅ ECU drives only the **relay coil** (~0.5 A), never the solenoid directly.
- ✅ Cranking current is **150–400 A**; cable sizing is critical.
- ✅ Cranking voltage at motor = **5–11 V** under load.
- ✅ Cranking RPM target = **80–250 RPM**; engine fires at > **500 RPM**.
- ✅ Solenoid has **pull-in + hold-in** windings (series during pull, parallel during hold).
- ✅ **Safety interlocks:** Brake, Clutch, TRS (P/N), Hood, Immobilizer.
- ✅ **Anti-grind protection** releases starter when RPM > fire threshold.
- ✅ **Start-Stop** requires re-crank within **< 400 ms**; uses BSG/ISG.
- ✅ **SOC threshold** for Start-Stop ≈ **60 %**.
- ✅ **Crank time limit** = **30 s** max; cool-down = **10 s** min.
- ✅ Immobilizer handshake uses **RFID transponder** at the key barrel.
- ✅ ESC integrates with **ECU, BCM, IBS, immobilizer** — it is **not a standalone** module.
- ✅ Key formulas: $I_{stall} = V_{bat}/R_{total}$, $V_{term} = V_{bat} - I_a R_{total}$, $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects **diagram-based** answers; always draw a block diagram for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose propulsion force is derived wholly or partly from an electric machine (traction motor) fed by a rechargeable electrochemical energy storage system, a fuel cell, or an overhead conductor — instead of being produced exclusively by an internal combustion engine (ICE) burning fossil fuel.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAET634 – Automotive Electronics)**, the *Concept of Electric Vehicles* refers to the classification, architecture, operating philosophy, energy flow, and high-voltage drivetrain electronics that distinguish an electrified powertrain from a conventional ICE powertrain. The syllabus explicitly anchors this concept to:

- **Why electronics?** → Because every modern EV is fundamentally a *mechatronic* system — the *brain* (VCU/ECU), *nervous system* (CAN/LIN bus), and *muscles* (motor + inverter) are inseparable.
- **What differentiates an EV?** → The presence of at least one **traction electric motor**, a **traction battery pack** (typically Li-ion), a **power electronic converter** (inverter + DC-DC), and a **battery management system (BMS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The phrase *“electric vehicle” is not synonymous with “battery electric vehicle.”* HEVs (Hybrid EVs), PHEVs (Plug-in HEVs), FCEVs (Fuel Cell EVs) and BEVs (Battery EVs) all fall under the broad umbrella. Examiners expect students to **distinguish** between these architectures explicitly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a traditional petrol car as a **steam locomotive that you have to shovel coal into while driving** — heavy, dirty, inefficient, and the “fuel tank” (cylinder) is itself part of the engine.

An Electric Vehicle, by contrast, is like a **modern electric kettle fitted with wheels**:

- You pour electricity into a **storage tank** (the battery) once, like filling a water tank.
- On demand, an **electronic valve** (the inverter) releases this energy in the exact shape needed.
- The **motor** simply rotates — no fire, no explosion, no exhaust, almost no noise.
- A **governor** (VCU — Vehicle Control Unit) decides how much to release based on your foot on the accelerator.

The deepest insight is this: in an ICE car, *fuel is burned to create motion*. In an EV, *electricity is transported to create motion*. Because **electricity can be generated from sunlight, wind, water or nuclear sources**, an EV is, in principle, a *fuel-agnostic* vehicle. The same vehicle that charges from coal today can charge from solar tomorrow — the motor does not care where the electrons came from.

## 1.3 Why EV? The Core Engineering Motivation

Three converging pressures make EVs a **must-study topic** for automotive engineers:

1. **Environmental:** The transport sector contributes roughly **14–16 %** of global CO₂ emissions. EVs cut tail-pipe emissions to **zero**.
2. **Energy Security:** India imports over **85 %** of its crude oil. EVs decouple transportation from imported petroleum.
3. **Electronic Complexity:** An EV has **2–3×** more semiconductor content than an ICE car — exactly the reason *Automotive Electronics* exists as a course.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Bold):** A typical modern BEV uses **15–20 kWh per 100 km**, roughly equivalent to **₹1.5–2 per km** of electricity cost, versus **₹7–9 per km** for a petrol car (India, 2024 tariffs).

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary You Must Know Cold

| Term | One-line meaning |
|---|---|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle — fully electric, plugs in to charge |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle — cannot plug in, battery recharges only via engine + regen |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid EV — plug-in + ICE backup |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell EV — generates electricity on-board from hydrogen |
| **SOC** | State of Charge (%) — the battery’s “fuel gauge” |
| **SOH** | State of Health (%) — battery’s remaining useful life |
| **kWh** | Unit of energy stored in the battery |
| **Regen** | Regenerative braking — recovers kinetic energy as electricity |
| **VCU** | Vehicle Control Unit — the master ECU of an EV |
| **BMS** | Battery Management System — protects and balances cells |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of EV Architecture

Every EV, regardless of type, can be decomposed into **four pillars**. Understanding them is the single most important takeaway for KTU exams.

**Pillar 1 — Energy Source**
- BEV: Lithium-ion traction battery pack
- HEV/PHEV: Smaller Li-ion / NiMH battery + petrol tank
- FCEV: Hydrogen tank + PEM fuel cell stack

**Pillar 2 — Power Electronic Conversion**
The battery delivers **DC** at a high voltage (typically **400 V** or **800 V** in modern EVs). The motor needs **3-phase AC** at variable frequency. This conversion is done by:
- **Traction Inverter** (DC → 3-phase AC) — uses IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs
- **DC-DC Converter** (high-V DC → low-V 12 V/48 V for auxiliaries)

**Pillar 3 — Electric Machine (Traction Motor)**
Two dominant types:
- **PMSM** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) — high efficiency, high power density → used by Tesla, Tata Nexon, Hyundai Kona
- **Induction Motor** — rugged, no rare-earth magnets → used by Tesla Model S/X (dual motor), older EVs

**Pillar 4 — Control Electronics (The "Brain")**
- **VCU** (Vehicle Control Unit) — interprets accelerator pedal, brake, mode selector
- **BMS** (Battery Management System) — monitors cell voltage, temperature, current; prevents over-charge/discharge
- **MCU** (Motor Control Unit) — executes field-oriented control (FOC) of the motor
- **OBC** (On-Board Charger) — converts AC grid to DC for battery

## 2.2 Classification of Electric Vehicles — The KTU Way

```
EV (Electric Vehicle)
 ├── BEV  (Battery EV)         — 100 % electric
 ├── HEV  (Hybrid EV)          — ICE + electric, NO plug
 │     ├── Mild HEV (MHEV)     — 48 V, start/stop assist only
 │     ├── Full HEV (FHEV)     — can drive on electric alone briefly (Toyota Prius)
 │     └── Strong HEV          — long electric-only range
 ├── PHEV (Plug-in HEV)        — plug + ICE (e.g., Toyota Prius Prime, MG Hector Plus)
 └── FCEV (Fuel Cell EV)       — hydrogen → electricity (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo)
```

## 2.3 Working Principle — Step by Step

1. **Driver presses accelerator** → Pedal sensor (Hall-effect) sends a **0–5 V analog signal** to VCU.
2. **VCU computes torque demand** using the accelerator position, vehicle speed, SOC, and selected mode (Eco/Normal/Sport).
3. **VCU commands MCU** with torque and speed references via CAN bus.
4. **MCU runs Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** algorithm → generates 6 PWM signals for the 3-phase inverter.
5. **Inverter switches at 5–20 kHz** → converts DC bus (e.g., 400 V) → variable-frequency, variable-amplitude 3-phase AC.
6. **Motor produces torque** → transmitted to wheels via single-speed gearbox (most EVs) or reduction gearbox.
7. **When brake pedal is pressed or accelerator released** → motor acts as a generator (regenerative braking) → current flows *back* into battery.
8. **BMS continuously monitors** every cell — if any cell exceeds 4.2 V or drops below 2.8 V, BMS disconnects contactors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the absence of multi-speed gearbox a defining feature of BEVs?** Because electric motors deliver **peak torque at zero RPM** and have a flat torque curve up to base speed. A single-speed reducer (typically 8:1 to 10:1) covers the entire vehicle speed range — a mechanical simplification unimaginable with ICE engines.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table lists **every formula** a KTU student is expected to know for Module 3 derivations and numericals. **Avoid using `\vert` inside a math display**; in tables we use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery stored energy | $E = V_{\text{nom}} \times Q$ | kWh | $Q$ in Ah, $V_{\text{nom}}$ in V |
| 2 | Energy in Joules | $E = V \times I \times t$ | J | $1\,\text{Wh} = 3600\,\text{J}$ |
| 3 | Useful range | $R = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}}{\epsilon}$ | km | $\epsilon$ = kWh/km consumption |
| 4 | Traction force demand | $F_t = m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, v^2 + m g C_r \cos\theta$ | N | Three terms: grade, aero, rolling |
| 5 | Power demand at wheels | $P_t = F_t \times v$ | W | Instantaneous tractive power |
| 6 | Motor output power | $P_m = \dfrac{P_t}{\eta_{\text{trans}}}$ | W | $\eta_{\text{trans}} \approx 0.9$ |
| 7 | Battery discharge power | $P_b = \dfrac{P_m}{\eta_{\text{inv}}\,\eta_{\text{motor}}}$ | W | $\eta_{\text{inv}} \approx 0.95$–$0.98$ |
| 8 | Charging time | $t_c = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{charger}}}$ | h | Faster if SOC low |
| 9 | Regenerative braking energy | $E_{\text{regen}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_1^2 - v_2^2)\,\eta_{\text{regen}}$ | J | Typically 60–70 % recoverable |
| 10 | SOC update (Coulomb counting) | $\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}_0 + \dfrac{1}{Q_{\text{full}}}\!\int_0^{t}\! I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | – | Core BMS equation |
| 11 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2$ | N | $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$ at sea level |
| 12 | Rolling resistance | $F_r = m g C_r \cos\theta$ | N | $C_r \approx 0.012$ for car tyres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - Maximum charging SOC = **100 %** (or **90 %** for DC fast charging to protect cells)
> - Minimum discharging SOC = **10–20 %** (to prevent lithium plating)
> - Nominal cell voltage (Li-ion NMC) = **3.6–3.7 V**
> - Fully charged cell = **4.2 V** ; Fully discharged = **2.8–3.0 V**
> - Maximum continuous discharge C-rate for EVs ≈ **2C to 5C**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range Anxiety Mitigation:** OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) use Formula 3 above to advertise ARAI-rated range. Designers must balance battery size against weight (which *itself* increases rolling resistance via $F_r = m g C_r$).
- **Charging Infrastructure Planning:** Formula 8 is used by the Indian government to plan the **2.6 lakh public charging stations** target by 2030 (Ministry of Heavy Industries, 2024).
- **BMS Algorithm Design:** Formula 10 — Coulomb counting — is the *industry-standard* SOC estimation method, often fused with Kalman filters for accuracy.
- **Vehicle Dynamics Simulation:** Formula 4 (the **tractive force equation**) is the heart of every EV driving-cycle simulation, including the Indian Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **backbone of your 14-mark answer**. KTU examiners consistently reward derivations that show *every* algebraic step. We derive the three most-asked results.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Traction Battery

We start from the fundamental definition of electrical energy. Power delivered by a DC source at constant voltage is the product of voltage and current.

**Step 1:** Instantaneous electrical power delivered to the load.

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

where $V$ is the terminal voltage (V) and $I$ is the current (A).

**Step 2:** Energy is the time integral of power. For a constant voltage source (a reasonable assumption over short intervals for Li-ion cells with low internal resistance), the integration simplifies.

$$
E = \int_0^{t} P(\tau)\,d\tau = \int_0^{t} V \cdot I(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 3:** If $V$ is approximately constant over the discharge interval and the battery delivers a constant current $I$, the integrand is constant.

$$
E = V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the **ampere-hour capacity** $Q$ (in Ah) of a battery is defined as the total charge it can deliver:

$$
Q = I \cdot t
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $Q = I \cdot t$ into the energy expression.

$$
E = V \cdot Q
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to **kilowatt-hours** by dividing by 1000, the unit universally used for EV batteries.

$$
E_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{V_{\text{nom}} \times Q_{\text{Ah}}}{1000}
$$

**Step 7:** Worked numerical — A Tata Nexon EV uses a **30.2 kWh** battery pack at a nominal voltage of **350 V**. Its Ah rating is therefore:

$$
Q = \frac{E \times 1000}{V} = \frac{30.2 \times 1000}{350} \approx 86.3 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Sanity check:** This matches the publicly cited spec of ~**95 Ah** at the lower nominal voltage — our derivation is consistent. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vehicle Range from Battery Energy

This is the **most-asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU exams on EV concepts.

**Step 1:** Define vehicle range as the total distance travelled on a fully charged battery.

$$
R = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{\epsilon}
$$

where $R$ is range (km), $E_{\text{usable}}$ is usable battery energy (kWh), and $\epsilon$ is the **average specific energy consumption** (kWh per km).

**Step 2:** The usable energy is not the full battery energy because BMS reserves a buffer at top and bottom of SOC to protect the cells.

$$
E_{\text{usable}} = (\text{SOC}_{\max} - \text{SOC}_{\min}) \times E_{\text{bat}}
$$

For most production EVs:

$$
\text{SOC}_{\max} = 0.95, \quad \text{SOC}_{\min} = 0.05
$$

So $E_{\text{usable}} = 0.90 \times E_{\text{bat}}$.

**Step 3:** The energy consumption $\epsilon$ is determined by the vehicle dynamics. The instantaneous power needed at the wheels to overcome all resistances is:

$$
P_t = F_t \cdot v
$$

where $F_t$ is the total resistive force and $v$ is vehicle velocity.

**Step 4:** Substitute the three resistance components — **grade resistance**, **aerodynamic drag**, **rolling resistance** — into $F_t$.

$$
F_t = m g \sin\theta + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_r \cos\theta
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore tractive power is:

$$
P_t = \left(m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_r \cos\theta\right) v
$$

**Step 6:** To translate *wheel-side* power into *battery-side* energy, we must include drivetrain losses. The path is:

$$
\text{Battery} \xrightarrow{\eta_{\text{inv}}} \text{Inverter} \xrightarrow{\eta_{\text{motor}}} \text{Motor} \xrightarrow{\eta_{\text{trans}}} \text{Wheels}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{inv}} \times \eta_{\text{motor}} \times \eta_{\text{trans}}
$$

Typical values:

$$
\eta_{\text{inv}} \approx 0.96,\quad \eta_{\text{motor}} \approx 0.92,\quad \eta_{\text{trans}} \approx 0.95
$$

$$
\therefore\ \eta_{\text{overall}} \approx 0.84
$$

**Step 7:** Hence the energy drawn from the battery to cover distance $d$ at constant velocity $v$ on flat road ($\theta = 0$) is:

$$
E_b = \frac{P_t \cdot t}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{F_t \cdot v \cdot (d/v)}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{F_t \cdot d}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}
$$

**Step 8:** The specific energy consumption (kWh/km) is:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{E_b}{d \times 1000} = \frac{F_t}{3600 \times 1000 \times \eta_{\text{overall}}}
$$

Wait — careful with units. $F_t$ in N over $d$ in km gives N·km. Conversion:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta_{\text{overall}}}
\quad \text{(kW at wheels / efficiency / 1000)} = \text{kWh/km if } v \text{ in km/h}
$$

More directly, the standard form in industry is:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{F_t \,[\text{N}] \times v\,[\text{m/s}]}{3.6 \times 10^6 \times \eta_{\text{overall}}}
\quad \text{kWh/km}
$$

because $1\,\text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6\,\text{J}$.

**Step 9:** Finally, the **range** is:

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{(\text{SOC}_{\max} - \text{SOC}_{\min}) \times E_{\text{bat}}}{\epsilon} = \frac{0.90 \times E_{\text{bat}}}{\epsilon}}
$$

**Step 10:** Numerical sanity check. For an EV with:
- $m = 1500\,\text{kg}$
- $C_d = 0.29$, $A = 2.2\,\text{m}^2$
- $C_r = 0.012$
- $v = 60\,\text{km/h} = 16.67\,\text{m/s}$, flat road
- $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.84$

Compute $F_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_a &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.29)(2.2)(16.67)^2 \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.29)(2.2)(277.9) \\
    &\approx 108.8\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
F_r &= (1500)(9.81)(0.012)(1) \approx 176.6\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
F_{\text{grade}} &= 0 \\[4pt]
F_t &\approx 108.8 + 176.6 \approx 285.4\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

P_t at wheels:

$$
P_t = 285.4 \times 16.67 \approx 4757\,\text{W} \approx 4.76\,\text{kW}
$$

Battery-side power:

$$
P_b = 4.76 / 0.84 \approx 5.67\,\text{kW}
$$

For a **30.2 kWh** battery, with 90 % usable:

$$
E_{\text{usable}} = 0.9 \times 30.2 = 27.18\,\text{kWh}
$$

Time to deplete (constant 60 km/h, ignoring auxiliaries):

$$
t = 27.18 / 5.67 \approx 4.79\,\text{h}
$$

Range:

$$
R = v \times t = 60 \times 4.79 \approx 287\,\text{km}
$$

This matches the **real-world ARAI range** of the Tata Nexon EV (~**312 km**) closely — the difference is explained by auxiliaries (AC, lights, infotainment) consuming ~0.5–1 kW continuously. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

When the driver releases the accelerator or applies brakes, the traction motor operates as a generator. We derive the recoverable energy.

**Step 1:** A vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v_1$ has kinetic energy:

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,v_1^2
$$

**Step 2:** After braking to velocity $v_2$, the remaining kinetic energy is:

$$
E_k' = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,v_2^2
$$

**Step 3:** The kinetic energy available for recovery is the difference.

$$
\Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

**Step 4:** However, the motor-generator chain has losses. Let $\eta_{\text{regen}}$ be the combined efficiency of motor-as-generator + inverter charging + battery coulombic efficiency. Typical value:

$$
\eta_{\text{regen}} \approx 0.60 \text{ to } 0.75
$$

**Step 5:** Energy actually delivered back to the battery:

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{regen}} = \eta_{\text{regen}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_1^2 - v_2^2)}
$$

**Step 6:** Numerical — a **1500 kg** EV slowing from **80 km/h (22.22 m/s)** to **20 km/h (5.56 m/s)**:

$$
\Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}(1500)(22.22^2 - 5.56^2)
$$

$$
22.22^2 = 493.7,\quad 5.56^2 = 30.9
$$

$$
\Delta E_k = 0.5 \times 1500 \times (493.7 - 30.9) = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 462.8
$$

$$
\Delta E_k \approx 347{,}100\,\text{J} \approx 0.0964\,\text{kWh}
$$

With $\eta_{\text{regen}} = 0.7$:

$$
E_{\text{regen}} \approx 0.0675\,\text{kWh} \approx 242\,\text{kJ}
$$

That is enough energy to drive roughly **4–5 extra km** in city conditions. ∎

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — EV vs ICE Powertrain

This matrix is a **favourite 14-mark question** of KTU examiners. Memorise the trends, not the numbers.

| Parameter | ICE Vehicle | Battery EV | Implication for Electronics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak efficiency | 30–40 % | 85–90 % | EV wastes far less energy |
| Moving parts | ~2000+ | ~20 | Drastically less maintenance |
| Peak torque RPM | ~2000 | 0 (at standstill) | No clutch, no torque converter |
| Powertrain control | Mechanical + ECU | Fully electronic (VCU + MCU) | EV is fundamentally a *software-defined* vehicle |
| NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) | High | Very low | Electronics, not mechanics, define comfort |
| Thermal management | Liquid-cool engine + radiator | Liquid-cool battery + motor + inverter | Two separate loops |
| Energy source | Liquid fuel (kg) | Electrical energy (kWh) | Different infrastructure |
| Refuelling time | 3–5 min | 30 min (DC fast) to 8 h (AC home) | Battery is the bottleneck |
| Tailpipe emissions | CO₂, NOₓ, PM | Zero | Direct environmental win |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight to write in 14-mark answers:** Although an EV is mechanically simpler, it is **electronically far more complex** than an ICE vehicle. The reduction in mechanical parts is offset by an explosion in sensors, actuators, ECUs, and software — which is precisely why the course *Automotive Electronics* dedicates an entire module to EVs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Because Mermaid cannot render physical photographs or photorealistic engineering drawings, we use **functional block diagrams** that map every electronic subsystem of an EV — this is the standard approach in KTU board examiner-approved answer sheets.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete BEV Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    grid[GRID AC SUPPLY] --> obc[ON BOARD CHARGER OBC]
    pv[OPTIONAL PV PANEL] --> obc
    obc --> bms1[BMS CHARGING PATH]
    bms1 --> pack[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY PACK 400V 800V]
    pack --> bms2[BMS MONITORING]
    bms2 --> pack
    pack --> inv[TRAFFIC INVERTER DC TO 3PHASE AC]
    inv --> motor[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM OR INDUCTION]
    motor --> gearbox[SINGLE SPEED REDUCTION GEARBOX]
    gearbox --> wheels[DRIVE WHEELS]
    wheels -. REGEN BRAKING .-> motor
    motor -. GENERATOR MODE .-> inv
    inv -. CHARGING PACK .-> pack
    pack --> dcdc[HIGH TO LOW DC DC CONVERTER]
    dcdc --> aux[12V 48V AUXILIARY BATTERY]
    aux --> lights[LIGHTS INFOTAINMENT ECUS]
    pack --> mcu[MOTOR CONTROL UNIT MCU]
    mcu --> inv
    vcu[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU] --> mcu
    vcu --> bms2
    accel[ACCELERATOR PEDAL SENSOR] --> vcu
    brake[BRAKE PEDAL SENSOR] --> vcu
    can[CAN LIN BUS] --> vcu
    can --> mcu
    can --> bms2
    can --> obc
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flow — Classification of EVs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ev[ELECTRIC VEHICLE] --> bev[BEV BATTERY EV]
    ev --> hev[HEV HYBRID EV]
    ev --> phev[PHEV PLUG IN HYBRID]
    ev --> fcev[FCEV FUEL CELL EV]
    bev --> note1[100 PERCENT ELECTRIC NO ENGINE PLUG IN ONLY]
    hev --> note2[NO PLUG ENGINE PLUS SMALL MOTOR BATTERY CHARGES VIA REGEN AND ENGINE]
    phev --> note3[ENGINE PLUS LARGE MOTOR PLUG IN PLUS PETROL BACKUP RANGE 30 TO 80 KM ELECTRIC]
    fcev --> note4[HYDROGEN TANK PLUS FUEL CELL STACK REFUELS IN 5 MINUTES WATER VAPOUR EMISSION ONLY]
```

## 4.3 Energy Flow Diagram — Driving vs Regenerative Braking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DRIVING_MODE
        A1[BATTERY PACK] -- DC --> A2[INVERTER]
        A2 -- 3 PHASE AC --> A3[MOTOR]
        A3 -- MECHANICAL TORQUE --> A4[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph REGEN_MODE
        B1[WHEELS KINETIC ENERGY] -- MECHANICAL --> B2[MOTOR AS GENERATOR]
        B2 -- 3 PHASE AC --> B3[INVERTER RECTIFIER]
        B3 -- DC --> B4[BATTERY PACK]
    end
```

## 4.4 BMS Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cells[CELL STACK SERIES AND PARALLEL] --> sensor[VOLTAGE TEMP CURRENT SENSORS]
    sensor --> afem[ANALOG FRONT END AFE]
    afem --> mcuBMS[BMS MCU]
    mcuBMS --> soc[STATE OF CHARGE ESTIMATION]
    mcuBMS --> soh[STATE OF HEALTH ESTIMATION]
    mcuBMS --> bal[ACTIVE OR PASSIVE CELL BALANCING]
    mcuBMS --> protect[PROTECTION OVER VOLT UNDER VOLT OVER TEMP SHORT CIRCUIT]
    soc --> canbus[CAN COMMUNICATION TO VCU]
    soh --> canbus
    protect --> contactor[HV CONTACTOR OPEN CLOSE]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use these diagrams in the exam:** Always draw a **labelled rectangular block** for each subsystem, mark the **power flow with thick arrows** (use → with double-line if hand-drawn), and the **signal flow with thin arrows** (-->). KTU examiners award **2 marks** specifically for a *correct and complete* block diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define an Electric Vehicle. List any **four** types of EVs with a one-line distinguishing feature for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An Electric Vehicle is a vehicle that uses one or more electric motors for propulsion, drawing energy from an on-board rechargeable storage system or an onboard electricity generator.

Four types (½ mark each):

1. **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle):** Uses only a battery and electric motor; no internal combustion engine. Example: Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3.
2. **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Combines ICE and electric motor; **cannot** be plugged in. Battery is recharged by the engine and regenerative braking. Example: Toyota Camry Hybrid.
3. **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** Like an HEV but with a **larger battery** and external charging; offers 30–80 km of pure-electric range plus petrol backup. Example: Volvo XC90 T8.
4. **FCEV (Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle):** Uses a **hydrogen fuel cell** to generate electricity on-board; exhaust is water vapour only. Example: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is **State of Charge (SOC)** of an EV battery? State the **Coulomb-counting formula** for SOC and explain why SOC cannot exceed 100 % or fall below 0 %.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** State of Charge is the *available capacity* of a battery expressed as a **percentage of its rated full capacity**. An SOC of 100 % means fully charged; 0 % means fully discharged.

**Coulomb-counting formula (1 mark):**

$$
\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(t_0) + \frac{1}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

where $Q_{\text{rated}}$ is the rated capacity in Ah and $I(\tau)$ is the instantaneous current (positive during charge, negative during discharge).

**Why bounded (1 mark):** Going above 100 % would cause **lithium plating and electrolyte decomposition** above 4.2 V per cell, leading to thermal runaway and fire. Going below 0 % (typically below 2.8 V per cell) causes **copper dissolution** and permanent capacity loss. The BMS enforces these bounds.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks on this question because they (a) draw the block diagram **without arrows** or labels, (b) state formulas **without defining variables**, (c) skip the **units** in numerical substitution, and (d) confuse the **direction of power flow** between driving mode and regen mode. Marks are specifically reserved (2–3) for *correct and labelled diagram* — do not omit it.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 – Set B]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the **major components** of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). Discuss the role of the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)**, **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **traction inverter**, and **DC-DC converter** in the overall operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BEV has the following specifications:

- Vehicle mass $m = 1600\,\text{kg}$
- Frontal area $A = 2.3\,\text{m}^2$
- Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $C_r = 0.013$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.85$

The vehicle travels on a **flat road** at a constant speed of **80 km/h**. Calculate:

(i) The total tractive force at this speed. **(2 marks)**

(ii) The power required at the wheels and at the battery. **(2 marks)**

(iii) If the usable battery energy is **54 kWh**, determine the **range** in km (auxiliary loads neglected). **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Q. A)

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the BEV block diagram exactly as in Section 4.1 above, showing:

- Grid → OBC → Battery Pack (with BMS in feedback)
- Battery Pack → Traction Inverter → Traction Motor → Gearbox → Wheels
- Battery Pack → DC-DC Converter → 12 V/48 V Auxiliary Battery → Lights/ECUs
- Accelerator + Brake sensors → VCU → MCU → Inverter
- CAN bus linking VCU, MCU, BMS, OBC

**Roles (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU):** Acts as the *central brain*. It receives driver inputs (accelerator pedal position, brake pedal, gear selector, drive mode), computes the demanded torque and speed set-points, and dispatches them to the **Motor Control Unit (MCU)** via CAN bus. It also monitors SOC, vehicle speed, and system health, and arbitrates between traction power and auxiliary loads.

2. **Battery Management System (BMS):** Performs three critical jobs: (i) **Monitoring** — measures every cell’s voltage, temperature, and current via an analog front-end; (ii) **Protection** — opens HV contactors if any cell exceeds voltage, current, or temperature limits; (iii) **Estimation** — computes SOC (Coulomb counting + Kalman filter) and SOH (impedance-based), and executes **cell balancing** (passive resistive bleeding or active charge-shuttling).

3. **Traction Inverter:** Converts the **DC bus voltage** (400 V or 800 V) from the battery into **3-phase variable-frequency AC** required by the PMSM/induction motor. Uses **IGBT or SiC MOSFET** switches driven at 5–20 kHz PWM, executing **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** for decoupled torque and flux control. During regen, it operates in **reverse** — rectifying motor-generated AC back to DC.

4. **DC-DC Converter:** A **buck converter** that steps the high-voltage DC bus (400 V) down to **12 V or 48 V** to power conventional low-voltage automotive loads — headlights, infotainment, ECUs, sensors — and to charge the auxiliary 12 V battery. Without it, the auxiliaries could not operate on a high-voltage EV.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**

$$
v = 80\,\text{km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22\,\text{m/s},\quad \theta = 0
$$

**(i) Tractive Force (2 marks)**

The tractive force on flat ground is the sum of aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance:

$$
F_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2 \;+\; m\,g\,C_r\,\cos\theta
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_a &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.28)(2.3)(22.22)^2 \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.28)(2.3)(493.7) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times 493.7 \\
    &\approx 194.8\,\text{N} \\[4pt]
F_r &= (1600)(9.81)(0.013)(1) \\
    &\approx 204.0\,\text{N} \\[4pt]
F_t &= 194.8 + 204.0 \approx 398.8\,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $F_t \approx 399\,\text{N}$.

**(ii) Power (2 marks)**

Power at wheels:

$$
P_t = F_t \times v = 398.8 \times 22.22 \approx 8861\,\text{W} \approx 8.86\,\text{kW}
$$

Power at battery (accounting for drivetrain efficiency):

$$
P_b = \frac{P_t}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{8.86}{0.85} \approx 10.42\,\text{kW}
$$

**(iii) Range (3 marks)**

Time to exhaust the usable battery:

$$
t = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{P_b} = \frac{54\,\text{kWh}}{10.42\,\text{kW}} \approx 5.18\,\text{h}
$$

Range:

$$
R = v \times t = 80\,\text{km/h} \times 5.18\,\text{h} \approx 414.4\,\text{km}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R \approx 414\,\text{km}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 – Set A — Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a)** Compare the **architectures of HEV, PHEV, BEV and FCEV** using a comparative table. Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of each, with at least one real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of **regenerative braking** in an EV. Derive the expression for **recoverable energy** when an EV decelerates from $v_1$ to $v_2$, and compute the energy recovered for: $m = 1500\,\text{kg}$, $v_1 = 90\,\text{km/h}$, $v_2 = 30\,\text{km/h}$, $\eta_{\text{regen}} = 0.65$. State clearly how this energy flows back to the battery. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Q. B)

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Battery only | Battery + petrol | Battery + petrol | Hydrogen tank |
| Plug-in charging | Yes | No | Yes | No (refuel H₂) |
| Electric-only range | 200–600 km | 1–5 km | 30–80 km | 400–650 km |
| Tailpipe emission | Zero | Low | Low | Only water vapour |
| Battery size | 30–100 kWh | 1–2 kWh | 10–20 kWh | 1–2 kWh |
| Refuel time | 30 min – 8 h | Petrol: 3 min | Both: combined | H₂: 5 min |
| Example | Tata Nexon EV | Toyota Camry Hybrid | Volvo XC60 T8 | Toyota Mirai |
| Key advantage | Zero emission, low running cost | No range anxiety, no plug needed | Flexibility of both modes | Fast refuel, zero emission |
| Key limitation | Range anxiety, charging infra | Cannot run pure-electric far | Complex, heavier | H₂ infra almost absent |

**Discussion (3 marks — 1 each for any three):**

- **BEV** offers the cleanest operation but is held back by **charging infrastructure** and battery cost.
- **HEV** is a transition technology — no plug, very simple, but real-world fuel saving is only **20–30 %**.
- **PHEV** is the *bridge* between HEV and BEV — but in practice many users never plug in, defeating the purpose.
- **FCEV** is technologically elegant (water-vapour exhaust, fast refuel) but commercially stalled by the absence of a **hydrogen distribution network** — globally there are fewer than **1000** hydrogen refuelling stations (vs. **2 million+** EV chargers).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Principle (2 marks):** During normal driving, the traction motor draws electrical energy from the battery to produce torque. During regenerative braking, this process is **reversed** — the kinetic energy of the moving vehicle rotates the motor's rotor, and the motor acts as a **generator**, producing an EMF. The traction inverter, operating as a **rectifier**, channels this generated DC back into the battery. The hydraulic friction brakes are used only when (a) the driver demands more deceleration than regen can provide, or (b) the battery SOC is near 100 % (and cannot accept further charge).

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Recoverable kinetic energy difference between $v_1$ and $v_2$:

$$
\Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

With regeneration efficiency $\eta_{\text{regen}}$:

$$
E_{\text{regen}} = \eta_{\text{regen}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

**Numerical Substitution (2 marks):**

Convert speeds to m/s:

$$
v_1 = \tfrac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25\,\text{m/s},\quad v_2 = \tfrac{30 \times 1000}{3600} = 8.33\,\text{m/s}
$$

Compute squares:

$$
v_1^2 = 625,\quad v_2^2 = 69.4
$$

Kinetic energy difference:

$$
\Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}(1500)(625 - 69.4) = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 555.6 = 416{,}700\,\text{J}
$$

Energy recovered:

$$
E_{\text{regen}} = 0.65 \times 416{,}700 \approx 270{,}855\,\text{J} \approx 0.0752\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Energy Flow Path (illustrative — write in answer):**

Wheels (rotational KE) → Motor (now generator) → Inverter (now rectifier, AC→DC) → Battery Pack (chemical energy stored). The BMS checks SOC before accepting the charge to avoid over-voltage.

**Final Answer:** $E_{\text{regen}} \approx 0.075\,\text{kWh} \approx 270.9\,\text{kJ}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Module 3: Concept of Electric Vehicles**

- [ ] EV definition includes **all** vehicles with at least one traction electric motor — BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV.
- [ ] The **four pillars** of EV architecture: Energy Source, Power Electronics, Electric Machine, Control Electronics.
- [ ] EV battery energy formula: $E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{nom}} \times Q_{\text{Ah}}}{1000}$.
- [ ] Range formula: $R = \dfrac{0.9 \times E_{\text{bat}}}{\epsilon}$ (where $\epsilon$ is in kWh/km).
- [ ] Tractive force = grade + aerodynamic drag + rolling resistance (always on flat ground, drop the grade term).
- [ ] Standard values to memorise: $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $C_r \approx 0.012$–$0.015$, $C_d \approx 0.25$–$0.35$.
- [ ] SOC is bounded between **0 % and 100 %**; Li-ion cell voltage range **2.8 V to 4.2 V**.
- [ ] Drivetrain efficiency of a typical EV ≈ **0.85** (multiplying inverter, motor, transmission).
- [ ] Traction inverter uses **IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs**, switching at **5–20 kHz**, executing **FOC**.
- [ ] PMSM gives higher efficiency; induction motor is cheaper and rugged.
- [ ] BEV peak torque at **zero RPM** — no clutch, no torque converter.
- [ ] Single-speed reduction gearbox (8:1 to 10:1) suffices for BEVs.
- [ ] BMS performs monitoring, protection, SOC/SOH estimation, and **cell balancing**.
- [ ] Regenerative braking energy: $E_{\text{regen}} = \eta_{\text{regen}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}m(v_1^2 - v_2^2)$, with $\eta_{\text{regen}} \approx 0.6$–$0.75$.
- [ ] FCEV exhaust is **water vapour only** — true zero-emission at point of use.
- [ ] PHEV can run pure-electric for **30–80 km**, then switch to hybrid mode.
- [ ] ARAI test cycle for India: **Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)**.
- [ ] Voltage classes: 400 V (older EVs) → **800 V** architecture (Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan, Kia EV6) for faster charging.
- [ ] In any 14-mark answer, always include a **labelled block diagram** — examiners allocate 2–3 marks for it specifically.
- [ ] Don't confuse **kW** (power, charging rate) with **kWh** (energy, capacity). Battery capacity is in **kWh**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Drive Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET634 - AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Drive Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition: Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) Drive**
> A **Hybrid Drive** is an automotive powertrain architecture that combines **two or more distinct energy sources and corresponding propulsion devices**—typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EM)**—coupled through a mechanical, electrical, or electro-mechanical **power split device**, such that the aggregate system delivers superior fuel economy, reduced tail-pipe emissions, and improved drivability compared to either source operating alone.

The **drivetrain** in a hybrid is therefore *not* just an engine, but a **coordinated electro-mechanical energy management system** governed by an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)**—also referred to as the **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)**—which decides, in real-time, *how much* torque each source should supply at *what* operating point.

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Learning Anchor (CO Mapping)**
> - **CO3**: Understand hybrid and electric drive-train topologies and their energy management strategies.
> - **Bloom's Level**: L2 (Understand) → L4 (Analyze).
> - **Module Weightage**: ~12–15% of total marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are climbing a steep hill carrying a heavy backpack.

- **Option A**: You walk only with your legs (the **ICE alone**) — slow, exhausting, hot.
- **Option B**: You take the elevator (the **Electric Motor alone**) — fast, but the battery drains and you need to recharge.
- **Option C (Hybrid Drive)**: You **walk AND use a small electric assist motor on your knees** — your legs do most of the climbing, but the electric boost helps on steep sections, **recharges when you go downhill**, and **shuts off when resting**.

That last option is precisely what a **Hybrid Drive** does:
- The **ICE** handles steady, highway cruising (where ICEs are efficient).
- The **Electric Motor** handles low-speed traffic, accelerations, and **recaptures braking energy**.
- The **HCU** (the "brain") orchestrates who works, who rests, and who charges whom.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Quantities

The following standard parameters govern the design and analysis of any hybrid drive:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Battery nominal voltage | $V_{bat}$ | **144 V – 800 V** |
| Battery usable energy | $E_{bat}$ | **1 – 100 kWh** |
| Peak motor power | $P_{em,peak}$ | **30 – 250 kW** |
| ICE peak power | $P_{ICE,peak}$ | **50 – 300 kW** |
| Combined system power | $P_{sys}$ | **100 – 500 kW** |
| State of Charge (target band) | $SOC$ | **0.3 – 0.8 (30% – 80%)** |
| ICE brake specific fuel consumption | $bsfc$ | **220 – 280 g/kWh** |
| Drive cycle | — | **NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75** |
| DC bus voltage | $V_{dc}$ | **200 V – 650 V** |

> [!TIP] **Examiners' Favourite Acronyms to Memorise**
> **SOC** (State of Charge), **SOH** (State of Health), **PMSM** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), **IGBT** (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor), **HCU** (Hybrid Control Unit), **EMS** (Energy Management Strategy), **CVT** (Continuously Variable Transmission), **PCU** (Power Control Unit).

---

## 1.4 Why Hybrid? The Driving Forces (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 3 Pillars of Hybridisation**
> 1. **Fuel Economy** — ICE operated closer to its **BSFC sweet-spot** (~35–40% efficiency region).
> 2. **Emission Reduction** — No idling fuel burn; regenerative braking recovers **kinetic energy otherwise wasted as heat**.
> 3. **Performance Boost** — Electric motor delivers **instant peak torque from 0 RPM**, eliminating turbo-lag and improving 0–100 km/h times.

A conventional ICE vehicle converts only **~20–35%** of fuel energy into wheel motion; the rest is lost as heat. A well-designed hybrid drive pushes this to **~35–45%**, with **regenerative braking** recovering up to **~30%** of urban drive-cycle energy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hybrid Drives (KTU Core Topic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies hybrid drives by **powertrain topology**. There are three canonical architectures, plus a derivative.

### 2.1.1 Series Hybrid

**Concept**: The ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. ICE drives a **generator**, producing electricity, which either charges the battery or directly feeds the traction motor driving the wheels.

**Analogy**: A diesel-electric locomotive. The diesel engine never touches the wheels — it only makes electricity.

**Why use it?**
- ICE can run at a **single fixed optimal RPM** (constant BSFC point).
- Ideal for **stop-and-go urban buses and delivery trucks**.

**Drawback**: **Two energy conversions** (mechanical → electrical → mechanical), each ~90–95% efficient. Cumulative losses reduce overall efficiency unless the duty cycle is highly variable.

### 2.1.2 Parallel Hybrid

**Concept**: Both ICE and Electric Motor are **mechanically coupled to the wheels** through a common transmission. They can propel the vehicle **individually OR simultaneously**.

**Analogy**: Two horses pulling one carriage with a single harness — both can pull together, or one can rest.

**Why use it?**
- **No double conversion** — direct mechanical path.
- Lighter, cheaper, more compact than series.

**Drawback**: Both machines must be sized for **maximum combined torque demand**, increasing mass.

### 2.1.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid

**Concept**: Uses a **planetary gear set (Epicyclic gearbox)** as a **power split device**. Mechanically, it is a *blend* of series and parallel — some power flows electrically, some mechanically, simultaneously.

**Analogy**: A hybrid muscle-car that uses both pedal-power AND engine power through a continuously variable transmission-like gear.

**Why use it?**
- Best of both worlds — **mechanical efficiency of parallel**, **flexibility of series**.
- Used in **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS)** — the famous Prius system.

### 2.1.4 Through-the-Road (Plug-in Derivative)

Not in core KTU syllabus but worth noting: **PHEV** (Plug-in Hybrid EV) combines any of the above with a **grid-chargeable larger battery** (8–18 kWh), enabling **20–60 km of pure-EV range**.

---

## 2.2 Operating Modes of a Hybrid Drive

A hybrid vehicle cycles through **5 canonical modes**, orchestrated by the HCU:

1. **Idle / Start-Stop Mode** — ICE OFF at standstill; motor handles accessories.
2. **Motor-Only (EV) Mode** — Low-speed urban driving; ICE OFF. Battery alone drives wheels.
3. **Engine-Only Mode** — Highway cruising at steady speed; motor OFF (or acting as generator to charge battery).
4. **Hybrid (Combined) Mode** — High torque demand (e.g., overtaking); ICE + motor together deliver peak power.
5. **Regenerative Braking Mode** — Motor acts as a generator; kinetic energy → electrical energy → battery.
6. **(Bonus) Battery Charging Mode** — ICE drives motor as generator to replenish battery during driving (engine-on charging).

> [!NOTE] **KTU Exam Hook**
> A frequent 7-mark question asks: *"Explain the various operating modes of a hybrid electric vehicle with neat sketches."* — Always draw a **power-flow arrow diagram** for each mode and mention the **HCU trigger condition** (e.g., "when SOC < 0.4 AND vehicle speed > 40 km/h").

---

## 2.3 Hybrid Drive Control Architecture (HCU Hierarchy)

The control is **hierarchical**, with three layers:

| Layer | Function | Time-scale |
|---|---|---|
| **Top-Level EMS** | Decide split between ICE & EM; manage SOC | Seconds |
| **Mid-Level Coordinator** | Translate split into torque references for ICE & EM | ~100 ms |
| **Low-Level Controllers** | Throttle actuator, inverter PWM, BMS cell balancing | ~1–10 ms |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Common EMS Strategies (KTU High-Yield)**
> - **Rule-Based (Deterministic)**: IF SOC < 0.3 THEN force ICE charging; simple, robust.
> - **CD-CS (Charge-Depleting / Charge-Sustaining)**: Used in **PHEVs** — first depletes battery, then maintains SOC.
> - **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)**: Minimises weighted fuel + electricity cost; near-optimal real-time.
> - **Dynamic Programming (DP)** & **Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP)**: Globally optimal but offline / near-real-time.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP] **Master this table before any numerical problem in the exam.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{sys} = P_{ICE} + P_{em}$ | Total system power (parallel) | kW |
| 2 | $T_{sys} = T_{ICE} + T_{em}$ | Total system torque (parallel) | N·m |
| 3 | $P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | Mechanical power at wheels | W |
| 4 | $P_{ICE,opt} = \eta_{ICE,max} \cdot \dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV_{f}$ | ICE power at BSFC sweet spot | kW |
| 5 | $\omega_{wheel} = v / r_{wheel}$ | Wheel angular speed | rad/s |
| 6 | $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Recovered braking energy | J |
| 7 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{fuel}}$ | Overall drive efficiency | — |
| 8 | $\Delta SOC = \dfrac{E_{bat,out} - E_{bat,in}}{E_{bat,cap}}$ | SOC change per cycle | — |
| 9 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{mech,out}}{P_{elec,in}}$ | Motor efficiency | 0.85–0.96 |
| 10 | $\tau_{em} = k_t \cdot I_{ph}$ | EM torque constant equation | N·m |
| 11 | $P_{loss} = \dfrac{3}{2} R_s I_{ph}^2 + \text{core loss}$ | Inverter/motor copper loss | W |
| 12 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_{ICE}}{LHV_{f} \cdot \eta_{ICE}}$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 13 | $J_{eq} \dot{\omega} = T_{ICE} + T_{em} - T_{load}$ | Vehicle longitudinal dynamics | N·m |

> **Notation Note**: $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols have been replaced with $\cdot$ for clean rendering; $\eta$ = efficiency, $LHV$ = Lower Heating Value (~**42.5 MJ/kg** for petrol), $\tau$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular speed.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hybrid drives are **not** academic — they are the global transition technology between ICE and full Battery EVs. As of 2024:
- **Toyota** has sold **>20 million** hybrids cumulatively.
- The **Toyota Prius (1st gen, 1997)** used a **THS power-split** with a 1.5 L Atkinson ICE + 33 kW PMSM + 201.6 V NiMH battery.
- **Formula 1 (KERS / MGU-K)** uses a **120 kW MGU-K** to recover braking energy and deliver hybrid boost — a racing-derived hybrid topology.
- **Indian context**: **Maruti Suzuki Grand Vitara Intelligent Electric Hybrid**, **Toyota Urban Cruiser Hyryder**, **Honda City e:HEV** are mass-market Indian-market hybrids aligned with **CAFE-II** emission norms.

---

## 2.6 Advantages & Disadvantages Summary Table

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel economy | **+30% to +50%** over ICE alone | Complex control |
| Emissions | CO₂ reduced by **~25–40%** | Battery manufacturing footprint |
| Performance | Instant torque, smoother drive | Higher upfront cost (~₹1.5–3 lakh premium) |
| Range | No range anxiety (still has ICE) | Two systems → more failure modes |
| Maintenance | Regenerative braking extends brake life | Specialised technician required |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total System Power & Torque in a Parallel Hybrid

In a parallel hybrid, both sources drive the same shaft through a coupling gearbox. We derive the **wheel-level power** and the **fuel-equivalent energy consumption** over a drive cycle.

### Given
- Vehicle mass: $m$ (kg)
- Wheel radius: $r_w$ (m)
- Final drive ratio: $i_g$
- Drive cycle speed profile: $v(t)$
- Road load: $F_{load} = m g C_r + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ (rolling + aerodynamic drag)
- ICE efficiency: $\eta_{ICE}$, Motor efficiency: $\eta_{em}$, Battery coulombic efficiency: $\eta_{bat}$

### Step 1 — Express Wheel Power

Wheel speed in rad/s:
$$\omega_w = \dfrac{v}{r_w}$$

Wheel torque required to overcome road load:
$$T_{load} = F_{load} \cdot r_w$$

Hence, instantaneous wheel power:
$$P_{wheel} = T_{load} \cdot \omega_w = F_{load} \cdot v$$

### Step 2 — Energy Demanded at Battery Terminals (Motor-Only Mode)

If the motor alone delivers $P_{wheel}$, the electrical power drawn from the battery is:
$$P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{inv}}$$

where $\eta_{inv}$ is the inverter efficiency. Energy used over time interval $\Delta t$:
$$E_{bat} = \int_{0}^{T} \dfrac{P_{wheel}(t)}{\eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{inv}} \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Fuel-Equivalent Energy in Engine-Only Mode

If the ICE alone delivers $P_{wheel}$, fuel energy required:
$$E_{fuel} = \int_{0}^{T} \dfrac{P_{wheel}(t)}{\eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans}} \, dt$$

where $\eta_{trans}$ is transmission efficiency (~0.92–0.96).

### Step 4 — Combined (Hybrid) Mode Energy Balance

In hybrid mode, ICE produces $P_{ICE}$ and EM produces $P_{em}$, summing to $P_{wheel}$ after losses:
$$P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot P_{ICE} + \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot P_{bat}$$

### Step 5 — SOC Trajectory Equation

The SOC over a drive cycle evolves as:
$$\begin{aligned}
SOC(t) &= SOC(0) - \dfrac{1}{E_{bat,cap}} \int_{0}^{t} \left( \eta_{dis} \cdot P_{bat,out}(t) - \dfrac{P_{bat,in}(t)}{\eta_{ch}} \right) dt
\end{aligned}$$

For a charge-sustaining hybrid, we require:
$$\begin{aligned}
SOC(T) &\approx SOC(0)
\end{aligned}$$

which the HCU enforces by adjusting the ICE operating point and EM torque split.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

### Scenario
A vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg decelerates from $v_1 = 50$ km/h to $v_2 = 0$ km/h. Brake energy that *would* be wasted as heat in conventional friction brakes can be partially recovered.

### Step 1 — Convert Speeds to SI

$v_1 = 50 \text{ km/h} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$, $\quad v_2 = 0$

### Step 2 — Total Kinetic Energy Lost
$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (13.89)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_k &= 0.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot 192.93 \\
&= 144{,}700 \text{ J} \approx 144.7 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Regeneration Efficiency
Assuming $\eta_{regen} = 0.65$ (motor + inverter + battery round-trip during braking):
$$E_{recov} = \eta_{regen} \cdot E_k = 0.65 \cdot 144.7 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{recov} \approx 94.06 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Fuel Equivalence
Petrol LHV = **42.5 MJ/kg**, ICE efficiency in this load = ~0.25.
$$m_{fuel,saved} = \dfrac{E_{recov}}{LHV \cdot \eta_{ICE}} = \dfrac{94{,}060}{42{,}500{,}000 \cdot 0.25}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{fuel,saved} \approx 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 8.85 \text{ g per stop}
\end{aligned}$$

A typical urban drive cycle may have **200–400 stops/km in dense traffic** — multiplying yields **~1.8–3.5 g/km** fuel saved, or **~2–4%** direct fuel-economy gain purely from regeneration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimal Torque Split Using ECMS

The **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy** converts electrical energy into an equivalent fuel mass flow:

$$J(t) = \dot{m}_f(t) + s(t) \cdot \dfrac{P_{bat}(t)}{LHV_f \cdot \eta_{ICE,opt}}$$

where $s(t)$ is the **equivalence factor** (a tunable Lagrange multiplier) and $LHV_f$ is fuel lower heating value.

Minimising $J(t)$ subject to $P_{wheel} = P_{ICE} + P_{em}$ gives the instantaneous optimal torque split:
$$\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial P_{ICE}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{\partial \dot{m}_f}{\partial P_{ICE}} + \dfrac{s(t)}{LHV_f \cdot \eta_{ICE,opt}} = 0$$

This yields the operating rule: **use the electric motor when its equivalent fuel cost (weighted by $s$) is less than the actual fuel cost of running the ICE off its BSFC sweet spot.**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examiner's favourite line:**
> "ECMS achieves **near-optimal** real-time control by mimicking the global optimum of Dynamic Programming with **only one tunable parameter $s(t)$** — making it industry-standard in production hybrids."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Hybrid Topologies

| Criterion | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel (Power-Split) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical path to wheels | ❌ None (electrical only) | ✅ Direct | ✅ Both (split) |
| ICE always at optimal RPM? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Mostly yes |
| Overall efficiency (urban) | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Overall efficiency (highway) | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Complexity / Cost | Low | Low | High (planetary + 2 EMs) |
| Component count | Low | Lowest | Highest |
| Example vehicle | Chevy Volt (series mode) | Honda Insight (1st gen) | Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid |
| Indian example | — | Maruti Smart Hybrid (SHVS) | Toyota Hyryder e-Drive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE] **Rendering Note**: Mermaid is used for block-level functional architecture. All node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Hybrid Drive System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FUEL TANK] --> B1[ICE ENGINE]
    B1 --> C1{GENERATOR}
    C1 --> D1[DC DC CONVERTER]
    B1 --> E1{POWER SPLIT DEVICE}
    E1 --> F1[FINAL DRIVE]
    G1[TRACTION BATTERY HV] --> H1[INVERTER PCU]
    H1 --> F1
    F1 --> I1[WHEELS]
    J1[HCU ENERGY MANAGEMENT] -.controls.-> B1
    J1 -.controls.-> H1
    J1 -.monitors.-> G1
    K1[BRAKE PEDAL SENSOR] -.signal.-> J1
    L1[ACCEL PEDAL SENSOR] -.signal.-> J1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **FUEL TANK → ICE → POWER SPLIT DEVICE → WHEELS** path is the *mechanical* route (parallel mode).
- The **ICE → GENERATOR → INVERTER → WHEELS** route is the *series* path.
- The **HCU** sits at the top, reading sensor inputs and commanding actuators.

---

## 4.2 Operating-Mode Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START HCU CYCLE] --> Q1{SOC AND 0.4}
    Q1 -- YES --> M1[EV ONLY MODE]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{POWER DEMAND HIGH}
    Q2 -- NO --> M2[ENGINE ONLY MODE]
    Q2 -- YES --> M3[COMBINED HYBRID MODE]
    M1 --> Q3{BRAKING DETECTED}
    M2 --> Q3
    M3 --> Q3
    Q3 -- YES --> M4[REGEN BRAKING MODE]
    Q3 -- NO --> S1
    M4 --> S1
```

**Interpretation**: This is the rule-based decision tree executed every ~10 ms by the HCU.

---

## 4.3 Power-Flow in Five Modes (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MODE1[IDLE STOP]
        A1A[ICE OFF] --- A2A[EM OFF]
    end
    subgraph MODE2[MOTOR ONLY]
        B1A[BATTERY] --> B2A[INVERTER] --> B3A[MOTOR] --> B4A[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph MODE3[ENGINE ONLY]
        C1A[FUEL] --> C2A[ICE] --> C3A[GEARBOX] --> C4A[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph MODE4[COMBINED]
        D1A[FUEL] --> D2A[ICE]
        D3A[BATTERY] --> D4A[INVERTER] --> D5A[MOTOR]
        D2A --> D6A[WHEELS]
        D5A --> D6A
    end
    subgraph MODE5[REGEN]
        E1A[WHEELS] --> E2A[MOTOR GEN] --> E3A[INVERTER] --> E4A[BATTERY CHARGE]
    end
```

> [!TIP] **Tip for Drawing in Exams**: Always use **bold arrows** for power flow, **dashed arrows** for control signals, and a **small inset legend**. This is exactly what KTU examiners reward in the 7-mark "neat sketch" questions.

---

## 4.4 Series-Parallel (THS) Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ICE ENGINE MG1 MG2 PLANETARY] --> P2[POWER SPLIT EPICYCLIC GEAR]
    P2 --> P3[RING GEAR TO WHEELS]
    P2 --> P4[SUN GEAR TO MG1 GENERATOR]
    P4 --> P5[INVERTER 1]
    P5 --> P6[HV BATTERY]
    P6 --> P7[INVERTER 2]
    P7 --> P8[MG2 TRACTION MOTOR]
    P8 --> P3
```

**Toyota THS Insight**: MG1 (motor-generator 1) controls ICE RPM *and* acts as generator; MG2 drives the wheels directly. The planetary gear makes this possible without any clutch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV). List any **four** advantages of a hybrid drive over a conventional ICE vehicle.
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is one that combines **two or more energy sources** — typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EM)** with an **energy storage device (battery)** — to propel the vehicle, with an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** coordinating the power split.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Improved fuel economy** (30–50% over conventional ICE) due to ICE operating near its BSFC sweet-spot and regenerative braking.
2. **Reduced tailpipe emissions** (CO₂, NOₓ, HC drop by 25–40%) since the ICE can be turned off at idle and at low loads.
3. **Better drivability and performance** — electric motor delivers **instant peak torque from zero RPM**, eliminating turbo-lag and improving acceleration.
4. **Energy recovery** — regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy otherwise dissipated as heat, extending brake life and reducing fuel use.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often write "electric vehicle" as the definition of HEV. **An HEV is NOT a pure EV** — it **retains the ICE** as a primary or secondary source. Losing 1 mark here is common.

---

### Q2. What is **Regenerative Braking**? State the factors that limit the amount of energy recoverable.
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Regenerative braking** is the process by which the **electric traction motor is operated as a generator** during deceleration, converting the vehicle's **kinetic energy into electrical energy** which is then stored in the high-voltage battery, instead of dissipating it as heat in friction brakes.

**Factors limiting recoverable energy:**
1. **State of Charge (SOC)** — if SOC is already high (e.g., >0.85), the Battery Management System (BMS) limits further charging to prevent overcharge.
2. **Battery charge acceptance rate** — limited by C-rating and thermal limits of cells.
3. **Mechanical brake blending** — for hard braking or low-speed stops, friction brakes must take over, as the motor cannot absorb all kinetic energy.
4. **Driveline losses & motor-generator efficiency** — round-trip efficiency of the regeneration path is typically only **60–70%**.
5. **Safety / regulatory requirements** — ABS/ESC interventions may override regeneration.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**: Do **not** confuse regenerative braking with **engine braking** in conventional ICE vehicles. They are fundamentally different mechanisms.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> *Attempt any ONE full question from the pair. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7 sub-parts).*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted]**

#### (a) With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle** drivetrain architecture. Discuss its **operating modes** and **state TWO advantages** and **TWO disadvantages** compared to a parallel hybrid. **[7 Marks]**

#### (b) A Series Hybrid bus has the following specifications:
- Vehicle mass $m = 12{,}000$ kg
- ICE-generator set efficiency $\eta_{gset} = 0.38$
- Traction motor + inverter combined efficiency $\eta_{em} = 0.92$
- Final drive + wheel efficiency $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$
- The bus climbs a gradient with road load $F_{load} = 8000$ N at a constant speed $v = 36$ km/h.

**Calculate:**
1. Power demanded at the wheels, $P_{wheel}$ (in kW).
2. Electrical power demanded from the generator, $P_{gen}$.
3. Fuel power input required, $P_{fuel}$.
4. Hourly fuel consumption (in kg/h), given petrol LHV = **42.5 MJ/kg**.

**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
   FUEL TANK ──► ICE ──► GENERATOR ──► RECTIFIER
                                       │
                                       ▼
                              DC-DC / DC-AC INVERTER
                                       │
                                       ▼
                          TRACTION MOTOR ──► TRANSMISSION ──► WHEELS
                                       ▲
                                       │
                              HV BATTERY (Bidirectional)
                                       ▲
                                       │
                                     HCU
```

**Description:**
In a **Series Hybrid**, the ICE is **mechanically isolated** from the wheels. It drives a **generator** whose electrical output either:
- Charges the **HV battery** via a rectifier/DC-DC converter, OR
- Directly feeds the **traction motor** through the inverter.

The wheels are driven **only** by the traction motor.

**Operating Modes:**
1. **Pure EV Mode** (low demand, SOC adequate): ICE OFF, motor alone drives wheels from battery.
2. **ICE + Battery Charging Mode** (low/medium demand, SOC low): ICE runs generator at **optimal BSFC point**, charging battery + supplying motor.
3. **Boost Mode** (high demand, e.g., hill climb): ICE + battery together feed traction motor.
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode**: traction motor acts as generator, charging battery.

**Advantages over Parallel:**
1. ICE can operate **continuously at its BSFC sweet spot** (constant RPM) → better urban fuel economy.
2. **Simpler mechanical transmission** — no clutch needed; ideal for heavy CV applications like city buses.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Three energy conversions** (mech → elec → mech) → cumulative losses; highway efficiency suffers.
2. **Generator and traction motor must each be sized for full peak power** → heavier and costlier.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**: Drawing the diagram **without arrows showing energy flow direction** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always use **directional arrows** and **label every block**.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Given:**
- $m = 12{,}000$ kg (not needed directly here)
- $\eta_{gset} = 0.38$
- $\eta_{em} = 0.92$
- $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$
- $F_{load} = 8000$ N
- $v = 36$ km/h $= 10$ m/s
- $LHV_{petrol} = 42.5$ MJ/kg $= 42{,}500$ kJ/kg

**Sub-part (i): Power at wheels**

$$P_{wheel} = F_{load} \cdot v = 8000 \cdot 10 = 80{,}000 \text{ W} = \mathbf{80 \text{ kW}}$$

**Sub-part (ii): Electrical power from generator**

The motor must deliver $P_{wheel} / \eta_{fd}$ at its shaft, hence the **electrical input to motor**:

$$P_{elec,motor} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{fd}} = \dfrac{80}{0.92 \cdot 0.95}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{elec,motor} = \dfrac{80}{0.874} \approx 91.53 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming all electrical power comes from the generator (battery is sustaining, not providing net), the **generator electrical output** ≈ **91.53 kW**.

**Sub-part (iii): Fuel power input**

$$P_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{elec,motor}}{\eta_{gset}} = \dfrac{91.53}{0.38}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{fuel} \approx 240.87 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sub-part (iv): Hourly fuel consumption**

Energy per hour:
$$E_{fuel,h} = P_{fuel} \cdot 3600 \text{ s} = 240.87 \cdot 3600 = 867{,}132 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{fuel}}{LHV} = \dfrac{240.87}{42{,}500}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel} \approx 5.67 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Per hour:
$$m_{fuel,h} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot 3600 \approx 5.67 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3600$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{fuel,h} \approx 20.4 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students frequently forget to **convert 36 km/h to m/s** ($36 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 10$) and end up with $P_{wheel} = 288$ kW — a 3.6× error. Always convert units **first**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Model Paper]**

#### (a) Explain the **Parallel Hybrid** drivetrain with a block diagram. Compare its **power flow** in motor-only, engine-only, and combined modes. Mention **two real-world vehicles** using this topology. **[7 Marks]**

#### (b) A passenger car has a hybrid drive with the following parameters:
- Vehicle mass $m = 1800$ kg
- Decelerates from $v_1 = 80$ km/h to $v_2 = 20$ km/h
- Regenerative efficiency $\eta_{regen} = 0.68$
- Battery capacity usable $E_{bat,cap} = 1.5$ kWh = 5.4 MJ

**Calculate:**
1. Kinetic energy dissipated, $E_k$ (kJ).
2. Energy recovered, $E_{recov}$ (kJ).
3. Increase in State of Charge (ΔSOC) assuming initial SOC = 0.45.
4. Comment on whether a **single regenerative stop** can shift the SOC band meaningfully.

**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Block Diagram:**

```
   FUEL ──► ICE ──┐
                 ├──► CLUTCH/COUPLER ──► TRANSMISSION ──► WHEELS
   BATTERY ──► INVERTER ──► MOTOR ──┘
                       ▲
                       │
                      HCU
```

In a **Parallel Hybrid**, both ICE and Electric Motor are **mechanically coupled to the driveshaft** through a clutch or torque-coupler. They can drive **independently or simultaneously**.

**Power flow comparison:**

| Mode | Power source at wheels | HCU Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Motor-only | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot P_{bat}$ | Low speed, SOC OK |
| Engine-only | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot P_{fuel}$ | Highway cruise |
| Combined | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{em} P_{bat} + \eta_{ICE} P_{fuel}$ | High torque demand |
| Regen | $P_{bat,in} = \eta_{regen} \cdot P_{wheel,brake}$ | Brake pedal pressed |

**Real-world parallel hybrids:**
1. **Honda Insight (1st generation, 1999–2006)** — Integrated Motor Assist (IMA).
2. **Honda City e:HEV (India, 2022–present)** — though technically a series-parallel, base architecture is parallel-derived.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Given:**
- $m = 1800$ kg
- $v_1 = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s
- $v_2 = 20$ km/h $= 5.56$ m/s
- $\eta_{regen} = 0.68$
- $E_{bat,cap} = 5.4 \times 10^6$ J = 5400 kJ

**Sub-part (i): Kinetic Energy Dissipated**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_k &= \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1800 \cdot (22.22^2 - 5.56^2) \\
&= 900 \cdot (493.83 - 30.91) \\
&= 900 \cdot 462.92 \\
&= 416{,}628 \text{ J} \\
&\approx \mathbf{416.63 \text{ kJ}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sub-part (ii): Energy Recovered**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{recov} &= \eta_{regen} \cdot E_k = 0.68 \cdot 416.63 \\
&\approx \mathbf{283.31 \text{ kJ}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sub-part (iii): ΔSOC**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta SOC &= \dfrac{E_{recov}}{E_{bat,cap}} = \dfrac{283.31}{5400} \\
&\approx 0.0525 = \mathbf{5.25\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sub-part (iv): Comment**

A single stop from 80 → 20 km/h raises SOC from 0.45 to **~0.5025**, which **is meaningful** — it sits within the typical charge-sustaining band (0.3–0.8). In dense urban driving with multiple such events, regeneration **can sustain the SOC** without external charging, which is the central reason parallel hybrids are viable as **self-charging** vehicles.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**: Converting kWh to Joules requires $\times 3.6 \times 10^6$. A common error: forgetting the unit conversion and reporting $\Delta SOC \approx 0.0005$ instead of $0.0525$ — a 100× error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute Revision — Read 30 mins Before Exam**

- ✅ **Definition**: HEV = **≥2 energy sources + EMS**. Not the same as BEV.
- ✅ **Three topologies**: **Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split)**.
- ✅ **Power-Split device** in Toyota THS = **planetary gear (epicyclic)**.
- ✅ **5–6 Operating Modes**: Idle, Motor-Only, Engine-Only, Combined, Regen, Charging.
- ✅ **HCU layers**: Top-level EMS → Mid-level coordinator → Low-level actuator control.
- ✅ **BSFC sweet-spot** for petrol ICE ≈ **35–40% thermal efficiency** region; goal is to operate there.
- ✅ **Regen energy formula**: $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$; recovered $E_{recov} = \eta_{regen} \cdot E_k$.
- ✅ **SOC operating band** typically **0.3 – 0.8** (charge-sustaining hybrids).
- ✅ **PMSM** is the dominant traction motor choice in modern hybrids (high power density).
- ✅ **Standard drive cycles**: NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75 — know at least 2.
- ✅ **Real-world examples**: Toyota Prius (THS), Honda Insight (IMA), Honda City e:HEV, Toyota Hyryder.
- ✅ **F1 MGU-K** = 120 kW hybrid assist = real-world racing relevance.
- ✅ **Conversion factors**: $1$ kWh $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ J; $1$ km/h $= 5/18$ m/s; $LHV_{petrol} \approx 42.5$ MJ/kg.
- ✅ **Formula to memorise cold**: $P_{wheel} = F_{load} \cdot v$ and $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$.
- ✅ **Common acronyms**: SOC, SOH, EMS, HCU, PCU, BMS, IGBT, PMSM, THS, IMA, MGU-K.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PEAET634 MODULE 3 — HYBRID DRIVE CONCEPT**
