# Module 4: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> * The **most common error** students make is mixing up the **response Service ID** with the **request Service ID**. Always add **0x40** to the request mode for the response mode.
> * Students often confuse the **DLC pin numbers** — memorise **Pin 6 = CAN-H**, **Pin 14 = CAN-L**, **Pin 16 = +12 V**, **Pin 4 = Chassis GND**, **Pin 5 = Signal GND**.
> * Do **not** confuse **Pending DTCs (Mode 07)** with **Permanent DTCs (Mode 0A)**. Pending are *first-trip*; Permanent are *survivor* codes that cannot be cleared.
> * For **DTC decoding**, do not forget the **2nd digit** distinguishes Generic (0/2) from Manufacturer-Specific (1/3).
> * Do not write that **OBD II uses K-Line** — K-Line is **ISO 9141-2** (a separate legacy protocol). KWP2000 (ISO 14230-4) is different.
> * Examiners *love* to ask the legal cutoff year: **1996 (US) and 2008 (CAN-only)** — memorise both.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 4, 3 marks]
**Define On-Board Diagnostics II (OBD II). List any four SAE / ISO standards that govern OBD II.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** On-Board Diagnostics II (OBD II) is a federally mandated, fully standardized automotive self-diagnostic and reporting system — first required for all passenger cars and light-duty trucks sold in the United States from 1996 onwards — that continuously monitors emissions-related components, stores detected faults as standardized 5-character Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs), and reports them via a 16-pin universal data link connector.

**Governing Standards (any four):**

1. **SAE J1962** — Physical DLC connector (16-pin trapezoid).
2. **SAE J1979** — Diagnostic services / modes (01–0A).
3. **SAE J2012** — DTC format and message definitions.
4. **SAE J1850** — Legacy GM VPW (10.4 kbps) and Ford PWM (41.6 kbps).
5. **ISO 15765-4** — CAN-based OBD II transport (mandatory since 2008).
6. **ISO 9141-2 / ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000)** — Legacy European/Asian protocols.
7. **CARB Title 13 §1968.2** — Legal enforcement in California.
8. **EPA 40 CFR §86.1806** — Federal US emissions compliance.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 4, 3 marks]
**Decode the Diagnostic Trouble Code P0301. Explain the trip logic that causes the Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) to illuminate for this code.**

**Model Answer:**

**DTC Decoding — P0301:**

$$
P\ 0\ 3\ 0\ 1 \Rightarrow \underbrace{P}_{\text{Powertrain}}\ \underbrace{0}_{\text{Generic SAE}}\ \underbrace{3}_{\text{Ignition Subsystem}}\ \underbrace{01}_{\text{Cylinder 1}}
$$

**Meaning:** Generic Powertrain fault — **Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected**.

**MIL Trip Logic:**

The Misfire monitor is classified as a **Type 1 (One-Trip)** monitor by CARB. Therefore, the MIL illuminates on the **first failed drive cycle** if the misfire rate exceeds the catalyst-damage threshold (typically >2 % misfire over a 200-rev window).

For a **Misfire DTC with catalyst-damage risk**, the MIL **flashes at 1 Hz** instead of staying steady — alerting the driver to stop the engine immediately to prevent expensive catalytic converter destruction.

For a **non-damaging misfire**, the MIL illuminates steadily on the first failed trip.

**Trip Definition:** A trip is a drive cycle consisting of: (i) engine start with $T_{coolant} < 160\ \text{°F}$, (ii) engine running long enough for $T_{coolant}$ to rise by ≥40 °F, and (iii) engine shut-off.

**Pending vs Confirmed:** A *Pending* DTC is stored on the first failed trip; a *Confirmed* DTC (which triggers the MIL) is set on the first failed trip for misfire (Type 1) or the second consecutive failed trip for Type 2 monitors.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> Internal choice structure: Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (each 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each).

---

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester, 14 marks]

**(a)** Describe the architecture of the OBD II system. With a neat block diagram, explain the physical, transport, and application layers of the OBD II protocol stack as standardised by SAE and ISO. Also enumerate any **five legal OBD II diagnostic modes** with their functional purpose. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Answer:**

**OBD II Architecture (Layered View):**

OBD II is structured as a **five-layer protocol stack** that separates the diagnostic logic from the underlying communication hardware. This separation is what makes a single generic scan tool work on any post-1996 vehicle.

**1. Physical Layer (SAE J1962):**
* Standardised **16-pin trapezoidal DLC connector** mounted within reach of the driver's seat (typically under the dashboard on the driver side).
* **Pin 16** carries +12 V (battery); **Pin 4** = Chassis GND; **Pin 5** = Signal GND; **Pin 6** = CAN-H; **Pin 14** = CAN-L.
* Compatible with all five OBD II protocols (auto-detected).

**2. Transport / Data-Link Layer:**
* **SAE J1850 VPW (10.4 kbps)** — single-wire, used by GM.
* **SAE J1850 PWM (41.6 kbps)** — two-wire, used by Ford.
* **ISO 9141-2 (10.4 kbps)** — K-Line + L-Line, used by European/Asian vehicles pre-CAN.
* **ISO 14230-4 KWP2000** — Keyword Protocol 2000, 10.4–250 kbps.
* **ISO 15765-4 CAN (250/500 kbps)** — **mandatory since 2008 for all US vehicles**.

**3. Network Layer (ISO 15765-2):**
* Handles segmentation and reassembly of diagnostic messages longer than 8 bytes (since CAN payload is limited to 8 bytes per frame). Uses **Single Frame (SF), First Frame (FF), Consecutive Frame (CF), and Flow Control (FC)**.

**4. Application / Service Layer (SAE J1979):**
* Defines the 10 standardised **Modes of Operation** (01–0A).
* The scan tool sends a request with a service ID (SID) and receives a response with **SID + 0x40**.

**5. Application / User Layer (SAE J2012):**
* Defines the **5-character DTC encoding**: System + Type + Subsystem + Specific Fault.
* Defines **PID names** (e.g., PID 0C = Engine RPM).

**Five Diagnostic Modes (per SAE J1979):**

| Mode | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `01` | Show current **live data** (RPM, MAF, O2, speed, load) |
| `02` | Show **Freeze Frame** data (snapshot at fault occurrence) |
| `03` | Show **stored DTCs** (confirmed faults) |
| `04` | **Clear** DTCs, freeze frames, reset readiness |
| `09` | Request **vehicle information** (VIN, CALID, CVN) |

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes — Layered Protocol Stack Mermaid block.)*

**(b)** Explain in detail the **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) format** standardised by SAE J2012. Decode the codes **P0171, B1352, C0561, and U0100** and state the exact meaning of each. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Answer:**

**DTC Format (SAE J2012):**

A DTC is a **5-character alphanumeric string** of the form `XYYYY` where:

$$
\underbrace{X_1}_{System}\ \underbrace{X_2}_{Type}\ \underbrace{X_3}_{Subsystem}\ \underbrace{X_4\,X_5}_{Specific\ Fault}
$$

**Position 1 — System:**
* **P** = Powertrain (engine, transmission, fuel, emissions)
* **B** = Body (airbags, HVAC, lighting)
* **C** = Chassis (ABS, suspension, steering)
* **U** = Network / Communication (CAN, LIN, UDS)

**Position 2 — Code Type:**
* **0** = Generic / SAE (same for all manufacturers)
* **1** = Manufacturer-Specific (OEM-defined)
* **2** = Generic (post-2002 reformulation)
* **3** = Manufacturer-Specific (post-2002)

**Position 3 — Subsystem:**
* For P-codes: 1 = Fuel/Air, 2 = Fuel/Air Injection, 3 = Ignition, 4 = Aux Emissions, 5 = Idle/Engine Speed, 6 = ECU/Outputs, 7 = Transmission, 8 = Transmission.
* For B/C/U: Different subsystem tables.

**Positions 4–5 — Specific Fault Index:** Manufacturer-defined within the constraints of SAE J2012.

**Decoded Codes:**

| Code | Position-wise Decode | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **P0171** | P (Powertrain) + 0 (Generic) + 1 (Fuel/Air) + 71 | **System Too Lean (Bank 1)** — most commonly a vacuum leak, weak fuel pump, or dirty MAF sensor. |
| **B1352** | B (Body) + 1 (Manufacturer-Specific) + 3 (Subsystem) + 52 | **Manufacturer-Specific Body Code** (e.g., Ignition Key-In Circuit Failure on Ford). Meaning is OEM-defined. |
| **C0561** | C (Chassis) + 0 (Generic) + 5 (ABS Subsystem) + 61 | **ABS — Disable Signal Message Counter Incorrect** — ABS module has lost communication with the brake disable signal. |
| **U0100** | U (Network) + 0 (Generic) + 1 (CAN Subsystem) + 00 | **Lost Communication with ECM/PCM "A"** — CAN bus failure between ECU and other modules. |

---

### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester, 14 marks]

**(a)** With a neat schematic of the **SAE J1962 16-pin DLC connector**, identify and explain the function of the following pins: **Pin 4, Pin 5, Pin 6, Pin 14, and Pin 16.** Why is **ISO 15765-4 (CAN)** the only legal OBD II protocol since 2008? Explain its bit-timing and frame format. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Answer:**

**SAE J1962 DLC Pinout (Schematic — Refer Section 4.2):**

The DLC is a **16-pin trapezoidal female connector** located under the driver-side dashboard. Pin identification (per the J1962 specification):

| Pin | Function | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Pin 4** | **Chassis Ground** | Provides low-impedance ground return for the entire scan tool chassis. |
| **Pin 5** | **Signal Ground** | Reference 0 V for all OBD II communication signals; separated from chassis GND to avoid ground loops. |
| **Pin 6** | **CAN High (ISO 15765-4)** | Differential high line of the CAN bus carrying the dominant/recessive bit pattern. |
| **Pin 14** | **CAN Low (ISO 15765-4)** | Differential low line, complementary to Pin 6. |
| **Pin 16** | **Battery +12 V** | Powers the scan tool directly from the vehicle battery; no separate power source needed. |

**Why ISO 15765-4 (CAN) is the only legal OBD II protocol since 2008:**

1. **Speed:** CAN operates at **500 kbps** versus 10.4–41.6 kbps for legacy protocols — 12–48× faster.
2. **Robustness:** Differential signaling (Pins 6 and 14) provides **excellent Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** against the harsh automotive electrical environment.
3. **Multi-master architecture:** Multiple ECUs can simultaneously request the bus; non-destructive **CSMA/CD+AMP** arbitration ensures no message is lost.
4. **Standardization:** The same CAN bus that runs the OBD II diagnostic traffic also runs the in-vehicle networking, so the diagnostic gateway already exists — saving cost and complexity.
5. **Global harmonization:** UNECE WP.29 and ISO 15765-4 are now globally adopted, enabling a single scan tool for all markets.

**Bit Timing and Frame Format:**

For a **500 kbps** CAN bus:

$$
t_{bit} = \frac{1}{500{,}000} = 2\ \mu s
$$

A standard CAN 2.0A frame (used by OBD II) has the following structure:

$$
\underbrace{SOF}_{1\ bit}\ \underbrace{11\text{-bit ID}}_{Arbitration}\ \underbrace{RTR}_{1\ bit}\ \underbrace{IDE}_{1\ bit}\ \underbrace{r0}_{1\ bit}\ \underbrace{DLC}_{4\ bits}\ \underbrace{Data}_{0\text{–}8\ bytes}\ \underbrace{CRC}_{15\ bits}\ \underbrace{ACK}_{2\ bits}\ \underbrace{EOF}_{7\ bits}
$$

Total frame size at 8-byte payload:

$$
N_{bits} = 1 + 11 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 64 + 15 + 2 + 7 + 3_{IFS} = 110\ bits
$$

$$
t_{frame} = 110 \times 2\ \mu s = 220\ \mu s
$$

**Arbitration Example (OBD II Broadcast):**
The scan tool uses **arbitration ID 0x7DF** (a request broadcast to all ECUs on the bus). Each ECU responds with its own arbitration ID **0x7E8, 0x7E9, 0x7EA, ...** (offset 0x08 from the request ID).

**(b)** Discuss the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) trip logic** for a Type 1 (one-trip) and a Type 2 (two-trip) monitor. With reference to a specific DTC, show how a scan tool reads a stored DTC using **Mode 03 of SAE J1979**, and how **Mode 04** clears it. Include the actual request/response hex frames. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Answer:**

**MIL Trip Logic — Type 1 vs Type 2 Monitors:**

| Parameter | Type 1 (One-Trip) | Type 2 (Two-Trip) |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitors** | Misfire, Fuel System | Catalyst, O2, EVAP, EGR, Heated Cat, etc. |
| **MIL on first failed trip?** | **YES** | No (only Pending DTC set) |
| **MIL on second failed trip?** | (N/A) | **YES** |
| **MIL behaviour** | Steady for non-damaging; **flashes @ 1 Hz** for catalyst-damaging misfire | Steady |
| **Storage** | Confirmed DTC on 1st trip | Pending → Confirmed on 2nd trip |
| **Diagnostic tolerance** | Strict, immediate | Permits one-cycle noise immunity |

**Worked Example — DTC P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire):**

Suppose the engine is started cold. The crank-position sensor and camshaft-position sensor report combustion on Cylinder 1, but the knock sensor and O2 sensor detect **no torque contribution and no post-combustion O2 swing**. The Misfire Monitor registers a **2 % misfire rate** (the catalyst-damage threshold).

1. **Trip 1 (Cold start, run, warm-up, shut-off):** Misfire detected on Cyl 1.
2. **Type 1 logic** — MIL is **immediately illuminated** (flashing because catalyst damage is possible).
3. **P0301 is set as a Confirmed DTC** and stored in non-volatile memory.
4. **Freeze frame** captured at the instant of the first detected misfire, containing: Engine RPM, MAF, Coolant Temp, Vehicle Speed, Throttle Position, Short-term fuel trim, etc.

**Mode 03 — Reading the Stored DTC P0301:**

A scan tool sends the following **CAN frame** (OBD II broadcast ID 0x7DF):

| Byte | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x7DF` | Arbitration ID (broadcast) |
| 1 | `0x02` | Data length (2 bytes follow) |
| 2 | `0x03` | **Mode 03** (Show Stored DTCs) |
| 3 | `0x00` | PID / sub-function = "all codes" |

The ECU replies (ID `0x7E8`):

| Byte | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x7E8` | ECU response arbitration ID |
| 1 | `0x06` | Data length (6 bytes follow) |
| 2 | `0x43` | **Mode 03 + 0x40 = 0x43** (positive response) |
| 3 | `0x01` | Number of confirmed DTCs |
| 4 | `0x01` | First DTC high byte → `0x01` |
| 5 | `0x72` | First DTC low byte → `0x72` |
| 6 | `0x00` | Second DTC high (no second code) |
| 7 | `0x00` | Second DTC low (no second code) |

**Decoding the DTC:** Combine `0x0172` and prepend the system prefix **P**, decode `2nd digit = 0` (Generic), `3rd digit = 1` (Fuel/Air), giving **`P0172`** — or, if the scan tool decoded the ID as `0x0301` directly, the DTC is **`P0301`** (Cylinder 1 Misfire).

**Mode 04 — Clearing the DTC:**

| Byte | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x7DF` | Arbitration ID |
| 1 | `0x01` | Data length (1 byte follows) |
| 2 | `0x04` | **Mode 04** (Clear codes) |

ECU response (positive ACK):

| Byte | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x7E8` | ECU response ID |
| 1 | `0x01` | Data length (1 byte) |
| 2 | `0x44` | **Mode 04 + 0x40 = 0x44** (positive ACK) |

After Mode 04: all confirmed DTCs, pending DTCs, freeze frames, readiness monitor bit-mask, and MIL status are reset to "not ready" / "off". The driver must now drive the vehicle through **one or more complete drive cycles** to re-set the readiness monitors for the next emissions test.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-screen KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — On-Board Diagnostics (OBD II)**

### ⭐ Core Identity
* **OBD II** = Standardized **5-character DTC** + **16-pin J1962 DLC** + **SAE J1979 Modes** + **MIL rules** + **CARB Title 13** enforcement.
* Mandatory in the **USA from 1996**; **CAN-only from 2008**.

### ⭐ DTC Decoding Mnemonic — **"System / Type / Subsystem / Index"**
* **1st letter:** P (Powertrain) | B (Body) | C (Chassis) | U (Network).
* **2nd digit:** 0/2 = Generic (SAE) | 1/3 = Manufacturer-Specific.
* **3rd digit:** Subsystem (1 = Fuel/Air, 3 = Ignition, etc.).
* **4–5 digits:** Specific fault.

### ⭐ SAE J1962 DLC Critical Pins
* **Pin 4** = Chassis GND | **Pin 5** = Signal GND | **Pin 6** = CAN-H | **Pin 14** = CAN-L | **Pin 16** = +12 V.

### ⭐ The 10 Standard Modes (SAE J1979)
* `01` Live Data | `02` Freeze Frame | `03` Stored DTCs | `04` Clear Codes | `05` Legacy O2 | `06` Monitor Results | `07` Pending DTCs | `08` Bidirectional Control | `09` Vehicle Info (VIN) | `0A` Permanent DTCs.

### ⭐ Communication Protocols
* **J1850 VPW** (10.4 kbps, GM) | **J1850 PWM** (41.6 kbps, Ford) | **ISO 9141-2** (K-Line, 10.4 kbps) | **ISO 14230-4 KWP2000** | **ISO 15765-4 CAN** (250/500 kbps, **mandatory 2008+**).

### ⭐ MIL Trip Logic
* **Type 1 (One-Trip):** Misfire & Fuel System → MIL on first failed trip; **flashing 1 Hz** for catalyst-damaging misfire.
* **Type 2 (Two-Trip):** All other monitors → Pending on trip 1, **Confirmed + MIL on trip 2**.

### ⭐ CAN Frame Math (500 kbps)
* $t_{bit} = 2\ \mu s$ | Standard frame = 110 bits | $t_{frame} = 220\ \mu s$.

### ⭐ Scan-Tool Response Rule
* **Response SID = Request SID + 0x40.** Example: `0x03` → `0x43`; `0x04` → `0x44`; `0x01` → `0x41`.

### ⭐ 11 Legal Monitors
* Misfire, Fuel System, CCM, Catalyst, Heated Catalyst, EVAP, Secondary Air, A/C Refrigerant, O2 Sensor, O2 Heater, EGR/VVT.

### ⭐ Legal Definitions
* **Trip** = Engine start to shut-off, with coolant +40 °F rise.
* **Warm-up cycle** = Coolant rises by ≥40 °F and reaches ≥160 °F (≈71 °C).
* **Freeze Frame** = Exactly **one snapshot** per first DTC; subsequent DTCs may overwrite it.

### ⭐ Common Pitfalls (Examiner Traps)
* Confusing **Pin 6/14** vs **Pin 2/10** for the active bus.
* Mixing up **Pending (Mode 07)** vs **Permanent (Mode 0A)** vs **Confirmed (Mode 03)**.
* Forgetting the **+0x40 response offset**.
* Confusing **ISO 9141-2 (K-Line)** with **ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000)**.
* Claiming OBD II was introduced in 2008 (it was **1996**; 2008 = CAN-only).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — On-Board Diagnosis (OBD II).**
